topics.title: Participation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Participation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Participation** in the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) refers to the formal accession, ratification, or adherence of a sovereign nation to international intellectual property treaties, conventions, and agreements administered by bodies such as the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**, the **World Trade Organization (WTO)**, and other multilateral fora. It signifies a country's voluntary commitment to harmonize its domestic IP laws with internationally recognized minimum standards and to engage in cross-border cooperation for the protection, enforcement, and administration of intellectual property rights.

Under the **TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights, 1994)**, participation extends beyond mere treaty accession to include active implementation through national legislation, establishment of administrative mechanisms, and provision of enforcement procedures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> Participation is NOT merely "signing a treaty." It is a three-tier process: **(1) Signature → (2) Ratification/Accession → (3) Domestic Implementation**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **international club of property owners** (the IP club). To become a member:

- **Joining the club** = Signing/ratifying the treaty (e.g., Paris Convention, PCT, TRIPS)
- **Following the club's rules** = Harmonizing domestic laws with treaty obligations
- **Paying annual fees** = Maintaining treaty membership through administrative/financial contributions
- **Attending meetings** = Active participation in WIPO assemblies, WTO ministerial conferences, and dispute settlement bodies

Once you join, you get **two big benefits**:
1. **Reciprocity** — Other member countries protect your IP just like they protect their own citizens' IP (called "National Treatment").
2. **Priority rights** — Your earlier filing date in one country can be claimed in another (like in the Paris Convention's right of priority).

But you also take on **obligations** — you must provide minimum standards of IP protection to foreign nationals in your country.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** India's accession to the **Paris Convention in 1998** and the **PCT in 1998** allowed Indian inventors to file patents internationally and claim priority based on Indian filing dates. This was India's formal *participation* in the global IP system.

## 1.3 Key Stakeholders in Participation

| Stakeholder | Role in Participation |
|---|---|
| **Sovereign States** | Sign, ratify, and implement treaties |
| **WIPO** | Administers 26+ international IP treaties |
| **WTO** | Enforces TRIPS through dispute settlement |
| **National IP Offices** | Domestic implementing agencies (e.g., IPO India, USPTO) |
| **Inventors/Creators** | Beneficiaries of international protection |
| **Developing Nations** | Often seek special flexibilities (e.g., TRIPS flexibilities) |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Concept Map:** Participation operates on three interconnected planes — **Legal** (treaty obligations), **Administrative** (national IP offices), and **Economic** (trade and technology transfer implications).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Participation in IP Treaties

### A. Participation in WIPO-Administered Treaties

**WIPO** is the global forum for IP services, policy, information, and cooperation. As of recent records, WIPO has **193 member states**, making it one of the most widely participated UN specialized agencies.

Key treaties and participation status:

| Treaty | Year | Purpose | India's Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paris Convention** | 1883 | Protection of industrial property | Member (since 1998) |
| **Berne Convention** | 1886 | Protection of literary & artistic works | Member (since 1928) |
| **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** | 1970 | International patent filing | Member (since 1998) |
| **Madrid Protocol** | 1989 | International trademark registration | Member (since 2013) |
| **Hague Agreement** | 1999 | International design registration | Member (since 2017) |
| **Budapest Treaty** | 1977 | Deposit of microorganisms | Member (since 2001) |
| **Nice Agreement** | 1957 | Classification of goods & services | Member (since 2009) |

### B. Participation in WTO/TRIPS

**TRIPS** is unique because participation is **mandatory for all WTO members**. Countries joining the WTO must accept TRIPS without deviation (single undertaking principle). India became a TRIPS participant upon joining the WTO on **1 January 1995**.

### C. Participation in Regional IP Agreements

Examples include:
- **European Patent Convention (EPC)** — Regional patent system for Europe
- **African Regional Intellectual Property Organization (ARIPO)**
- **European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO)**

## 2.2 Modes of Becoming a Party to a Treaty

A state can become a party to an international treaty through any of the following modes:

**(1) Signature:** Initial expression of consent. Often followed by ratification for the treaty to enter into force domestically.

**(2) Ratification:** Formal confirmation by the state, after which the treaty becomes legally binding.

**(3) Accession (or Adherence):** Used when a state did not sign the treaty initially but later wishes to join. It has the same legal effect as ratification.

**(4) Acceptance or Approval:** Alternative terms for ratification used in some treaties (e.g., WTO agreements).

**(5) Succession:** When a newly independent state declares it will continue treaty obligations of the predecessor state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Signature** alone ≠ binding obligation (requires ratification in most cases)
> - **Ratification/Accession** = legally binding
> - **Entry into Force** = different from ratification; it is when the treaty becomes operative internationally (often after a minimum number of ratifications)

## 2.3 Benefits of Participation

1. **National Treatment Principle:** Foreign IP holders receive the same protection as nationals (Article 2 of Paris Convention, Article 3 of TRIPS).
2. **Right of Priority:** A 12-month priority period (Paris Convention) for patents, allowing inventors to file in multiple countries based on the first filing date.
3. **International Filing Mechanisms:** PCT enables a single international application that can later be pursued in 150+ contracting states.
4. **Technology Transfer Facilitation:** Multilateral frameworks encourage cross-border licensing and technology flows.
5. **Dispute Resolution Access:** WTO's Dispute Settlement Body (DSB) provides enforcement mechanisms for IP disputes between member states.
6. **Enhanced Credibility:** Active participation signals commitment to rule-based international trade and investment.

## 2.4 Obligations Imposed by Participation

Upon participation, a state must:

- **Provide minimum standards** of IP protection (TRIPS Articles 27–34 for patents, etc.)
- **Enforce IP rights** through civil, criminal, and administrative procedures (TRIPS Part III)
- **Allow parallel imports limitations** as per treaty provisions
- **Provide for border measures** against counterfeit goods
- **Establish administrative and judicial review** mechanisms
- **Comply with Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) treatment** (TRIPS Article 4)

## 2.5 Special Provisions for Developing Countries and LDCs

TRIPS recognizes that **not all countries are at the same developmental stage**. Therefore, it provides:

- **Transition Periods:** Developing countries were given until **1 January 2000** (extended to **1 January 2005** for pharmaceutical patents under the Doha Declaration).
- **TRIPS Flexibilities:** Compulsory licensing, parallel importation, Bolar exception.
- **LDC Extension:** Least Developed Countries had until **1 July 2021** (extended further by WTO MC12 to **1 January 2034** for pharmaceutical products).

## 2.6 India's Specific Participation Trajectory

| Event | Date | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Berne Convention accession | 1928 | Early participation in copyright cooperation |
| Paris Convention accession | 1998 | Industrial property protection |
| PCT ratification | 1998 | International patent filing |
| WTO/TRIPS membership | 1 Jan 1995 | Mandatory IP standards |
| Madrid Protocol | 2013 | International trademark filing |
| Hague Agreement | 2017 | Design protection |
| Accession to WIPO | 1975 | Full membership in IP forum |

> [!NOTE]
> India's delayed accession to the Paris Convention (until 1998) was due to the historical preference for a **process-patent regime** under the Indian Patents Act 1970, which conflicted with Paris Convention's product-patent expectations. This was resolved only after the **2005 Patents Amendment**.

## 2.7 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet (Summary Table)

| Concept | Key Point |
|---|---|
| Modes of Participation | Signature, Ratification, Accession, Acceptance, Succession |
| National Treatment | Foreigners treated as nationals (Paris Art. 2, TRIPS Art. 3) |
| Right of Priority | 12 months (patents), 6 months (industrial designs, trademarks) |
| PCT | Single international patent application in 150+ countries |
| TRIPS Transition (Developing) | 1 January 2000 |
| TRIPS Transition (LDCs Pharma) | Extended to 1 January 2034 |
| WIPO Members | 193 states |
| India's Paris Convention | 1998 |
| Doha Declaration | 2001 — affirmed TRIPS flexibilities for public health |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Process of Country Participation in an IP Treaty

This section presents the **logical sequence** by which a country formally participates in an international IP treaty. Each step is mandatory for proper participation.

### Step 1: Policy Formulation at the National Level
A country first decides that participation serves its economic, technological, or strategic interests. This involves:
- Stakeholder consultations (industry, academia, civil society)
- Cost-benefit analysis of treaty obligations
- Assessment of flexibilities available

### Step 2: Vetting by the Executive Branch
The executive (Cabinet/Council of Ministers) approves the proposal and authorizes the relevant ministry (e.g., Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade — DPIIT, in India) to proceed.

### Step 3: Signature
The authorized representative signs the treaty at a diplomatic ceremony. Signature indicates:
- Intent to ratify
- Provisional obligation not to defeat the object and purpose of the treaty (per **Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties, 1969, Article 18**)

### Step 4: Domestic Legislative Approval
Under Article 253 of the Indian Constitution, Parliament must enact enabling legislation to give domestic effect to the treaty. Example: The **Patents (Amendment) Act, 1999** gave effect to India's TRIPS and PCT obligations.

### Step 5: Ratification/Accession
The country deposits an instrument of ratification/accession with the treaty depositary (usually WIPO or WTO). This is the formal act of consent to be bound.

### Step 6: Entry into Force for the State
The treaty enters into force for the participating state after:
- The treaty's specified period (e.g., 3 months after deposit for PCT)
- OR the treaty's general entry into force threshold is met

### Step 7: Domestic Implementation
The country enacts or amends national IP laws to comply with treaty obligations. For TRIPS, this involved India amending the **Patents Act 1970** in **1999, 2002, and 2005**.

### Step 8: Continuous Compliance and Reporting
The state must:
- Submit notifications to treaty bodies
- Implement enforcement measures
- Participate in dispute settlement (if WTO member)

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Participation vs. Non-Participation

| Dimension | Participating State | Non-Participating State |
|---|---|---|
| **IP Protection Abroad** | Reciprocal protection via National Treatment | Must rely on bilateral agreements |
| **Patent Filing Strategy** | Can use PCT for 150+ countries | Must file separately in each country |
| **Dispute Resolution** | WTO DSB available | Limited to bilateral arbitration |
| **Trade Integration** | Smooth WTO/TRIPS compliance | Risk of trade sanctions |
| **Technology Access** | Multilateral cooperation benefits | Restricted cross-border licensing |
| **Sovereignty Concerns** | Bound by treaty obligations | Full legislative freedom |
| **Cost** | Treaty fees + implementation costs | Lower direct cost |

## 3.3 Analytical Logic: Why the Three-Tier Distinction Matters

```
Signature         →  Political commitment (not yet binding)
Ratification      →  International legal binding (but may not be domestically operative)
Implementation    →  Domestic legal binding (treaty has effect inside the country)
```

A country can be **internationally bound** by a treaty but not yet **domestically enforceable** if no implementing legislation exists. This is why the Indian Supreme Court in **Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997)** established that international treaties are not automatically part of domestic law unless incorporated by Parliament (under the dualist system that India follows).

## 3.4 Mathematical/Audit-Style Analysis: Calculating Priority Period Usage

Although this is a legal topic, a precise analytical example illustrates priority date arithmetic:

**Scenario:** An Indian inventor files a patent in India on **1 March 2025**.

$$
\text{Priority Expiry} = \text{Filing Date} + \text{12 Months}
$$

$$
\text{Priority Expiry} = 1 \text{ March } 2025 + 12 \text{ months} = 28 \text{ February } 2026
$$

The inventor must file corresponding applications in other Paris Convention countries **on or before 28 February 2026** to claim the priority date of 1 March 2025.

$$
\text{Available Filing Window} = 12 \text{ months}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **12-month priority period** under Article 4 of the Paris Convention is one of the most examiner-favored topics in KTU exams. Students must remember the priority period for **trademarks and industrial designs is only 6 months**.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Content: TRIPS Transition Period Calculation for India

| Country Category | Original TRIPS Deadline | Extended Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Developed countries | 1 January 1996 | No extension |
| Developing countries (including India) | 1 January 2000 | Pharmaceutical patents: 1 January 2005 |
| Least Developed Countries (LDCs) | 1 January 2006 | Pharma products: 1 January 2034 (per MC12 decision) |

India, as a developing country, was required to:
- Implement product patents in **all fields** by **1 April 2005**
- Provide mailbox facility for pharmaceutical and agricultural chemical product patent applications from **1 January 1995**
- Grant Exclusive Marketing Rights (EMRs) from **1 January 1995** to **31 December 2004**

## 3.6 Logical Flow of Right of Priority (Paris Convention Article 4)

```
FIRST FILING (Country A)
       │
       │   ◄──── 12-Month Priority Period ────►
       │
       ▼
SECOND FILINGS (Countries B, C, D...)
       │
       ▼
TREATMENT: Filed as if first filed on the priority date in each country
```

This means the **novelty assessment** in country B is made as of the priority date, not the actual filing date in country B.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern Recognition:** Questions often ask "Differentiate between Signature, Ratification, and Accession" (3 marks) or "Explain the benefits of Paris Convention participation" (7 marks). Both directly test participation concepts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: Country Participation in an International IP Treaty

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[National Policy Decision] --> B[Executive Approval by Cabinet]
    B --> C[Signature of Treaty]
    C --> D[Parliamentary Enactment of Implementing Law]
    D --> E[Deposit Instrument of Ratification or Accession]
    E --> F[Treaty Entry into Force for the State]
    F --> G[Domestic Implementation through IP Statutes]
    G --> H[Ongoing Compliance and Reporting to WIPO or WTO]
    H --> I[Dispute Settlement if Required]
    I --> J[Continued Membership with Annual Contributions]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Stakeholder Ecosystem in IP Treaty Participation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTERNATIONAL["International Level"]
        WIPO1[WIPO Secretariat]
        WTO1[WTO Trade Body]
        UN1[UN General Assembly]
    end

    subgraph NATIONAL["National Level"]
        GOV[Government of Member State]
        NIPO[National IP Office]
        JUD[Courts and Tribunals]
    end

    subgraph LOCAL["Local Level"]
        INV[Inventors and Creators]
        IND[Industry and Businesses]
        ACAD[Academic and Research Institutions]
    end

    WIPO1 -->|Administers Treaties| GOV
    WTO1 -->|Enforces TRIPS| GOV
    UN1 -->|Policy Coordination| WIPO1

    GOV -->|Establishes| NIPO
    GOV -->|Empowers| JUD
    NIPO -->|Grants Rights| INV
    NIPO -->|Registers| IND
    JUD -->|Resolves Disputes| IND
    ACAD -->|Generates IP| NIPO
    INV -->|Pays Fees| NIPO
```

## 4.3 Comparative Tree: Modes of Treaty Participation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[How does a State become Party to an IP Treaty]

    M --> S1[Signature]
    M --> R1[Ratification]
    M --> A1[Accession]
    M --> AP1[Acceptance or Approval]
    M --> SU1[Succession]

    S1 --> S1D[Provisional binding under VCLT Article 18]
    R1 --> R1D[Formal binding after domestic approval]
    A1 --> A1D[Joining without prior signature]
    AP1 --> AP1D[Alternative to ratification in some treaties]
    SU1 --> SU1D[Continuation by new state]
```

## 4.4 Timeline Diagram: India's IP Treaty Participation Journey

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[1928 Berne Convention] --> T2[1975 WIPO Membership]
    T2 --> T3[1995 WTO and TRIPS]
    T3 --> T4[1998 Paris Convention]
    T4 --> T5[1998 PCT]
    T5 --> T6[2005 Product Patent Regime]
    T6 --> T7[2013 Madrid Protocol]
    T7 --> T8[2017 Hague Agreement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow is preferred over physical drawings because the topic involves abstract legal processes, hierarchies, and timelines. The mermaid syntax has been kept strictly alphanumeric to ensure compilation safety.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

Define "Participation" in the context of international intellectual property treaties. List any two modes by which a state becomes a party to a treaty.

**Model Answer:**

Participation in IPR refers to the formal accession, ratification, or adherence of a sovereign state to international IP treaties, conventions, and agreements, by which it accepts minimum standards of IP protection and harmonizes its domestic laws accordingly.

Two modes of becoming a party:
1. **Signature** — initial expression of consent to a treaty
2. **Ratification** — formal confirmation making the treaty legally binding

(Any other correct mode such as Accession, Acceptance, or Succession may also be credited.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

What is meant by the "Right of Priority" under the Paris Convention?

**Model Answer:**

The Right of Priority under **Article 4 of the Paris Convention** provides that, on the basis of a regular first application filed in one of the member countries, the applicant (or its successor in title) has the right to apply for protection in all other member countries within a fixed period. These subsequent applications will be treated as if filed on the **same date as the first application**, i.e., the priority date.

The priority period is:
- **12 months** for patents and utility models
- **6 months** for industrial designs and trademarks

This right prevents third parties from filing identical inventions in intervening countries during the priority period.

---

## Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the various modes by which a state becomes a party to an international intellectual property treaty. Distinguish clearly between "Signature" and "Ratification." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the benefits and obligations that accrue to a state upon participation in the Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a):**

A state can become a party to an international IP treaty through the following modes:

1. **Signature:** The authorized representative of a state appends their signature to the treaty text. Signature alone does not usually bind the state definitively, but under **Article 18 of the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties, 1969**, the signatory state is obligated to refrain from acts that would defeat the object and purpose of the treaty until it has made its intention clear not to become a party.

2. **Ratification:** This is the formal act by which a state establishes its consent to be bound by a treaty, usually following signature. Ratification is completed by depositing an instrument of ratification with the treaty depositary. Under **Article 253 of the Indian Constitution**, treaties require Parliamentary approval before ratification.

3. **Accession:** When a state did not sign the treaty initially but later wishes to join, it does so by accession. The legal effect is identical to ratification. Example: Many states acceded to the PCT well after 1970.

4. **Acceptance or Approval:** Alternative procedures used in multilateral treaties (notably WTO agreements) where states "accept" or "approve" the treaty text instead of ratifying it.

5. **Succession:** A newly independent state may declare that it continues the treaty obligations of its predecessor state.

**Distinction between Signature and Ratification:**

| Aspect | Signature | Ratification |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Preliminary consent | Definitive consent |
| Legal Effect | Provisional — bound not to defeat object/purpose | Fully bound internationally |
| Domestic Effect | Generally none without implementing law | None automatically without implementation |
| Sequence | Comes first | Follows signature |
| Reversibility | Can refuse to proceed | Once ratified, binding unless withdrawn |

**Model Answer (b):**

The Paris Convention (1883), administered by WIPO, provides several benefits to participating states:

**Benefits:**

1. **National Treatment (Article 2):** Nationals of member states enjoy the same protection in all other member states as is accorded to nationals of those states. This eliminates discrimination based on nationality.

2. **Right of Priority (Article 4):** A 12-month priority period for patents and 6-month for designs/trademarks, allowing inventors to file in multiple countries based on the first filing date.

3. **Common Rules for Patents:** Including independence of patents (Article 4bis), restrictions on compulsory licensing (Article 5), and grace periods for novelty (Article 11).

4. **Protection Against False Indications:** Article 10 protects against false indications of source or identity of goods.

5. **Reciprocal Trademark and Design Protection:** Member states must provide protection for service marks, collective marks, and industrial designs under common minimum rules.

**Obligations:**

1. **Legislative Compliance:** Each member state must enact or amend domestic IP laws to provide the minimum standards specified in the Convention.

2. **Administrative Cooperation:** Establishment of a national industrial property office for the administration of IP rights.

3. **Priority Right Protection:** Member states must respect priority dates claimed by foreign applicants.

4. **Enforcement Mechanisms:** Members must ensure that the rights granted can be effectively enforced through judicial or administrative procedures.

5. **Membership Dues:** Payment of contributions to WIPO for maintaining membership in the Paris Union.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the meaning of "National Treatment" and "Right of Priority" as foundational principles of participation in international IP treaties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace India's participation in major international IP treaties and conventions. Highlight the significance of the 2005 Patents Amendment in fulfilling India's TRIPS obligations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a):**

**National Treatment Principle:**

The principle of National Treatment is enshrined in **Article 2 of the Paris Convention** and **Article 3 of the TRIPS Agreement**. It mandates that any member state must grant to nationals of other member states the same protection in respect of intellectual property rights as it grants to its own nationals. 

In effect, this eliminates discrimination on the basis of nationality. The principle is subject to exceptions explicitly provided in the treaty (e.g., procedural requirements like appointment of a local agent, designation of an address for service).

**Right of Priority Principle:**

Article 4 of the Paris Convention establishes the Right of Priority. Any person who has duly filed an application for a patent, utility model, trademark, or industrial design in one member country enjoys, for a specified period, a right of priority to file in other member countries. These subsequent filings are treated as if filed on the date of the first application.

- **Priority period:**
  - Patents and utility models: **12 months**
  - Industrial designs and trademarks: **6 months**

**Significance for Participation:**

Both principles are foundational because they form the **cornerstone of international IP cooperation**. Without National Treatment, foreign IP holders would face discriminatory barriers. Without the Right of Priority, inventors would be vulnerable to third parties copying their inventions in third countries during the gap between the first and subsequent filings.

**Model Answer (b):**

**India's Major Treaty Participations:**

| Year | Treaty/Event | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 1928 | Berne Convention | Early participation in copyright cooperation |
| 1975 | WIPO Membership | Global IP forum participation |
| 1995 | WTO/TRIPS | Mandatory global IP standards |
| 1998 | Paris Convention | Industrial property reciprocity |
| 1998 | PCT | International patent filing mechanism |
| 2001 | Budapest Treaty | Deposit of microorganisms |
| 2009 | Nice Agreement | Classification of trademarks |
| 2013 | Madrid Protocol | International trademark filing |
| 2017 | Hague Agreement | Design protection |

**The 2005 Patents Amendment — Significance:**

The **Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005** was a watershed moment in India's IP history. Its significance lies in the following:

1. **TRIPS Compliance:** India transitioned from a process-patent regime (under the 1970 Patents Act) to a **product-patent regime** in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals, fulfilling its TRIPS obligations from the extended deadline of **1 January 2005**.

2. **Repeal of EMR Provisions:** The Exclusive Marketing Rights (EMR) regime, operative from 1995–2004, was repealed as product patents were introduced.

3. **Mailbox Applications Disposal:** Pending mailbox applications (filed between 1995–2005) were examined and granted product patents.

4. **Compulsory Licensing:** Strengthened the grounds for compulsory licensing under Section 84, balancing IP rights with public interest.

5. **Bolar Exception:** Section 107A allows generic manufacturers to use patented inventions for regulatory approval, facilitating market entry upon patent expiry.

The 2005 amendment demonstrated India's **successful transition from a developing country with process-patent preference to a TRIPS-compliant regime**, marking its mature participation in the global IP system.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Loss Points:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Signature with Ratification:** Many students write that "signing makes a treaty binding." This is incorrect. Signature alone does not bind; ratification does.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Priority Periods:** Examiners expect students to recall that trademarks and designs have a **6-month** priority period, NOT 12 months. Conflating this costs 1 mark easily.
>
> 3. **Missing the Indian Context:** Generic answers on WIPO lose marks. Examiners reward students who cite India's specific accession dates (e.g., Paris 1998, PCT 1998, Madrid 2013).
>
> 4. **Ignoring the 2005 Amendment:** Many students skip the significance of the **2005 Patents Amendment** when discussing TRIPS participation. This is a frequently-tested high-yield topic.
>
> 5. **Treating TRIPS as Optional:** TRIPS is MANDATORY for WTO members. Writing that TRIPS is "voluntary" is factually wrong and costs 2–3 marks immediately.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Participation** = formal accession, ratification, or adherence to international IP treaties — not just signing.
- **Three-tier structure:** Signature → Ratification → Domestic Implementation
- **Modes of Participation:** Signature, Ratification, Accession, Acceptance/Approval, Succession
- **Signature** = preliminary consent; **Ratification** = binding international consent
- **National Treatment** = no discrimination based on nationality (Paris Art. 2, TRIPS Art. 3)
- **Right of Priority:** **12 months** for patents/utility models; **6 months** for trademarks/designs
- **PCT** = single international patent application valid in 150+ contracting states
- **WIPO Members:** 193 states
- **India's Paris Convention Accession:** 1998
- **India's PCT Ratification:** 1998
- **India's Madrid Protocol:** 2013
- **India's Hague Agreement:** 2017
- **TRIPS Transition for Developing Countries:** 1 January 2000
- **TRIPS Pharmaceutical Transition for LDCs:** Extended to **1 January 2034** (per WTO MC12 decision)
- **2005 Patents Amendment** = product-patent regime, India's TRIPS compliance milestone
- **Doha Declaration (2001)** affirmed TRIPS flexibilities for public health
- **Article 253 of Indian Constitution** = basis for treaty implementation by Parliament
- **India follows dualist system** = treaties not automatically enforceable domestically
- **Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (1969), Article 18** = signature creates provisional obligation
- **TRIPS is mandatory** for all WTO members (single undertaking principle)
- **Compulsory licensing, parallel imports, Bolar exception** = TRIPS flexibilities
- **Right of Priority prevents third-party exploitation** during the priority window
- **Reciprocity, dispute settlement, technology transfer** = benefits of participation
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: People
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** People

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) and Cyber Laws**, the term **"People"** refers to the **natural persons and legal entities** who are recognized by the legal framework as legitimate **creators, owners, controllers, transferees, licensees, or beneficiaries** of intellectual property assets, and whose **rights, obligations, and liabilities** are governed by statutes such as the **Copyright Act, 1957**, the **Patents Act, 1970**, the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**, the **Information Technology Act, 2000**, and the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**.

The "people" construct in IP law classifies stakeholders into the following juridical categories:

- **Author** — the natural person who creates a literary, artistic, musical, or dramatic work.
- **Inventor** — the natural person who contributes to the conception of an invention.
- **Proprietor / Owner** — the person or entity in whom the IP right vests (either by statute or by assignment).
- **Assignee** — the person to whom rights have been transferred through a valid assignment deed.
- **Licensee** — the person permitted to exercise limited rights under a contractual licence.
- **Data Principal** — the individual to whom personal data relates under DPDP Act, 2023.
- **Data Fiduciary** — the person who determines the purpose and means of processing personal data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Indian legal framework distinguishes between **moral rights** (which are *inalienable* and remain with the author) and **economic rights** (which are *transferable* through assignment or licence). This dualist model is a high-weightage examination area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine IP as a **piece of real estate land**:

| IP Concept | Real Estate Analogy |
|---|---|
| Author / Inventor | The mason who physically built the house |
| Owner / Proprietor | The person whose name is on the title deed |
| Assignment | Selling the house outright |
| Licence | Renting out the house with conditions |
| Moral Right | The mason's right to be acknowledged (cannot be sold) |
| Economic Right | The right to collect rent or sale proceeds |

The **"people"** are simply the humans (and corporate bodies) who stand at different points in this chain — from the brick-layer (creator) to the landlord (owner) to the tenant (licensee).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** "People" in IP law = *everyone who touches the IP lifecycle* — from the moment of creation (author/inventor), through ownership (proprietor), to commercial exploitation (licensee/assignee), and finally to regulatory supervision (tribunals, courts).

## 1.3 Standard Statutory Metrics & Definitions

- **Author (Sec. 2(d), Copyright Act, 1957):** The person who causes the work to be created; for literary/dramatic works = the writer; for musical works = the composer; for artistic works = the artist; for photographs = the photographer; for computer-generated works = the person who causes the work to be created.
- **Inventor (Sec. 2(1)(y), Patents Act, 1970):** A person who has contributed to the invention — *true and first inventor* or the *assignee* (in the case of convention applications).
- **Data Principal (Sec. 2(i), DPDP Act, 2023):** The individual to whom personal data relates.
- **Data Fiduciary (Sec. 2(j), DPDP Act, 2023):** Any person who alone or in conjunction with others determines the purpose and means of processing personal data.
- **Joint Ownership:** Arises when work is created by two or more authors whose contributions are inseparable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students confuse **"Author"** under Copyright with **"Inventor"** under Patent Law. They are *not* interchangeable. Copyright vests automatically in the *author*; Patent vests only on *grant* by the Controller General.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of "People" under Indian IP & Cyber Law Framework

The legal framework recognizes the following structured hierarchy of persons:

### A. CREATORS (Originators of IP)

1. **Author** — Originator of copyrightable works (Sec. 2(d), Copyright Act, 1957).
2. **Inventor** — True and first contributor to an invention (Sec. 2(1)(y), Patents Act, 1970).
3. **Designer** — Creator of an industrial design (Sec. 2(d), Designs Act, 2000).
4. **Composer** — Author of a musical work.
5. **Photographer / Cinematographer** — Author of photographic/visual works.

### B. OWNERS (Holders of Rights)

1. **First Owner (Copyright)** — Default rule under **Sec. 17, Copyright Act, 1957**.
2. **Proprietor (Trademark)** — Person in whose name the mark is registered.
3. **Patentee** — Person for the time being entered in the register as the grantee/owner of patent.

### C. TRANSFEREES (Persons Acquiring Rights)

1. **Assignee** — Recipient of an absolute transfer.
2. **Exclusive Licensee** — Holds exclusive rights to exploit.
3. **Non-Exclusive Licensee** — Holds limited, non-exclusive rights.
4. **Mortgagee / Pledgee** — Holders of security interests.

### D. REGULATORY PERSONS / AUTHORITIES

1. **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)**.
2. **Registrar of Copyrights**.
3. **Adjudicating Officers** under IT Act, 2000.
4. **Data Protection Board** under DPDP Act, 2023.

### E. DATA ECOSYSTEM PERSONS (DPDP Act, 2023)

1. **Data Principal** — The individual whose data is processed.
2. **Data Fiduciary** — Determines purpose and means.
3. **Data Processor** — Processes data on behalf of the Data Fiduciary.
4. **Consent Manager** — An intermediary managing consent.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis: Who is the First Owner?

The following decision-tree governs ownership under Indian law:

1. **Step 1:** Is the work created in the course of *employment* under a contract of service?  
   → Yes → **Employer is the first owner** (Sec. 17(c), Copyright Act).

2. **Step 2:** Is the work created under a contract for *specific commissioned work*?  
   → Yes → **The person who commissioned and paid for the work is the first owner** (Sec. 17(b)).  
   → **Exception:** Photographs, portraits, engravings — author retains ownership.

3. **Step 3:** Is the work created by an *employee* engaged in newspaper/magazine work?  
   → Yes → **Proprietor of the newspaper** is the owner for newspaper purposes only.

4. **Step 4:** Default (no employment, no commission)?  
   → The **author himself/herself is the first owner**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Statutory Provision | Key Rule | Trigger Event |
|---|---|---|---|
| Authorship (Copyright) | Sec. 2(d) | Natural person creating work | Work creation |
| Inventorship (Patent) | Sec. 2(1)(y) | True & first inventor | Filing application |
| First Ownership | Sec. 17, CA 1957 | Employer / Commissioner / Author | As per contract |
| Moral Rights | Sec. 57, CA 1957 | Inalienable; remains with author | Birth of work |
| Assignment | Sec. 18, CA 1957 | Must be in writing, signed | Executed deed |
| Licence | Sec. 30, CA 1957 | Grants permission to use | Written agreement |
| Data Principal | Sec. 2(i), DPDP 2023 | Natural person whose data | Data collection |
| Data Fiduciary | Sec. 2(j), DPDP 2023 | Determines purpose | Processing initiated |
| Joint Ownership | Sec. 13, CA 1957 | Multiple authors sharing | Inseparable work |
| Patentee | Sec. 2(1)(o), PA 1970 | Registered patent holder | Grant of patent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Understanding who the "person" is in any IP transaction determines **who can sue, who can be sued, and who can license**. This is the foundation of any IP due-diligence exercise in M&A, technology transfer, and corporate structuring.

## 2.4 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Classification

- **Why classify creators separately from owners?** Because copyright subsists *automatically* upon creation, but economic exploitation is typically done by commercial entities (publishers, producers, companies). This separation protects the *human creator's* moral rights while enabling commerce.
- **Why are moral rights inalienable?** Because they protect the *personal bond* between creator and work — the right to be named and the right to integrity of the work. These cannot be sold, lest the creator's reputation be harmed.
- **Why distinguish Data Principal from Data Fiduciary?** Because under the DPDP Act, 2023, *accountability* is fixed on the entity that determines purpose (Fiduciary), while *protection* flows to the individual (Principal).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Legal Derivation: Flow of Rights from Creator to Commercial Exploiter

We model the transfer of rights as a structured sequence. Let:

$$R_{\text{creator}} \rightarrow \text{right vested at moment of creation}$$

$$R_{\text{economic}} \subseteq R_{\text{total}}$$

$$R_{\text{moral}} \subset R_{\text{total}} \mid R_{\text{moral}} \cap R_{\text{economic}} = \emptyset$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{total}}$ = Full bundle of rights in the IP.
- $R_{\text{creator}}$ = Rights vesting in the creator at $t = 0$ (creation).
- $R_{\text{moral}}$ = Non-economic, inalienable rights.
- $R_{\text{economic}}$ = Transferable exploitation rights.

### Step 1: Initial Allocation at Creation

At the moment of creation ($t = 0$):

$$R_{\text{author}} = R_{\text{moral}} \cup R_{\text{economic}}$$

The author holds the complete bundle.

### Step 2: Statutory Re-Allocation under Sec. 17

For works created in the course of employment:

$$R_{\text{employer}} \rightarrow \text{owner of } R_{\text{economic}}$$

$$R_{\text{moral}} \rightarrow \text{remains with the employee-author}$$

This is expressed as:

$$R_{\text{employer}} = R_{\text{economic}} \mid R_{\text{employee}} = R_{\text{moral}}$$

### Step 3: Voluntary Transfer (Assignment under Sec. 18)

A valid assignment requires:
1. Written instrument.
2. Signed by the assignor or authorized agent.
3. Specification of rights assigned and duration.
4. Territory of application.

Mathematically:

$$R_{\text{assignee}} \subseteq R_{\text{assignor}} \text{ and } R_{\text{assignee}} \cap R_{\text{moral of assignor}} = \emptyset$$

The moral rights **cannot** be transferred.

### Step 4: Licensing (Sec. 30)

A licence is a *limited* transfer:

$$L = \{ r \mid r \in R_{\text{licensor}} \text{ and } r \text{ is explicitly granted} \}$$

Types:
- **Exclusive Licence** — Only the licensee can exercise, even to the exclusion of the licensor.
- **Non-Exclusive Licence** — Licensor may grant identical rights to others.

### Step 5: Joint Ownership (Sec. 13)

For works of joint authorship:

$$R_{\text{joint}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_{\text{author}_i}$$

Each co-owner has an undivided share, subject to a duty to account to other co-owners for profits received from licensing.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Rights of Different "People"

| Person Type | Moral Rights? | Economic Rights? | Can Assign? | Can Sue for Infringement? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Author (individual) | Yes (Sec. 57) | Yes | Economic only | Yes |
| Joint Author | Yes | Yes (undivided share) | Yes (own share) | Yes |
| Employer (commissioned work) | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Assignee | No | Yes (as per deed) | Yes (subject to deed) | Yes |
| Exclusive Licensee | No | Yes (as per deed) | Cannot sub-license beyond deed | Yes |
| Non-Exclusive Licensee | No | Limited | No | No (only contractual remedies) |
| Data Principal | Right to consent, erasure | — | — | Yes (before DPB) |
| Data Fiduciary | — | Right to process under Act | — | Yes / Liable as well |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Rights Flow in Software Development

**Scenario:** A software developer ("Arjun") is employed by TechCorp under a contract of service. Arjun creates a payroll module during office hours using company resources.

**Step-by-step legal analysis:**

1. **Who is the author?**  
   Arjun is the author under Sec. 2(d) because he caused the work to be created.

2. **Who is the first owner?**  
   TechCorp is the first owner under Sec. 17(c) because the work was created in the course of employment.

3. **What rights does Arjun retain?**  
   Arjun retains moral rights under Sec. 57: right to claim authorship and right to integrity.

4. **Can Arjun take the code and publish it elsewhere?**  
   No — the economic rights vest in TechCorp. Arjun's act of copying/distributing would constitute infringement.

5. **Can TechCorp sell the software?**  
   Yes — TechCorp holds the economic rights and can assign or license them.

6. **What if the contract is silent on IP?**  
   The default statutory rule (Sec. 17(c)) still applies in favour of the employer.

**Final Ownership Map:**

$$R_{\text{TechCorp}} = R_{\text{economic}} \cup R_{\text{reproduction}} \cup R_{\text{distribution}} \cup R_{\text{adaptation}}$$

$$R_{\text{Arjun}} = \{ \text{right of paternity}, \text{right of integrity} \}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Author" under the Copyright Act, 1957. Who is considered the author of a literary, musical, and computer-generated work?**

**Model Answer:**

As per **Section 2(d) of the Copyright Act, 1957**, "author" means:
- **Literary or Dramatic Work** — the writer/compiler of the work.
- **Musical Work** — the composer.
- **Artistic Work** — the artist (painter, sculptor, architect, photographer).
- **Photograph** — the photographer.
- **Cinematograph Film** — the producer (person who causes the film to be made).
- **Sound Recording** — the producer (person who records the sounds).
- **Computer-Generated Work** — the person who *causes* the work to be generated by a computer (Sec. 2(d)(vi)).

The author is the natural person (or in some cases, a corporate body) whose intellectual effort brings the work into existence. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between a Data Principal and a Data Fiduciary under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Data Principal | Data Fiduciary |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (Sec. 2)** | Sec. 2(i) | Sec. 2(j) |
| **Identity** | The individual to whom personal data relates | Any person who determines purpose and means of processing |
| **Role** | Owner of the personal data | Controller / decision-maker |
| **Rights** | Right to consent, access, correction, erasure, grievance redressal | Right to process data subject to compliance |
| **Obligations** | Must give informed consent | Must ensure compliance, security, breach notification |
| **Example** | A customer using a mobile app | The company running the mobile app |

**[3 Marks]**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the rights and obligations of an **"Author"** under the Copyright Act, 1957, including the concept of **moral rights** under Sec. 57. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the legal position of **joint authorship** under Sec. 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957, with appropriate case law. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Rights and Obligations of an Author (7 Marks)**

The author is the most important "person" in copyright law. The following rights vest in the author:

1. **Economic Rights** — Bundle of rights under Sec. 14:
   - Right to reproduce (Sec. 14(a)(i))
   - Right to issue copies to the public (Sec. 14(a)(ii))
   - Right to perform/publicly communicate (Sec. 14(a)(iii))
   - Right to make translations/adaptations (Sec. 14(a)(iv))

2. **Moral Rights under Sec. 57** — These are *inalienable* and *perpetual* (lasting till copyright subsists):
   - **Right of Paternity (Sec. 57(1)(a))** — Author may claim authorship and require that his name be mentioned on all reproductions of his work.
   - **Right of Integrity (Sec. 57(1)(b))** — Author may restrain any distortion, mutilation, or modification of his work that would be prejudicial to his honour or reputation.
   - **Right against False Attribution (Sec. 57(1)(c))** — Author may prevent any person from claiming authorship of a work that is not his.

3. **Special Rights in Case of Visual Artists (Sec. 57(2))** — Right to share in the resale price of original artistic works (*droit de suite*).

4. **Obligations of the Author:**
   - Must formally assign or license economic rights to enable commercial use.
   - Cannot infringe others' rights while exercising own rights.
   - Must follow procedural formalities if challenging infringement (e.g., giving notice under Sec. 53A).

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Law:** In *Mannu Bhandari v. Kala Vikas Pictures* (AIR 1987 Del 13), the Delhi High Court held that moral rights are personal to the author and cannot be assigned.

**(b) Joint Authorship under Sec. 13 (7 Marks)**

Section 13(1) of the Copyright Act, 1957, defines a "work of joint authorship" as:

> *"A work produced by the collaboration of two or more authors in which the contribution of each author is not separate and identifiable from the contribution of the other authors."*

**Essential Conditions for Joint Authorship:**

1. **Collaboration** — Two or more authors must have worked together.
2. **Inseparability** — Their contributions must not be distinct or separable.
3. **Common Design** — The collaboration must be pursuant to a common design or intent.

**Rights of Joint Authors:**

- Each joint author holds an **undivided share** in the entire work (Sec. 13(3)).
- Any one joint author can grant a non-exclusive licence without consent of others, provided the others receive their share of royalties (Sec. 30(3)).
- For **exclusive licensing**, consent of *all* joint authors is required.

**Case Law:**

- *Cesc Ltd v. Subhash Chandra Bose* — Joint authorship requires a *common intent* to create a joint work, not merely simultaneous creation.
- *Ramdas Divanji v. M/s Vimal Enterprises* — Court held that mere collaboration without common design does not give rise to joint authorship.

**Distinction from Composite Work:**

A **composite work** has contributions that are *separate and identifiable* (e.g., an anthology of poems by different poets). In composite works, each contributor holds copyright only in their own contribution, not in the composite whole.

**[Total: 14 Marks]**

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Who is the **"first owner"** of copyright under the Copyright Act, 1957? Discuss the **employer–employee** and **commissioned work** exceptions with case laws. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Data Principal and Data Fiduciary** under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, along with the rights of a Data Principal and obligations of a Data Fiduciary. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) First Owner of Copyright (7 Marks)**

The general rule under **Sec. 17** of the Copyright Act, 1957, is that the **author is the first owner** of copyright. However, there are important statutory exceptions:

**Rule 1: General Rule (Sec. 17(a))**
- The author is the first owner of copyright.
- Example: A freelance writer writing a novel on her own time retains copyright.

**Rule 2: Work under Contract of Employment (Sec. 17(c))**
- Where a work is created by an employee in the **course of employment** under a **contract of service**, the **employer** is the first owner.
- This applies to employees, not independent contractors.
- *Example*: A software developer employed by TCS creates a payroll module during office hours → TCS is the owner.

**Rule 3: Commissioned Work for Valuable Consideration (Sec. 17(b))**
- Where a work is commissioned for valuable consideration and falls within the nine specified categories, the **person who commissioned the work** is the first owner.
- Specified categories: paintings, portraits, engravings, photographs, cinematograph films, sound recordings, works of architecture, dramatic works, musical works.
- *Exception*: For photographs, portraits, and engravings, the author retains copyright unless agreed otherwise.

**Rule 4: Work in Newspapers, Magazines, etc. (Sec. 17(d))**
- For literary, dramatic, or artistic works created for newspapers/magazines, the **proprietor** is the owner for the limited purpose of publication in that medium.

**Rule 5: Government and International Organizations (Sec. 17(c) proviso, Sec. 17(k))**
- Government works vest in the Government.
- Works of international organizations vest in the organization.

**Case Laws:**

1. *Beloff v. Pressdram Ltd.* — Employer–employee relationship is determined by the *control test*.
2. *University of London Press v. University Tutorial Press* — Where the author is paid for creation under a contract, the payer is the owner.

**(b) Data Principal and Data Fiduciary under DPDP Act, 2023 (7 Marks)**

**Data Principal (Sec. 2(i)):** The individual to whom **personal data** relates, and where such individual is a child, includes the **parents or lawful guardian**.

**Data Fiduciary (Sec. 2(j)):** Any person who **alone or in conjunction with other persons** determines the purpose and means of processing of personal data. This includes the Government.

**Rights of Data Principal:**

1. **Right to Consent (Sec. 6)** — Consent must be free, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
2. **Right to Withdraw Consent (Sec. 6(4))** — Easier than the GDPR.
3. **Right to Access Information (Sec. 11)** — Right to a summary of personal data and processing activities.
4. **Right to Correction and Erasure (Sec. 12)** — Right to correct inaccurate data and erase personal data.
5. **Right of Grievance Redressal (Sec. 13)** — Right to complain to the Data Protection Board.
6. **Right to Nominate** — Right to nominate another individual to exercise rights in case of death or incapacity.

**Obligations of Data Fiduciary:**

1. **Lawful Processing (Sec. 4)** — Process only for lawful purposes with consent.
2. **Accuracy (Sec. 8(3))** — Maintain accuracy of personal data.
3. **Security Safeguards (Sec. 8(1))** — Implement reasonable security practices.
4. **Breach Notification (Sec. 8(5))** — Inform the Board and affected Data Principals of any personal data breach.
5. **Data Retention Limitation (Sec. 8(7))** — Erase personal data when the purpose is no longer being served.
6. **Significant Data Fiduciary Obligations (Sec. 10)** — Additional obligations including DPO appointment, audit, and DPIA for entities notified as Significant Data Fiduciaries.

**Penalties (Sec. 33):** Up to **₹250 crore** per instance for failure to take reasonable security safeguards.

**[Total: 14 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Author" with "Inventor."** Authors are for copyright; inventors are for patents.
> 2. **Sec. 17(c) applies only to employees under contract of service**, *not* to independent contractors. Many students lose marks by failing to distinguish.
> 3. **Moral rights are NOT assignable** in India (unlike copyright in works made for hire in the US). Always mention this distinction.
> 4. **Joint authorship requires inseparability of contributions** — merely simultaneous creation is insufficient.
> 5. **Under DPDP Act, 2023, parental consent is required for processing children's data** (defined as under 18 years). Many students incorrectly use 13 or 16 years (GDPR standard).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Creator-Owner Distinction:** Author/Inventor ≠ First Owner (especially under Sec. 17).
- **Moral Rights (Sec. 57):** Three rights — Paternity, Integrity, False Attribution — all *inalienable*.
- **First Owner Rule (Sec. 17):** Author by default → Employer (employee works) → Commissioner (specified works) → Government (Govt works).
- **Joint Authorship (Sec. 13):** Requires *collaboration + inseparability + common design*.
- **Assignment (Sec. 18):** Must be in writing, signed, specifies rights, territory, duration.
- **Licence (Sec. 30):** Exclusive requires written deed; non-exclusive can be oral (for works other than dramatic/contrived).
- **Data Principal vs Data Fiduciary:** Principal = individual; Fiduciary = decision-maker; accountability flows to Fiduciary.
- **Children's Data:** Parental consent mandatory under DPDP Act, 2023 (below 18 years).
- **Penalties under DPDP Act:** Up to ₹250 crore for non-compliance with security safeguards.
- **Infringement Remedy:** Civil (Sec. 55), Criminal (Sec. 63), Administrative, Border seizure (Sec. 53).
- **Case Laws to Memorise:** *Mannu Bhandari*, *University of London Press*, *Beloff v. Pressdram*, *Cesc Ltd v. Subhash Chandra Bose*.
- **High-Yield Keywords for Examinations:** *Paternity*, *Integrity*, *Undivided share*, *Course of employment*, *Contract of service*, *Inalienable*, *Sub-licensing*, *Reasonable security safeguards*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Profession

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a "Profession"

In the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) and Cyber Law, the term **"Profession"** refers to a category of **vocational activity** requiring specialized knowledge, intellectual skill, and often regulated formal qualifications, undertaken by an individual or group as a **means of livelihood**. Unlike a "trade" (focused on goods) or "commerce" (focused on the exchange/distribution of goods and services), a profession is fundamentally **knowledge-driven** and **service-oriented**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Working Definition (KTU Context):**
> A *profession* is an occupation that requires **extensive intellectual training**, is governed by a **code of professional ethics**, is organized into a **professional body or association**, and renders **personal services of a specialized nature** to clients or the public.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Specialist Doctor"**
>
> Imagine a small town where three economic actors operate:
> 1. A **shopkeeper** (a *trader*) — he buys and sells goods.
> 2. A **wholesaler** (a *commerce* person) — he distributes goods in bulk.
> 3. A **doctor** (a *professional*) — he applies years of specialized education, follows a strict code of medical ethics, and is licensed by a regulatory body (the Medical Council).
>
> The doctor is the *professional*. The key differentiator is the **specialized knowledge**, the **ethical code**, and the **regulatory body**. The same logic applies to lawyers, chartered accountants, architects, engineers, performers, and software professionals — all of whom fall within the legal umbrella of "professions" relevant to IPR and cyber law.

### 1.3 Key Attributes of a Profession

A true profession is characterized by the following attributes:

- **Specialized Knowledge**: Requires advanced intellectual training and continuous learning.
- **Code of Ethics**: Governed by a self-regulating body enforcing ethical standards.
- **Licensing / Registration**: Membership or registration is typically mandatory.
- **Personal Responsibility**: Professional accountability for the service rendered.
- **Service Motive**: Focus on public welfare alongside economic gain.
- **Autonomy**: The professional exercises significant independent judgment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):**
> Under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957, performers are explicitly recognized as a class of "professionals" whose rights are protected. Similarly, under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, the concept of *profession* defines the scope of registrable service marks (Class 45).

### 1.4 Classification of Human Economic Activity

For a KTU-level analysis, every economic activity can be broadly classified as follows:

| Category | Core Object | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Profession** | Specialized personal services based on knowledge | Doctor, Lawyer, Engineer, Performer |
| **Trade** | Buying and selling of goods | Shopkeeper, Retailer |
| **Commerce** | Exchange, distribution, and transport of goods/services | Banking, Insurance, Logistics |
| **Industry** | Mass production of goods or services | Manufacturing, Software Development |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The "Profession" in Indian Legal Framework

In Indian jurisprudence, the term "profession" is referenced across multiple statutes, each with a slightly different scope:

### 2.1.1 Under the Copyright Act, 1957

The Copyright Act introduces the concept of the **"Performer"** — a key professional category. Performer includes any actor, singer, musician, dancer, or any other person who performs a literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 2(q) of the Copyright Act, 1957 — Performer:**
> *"performer" includes an actor, singer, musician, dancer, conjurer, juggler, snake charmer, lecturer, or any other person who makes a performance; and in relation to a record or a performance recorded on any communication medium, includes the producer of such record or the person who causes such communication medium to be used for the purpose of making the performance.*

### 2.1.2 Under the Trade Marks Act, 1999

The concept of *profession* is integral to the definition of **"service marks"**. A service mark is used in the rendering of professional services, distinguishing them from goods.

### 2.1.3 Under the Information Technology Act, 2000

The IT Act recognizes the role of professionals (e.g., digital signature certifying authorities, cyber law practitioners, and intermediaries) as a regulated professional class.

## 2.2 Professional Conduct and IPR

Professionals occupy a unique position in the IPR ecosystem. They are simultaneously:

1. **Producers of IP** — e.g., an author produces copyrighted work; an inventor produces a patent.
2. **Consumers of IP** — e.g., a software engineer uses proprietary libraries; a researcher references prior art.
3. **Stewards of IP** — e.g., lawyers, patent agents, and IP attorneys administer IP rights on behalf of others.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Concept:**
> The intersection of *profession* and *IPR* creates a fiduciary relationship. Professionals are bound by duties of confidentiality, non-disclosure, and ethical use of client IP — breaches of which can trigger both civil and criminal liability under the respective IP statutes.

## 2.3 The Concept of "Professionals" under Section 2(q) — Analytical Breakdown

The legal definition can be broken into structured logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Activity Class**: The activity must be a *performance* (i.e., a live execution of a work).
- **Step 2 — Confirm the Subject Matter**: The performance must involve a copyrightable work (literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic).
- **Step 3 — Recognize the Actor**: Any natural person who makes the performance qualifies.
- **Step 4 — Extend to Recorded/Communicated Works**: The definition also extends to producers of records and persons using communication mediums.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet — Profession Module**

| Concept / Term | Legal Source | Core Definition | KTU Significance |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Profession** | General Law | Knowledge-based vocational activity | Distinguishes from trade/commerce |
| **Performer** | Copyright Act, Section 2(q) | Person making a performance | Grants rights under Sections 38, 38A |
| **Performer Rights** | Sections 38–39 of CA 1957 | Exclusive rights to record/reproduce/broadcast | First-time recognition in 1994 amendment |
| **Service Mark** | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Mark used for professional services | Registered under Class 45 |
| **Professional Misconduct** | Respective Council Acts | Breach of ethical code | Can lead to de-registration |
| **Fair Dealing** | Section 52 of CA 1957 | Permitted professional use | Defense against infringement claims |
| **Cyberspace Profession** | IT Act, 2000 | E-commerce, digital services | Cyber law regulates professional conduct online |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Performers' Rights:**
> 1. Rights vest only when a *copyrightable work* is performed.
> 2. Rights exist for a period of **25 years** from the date of performance (Section 39).
> 3. Performer must be a national of a country that grants reciprocal rights (Section 40).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering practice, the concept of "profession" is critical in:

- **Software Engineering**: Distinguishing proprietary software development (a *trade*) from consulting engineering services (a *profession*).
- **Patent Law**: Engineers are inventors (professionals producing IP) and patent attorneys are IP professionals.
- **Cyber Law**: IT professionals handling data are governed by professional conduct rules under the IT Act and SPDI Rules.
- **Architectural Works**: Recognized professionals whose designs attract copyright protection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Legal Derivations

## 3.1 Derivational Analysis — Distinguishing Profession from Trade/Commerce

To rigorously distinguish a *profession* from *trade* and *commerce*, we apply a **multi-factor test** (analogous to the legal tests used by Indian courts). Each factor is assigned a binary classification (Y/N), and the cumulative profile determines the categorization.

### 3.1.1 The Multi-Factor Test

Let the following factors be defined:

- $F_1$ = Specialized knowledge required
- $F_2$ = Existence of a regulatory professional body
- $F_3$ = Mandatory code of ethics
- $F_4$ = Personal service rendered
- $F_5$ = Mass production / scalability of output

For an activity $A$ to qualify as a *profession*, it must satisfy:

$$F_1 = \text{Yes} \quad \land \quad F_2 = \text{Yes} \quad \land \quad F_3 = \text{Yes} \quad \land \quad F_4 = \text{Yes}$$

If any one of these conditions fails, the activity defaults to *trade* or *commerce*. Conversely:

$$F_5 = \text{Yes} \quad \rightarrow \quad A \in \text{Trade / Commerce}$$

This gives the formal logical separation.

### 3.1.2 Application of the Test — Worked Example

Consider four real-world activities:

| Activity | $F_1$ | $F_2$ | $F_3$ | $F_4$ | $F_5$ | Classification |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Medical Practice | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | **Profession** |
| Grocery Shop | No | No | No | No | Yes | **Trade** |
| Banking | Partial | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | **Commerce** |
| Software Consulting | Yes | Partial | Yes | Yes | No | **Profession** |

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Performer Rights Duration

The duration of performer rights under the Copyright Act is computed as follows:

Given:
- Date of Performance = $D_p$
- Statutory Term = $T_s = 25$ years
- Date of Expiry = $D_e$

Then:

$$D_e = D_p + T_s$$

$$D_e = D_p + 25 \text{ years}$$

**Example**: If a performer makes a performance on 15-Aug-2010, the rights subsist until:

$$D_e = \text{15-Aug-2010} + 25 \text{ years}$$

$$D_e = \text{15-Aug-2035}$$

After 15-Aug-2035, the performance enters the **public domain**, and any party may use it without seeking permission from the performer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Computational Note:**
> The 25-year term is counted from the *end of the calendar year* of the performance in some interpretations. Always refer to the exact statutory language for examination purposes.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Rights of Performers vs. Rights of Authors

A comparative analytical matrix is essential for KTU Module 2 examinations:

| Parameter | Author of Copyright Work | Performer |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Legal Source** | Section 13–14, CA 1957 | Section 38–39, CA 1957 |
| **Nature of Right** | Economic + Moral Rights | Economic Rights only (no moral rights for performers in India) |
| **Duration** | Life + 60 years | 25 years from performance |
| **Subject Matter** | Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works | The live or recorded performance |
| **Infringement Provision** | Section 55 | Section 55 (read with Section 39) |
| **Transferability** | Assignable / Licensable | Assignable / Licensable |
| **Recognition Year (India)** | 1957 | 1994 (amendment) |

### 3.3 Derivation of Professional Liability under Cyber Law

Under the IT Act, 2000, a professional (e.g., a Body Corporate handling Sensitive Personal Data) can be held liable under **Section 43A** if it is negligent in implementing reasonable security practices.

The liability condition can be expressed as:

$$L = (N \land C \land D)$$

where:
- $L$ = Liability of the professional body
- $N$ = Negligence in maintaining security
- $C$ = Compensation caused to a person
- $D$ = Damage by wrongful loss or wrongful gain

If all three conditions coexist, the body corporate is liable to pay damages **not exceeding ₹5 crore** to the affected person.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
