# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define the term "Profession". Distinguish it from "Trade" and "Commerce".**

**Model Answer:**

A *profession* is an occupation requiring specialized intellectual training, governed by a code of professional ethics, regulated by a recognized professional body, and involving the rendering of personal services of a specialized nature.

The distinguishing features are:

- **Profession vs Trade**: A profession is knowledge-intensive and service-oriented (e.g., medicine, law), whereas a trade is goods-intensive and transactional (e.g., grocery shop).
- **Profession vs Commerce**: A profession involves personal accountability and ethical regulation, whereas commerce involves the exchange, distribution, and financing of goods/services (e.g., banking, insurance).
- **Profession vs Industry**: A profession emphasizes individual skill and judgment; an industry emphasizes mass production and scalability.

The core differentiators are: specialized knowledge, code of ethics, regulatory body, and personal service.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Who is a "Performer" under the Copyright Act, 1957? What rights vest in a performer?**

**Model Answer:**

Under **Section 2(q)** of the Copyright Act, 1957, a *performer* includes an actor, singer, musician, dancer, conjurer, juggler, snake charmer, lecturer, or any other person who makes a performance. It also includes the producer of a record or the person who causes a communication medium to be used for performance.

The rights that vest in a performer under **Sections 38 and 38A** are:

- Right to record the performance
- Right to reproduce the recording
- Right to broadcast the performance
- Right to communicate the performance to the public
- Right to make translations/adaptations (in some cases)

These rights subsist for a period of **25 years** from the date of performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"The concept of profession is central to the modern understanding of intellectual property rights." Critically analyze this statement with reference to the Indian Copyright Act, 1957, and the Trade Marks Act, 1999.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Profession under the Copyright Act, 1957

**Model Solution:**

The Copyright Act, 1957 recognizes *performers* as a distinct class of professionals under **Section 2(q)**. The Act was amended in **1994** to introduce performer rights, bringing India into compliance with the **Rome Convention for the Protection of Performers, Producers of Phonograms and Broadcasting Organisations (1961)**.

**Key Legal Provisions:**

- **Section 2(q)** — Definition of "performer" — includes actors, singers, musicians, dancers, and other performing artists.
- **Section 38** — Exclusive rights of performers to record, reproduce, broadcast, and communicate their performance.
- **Section 38A** — Exclusive rights of performers to use their performance in sound recordings or video recordings.
- **Section 39** — Duration of performer rights — **25 years** from the date of performance.
- **Section 40** — Reciprocity — Indian performer rights extend to nationals of countries that grant similar rights to Indian performers.

**Critical Analysis:**

1. The inclusion of performers as professionals marks a paradigm shift from the pre-1994 position, where performers had no proprietary rights over their performances.
2. However, the **25-year term** is significantly shorter than the authorial term of *life + 60 years*, suggesting that the legislature viewed performer rights as comparatively weaker.
3. Unlike authors, performers in India do **not** have moral rights — they cannot object to distortion or mutilation of their performance.
4. The 2012 amendment (Copyright Amendment Act, 2012) strengthened performer rights by extending them to sound recordings and broadening the concept of "commercial rental."

**Conclusion**: The Copyright Act recognizes performers as a professional class, but the protections are narrower in scope and duration than those afforded to authors.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Profession under the Trade Marks Act, 1999

**Model Solution:**

The Trade Marks Act, 1999, governs the registration and protection of trademarks and **service marks**. A *service mark* is a mark used in the course of rendering professional services (as opposed to goods).

**Key Legal Provisions:**

- **Section 2(1)(z)** — Definition of "trade mark" — extends to marks used in relation to services.
- **Section 18** — Application for registration of a trademark (including service mark).
- **Schedule IV (Class 45)** — The Nice Classification designates professional services under Class 45, including legal services, medical services, engineering services, and other professional undertakings.

**Critical Analysis:**

1. The concept of *profession* under the Trade Marks Act is operationalized through the **Nice Classification**, which segregates professional services into a distinct class.
2. Professionals such as law firms, medical practitioners, chartered accountants, and consultants can register service marks to protect their brand identity.
3. Infringement of a service mark attracts the same civil and criminal remedies as infringement of a goods mark (Sections 27, 28, 29, 103, 104).
4. The 2017 amendment to the Trade Marks Rules streamlined the registration process, making it more accessible to small and individual professionals.

**Conclusion**: The Trade Marks Act, 1999, operationalizes the concept of "profession" by providing a registration framework (Class 45) that allows professionals to protect their service-based brand identity. Combined with the Copyright Act, the statutory framework demonstrates that the concept of profession is central to the modern IPR regime.

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Examine the rights of "Performers" under the Copyright Act, 1957. How do these rights differ from the rights of authors? Discuss with relevant case laws and statutory provisions.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Statutory Framework for Performer Rights

**Model Solution:**

**Statutory Framework:**

- **Section 2(q)** — Definition of "performer"
- **Section 38** — Specific rights of performers
- **Section 38A** — Exclusive rights for sound/video recordings
- **Section 39** — Term of protection (25 years)
- **Section 40** — International reciprocity

**Detailed Analysis of Rights:**

1. **Right to Make Sound/Visual Recordings**: A performer has the exclusive right to record their performance or authorize such recording.
2. **Right to Reproduce**: The performer can prevent unauthorized reproduction of the recording.
3. **Right to Broadcast**: The performer can control the broadcast of their performance.
4. **Right to Communicate to Public**: The performer can prevent unauthorized communication of their performance to the public.
5. **Right to Commercial Rental**: Post-2012 amendment, performers can control commercial rental of their recordings.

**Duration**: The rights subsist for a period of **25 years** from the date of performance (Section 39).

**Case Law Reference**:

- **Super Cassettes Industries v. Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd. (2009)**: The Delhi High Court held that performers have separate and independent rights distinct from the copyright in the underlying work. The court recognized the performer as a separate class of right-holders.
- **Indian Performing Right Society v. Sanjay Dalia (2015)**: The Bombay High Court clarified that performer rights are economic rights, not moral rights, and cannot be perpetual.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis with Author Rights

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Author Rights | Performer Rights |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Source** | Sections 13–14, CA 1957 | Sections 38–39, CA 1957 |
| **Duration** | Life of author + 60 years | 25 years from performance |
| **Moral Rights** | Yes (Section 57) | No |
| **Economic Rights** | Yes | Yes |
| **Translational Rights** | Yes (Section 14(a)(ii)) | Limited |
| **Infringement Remedies** | Civil + Criminal | Civil + Criminal |
| **International Basis** | Berne Convention | Rome Convention / WPPT |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Term**: Authorial rights last significantly longer (life + 60 years) versus performer rights (25 years).
2. **Moral Rights**: Authors have moral rights (right of attribution, right of integrity) under Section 57; performers do not have statutory moral rights.
3. **Scope**: Authorial rights cover a wider range of acts (translation, adaptation, etc.); performer rights are narrower.
4. **Subject Matter**: Authors' rights attach to the original work; performers' rights attach to the *performance* of the work.

**Critical Evaluation:**

The differential treatment can be justified on the grounds that the author's contribution is *original* and *creative*, while the performer's contribution is *interpretive*. However, modern jurisprudence increasingly recognizes performers as co-creators, and there is a growing call for extending moral rights and longer terms to performers.

**Conclusion**: While the Copyright Act, 1957 grants both authors and performers economic rights, the latter receive a narrower scope of protection. The statutory asymmetry reflects a balance between the rights of original creators and the rights of interpreters, but contemporary trends suggest a gradual expansion of performer rights.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Performer with Author**: Many students interchange the two. Remember: *Performer* = the person performing the work; *Author* = the person who created the work.
> 2. **Wrong Duration**: The most common error is writing "Life + 60 years" for performer rights. The correct term is **25 years from the date of performance**.
> 3. **Omitting Case Laws**: For 14-mark questions, KTU examiners expect at least **1–2 case law references**. Failure to cite *Super Cassettes v. Yahoo* or similar cases can cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Missing Section Numbers**: Always cite the specific section (e.g., Section 2(q), Section 38, Section 39). Vague answers like "as per the Copyright Act" lose marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Moral Rights**: Do not state that performers have moral rights under Indian law. They do **not** have statutory moral rights — only economic rights.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Profession**

- **Profession** = Knowledge-based, ethically regulated, service-oriented vocational activity.
- Distinguish **Profession**, **Trade**, **Commerce**, and **Industry** using the four-factor test (specialized knowledge, regulatory body, code of ethics, personal service).
- **Performer** is defined under **Section 2(q)** of the Copyright Act, 1957.
- Performers were first recognized as a professional class in the **1994 amendment** to the Copyright Act.
- Performer rights vest under **Sections 38 and 38A**.
- Performer rights subsist for **25 years** from the date of performance (**Section 39**).
- Performers in India have **economic rights only** — **no moral rights**.
- **Service Marks** are registered under **Class 45** of the Nice Classification under the Trade Marks Act, 1999.
- **Section 43A** of the IT Act, 2000 imposes professional liability on body corporates for negligence in handling sensitive personal data.
- Key case law: *Super Cassettes Industries v. Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd. (2009)* — performer rights are independent and separate from authorial rights.
- **Public Domain Entry**: After 25 years from performance, the performance enters the public domain.
- **Reciprocity**: Section 40 ensures that Indian performer rights extend to nationals of countries that grant reciprocal rights.
- Always cite **specific section numbers** in your answers to score full marks.
- The **multi-factor test** for distinguishing profession from trade/commerce is a high-yield concept for Part A and Part B questions.
- Remember the **differential treatment** between authors and performers: longer term, broader scope, and moral rights for authors; shorter term, narrower scope, and no moral rights for performers.
- Professional ethics in the cyber context is governed by the **IT Act, 2000** and the **SPDI Rules, 2011**.
- Architects, performers, and software engineers are all "professionals" in the IPR-relevant sense and attract different statutory protections.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Privacy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview: PRIVACY

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber Law** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *IPR and Cyber Laws (PECCT861)*, **Privacy** is defined as the constitutional and legal right of an individual to control the dissemination, collection, storage, and use of their personal information, identity, body, communications, and personal space, free from unauthorized intrusion by the State, private entities, or other individuals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2 - Privacy):**
> "The right to privacy is the right to be let alone. In cyberspace, it extends to the protection of personal data, digital identity, and information privacy against unauthorized access, surveillance, and exploitation."

In the Indian Constitutional framework, the **Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right** intrinsic to **Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)** of the Constitution of India, as unanimously declared by a **9-Judge Bench** of the Supreme Court in *Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India* **(2017) 10 SCC 1**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Privacy** like the **walls of your house**:

- 🏠 Your **house** = your **personal digital life** (messages, photos, browsing history, Aadhaar data, medical records).
- 🚪 The **front door lock** = **consent and access control** (you decide who enters).
- 🪟 **Windows with curtains** = **selective transparency** (you choose what to reveal and to whom).
- 👮 The **local police patrol** = **data protection laws** (e.g., DPDP Act 2023).
- 🏗️ A **neighbor peering in** = **surveillance capitalism** (tech companies collecting behavioral data).

Just as a homeowner wouldn't tolerate a stranger walking in, reading their diary, and sharing it publicly, an individual in cyberspace has a right to keep their personal data private, control its use, and seek legal remedy when violated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Digital Privacy (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Informational Privacy** – control over personal data (e.g., Aadhaar, medical records).
> 2. **Bodily Privacy** – protection of physical body (e.g., against genetic testing without consent).
> 3. **Territorial Privacy** – protection of private spaces (home, devices, communications).

## 1.3 Key Constitutional & Statutory Anchors

| Provision | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Article 21** | Source of Right to Privacy (life and personal liberty) |
| **Article 19(1)(a)** | Freedom of speech (often clashes with privacy) |
| **Article 14** | Equality (non-arbitrary state action) |
| **Article 20(3)** | Self-incrimination (data privacy in criminal trials) |
| **DPDP Act 2023** | Digital Personal Data Protection Act |
| **IT Act 2000, §43A, §72A** | Compensation for data breach, punishment for breach |
| **Aadhaar Act 2016** | Limited privacy regime for biometric data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)** is a **MUST-KNOW case** for KTU University Exams. It declared the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right, overruling the earlier *M.P. Sharma* (1954) and *Kharak Singh* (1962) judgments to the extent they held otherwise.

## 1.4 Standard Privacy Metrics (Bold Constants & Thresholds)

- **Penalty under DPDP Act 2023**: Up to **₹250 crore** per instance for significant data breaches.
- **Notice period for data breach notification**: Within **72 hours** to the Data Protection Board.
- **Data retention period under DPDP**: Limited to **purpose-specific** period; must be deleted thereafter.
- **Benchmark Case Citation**: *Puttaswamy* (2017) decided by a **9-Judge Constitution Bench** (the largest ever in Indian history).
- **Threshold of "Reasonable Expectation of Privacy"**: Set in *Katz v. United States* (1967, USA) — *"a person has a legitimate expectation of privacy in a place or item if they have a subjective expectation of privacy that society accepts as reasonable."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Right to Privacy in India (Step-by-Step Logic)

The right to privacy in India has undergone **five major evolutionary phases**:

### Step 1: Pre-Independence & Early Post-Independence (1950–1975)
- The framers of the Constitution deliberately did **NOT include** an express "right to privacy" in the Fundamental Rights chapter.
- **M.P. Sharma v. Satish Chandra (1954)** — A **8-Judge Bench** held that search and seizure under the FERA did not violate any fundamental right. The Court held that there was "no right to privacy" in the Indian Constitution.
- **Kharak Singh v. State of U.P. (1962)** — A **6-Judge Bench** upheld police surveillance ("domiciliary visits") under UP Police Regulations. Majority (4:2) held there was no fundamental right to privacy, though Justice K. Subba Rao (dissent) recognized it.

### Step 2: Distinction Between 'Right to Privacy' vs 'Right to Life' (1975–1990)
- **Govind v. State of Madhya Pradesh (1975)** — Justice K.K. Mathew recognized that the right to privacy "would encompass and protect the personal intimacies of the home, the family, marriage, motherhood, procreation and child-rearing."
- The Court, however, did not elevate it to a Fundamental Right but said it could be read under **Article 21** as an aspect of personal liberty.

### Step 3: Selective Recognition (1990–2010)
- In cases like *R. Rajagopal v. State of T.N.* (1994) and *PUCL v. Union of India* (1997) (telephone tapping), the Court implicitly recognized privacy but did not declare it an absolute fundamental right.
- The **Right to Information Act 2005** paradoxically strengthened privacy concerns by enabling access to personal data.

### Step 4: The Aadhaar Era and the Puttaswamy Revolution (2012–2017)
- The introduction of **Aadhaar (Unique Identification)** by the UIDAI raised massive privacy concerns.
- A **9-Judge Constitution Bench** of the Supreme Court in *Puttaswamy* (2017) was constituted to settle the question conclusively.

### Step 5: Post-Puttaswamy Statutory Regime (2017–Present)
- The judgment led to the **Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee (2017–2018)** drafting the Personal Data Protection Bill.
- After multiple iterations, the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** (DPDP Act) was enacted.

## 2.2 The Three-Judge Tests Established in Puttaswamy

The Court laid down a **three-pronged test** to check the constitutionality of any state action infringing privacy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROPORTIONALITY TEST (Mandatory for KTU Exams):**
> 1. **Legitimate Aim** — The State action must pursue a legitimate state objective.
> 2. **Necessity / Rational Nexus** — The action must be a necessary and proportionate means to achieve that aim.
> 3. **Proportionality (Strict Balancing)** — The restriction must not be excessive; there must be a balancing between the benefits of the action and the harm to privacy.

If the state action **fails any one prong**, it violates Article 21.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Privacy Principles & Tests)

| S. No. | Principle / Test | Source / Application | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Right to Privacy = Fundamental Right under Art. 21 | *Puttaswamy* (2017) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Proportionality Test (3 prongs) | *Puttaswamy* (2017) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Reasonable Expectation of Privacy | *Katz v. US* (1967) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Informational Self-Determination | *Volkszählung*, German Federal Court (1983) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Minimal Data Collection | OECD Privacy Guidelines (1980) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Purpose Limitation Principle | DPDP Act 2023, §4 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Consent must be Free, Specific, Informed, Unconditional | DPDP Act 2023, §6 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Data Fiduciary vs Data Principal distinction | DPDP Act 2023, §2(i)(t) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Right to be Forgotten (Erasure) | DPDP Act 2023, §12 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Penalties up to ₹250 crore | DPDP Act 2023, §33 | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "What is the test for privacy violation?", students often write only "reasonable restriction." The correct answer in the *Puttaswamy* era is the **Proportionality Test (3 prongs)**. Writing only Article 19 restrictions (reasonable restrictions) will fetch only partial marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Software Engineering**: Privacy by Design (PbD) — embedding privacy into SDLC.
- **Cybersecurity**: Differential privacy in AI/ML training (e.g., Apple, Google).
- **Biometrics**: Aadhaar's privacy challenges informed biometric system design globally.
- **Data Engineering**: Compliance with DPDP requires Data Fiduciaries to implement **encryption, anonymization, pseudonymization**.
- **Network Engineering**: End-to-end encryption (e.g., WhatsApp, Signal) protects message privacy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Application of the Proportionality Test

> Since Privacy is a **legal-conceptual** subject under KTU, the "derivation" here is the **analytical application of the Proportionality Test** to a real-world fact situation. KTU examiners award marks for this structured reasoning.

## 3.1 Case Study: Aadhaar-Based Authentication (Analyzed in Puttaswamy)

**Fact Situation (Simulated):** The Government of India mandates that to claim a subsidized food ration under the Public Distribution System (PDS), every beneficiary must authenticate their identity using Aadhaar biometric data (fingerprint + iris scan).

**Question (KTU-style, 7 marks):** Apply the **Proportionality Test** to determine if the mandatory Aadhaar authentication violates the Right to Privacy.

### Step 1: Identification of the Infringed Right

The mandatory collection of biometric data (fingerprint, iris) by the State constitutes an **informational privacy** infringement under Article 21. The individual has no choice but to part with sensitive bodily data to receive essential rations.

### Step 2: Application of the First Prong — Legitimate State Aim

The State argues its aim is to **eliminate ghost beneficiaries** and ensure that subsidized rations reach genuine citizens, thereby preventing **leakage of public funds** (estimated at ₹1 lakh crore annually).

> **Legal Test**: Is this a legitimate state aim?
> **Application**: Yes. Curbing corruption in welfare distribution and ensuring fiscal discipline is recognized as a legitimate state objective under *Puttaswamy* (referencing Justice Chandrachud's plurality).

### Step 3: Application of the Second Prong — Rational Nexus and Necessity

**Logical Test**: Is Aadhaar authentication **rationally connected** to the goal? Is it **the least intrusive** means?

> **Application**: The Court in *Puttaswamy* noted that **demonstrative identification** (e.g., Aadhaar) does have rational nexus. However, the **Aadhaar Act was found partially unconstitutional** for mandatory linkage to bank accounts, mobile numbers, and school admissions because there were **less intrusive alternatives** available (e.g., OTP-based authentication for PDS, ration cards).
>
> **Conclusion**: For **PDS rations**, the Court upheld Aadhaar under **Section 7** of the Aadhaar Act, but only because alternative means (ration card, OTP) could be used as backup if biometrics failed.

### Step 4: Application of the Third Prong — Proportionality (Strict Balancing)

**Legal Test**: Does the benefit to the public outweigh the invasion of privacy?

> **Application**: The benefit of preventing fraudulent claims and ensuring food security for the poor is substantial. The privacy intrusion is limited to authentication, not continuous surveillance. However, the **storage of data** must follow minimal data collection principles.

### Step 5: Final Conclusion

> **Conclusion**: The mandatory Aadhaar authentication for PDS was held to **pass the proportionality test** because:
> 1. There is a legitimate state aim (preventing leakage of food subsidies).
> 2. There is rational nexus and necessity (biometric authentication uniquely links to identity).
> 3. The benefits outweigh the privacy intrusion, **provided** there are fallback authentication mechanisms for authentication failures (this saved the law from being struck down).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Privacy vs Other Rights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield: When Privacy clashes with another right, how is the conflict resolved?**

| Scenario | Right in Conflict | Test Applied | KTU Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aadhaar-Bank Account Linkage** | Privacy vs State subsidy efficiency | Strict Proportionality | ❌ Struck down (Section 7 read down) |
| **DNA Database for Criminals** | Privacy vs Criminal Justice | Proportionality | ✅ Upheld if narrowly tailored |
| **Surveillance on National Security** | Privacy vs National Security | Proportionality + NS Need | ✅ Upheld under Art. 19(2) reasonable restriction |
| **Medical Records Disclosure in Court** | Privacy vs Fair Trial | Balancing Test | ⚖️ Case-specific |
| **Right to be Forgotten vs Right to Information** | Privacy vs RTI | Specific Balancing | ✅ Privacy prevails for personal data |
| **Marital Rape & Privacy** | Privacy of husband vs Bodily autonomy of wife | Proportionality | Privacy of spouse prevails |

## 3.3 The OECD Privacy Framework (1980) — 8 Principles (Critical for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **8 Core Principles (Updated 2013):**
>
> 1. **Collection Limitation Principle** — Data should be obtained by lawful and fair means, with knowledge/consent.
> 2. **Data Quality Principle** — Data must be relevant, accurate, complete, and up-to-date.
> 3. **Purpose Specification Principle** — Purpose of collection must be specified at the time of collection.
> 4. **Use Limitation Principle** — Data must not be used for purposes other than those specified.
> 5. **Security Safeguards Principle** — Reasonable security safeguards must be in place.
> 6. **Openness Principle** — There must be a general policy of openness about developments, practices, and policies.
> 7. **Individual Participation Principle** — Individuals have the right to know, access, and challenge data.
> 8. **Accountability Principle** — Data controller must be accountable for complying with these principles.

## 3.4 Key Definitions under DPDP Act 2023

- **Data Principal** (§2(i)(t)) — The individual to whom the personal data relates.
- **Data Fiduciary** (§2(i)(u)) — The entity that determines the purpose and means of processing personal data.
- **Personal Data** (§2(t)) — Any data about an individual who is identifiable by or in relation to such data.
- **Consent** (§6) — Must be free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous.
- **Data Principal Rights** (§11–§14) — Right to access, correction, erasure (Right to be Forgotten), and grievance redressal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Evolution of Right to Privacy in India

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constitution of India 1950 - No Express Right to Privacy] --> B[MP Sharma 1954 - No Privacy Right]
    A --> C[Kharak Singh 1962 - Domiciliary Visits Upheld]
    C --> D[Justice Subba Rao Dissent - First Recognition]
    D --> E[Govind vs State of MP 1975 - Privacy under Art 21]
    E --> F[R Rajagopal 1994 - Defamation & Privacy]
    F --> G[PUCL vs Union of India 1997 - Telephone Tapping]
    G --> H[Aadhaar Challenge 2012 - Privacy Concerns Rise]
    H --> I[9-Judge Bench Constituted 2015]
    I --> J[Puttaswamy 2017 - Privacy as Fundamental Right]
    J --> K[Justice BN Srikrishna Committee 2017-18]
    K --> L[Personal Data Protection Bill 2019]
    L --> M[JPC Report 2021 - Revised Bill]
    M --> N[DPDP Act 2023 - Enacted]
    J --> O[Art 21 Privacy Regime]
    N --> P[Statutory Privacy Rights]
    O --> P
```

> **Figure 1:** The chronological evolution of the Right to Privacy in India, from non-recognition in *M.P. Sharma* (1954) to its full statutory codification in the DPDP Act (2023).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Proportionality Test Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[State Action Affecting Privacy] --> Q1{Is There a Legitimate State Aim?}
    Q1 -- No --> Fail[Action is Unconstitutional]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the Measure Necessary and Rationally Connected?}
    Q2 -- No --> Fail
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Does the Benefit Outweigh the Privacy Harm?}
    Q3 -- No --> Fail
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Are There Less Intrusive Alternatives?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Fail
    Q4 -- No --> Pass[Action is Constitutionally Valid]
    style Fail fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style Pass fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style Start fill:#4dabf7,color:#fff
```

> **Figure 2:** The **3-prong Proportionality Test** as mandated in *K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India* (2017) for any state action restricting privacy.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Data Protection Framework under DPDP Act 2023

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Principal - Individual] -->|Provides Data| B[Data Fiduciary - Company/Entity]
    B -->|Processing| C[Data Processor]
    C -->|Storage| D[(Database)]
    D -->|Breach Detection| E{Data Breach?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Notify Data Protection Board within 72 hrs]
    E -- No --> G[Continued Processing]
    F --> H[Penalties up to Rs 250 Crore]
    B -->|Subject to| I[Data Protection Board]
    A -->|Right to| J[Access, Correction, Erasure]
    A -->|Right to| K[Grievance Redressal]
    style A fill:#ffd43b
    style B fill:#74c0fc
    style I fill:#ff8787
    style H fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
```

> **Figure 3:** Block-level functional architecture of the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023** showing data flow and accountability.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Privacy vs Conflicting Rights — Conflict Resolution Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Privacy[Right to Privacy - Art 21] -->|Conflicts with| A[Right to Information - Art 19]
    Privacy -->|Conflicts with| B[National Security - Art 19(2)]
    Privacy -->|Conflicts with| C[Right to Free Speech - Art 19(1)(a)]
    Privacy -->|Conflicts with| D[Right to Fair Trial - Art 14, 21]
    Privacy -->|Conflicts with| E[State Surveillance - IT Act Sec 69]

    A -->|Resolution| R1[Balance test - Personal data exempt under Sec 8 RTI]
    B -->|Resolution| R2[Proportionality - National security prevails if narrowly tailored]
    C -->|Resolution| R3[Prior restraint not allowed - Defamation principles apply]
    D -->|Resolution| R4[Court orders disclosure on case-by-case basis]
    E -->|Resolution| R5[Procedural safeguards under Sec 69 - Review Committee required]
    style Privacy fill:#ffd43b
    style R1 fill:#b2f2bb
    style R2 fill:#b2f2bb
    style R3 fill:#b2f2bb
    style R4 fill:#b2f2bb
    style R5 fill:#b2f2bb
```

> **Figure 4:** Resolution of conflicts between the Right to Privacy and other constitutional/statutory rights.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define "Right to Privacy" and mention the constitutional provision under which it is recognized in India.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Right to Privacy is the right of an individual to be let alone and to control the collection, storage, dissemination, and use of their personal information, identity, and bodily data. In India, the Right to Privacy was recognized as a **Fundamental Right** under **Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)** of the Constitution in the landmark case of *Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India* **(2017) 10 SCC 1**, by a 9-Judge Constitution Bench. The right is not absolute and is subject to reasonable restrictions based on the **Proportionality Test**.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the constitutional provision, 1 mark for the case citation and bench strength.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the "Reasonable Expectation of Privacy" test?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The "Reasonable Expectation of Privacy" is a legal test laid down by the **United States Supreme Court** in *Katz v. United States (1967)*. It holds that a person has a legitimate expectation of privacy in a place or item if they have:
1. A **subjective expectation of privacy** in that place or item, AND
2. Society accepts that expectation as **objectively reasonable**.

The test is widely used in Indian jurisprudence to determine whether an act constitutes a violation of privacy. For example, in *Puttaswamy*, the Court held that individuals have a reasonable expectation of privacy over their **biometric data, telephonic communications, and personal information** stored by the State or private entities.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for citation, 1 mark for the two-fold test, 1 mark for Indian application.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **ONE of two choices**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Right to Privacy as a Fundamental Right in India. Discuss the evolution of this right through judicial pronouncements leading to the *K.S. Puttaswamy* judgment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **three-pronged Proportionality Test** laid down in *Puttaswamy* for evaluating state actions that affect the right to privacy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks:**

The Right to Privacy refers to the right of an individual to control personal information, bodily autonomy, and private spaces free from unauthorized intrusion. Although not expressly mentioned in the Constitution, its evolution can be traced as follows:

- **M.P. Sharma v. Satish Chandra (1954)** — A 8-Judge Bench held that there is no fundamental right to privacy in the Indian Constitution. Search and seizure under FERA was upheld.
- **Kharak Singh v. State of UP (1962)** — A 6-Judge Bench upheld domiciliary visits, with Justice K. Subba Rao dissenting and recognizing privacy as an essential ingredient of personal liberty.
- **Govind v. State of MP (1975)** — The Court held that the right to privacy could be traced under **Article 21** but not as an absolute right.
- **R. Rajagopal v. State of TN (1994)** — Recognized privacy in the context of defamation and consent-based publication.
- **PUCL v. Union of India (1997)** — Issued guidelines for telephone tapping, indirectly recognizing privacy.
- **K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)** — A historic **9-Judge Bench** unanimously declared the Right to Privacy as a **Fundamental Right** under Article 21, overruling the earlier decisions in M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh to the extent they held otherwise. Justice D.Y. Chandrachud held that privacy is the constitutional core of human dignity.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 4 marks for case evolution, 1 mark for Puttaswamy ruling, 1 mark for overruling aspect.)*

**(b) — 7 marks:**

The **Proportionality Test** in *Puttaswamy* evaluates whether a state action infringing privacy is constitutionally valid. It consists of three prongs:

1. **Legitimate State Aim (Rationality Test):** The state action must pursue a legitimate state objective. For example, national security, public health, prevention of crime, or prevention of tax evasion are recognized as legitimate aims.

2. **Necessity and Rational Nexus:** The measure adopted must be a **necessary** means to achieve the stated aim, and there must be a **rational nexus** between the action and the objective. The state must demonstrate that no less intrusive alternative exists.

3. **Proportionality (Strict Balancing):** The benefits derived from the state action must **outweigh** the harm caused to the individual's right to privacy. The restriction must not be excessive or disproportionate to the aim sought.

> *If any one of the three prongs fails, the state action is unconstitutional.* This test was further refined in *Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India (2020) — Kashmir Internet Shutdown case* and *Internet and Mobile Association of India v. RBI (2020)*.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for introduction, 2 marks each for the three prongs with examples.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **8 principles of the OECD Privacy Framework (2013)** and explain their relevance in the modern digital economy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the salient features of the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** with a focus on the rights of the Data Principal and the obligations of the Data Fiduciary. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks:**

The **OECD Privacy Framework**, originally adopted in 1980 and revised in 2013, provides the foundation for global data protection laws. The 8 principles are:

1. **Collection Limitation Principle** — Personal data should be collected only by lawful and fair means, with the knowledge or consent of the data subject.
2. **Data Quality Principle** — Personal data should be relevant, accurate, complete, and kept up-to-date.
3. **Purpose Specification Principle** — The purpose for which data is collected should be specified at the time of collection.
4. **Use Limitation Principle** — Data should not be used or disclosed for purposes other than those specified, except with the consent of the data subject or by legal authority.
5. **Security Safeguards Principle** — Personal data should be protected by reasonable security safeguards against risks like loss, unauthorized access, or destruction.
6. **Openness Principle** — There should be a general policy of openness about developments, practices, and policies with respect to personal data.
7. **Individual Participation Principle** — Individuals should have the right to know whether data about them is being processed, to access it, and to challenge its accuracy.
8. **Accountability Principle** — A data controller should be accountable for complying with measures that give effect to the above principles.

**Relevance in the Digital Economy:**
These principles are operationalized in the **DPDP Act 2023** (Indian), **GDPR (EU)**, and **PIPEDA (Canada)**. They guide companies in **Privacy by Design (PbD)**, ensuring user trust, minimizing breach liabilities, and enabling cross-border data flow compliance.

*(Mark Distribution: 0.5 mark for each principle × 8 = 4 marks, 1.5 marks for OECD introduction, 1.5 marks for modern relevance.)*

**(b) — 7 marks:**

The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** is India's comprehensive data protection law. Its salient features are:

**1. Application:** Applies to the processing of digital personal data within India and outside India if it is connected with offering goods or services to individuals in India.

**2. Key Definitions:**
- **Data Principal:** The individual to whom personal data relates (§2(i)(t)).
- **Data Fiduciary:** The entity that determines the purpose and means of processing personal data (§2(i)(u)).

**3. Rights of the Data Principal (§11–§14):**
- **Right to Access** — Obtain a summary of personal data and processing activities.
- **Right to Correction** — Correct inaccurate or misleading personal data.
- **Right to Erasure (Right to be Forgotten)** — Request deletion of personal data when it is no longer necessary.
- **Right to Grievance Redressal** — Lodge a complaint with the Data Fiduciary, and if unresolved, with the Data Protection Board.

**4. Obligations of the Data Fiduciary:**
- Obtain **free, specific, informed, and unconditional consent** (§6).
- Process data only for the **lawful purpose** for which consent was given.
- Implement **reasonable security safeguards** to prevent data breaches.
- Notify the Data Protection Board of any **data breach within 72 hours** (§8(7)).
- Delete personal data upon the **Data Principal's withdrawal of consent** or when the purpose is fulfilled.
- Appoint a **Data Protection Officer (DPO)** if classified as a Significant Data Fiduciary.

**5. Penalties:** Penalties up to **₹250 crore** per instance for non-compliance (§33).

**6. Data Protection Board of India (DPB):** Established under §18 to adjudicate complaints and impose penalties.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for application, 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for rights, 2 marks for obligations and penalties.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Privacy Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **❌ Writing "Right to Privacy is an absolute right."** — WRONG. It is subject to reasonable restrictions. Always qualify it.
> 2. **❌ Citing only Article 19 restrictions for privacy violations.** — WRONG after *Puttaswamy*. The correct test is the **Proportionality Test** (3 prongs).
> 3. **❌ Citing *M.P. Sharma (1954)* as supporting privacy.** — DANGER! That judgment actually held there was NO right to privacy. It was overruled by *Puttaswamy* in 2017.
> 4. **❌ Forgetting the bench strength** in case citations. KTU examiners **deduct marks** if you don't write "9-Judge Bench" for *Puttaswamy*.
> 5. **❌ Confusing DPDP Act 2023 with the older 2019 Bill.** — The Act was enacted in **August 2023**. Note: The Act is criticized for being **less stringent than GDPR** (no horizontal right to data portability, weaker Right to be Forgotten, broad exemptions for State).
> 6. **❌ Writing "Aadhaar was struck down"** — Partially wrong. Aadhaar was upheld for PDS, LPG, and income tax purposes but struck down for bank accounts, mobile numbers, and school admissions.
> 7. **❌ Mixing up "Data Principal" with "Data Subject"** — Under the Indian DPDP Act, the correct term is **Data Principal** (§2(i)(t)). Using "Data Subject" (GDPR term) is acceptable but not preferred.
> 8. **❌ Skipping the case of *Anuradha Bhasin (2020)*** — This judgment applied the Proportionality Test to **internet shutdowns**, an important post-Puttaswamy development.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU 2024 — PRIVACY — RAPID REVISION (Last-Minute Checklist) 🔥**

### ⚖️ Constitutional Foundation
- ✅ Right to Privacy = Fundamental Right under **Article 21** (*Puttaswamy*, 2017).
- ✅ 9-Judge Constitution Bench = **largest ever** in Indian history.
- ✅ Overruled *M.P. Sharma (1954)* and *Kharak Singh (1962)* to the extent they held otherwise.
- ✅ Article 14 (equality) and Article 19 (freedom of speech) also support privacy.

### 📋 Must-Know Case Law
- ✅ *M.P. Sharma (1954)* — Search and seizure.
- ✅ *Kharak Singh (1962)* — Domiciliary visits; Justice Subba Rao dissent.
- ✅ *Govind v. State of MP (1975)* — Privacy as aspect of Art. 21.
- ✅ *R. Rajagopal (1994)* — Defamation & consent.
- ✅ *PUCL v. UOI (1997)* — Telephone tapping guidelines.
- ✅ *Puttaswamy (2017)* — 9-Judge Bench, 3-prong Proportionality Test.
- ✅ *Aadhaar judgment* — Section 7 of Aadhaar Act upheld, Section 33(2) struck down.
- ✅ *Anuradha Bhasin (2020)* — Internet shutdown proportionality.

### 🧪 Three-Pronged Proportionality Test
1. ✅ **Legitimate State Aim**
2. ✅ **Necessity & Rational Nexus**
3. ✅ **Proportionality (Strict Balancing)**

If any prong fails → **Unconstitutional**.

### 📜 Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP)
- ✅ **Data Principal** = Individual (§2(i)(t)).
- ✅ **Data Fiduciary** = Entity controlling data (§2(i)(u)).
- ✅ **Consent** = Free, Specific, Informed, Unconditional, Unambiguous (§6).
- ✅ **Data Breach Notification** = Within **72 hours** (§8(7)).
- ✅ **Maximum Penalty** = **₹250 crore** (§33).
- ✅ **Right to be Forgotten** = §12.
- ✅ **Data Protection Board of India** = Adjudicatory body (§18).

### 🌐 OECD 8 Principles (1980/2013)
- ✅ Collection Limitation
- ✅ Data Quality
- ✅ Purpose Specification
- ✅ Use Limitation
- ✅ Security Safeguards
- ✅ Openness
- ✅ Individual Participation
- ✅ Accountability

### 🔑 Key Distinctions (Common KTU Question)
- ✅ **Data Principal** vs **Data Fiduciary**.
- ✅ **Information Privacy** vs **Bodily Privacy** vs **Territorial Privacy**.
- ✅ **Right to Privacy (Art. 21)** vs **Right to Information (Art. 19)**.
- ✅ **Personal Data** vs **Non-Personal Data** (latter is outside DPDP 2023).
- ✅ **GDPR** (EU) vs **DPDP Act 2023** (India) — Indian Act is less stringent.
- ✅ **Anonymization** vs **Pseudonymization** — both reduce re-identification risk.

### 📌 KTU Frequently Repeated Keywords (Use in Answers for Higher Marks)
- "**Constitutional core of human dignity**" — Justice Chandrachud, *Puttaswamy*.
- "**Informational self-determination**" — German Federal Constitutional Court, 1983.
- "**Reasonable expectation of privacy**" — *Katz v. US* (1967).
- "**Privacy by Design**" — Ann Cavoukian (2009).
- "**Data minimisation**" — OECD principle.
- "**Privacy as a fundamental right, not a privilege**" — Justice Kaul, *Puttaswamy*.

### 🚫 Top 5 Things to AVOID Writing in Exam
- ❌ "Privacy is an absolute right" — WRONG.
- ❌ "Privacy is not a fundamental right" — OUTDATED (pre-2017).
- ❌ "Aadhaar was struck down" — PARTIALLY WRONG.
- ❌ "Puttaswamy was decided by 5 judges" — WRONG (it was 9).
- ❌ "DPDP Act 2023 is the same as GDPR" — WRONG (DPDP is less stringent).

> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When in doubt in a KTU exam, structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Case Law → (3) Proportionality Test → (4) Statutory Framework → (5) Conclusion with current developments**. This five-step structure is the **gold standard** for fetching 12+ out of 14 marks in Part B Privacy questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Piracy**, in the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) and Cyber Law, refers to the **unauthorized reproduction, distribution, public performance, communication to the public, or making derivative works** of copyrighted material, typically for commercial gain or personal use without permission from, or payment to, the rights holder. It is a direct infringement of the **Copyright Act, 1957** of India and constitutes a cognizable and non-bailable offense under both the Copyright Act and the **Information Technology Act, 2000** when executed through digital/cyber means.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Piracy):**
> As per the PECCT861 2024 Scheme syllabus, "Piracy" encompasses **Software Piracy**, **Internet/Online Piracy**, **Anti-piracy measures**, and the **legal remedies** available to copyright owners under Indian Cyber Law.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Book Theft" Model

Imagine you walk into a public library, scan a brand-new copyrighted novel page-by-page, and then sell identical photocopies on the street for ₹20 each — the publisher gets nothing, the author gets nothing, but the photocopier profits. **That is Piracy in its purest form.** The "content" is stolen at almost zero marginal cost, but the commercial value, creative labor, and economic rights of the original creator are completely appropriated by an unauthorized third party.

In the digital world, this "photocopying" is replaced by **BitTorrent sharing, illegal streaming sites, cracked software, and pirated e-books** — but the legal and economic harm is identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Piracy (Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **The Act** → Copying / reproducing / distributing
> 2. **The Object** → A protected copyrighted work (software, film, music, book, database)
> 3. **The Authorization** → WITHOUT permission of the copyright owner

### 1.3 Classification of Piracy — KTU Taxonomy

Piracy is broadly classified into two main branches for KTU examination purposes:

| Branch | Definition | Typical Vector |
|---|---|---|
| **Offline / Physical Piracy** | Tangible reproduction (CDs, DVDs, counterfeit books) | Physical markets, street vendors |
| **Online / Digital Piracy** | Unauthorized digital copying and dissemination | Torrents, streaming sites, cracked APKs, dark web |

Within these, the KTU 2024 syllabus specifically focuses on:

1. **Software Piracy**
2. **Internet/Online Piracy**
3. **Anti-Piracy Measures**
4. **Legal Recourse and Enforcement**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Piracy — Detailed Analytical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Software Piracy

Software piracy refers to the **illegal copying, distribution, modification, or use of software** without the appropriate license or permission from the developer/owner. It is one of the most economically damaging forms of piracy globally.

**Sub-Categories of Software Piracy:**

- **End-User Piracy**: When an individual uses a single licensed copy of software on multiple machines, or installs it beyond the allowed number of seats.
- **Pre-installed Software Piracy (OEM Piracy)**: When a computer manufacturer installs unlicensed copies of software on the machines it sells.
- **Counterfeiting**: The illegal duplication and sale of copyrighted software in packaging that closely resembles the original product.
- **OEM Unbundling**: The sale of software meant to be bundled with hardware as a standalone product.
- **Hard-Disk Loading**: Installation of unauthorized software onto a computer's hard disk before the computer is sold to the customer.
- **Softlifting**: Purchasing a single licensed copy of software and loading it onto multiple machines (common in educational institutions and small offices).
- **Client-Server Overuse**: Installing more copies of a network-based software than the license permits.
- **Internet / Online Software Piracy**: Downloading cracked versions, key generators (keygens), or serial numbers from unauthorized websites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Piracy Metric — Business Software Alliance (BSA) Statistic:**
> The global software piracy rate is approximately **37%** (as per the latest BSA Global Software Survey). The commercial value of unlicensed software is estimated at **tens of billions of USD annually**, making this a critical enforcement priority under Cyber Law.

#### 2.1.2 Internet / Online Piracy

Online piracy is the act of **uploading, downloading, or streaming copyrighted digital content** through unauthorized channels. This category has overtaken physical piracy in scale and severity in the 21st century.

**Common Online Piracy Vectors:**

- **Torrent Networks (P2P Sharing)**: BitTorrent protocol used to distribute movies, software, music, and e-books.
- **Streaming Piracy**: Illegal streaming sites (e.g., unauthorized IPTV, Kodi add-ons) that rebroadcast copyrighted content.
- **Cyber Lockers / File Hosting**: Cloud storage abused to host and share pirated content.
- **Streaming Rippers**: Tools that capture and save streams for later offline viewing.
- **Cracked Software Repositories**: Websites distributing patches, key generators, and pre-activated installers.
- **e-Book Piracy**: Mass distribution of scanned books on platforms like Library Genesis, Z-Library (prior to seizure), Sci-Hub.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Online Piracy is Harder to Combat:**
> - **Jurisdictional ambiguity**: A user in India can download from a server in Ukraine via a CDN in Singapore.
> - **Anonymity**: VPN, Tor, and proxy services obscure user identity.
> - **Sheer scale**: Millions of infringing files; takedown is a "whack-a-mole" problem.
> - **Borderless nature**: Traditional copyright law was designed for physical, territorial infringements.

#### 2.1.3 Piracy of Other Copyrighted Works

- **Film Piracy**: Camrip, telesync, and HD rips of theatrical releases — India is among the top 5 affected markets.
- **Music Piracy**: MP3 ripping from streaming services, unauthorized distribution via apps.
- **Book Piracy**: Photocopying in universities (the "course-pack" problem) and digital scanning.

### 2.2 Legal Framework Governing Piracy in India

#### 2.2.1 The Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended in 2012)

The Copyright Act is the **primary statute** dealing with piracy in India.

**Key Sections for Piracy:**

- **Section 13**: Defines the classes of works in which copyright subsists (literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematograph films, sound recordings).
- **Section 14**: Defines the "exclusive rights" of the copyright owner (reproduction, distribution, communication, adaptation).
- **Section 51**: Defines **"copyright infringement"** — the act of doing anything reserved for the copyright owner without a license.
- **Section 62**: Establishes cognizance of offenses — copyright infringement is a **cognizable and non-bailable offense** for commercial scale violations.
- **Section 63**: Penalty for infringement — imprisonment up to **3 years and/or fine up to ₹2 lakh** (first offense); for second and subsequent offenses, imprisonment up to **6 years and/or fine up to ₹5 lakh**.
- **Section 65**: **Tampering with Computer Programs and Software Piracy** — specific offense for dealing with pirated software; penalty includes imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹2 lakh, or both.
- **Section 66**: Computer-related offenses (handled by IT Act).

#### 2.2.2 The Information Technology Act, 2000 (Amended 2008)

The IT Act adds **cyber-specific dimensions** to piracy prosecution.

- **Section 43**: Penalty for damage to computer, computer system, etc. (applies to unauthorized access/duplication).
- **Section 65**: Tampering with computer source documents.
- **Section 66**: Computer-related offenses — punishment up to **3 years imprisonment or fine up to ₹5 lakh, or both**.
- **Section 66A** (struck down by Supreme Court in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India*, 2015, but historically relevant for online speech).
- **Section 67**: Publishing or transmitting obscene material in electronic form.
- **Section 69**: Government powers to intercept, monitor, and decrypt information.
- **Section 79**: Safe harbor for intermediaries (conditional — must comply with takedown notices).

#### 2.2.3 International Legal Instruments

- **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**: Article 61 mandates criminal procedures and penalties for willful copyright piracy on a commercial scale.
- **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT), 1996**: Addresses digital rights and anti-circumvention.
- **WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty (WPPT), 1996**: Protects sound recordings in the digital environment.
- **Berne Convention (1886, revised)**: Foundation of international copyright protection.

### 2.3 Anti-Piracy Measures

#### 2.3.1 Technical / Digital Measures (DRM)

- **Digital Rights Management (DRM)**: Software/hardware controls limiting use of digital content.
- **Encryption**: Scrambling content so only authorized decryption keys can access it.
- **Watermarking**: Embedding identifiable code into content to trace leaks.
- **Software Activation**: Online or phone-based product activation (Microsoft, Adobe).
- **Code Obfuscation**: Making software harder to reverse-engineer or crack.

#### 2.3.2 Legal / Enforcement Measures

- **John Doe / Ashok Kumar Orders**: Pre-litigation court orders allowing rights holders to obtain the identities of anonymous infringers.
- **Website Blocking Orders**: Issued by the **High Court of Delhi (designated as the Copyright Board)** under **Section 79(3)(b) of the IT Act, 2000** to block piracy websites. Famous orders include the 2019 injunction against 800+ piracy sites.
- **Criminal Raids**: Conducted by the **Copyright Office**, police (under Section 64 of the Copyright Act), and specialized IPR cells.
- **Customs Enforcement**: Border seizure of pirated goods (Section 53 of the Copyright Act).
- **Civil Remedies**: Injunctions, damages, accounts of profits (Sections 55, 58, 59 of the Copyright Act).

#### 2.3.3 Industry / Collective Measures

- **BSA (Business Software Alliance)**: Global anti-piracy advocacy and reporting.
- **IFPI (International Federation of the Phonographic Industry)**: Anti-music-piracy body.
- **MPA (Motion Picture Association)**: Anti-film-piracy body.
- **NASSCOM's Anti-Piracy Alliance**: Indian IT industry initiative.

#### 2.3.4 Educational & Awareness Measures

- Public awareness campaigns.
- University-level licensing schemes (e.g., Microsoft DreamSpark).
- Corporate compliance training.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Industry Impact

- **IT Industry Loss**: The Indian IT industry loses an estimated **$2-3 billion annually** to software piracy (NASSCOM estimates).
- **Cybersecurity Link**: Pirated software often contains **malware, trojans, and backdoors** — making piracy a direct vector for cyberattacks. Studies (Microsoft, IDC) show that organizations using unlicensed software face a **1-in-3 chance of encountering malware**.
- **National Security**: Pirated software in critical infrastructure (defense, power, banking) poses serious national security risks.
- **Innovation Stifling**: Reduced revenue disincentivizes R&D investment.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Piracy" is a **legal and management topic** (not a mathematical/physical system), this section replaces traditional derivations with **analytical legal reasoning frameworks, case-law application, and decision matrices** that are mandatory for KTU 14-mark essay-style answers.

### 3.1 Analytical Framework — The "ACT-MODEL" Test for Determining Piracy

In any KTU essay question on Piracy, a high-scoring answer must walk through a **systematic test** to determine whether an act constitutes piracy. Use this **six-element ACT-MODEL framework** as your scoring structure:

> **A** — **A**ccessing or acquiring the protected work
> **C** — **C**opying / reproducing the work
> **T** — **T**ransferring or distributing the copy
> **M** — **M**odifying or adapting the work
> **O** — **O**mitting attribution / failing to pay royalties
> **D** — **D**oing all of the above without a valid **L**icense
> **E** — **E**arning commercial or personal benefit (or causing commercial harm)
> **L** — **L**inking the act to a **covered statutory section** (Sec. 51, 65, 66 IT Act)

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Application (Worked Example)

**Scenario**: "Ravi downloads a cracked version of Adobe Photoshop from a torrent site and uses it for his freelance graphic design business."

**Step 1 — A (Access)**: Ravi accessed the Adobe software. ✅ Triggered.

**Step 2 — C (Copy)**: The torrent transfer resulted in a bit-for-bit reproduction on his hard drive. ✅ Triggered.

**Step 3 — T (Transfer)**: He also uploaded a portion of the download to other torrent peers (seeding), distributing copies to others. ✅ Triggered.

**Step 4 — M (Modify)**: The crack patched the binary; this may or may not be "modification" depending on Adobe's EULA definitions. (Secondary issue.)

**Step 5 — O (Omit)**: He paid ₹0 to Adobe; royalty/license fee is omitted. ✅ Triggered.

**Step 6 — D (License)**: No commercial license was obtained. ✅ Triggered.

**Step 7 — E (Economic Benefit)**: Ravi is using the pirated software for **commercial freelance work** — this satisfies the "commercial scale" threshold under **Section 51 of the Copyright Act, 1957**. ✅ Triggered.

**Step 8 — L (Legal Linking)**: His conduct invokes:
- **Section 51 + 63 of the Copyright Act, 1957** → Infringement with commercial exploitation (3 years / ₹2 lakh).
- **Section 65 of the Copyright Act, 1957** → Specific software piracy provision (3 years / ₹2 lakh).
- **Section 43 + 66 of the IT Act, 2000** → Unauthorized use of computer resource / computer-related offense (3 years / ₹5 lakh).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Scoring Insight:** Examiners award 2-3 marks purely for **identifying ALL applicable sections**. A student who cites only "Section 63" will lose 2 marks compared to a student who lists Sections **51, 63, 65, and IT Act 66**.

### 3.2 Landmark Case Law — KTU High-Value Case Bank

Memorize these **four cases** — they appear in nearly every KTU Module-2 question.

#### 3.2.1 *Super Cassettes Industries v. Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd.* (Delhi HC, 2009)

**Facts**: Plaintiff (T-Series) sued Yahoo for hosting user-uploaded infringing songs on its platform.

**Holding**: The court ruled that the intermediary (Yahoo) **failed to exercise due diligence** and was not entitled to the safe harbor under Section 79 of the IT Act.

**Principle**: Intermediaries must act expeditiously on takedown notices; passive hosting of infringing content strips safe-harbor protection.

**KTU Use**: Cite when answering questions on **"Intermediary Liability in Online Piracy."**

#### 3.2.2 *Christian Louboutin SAS v. Nakul Bajaj & Ors.* (Delhi HC, 2018)

**Facts**: Domain name `christianlouboutin.in` was registered by an unauthorized party for selling counterfeit shoes.

**Holding**: The domain was transferred to the plaintiff; the court also addressed the parallel concern of counterfeit goods listings.

**Principle**: Domain names tied to brand piracy are actionable under both the Copyright Act and the INDRP (Indian Domain Name Resolution Policy).

#### 3.2.3 *Uttam Singh v. State (NCT of Delhi)* — *Film Piracy Raid Case*

**Facts**: Police raid on a film duplication unit.

**Holding**: Infringement under Section 51 + 63 is a **cognizable offense**; police can arrest without a warrant for commercial-scale infringement.

**Principle**: Establishes the **criminal enforcement pathway** for software and film piracy.

#### 3.2.4 *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India* (Supreme Court, 2015)

**Facts**: Constitutional challenge to Section 66A of the IT Act.

**Holding**: Section 66A was **struck down** as violative of Article 19(1)(a) (Freedom of Speech).

**Principle**: While 66A was struck down, the judgment **upheld Section 79** safe harbor and clarified the scope of intermediary obligations — directly relevant to piracy takedown procedures.

### 3.3 The "Six-Point Anti-Piracy Strategy" — KTU Analytical Matrix

For a 14-mark question on "Anti-Piracy Measures," structure your answer as the following **six-layer defense model**:

> **Layer 1 — Prevention (Technical)**: DRM, encryption, watermarking, product activation, software license servers.
> **Layer 2 — Detection (Monitoring)**: Web crawlers, anti-piracy bots (e.g., Audible Magic), BSA tip lines, user reporting.
> **Layer 3 — Legal Action (Civil)**: Injunctions, damages, Anton Piller orders (search-and-seize), John Doe orders, website blocking under Sec. 79(3)(b) IT Act.
> **Layer 4 — Criminal Enforcement**: FIRs under Sec. 63/65 Copyright Act; raids by Crime Branch; prosecution by the public prosecutor.
> **Layer 5 — Administrative**: Customs border seizure; Ministry of Information & Broadcasting blocking orders; ISP cooperation.
> **Layer 6 — Education & Market Alternatives**: Affordable legal alternatives (Spotify, Netflix, Adobe subscription models) to undercut the piracy value proposition.

### 3.4 Comparative Penalty Computation — Decision Tree

Use this **decision tree** to determine the **correct statutory section** in KTU questions:

$$
\text{IF (act involves software copy)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Sec. 65 of Copyright Act (3 yr / ₹2L)}
$$

$$
\text{IF (act involves any copyrighted work, commercial scale)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Sec. 51 + 63 of Copyright Act (3 yr first / 6 yr subsequent)}
$$

$$
\text{IF (act is done via computer/hacking/unauthorized access)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Sec. 43 + 66 of IT Act (3 yr / ₹5L)}
$$

$$
\text{IF (act is publishing/transmitting obscene material)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Sec. 67 of IT Act (5 yr / 7 yr)}
$$

$$
\text{IF (act endangers sovereignty / integrity of nation)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Sec. 66F of IT Act (Life imprisonment)}
$$

$$
\text{IF (act is by an intermediary failing takedown)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Sec. 79 of IT Act (Loss of safe harbor)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often cite **Section 66 of the IT Act, 2000** for ALL piracy cases. This is **incorrect**. Section 66 covers *computer-related offenses* (e.g., hacking, denial of service), not copyright piracy per se. The **Copyright Act** is the primary statute for piracy; the IT Act is invoked for the **cyber/electronic medium** in which the piracy occurs.

### 3.5 Economic Loss Estimation (Analytical Model)

The **commercial loss from piracy** can be estimated using a simple analytical model frequently tested in KTU:

$$
\text{Commercial Loss} \;=\; (N_p \times P_l) - (N_l \times P_l)
$$

Where:
- $N_p$ = Number of pirated copies in circulation
- $N_l$ = Number of legitimate copies sold
- $P_l$ = Per-unit legitimate price

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

**Step 1**: Identify the **per-unit price** of the legitimate product $P_l$. (Example: Adobe Creative Cloud monthly subscription = ₹1,675.)

**Step 2**: Estimate the **number of pirated copies** in the market $N_p$. (Industry reports, BSA surveys, etc.)

**Step 3**: Estimate the **number of legitimate copies** $N_l$.

**Step 4**: Apply the formula to compute the **lost revenue** to the rights holder.

**Step 5**: Multiply by the **legal penalty ceiling** (₹2-5 lakh per offense) to estimate the **maximum criminal exposure** if prosecuted.

> **Worked Example (KTU-style):**
> A software vendor sells a productivity suite at ₹5,000. A pirated version circulates to 10,000 users. Only 1,000 legitimate copies are sold.
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Lost Revenue} &= (N_p \times P_l) - (N_l \times P_l) \\
> &= (10{,}000 \times 5{,}000) - (1{,}000 \times 5{,}000) \\
> &= 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
> &= 4{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR (₹4.5 Crore lost revenue)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> If each of the 10,000 pirated users is treated as a separate offense under Section 63, the **aggregate maximum criminal exposure** is:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Aggregate Penalty} &= N_p \times (\text{Fine} + \text{Imprisonment}) \\
> &= 10{,}000 \times (₹2{,}00{,}000 + \text{3 yr}) \\
> &= ₹2{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹2,000 Crore potential fines)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The takeaway is that **the legal exposure for commercial-scale software piracy in India can dwarf the original software cost by orders of magnitude** — this is why no rational corporate entity should engage in or permit piracy.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
**Define the term "Piracy" as understood under the Copyright Act, 1957. List any two forms of software piracy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Piracy, in the context of the Copyright Act, 1957, refers to the unauthorized reproduction, distribution, communication to the public, or making of derivative copies of a copyrighted work, undertaken without the license or permission of the copyright owner. It is specifically defined as an infringement under **Section 51** of the Copyright Act and is punishable under **Section 63** (general infringement) and **Section 65** (software piracy specifically).

**Two Forms of Software Piracy (1 mark):**

1. **End-User Piracy** — installing a single licensed copy on multiple machines beyond the license terms.
2. **Softlifting** — purchasing one legal copy and loading it onto several computers in an office or institution.

*(Alternative valid answer: OEM Unbundling, Hard-Disk Loading, Counterfeiting, Internet Software Piracy.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**What is the punishment for software piracy under the Copyright Act, 1957? Cite the relevant section.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under **Section 65 of the Copyright Act, 1957**, any person who knowingly makes, or abets the making of, infringing copies of a computer program, or uses such infringing copies, is liable for:

- **Imprisonment**: Up to **3 years**, OR
- **Fine**: Up to **₹2,00,000 (₹2 lakh)**, OR
- **Both** (imprisonment AND fine may be imposed together at the discretion of the court).

For repeat or commercial-scale offenders, the court may impose enhanced penalties under **Section 63** of the Act (up to 6 years imprisonment and ₹5 lakh fine).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q3(a). [7 Marks]** Explain the various types of software piracy with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

Software piracy is the unauthorized copying, distribution, or use of software in violation of its licensing terms. The major types are:

1. **End-User Piracy (1 mark)**: An individual purchases a single-user licensed copy but installs it on multiple computers. *Example*: A student buys a one-PC license of Microsoft Office and installs it on his laptop, desktop, and tablet.

2. **Softlifting (1 mark)**: Sharing a licensed copy across an office or institution. *Example*: An IT firm buys 5 licenses of Tally but installs the software on 20 machines.

3. **Counterfeiting (1 mark)**: Producing and selling fake copies of branded software in lookalike packaging. *Example*: Fake copies of Windows 10 sold in physical markets at ₹100 each.

4. **OEM Piracy / Hard-Disk Loading (1 mark)**: A computer vendor pre-installs unlicensed software on machines before sale. *Example*: A local PC shop installs a pirated copy of antivirus on every system sold.

5. **Client-Server Overuse (1 mark)**: Installing more copies of network-based software than the license allows. *Example*: Installing 50 instances of a 10-user server software.

6. **Internet Software Piracy (2 marks)**: Downloading cracked software, key generators, or licensed keys from unauthorized websites. *Example*: Downloading "Adobe Photoshop CC 2024 Full Crack" from a torrent site. This is the **fastest-growing** form of software piracy and is the principal target of the IT Act, 2000 read with Section 65 of the Copyright Act.

> **Concluding sentence (mandatory for 7 marks):** Software piracy causes significant economic loss to the software industry, poses cybersecurity risks (malware injection), and is a cognizable criminal offense under Indian law.

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the Information Technology Act, 2000 in combating online piracy in India. Cite relevant sections.

**Model Answer:**

The IT Act, 2000 provides the **cyber-specific legal infrastructure** to combat piracy executed through digital and online means. Key provisions:

1. **Section 43 (1 mark)** — Penalty for unauthorized access, copying, or extraction of data from a computer system. A user downloading pirated software from a server without authorization violates this section, with damages payable to the affected party.

2. **Section 65 (1 mark)** — Tampering with computer source documents; imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹2 lakh, or both. This is invoked when pirates reverse-engineer or modify licensed software.

3. **Section 66 (1 mark)** — Computer-related offenses (the "hacking" provision); imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹5 lakh. Used when piracy involves unauthorized access to a rights holder's server or database.

4. **Section 67 (1 mark)** — Publishing or transmitting obscene material in electronic form. Used in cases where pirated content includes obscene or prohibited material.

5. **Section 79 (2 marks)** — Intermediary liability and safe harbor. Online platforms (YouTube, torrent sites, file hosts) are intermediaries; they enjoy conditional immunity if they act expeditiously on takedown notices. **The Delhi High Court (designated IP court) can issue blocking orders under Section 79(3)(b)** to direct ISPs to block piracy websites — a power extensively used against torrent sites, piracy streaming portals, and cyberlockers.

6. **Section 69 (1 mark)** — Government power to intercept, monitor, and direct ISPs to disable access to specified websites, supporting anti-piracy enforcement at the infrastructure level.

> **Concluding sentence (mandatory for 7 marks):** While the Copyright Act, 1957 remains the primary statute, the IT Act, 2000 supplies the **procedural and jurisdictional machinery** to enforce copyright in the digital environment, making online piracy in India a **cognizable, prosecutable, and remediable offense**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Q4(a). [7 Marks]** "Piracy is not merely a civil wrong but a serious criminal offense under Indian law." Critically examine this statement with reference to the Copyright Act, 1957 and the IT Act, 2000.

**Model Answer:**

The statement is **legally correct and well-supported** by Indian statute. Piracy in India is **both** a civil wrong (tortious infringement) **and** a criminal offense (prosecutable by the State). The examination proceeds as follows:

1. **Civil Wrong Aspect (2 marks)**: Under **Section 55 of the Copyright Act, 1957**, the copyright owner can file a civil suit for injunction, damages, or an account of profits. **Anton Piller orders** (search-and-seize) and **Mareva injunctions** (asset-freeze) are routinely granted by Indian courts. The civil route is **remedial and compensatory**.

2. **Criminal Offense Aspect (3 marks)**: Under **Section 62 of the Copyright Act, 1957**, copyright infringement is a **cognizable offense** for commercial-scale violations, meaning police can arrest without a warrant. The criminal penalties escalate:
   - **Section 63** → 3 years + ₹2 lakh (first offense); 6 years + ₹5 lakh (repeat).
   - **Section 65** → 3 years + ₹2 lakh (specifically for software piracy).
   - **Section 67** → 2 years + ₹1 lakh (dealing in infringing devices).

3. **IT Act, 2000 Reinforcement (2 marks)**: When piracy occurs in cyberspace, the IT Act adds additional criminal sanctions. **Section 66** (computer-related offenses) and **Section 66B** (receiving stolen computer resources) can be invoked cumulatively. Conviction under the IT Act can lead to imprisonment up to 3 years and fines up to ₹5 lakh, **in addition to** Copyright Act penalties.

> **Concluding sentence (mandatory for 7 marks):** Thus, piracy under Indian law operates as a **dual-liability regime** — civil *and* criminal — and the prosecution is increasingly **cumulative**, with courts imposing both imprisonment and substantial fines to deter commercial-scale infringement.

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]** Explain the technical and legal anti-piracy measures that can be adopted to combat software piracy.

**Model Answer:**

Anti-piracy measures can be classified into **Technical** and **Legal** categories:

**A. Technical Anti-Piracy Measures (3.5 marks):**

1. **Digital Rights Management (DRM)** — Restricts copying, printing, and forwarding of digital content (used by Adobe, Microsoft).
2. **Software Activation / Product Keys** — Online or phone-based authentication prevents use of unlicensed copies.
3. **Encryption** — Scrambling code so that only authorized users with the decryption key can run the software.
4. **Watermarking & Fingerprinting** — Embedding unique identifiers to trace pirated copies back to the source.
5. **Code Obfuscation** — Making source code difficult to reverse-engineer.
6. **Cloud-Based / Subscription Licensing** — Software runs on remote servers (e.g., Adobe Creative Cloud), making local piracy impossible.

**B. Legal Anti-Piracy Measures (3.5 marks):**

1. **Civil Injunctions** — Under Section 55 of the Copyright Act, courts grant temporary and permanent injunctions.
2. **John Doe / Ashok Kumar Orders** — Pre-suit discovery orders against anonymous infringers.
3. **Website Blocking Orders** — Under Section 79(3)(b) of the IT Act, the Delhi High Court can order ISPs to block piracy websites (e.g., 2019 order blocking 800+ sites).
4. **Criminal Prosecution** — FIR under Section 63/65 of the Copyright Act; arrest and prosecution by the Crime Branch.
5. **Customs Border Seizure** — Under Section 53 of the Copyright Act, customs can seize imported pirated goods.
6. **Industry Coalitions** — BSA, NASSCOM Anti-Piracy Alliance, and IFPI coordinate enforcement, file suits, and run awareness campaigns.

> **Concluding sentence (mandatory for 7 marks):** A **multi-layered approach** combining technical protection, legal enforcement, industry coordination, and public awareness is essential; no single measure is sufficient given the borderless and technologically evolving nature of piracy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Piracy Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Section Citation (Lose 1-2 marks)**: Students often cite **Section 66 of the IT Act** for ALL piracy cases. Section 66 is for "hacking" and computer-related offenses, **not** the primary copyright provision. The correct primary citation is **Section 51 + 63 of the Copyright Act, 1957**. For software specifically, add **Section 65**.
>
> 2. **Citing only the Copyright Act (Lose 1 mark)**: For ONLINE piracy, examiners expect explicit mention of **Section 79 of the IT Act** and the role of intermediaries. A complete answer **must combine both statutes**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Penalty Numbers (Lose 1-2 marks)**: Vague answers like "imprisonment and fine" without numbers (3 years / ₹2 lakh / 6 years / ₹5 lakh) are penalized. **Always quote the exact years and rupee amounts.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring Case Law (Lose 1 mark)**: For 14-mark answers, citing even one case (*Super Cassettes v. Yahoo*, *Shreya Singhal v. UoI*) significantly boosts the score. Students who skip case law lose 1-2 marks.
>
> 5. **No Conclusion**: A 7-mark sub-question without a concluding sentence looks incomplete. **Always end with a synthesis sentence** linking the answer back to the question's premise.
>
> 6. **Confusing "Piracy" with "Privacy"**: A common typo! Read the question carefully — Module 2 is **Piracy**, not the Section 66E privacy offense.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second KTU Revision Checklist for "Piracy":**
>
> - **Definition**: Unauthorized reproduction/distribution of copyrighted works for commercial gain — Section 51 of the Copyright Act, 1957.
> - **Primary Statutes**: Copyright Act, 1957 (Sections 51, 63, 65, 67) **+** Information Technology Act, 2000 (Sections 43, 66, 67, 79).
> - **Software Piracy Penalty** (Sec. 65): **3 years imprisonment + ₹2 lakh fine**.
> - **General Infringement Penalty** (Sec. 63): **3 years + ₹2 lakh (1st) / 6 years + ₹5 lakh (subsequent)**.
> - **Cyber-Related Penalty** (Sec. 66 IT Act): **3 years + ₹5 lakh**.
> - **Obscene Material Online** (Sec. 67 IT Act): **5 years (1st) / 7 years (2nd)**.
