Standard governance body for a cyber policy:
- **Sponsor:** CEO / MD.
- **Owner:** Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Chief Risk Officer.
- **Drafting Committee:** Legal, IT, HR, Compliance, Internal Audit.
- **Reviewer:** Data Protection Officer (DPO) (mandatory under DPDPA 2023 for Significant Data Fiduciaries).

### **Step 4 — Conduct Risk Assessment**

Apply a standard risk model:

$$R = P \times I \times C$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk score**
- $P$ = **Probability** of the threat materializing (scale 1–5)
- $I$ = **Impact** of the consequence (scale 1–5)
- $C$ = **Control gap** (existing mitigation adequacy, scale 1–5)

A policy must address risks whose $R \geq 15$ (on a 1–125 scale).

### **Step 5 — Draft the Policy**

Apply the **structure from Section 2.1** (10 components). Use plain, normative language:
- Use **"shall"** for mandatory requirements.
- Use **"should"** for recommended actions.
- Use **"may"** for discretionary permissions.

### **Step 6 — Internal Review & Legal Vetting**

Mandatory before approval:
- **Legal review** for compliance with IT Act, DPDPA 2023, Indian Contract Act.
- **HR review** for labour-law alignment (Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946).
- **Union/employee consultation** if it affects working conditions.

### **Step 7 — Approval by Competent Authority**

Approval authority varies by policy type:
- **Acceptable Use Policy:** CISO + CEO.
- **Data Protection Policy:** Board of Directors (for Significant Data Fiduciaries).
- **National-Level Policy:** Union Cabinet (e.g., National Cyber Security Policy 2013).

### **Step 8 — Communication & Training**

A policy is *not effective* until communicated. Methods:
- **Mass email** + **read-receipt acknowledgement**.
- **Mandatory e-learning module** (LMS).
- **Posters, intranet portals, and onboarding kits**.
- **Manager-led cascade training**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners commonly deduct marks if a student writes "policy was drafted" but does not mention the **acknowledgement by employees**. Under Indian labour law and DPDPA 2023, an uncommunicated policy is *unenforceable*.

### **Step 9 — Enforcement & Monitoring**

Enforcement mechanisms:
- **Technical controls** (DLP, SIEM, UEBA, NAC).
- **Administrative controls** (HR disciplinary procedures).
- **Audit & compliance checks** (internal audit, statutory auditor, ISO 27001 surveillance audit).

The **monitoring KPI** can be expressed as:

$$C_{\text{compliance}} = \frac{N_{\text{acknowledged}}}{N_{\text{total in-scope}}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{compliance}}$ = Compliance percentage
- $N_{\text{acknowledged}}$ = Number of users who have read, understood, and acknowledged the policy
- $N_{\text{total in-scope}}$ = Total number of users within the policy scope

**Target:** $C_{\text{compliance}} \geq 95\%$ within 30 days of policy rollout.

### **Step 10 — Review & Continuous Improvement**

The review cycle:
- **Annual scheduled review**, OR
- **Event-driven review** (post-incident, new law, new technology).

Re-enter the cycle at **Step 1**.

---

## 3.2 Policy Formulation Analytical Matrix (KTU 2024 Module 2)

| Step | Input | Activity | Output | KTU CO Mapped |
|------|-------|----------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Trigger event | Identify driver | Policy Need Statement | CO4 |
| 2 | Business context | Define scope | Scope Document | CO2 |
| 3 | Org chart | Form drafting committee | Charter | CO2 |
| 4 | Threat catalogue | Risk assessment | Risk Register | CO3 |
| 5 | Gap analysis | Draft policy | Policy Document v0.1 | CO4 |
| 6 | Internal stakeholders | Review & vet | Policy Document v0.9 | CO4 |
| 7 | Board / Top Mgmt | Approve | Approved Policy v1.0 | CO5 |
| 8 | User base | Communicate & train | Acknowledgement Records | CO4 |
| 9 | SIEM, audit logs | Monitor & enforce | Compliance Report | CO3 |
| 10 | Feedback, new threats | Review & revise | Policy v2.0 | CO5 |

---

## 3.3 The Interplay Between Policy, Law, and Standards (Triangulation)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **guaranteed 14-mark theory question** in KTU ESE. The examiner expects the student to articulate the hierarchical relationship.

In the Indian cyber law ecosystem, the **Policy** acts as the *operational bridge* between the *Law* (statute) and the *Standard* (technical specification).

$$\text{Law (What is legally required?)} \longrightarrow \text{Policy (What will the org do?)} \longrightarrow \text{Standard (How will it be implemented technically?)} \longrightarrow \text{Procedure (Step-by-step actions)}$$

### Worked Example — Data Encryption

- **Law:** *Section 43A of IT Act, 2000* — body corporate must protect *Sensitive Personal Data or Information (SPDI)* by "reasonable security practices".
- **Policy (organizational):** *"All customer PII shall be encrypted at rest and in transit."*
- **Standard (technical):** *AES-256 at rest; TLS 1.3 in transit.*
- **Procedure (operational):** *Step-by-step key management process via AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault.*

---

## 3.4 Numerical / Analytical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU-ESE Style Numerical Question:** A company has 5,000 in-scope users. A new Information Security Policy has been rolled out. After 30 days, it is found that 4,650 users have acknowledged the policy. Calculate the compliance percentage and assess if the target of **95%** has been met.

**Solution:**

$$C_{\text{compliance}} = \frac{N_{\text{acknowledged}}}{N_{\text{total in-scope}}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the values:

$$C_{\text{compliance}} = \frac{4650}{5000} \times 100\%$$

$$C_{\text{compliance}} = 0.93 \times 100\%$$

$$C_{\text{compliance}} = 93\%$$

Since $93\% < 95\%$, the **target compliance is NOT met**.

**Examiner's expected remark:** The CISO must initiate a top-down escalation drive, identify the 350 non-compliant users, send auto-reminders via the LMS, and impose a system-level block on critical applications for users who do not acknowledge within 7 more days. The matter should be reported to the Audit Committee of the Board.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Cyber Law Policy Governance Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the *full governance architecture* of a Policy in the cyber law domain, from the constitutional/statutory layer down to the operational procedure. The diagram complies with the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10** Mermaid safeguards: purely alphanumeric node IDs, raw uppercase alphanumeric labels, and no reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[STATUTORY LAYER - CONSTITUTION OF INDIA - ART 14 19 21]
    A1[NATIONAL LAWS - IT ACT 2000 2008 - DPDPA 2023 - COPYRIGHT ACT 1957]
    A2[REGULATORY RULES - SPDI RULES 2011 - INTERMEDIARY RULES 2021 - CERT IN DIRECTIONS 2022]
    B1[ORGANIZATIONAL GOVERNANCE - BOARD OF DIRECTORS - AUDIT COMMITTEE]
    B2[CORPORATE POLICIES - INFORMATION SECURITY POLICY - PRIVACY POLICY - CYBER SECURITY POLICY - IPR POLICY - HR IT CONVERGED POLICY]
    C1[STANDARDS - ISO IEC 27001 2022 - NIST CSF 2.0 - PCI DSS 4.0 - RBI FRAMEWORK]
    C2[PROCEDURES - ACCESS CONTROL STEPS - INCIDENT RESPONSE RUNBOOK - DATA BREACH NOTIFICATION PROCESS]
    D1[TECHNICAL CONTROLS - FIREWALL - SIEM - DLP - EDR - IAM]
    D2[ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS - TRAINING - AUDITS - VENDOR DUE DILIGENCE]
    E1[USER ACKNOWLEDGEMENT - READ SIGN - LMS MODULE - ANNUAL REFRESHER]
    E2[CONTINUOUS MONITORING - KPI DASHBOARD - COMPLIANCE SCORECARD]
    F1[REVIEW AND UPDATE - ANNUAL - EVENT DRIVEN - PDCA CYCLE]
    
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> B1
```

---

## 4.2 The CERT-In Incident Reporting Workflow (Policy-Driven Process)

> [!NOTE]
> This **Mermaid sequence-flow diagram** maps the *statutorily mandated incident reporting process* under Section 70B(6) of the IT Act, 2000 and the **CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022**. This is a high-yield KTU visual question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[INCIDENT DETECTED - INTERNAL SOC OR EMPLOYEE]
    P2[INITIAL TRIAGE - CISO OR SOC ANALYST]
    P3[CONTAINMENT - BLOCK IP - ISOLATE HOST - REVOKE TOKENS]
    P4[LOG SYNCHRONISATION - NTP SERVERS TO UTC]
    P5[INCIDENT CLASSIFICATION - CATEGORISE AS PER CERT IN LIST]
    P6[REPORT TO CERT IN - WITHIN 6 HOURS]
    P7[PROVIDE ADDITIONAL DETAILS - LOGS - IP ADDRESSES - TIMESTAMPS]
    P8[COORDINATE WITH CERT IN - FOLLOW UP AND REMEDIATE]
    P9[POST INCIDENT REVIEW - ROOT CAUSE - POLICY UPDATE]
    
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P3
```

## 4.3 The Policy Lifecycle (Closed-Loop PDCA Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> This **Mermaid cyclical flowchart** depicts the continuous improvement loop for cyber law policy management, based on the **ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) methodology**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[PLAN - IDENTIFY POLICY NEED - SCOPE - DRIVERS]
    Q2[DO - DRAFT POLICY - REVIEW - APPROVE - COMMUNICATE - TRAIN]
    Q3[CHECK - MONITOR COMPLIANCE - AUDIT - KPI MEASUREMENT]
    Q4[ACT - REVIEW FINDINGS - REVISE POLICY - RE CERTIFY]
    
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part A carries **2 questions of 3 marks each (no choice)**. Answers must be concise, point-based, and directly aligned with statutory provisions.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Dec 2023]**
**Define a "Policy" in the context of cyber law. List any four essential components of a well-drafted Information Security Policy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — word target: 60–80 words):**

A **Policy** in cyber law is a *formally documented, authoritative set of rules, principles, and guidelines* that governs the acceptable, lawful, and secure use of information technology assets, data, and digital operations within an organization or jurisdiction.

The four essential components of a well-drafted Information Security Policy are:
1. **Purpose & Scope** — defining *why* the policy exists and to whom it applies.
2. **Policy Statements** — the substantive "shall / shall not" directives.
3. **Roles & Responsibilities** — the RACI matrix (e.g., CISO, DPO, employees).
4. **Compliance, Enforcement & Review** — the consequences of violation and the revision cycle.

*(Examiner's note: Each component cited correctly = 0.75 marks × 4 = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the mandatory incident reporting timeline prescribed by the CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022. Under which section of the IT Act, 2000 are these directions issued?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — word target: 50–60 words):**

As per the **CERT-In Directions dated 28 April 2022**, all service providers, intermediaries, data centres, and body corporates must report cyber security incidents to **CERT-In within 6 hours** of noticing the incident.

These directions are issued under **Section 70B(6)** of the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)**, read with Section 70B(1) and (4), which empowers CERT-In to coordinate incident response and call for information.

*(Examiner's note: "6 hours" = 1 mark; "CERT-In" = 1 mark; "28 April 2022" = 0.5 marks; "Section 70B(6)" = 0.5 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Regulation:** Part B contains **2 questions** (the student answers **ONE** full question with internal choice). Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Model answers for both alternatives are given below.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question on Module 2 — Policy]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **National Cyber Security Policy (NCSP) 2013** of India — its vision, mission, objectives, and key strategy pillars. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **key components of a Privacy Policy** that a Data Fiduciary is required to publish under the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — (a) National Cyber Security Policy 2013 (7 Marks)**

**1. Genesis and Authority (1 Mark)**
The **National Cyber Security Policy, 2013** was released by the **Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), Government of India**. It is a *vision and strategy document* — non-statutory and aspirational — designed to build a secure and resilient cyberspace for citizens, businesses, and the government.

**2. Vision (1 Mark)**
To create a secure cyberspace ecosystem that enables **trusted transactions, information flow, and protection of national critical infrastructure**, while ensuring the **rights of citizens** as enshrined in the Constitution.

**3. Mission (1 Mark)**
To protect information and information infrastructure in cyberspace, build capabilities to prevent and respond to cyber threats, reduce vulnerabilities, and minimize damage from cyber incidents.

**4. Key Objectives (2 Marks)**
- To create a **secure cyber ecosystem** with trust and confidence.
- To create an **assured and protected IT infrastructure** for government and critical sectors.
- To strengthen **regulatory and legal frameworks** for cybersecurity.
- To build a **skilled workforce of cybersecurity professionals** (5 lakh target).
- To promote **Research & Development** in cybersecurity.
- To enhance **global cooperation** in combating cyber threats.

**5. Strategy Pillars (2 Marks)**
- **Pillar 1 — Designate a National Nodal Agency** (CERT-In) for coordination.
- **Pillar 2 — Security of Critical Information Infrastructure** (Section 70 IT Act).
- **Pillar 3 — Security of Government IT Infrastructure** — NIC, e-Governance Bus.
- **Pillar 4 — Security of Business and Commercial IT** — encourage ISO 27001 adoption.
- **Pillar 5 — Legal Framework** — promote cybersecurity audits and standards.
- **Pillar 6 — Skill Development & Training**.
- **Pillar 7 — Public-Private Partnership (PPP)**.
- **Pillar 8 — R&D and Innovation**.

---

#### **Model Answer — (b) Components of a Privacy Policy under DPDPA 2023 (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 Mark)**
The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** (DPDPA) is India's first comprehensive data protection statute. **Section 8(1)** mandates every Data Fiduciary to publish, on its website and the mobile application, a **clear and accessible Privacy Policy / Notice**.

**Mandatory Components (6 Marks — distribute as 6 × 1 mark each):**

1. **Identity and Contact Details of the Data Fiduciary** — name, address, contact of the Data Fiduciary and the *Data Protection Officer (DPO)* (if appointed).

2. **Description of Personal Data being collected** — categories of data (name, Aadhaar, mobile, biometric, etc.) and whether it is sensitive.

3. **Purpose of Processing** — the specific, clear, and lawful purpose for which the personal data is being collected and used (DPDPA Section 4).

4. **Basis of Processing** — whether the processing is based on **consent** (Section 6) or on certain **legitimate uses** (Section 7).

5. **Procedure for Data Subject Rights** — how the data principal can exercise the right to:
   - **Access** a summary of personal data and processing activities (Section 11).
   - **Correction and Erasure** of personal data (Section 12).
   - **Grievance Redressal** within **30 days** (Section 13).
   - **Right to Nominate** another individual to exercise rights in case of death or incapacity (Section 14).

6. **Procedure for Filing a Complaint with the Data Protection Board (DPB)** — the URL/portal of the DPB and the steps to escalate.

7. **Data Retention and Storage** — the period for which the data will be retained, and the procedure for erasure upon purpose completion or withdrawal of consent.

8. **Security Safeguards** — reasonable security practices to protect personal data (encryption, access controls, audits).

**Conclusion (Bonus line)**
A non-compliant Privacy Policy attracts penalties up to **₹250 crore** for Significant Data Fiduciaries under DPDPA 2023.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the **ten essential components** of a well-drafted Information Security Policy. Illustrate with an example of an **Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)** for an engineering college. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022**. List the categories of incidents that must be reported and the **6-hour reporting rule**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — (a) Components of Information Security Policy + AUP for a College (7 Marks)**

**1. Ten Essential Components (5 Marks — 0.5 mark each)**

| # | Component | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Policy Identifier & Version** | Unique ID, version, effective date |
| 2 | **Classification** | Public, Internal, Confidential |
| 3 | **Owner & Approver** | CISO and CEO / Principal |
| 4 | **Purpose Statement** | Why the policy exists |
| 5 | **Scope** | Who and what it covers |
| 6 | **Definitions** | Glossary of terms |
| 7 | **Policy Statements** | "Shall" and "shall not" rules |
| 8 | **Roles & Responsibilities** | RACI matrix |
| 9 | **Compliance & Enforcement** | Penalties for violation |
| 10 | **Review & Revision** | Annual or event-driven |

**2. Example — Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) for ABC Engineering College, Kerala (2 Marks)**

> *"All students, faculty, and staff of ABC Engineering College shall use the institutional IT resources (Wi-Fi, ERP, email, library portal, lab systems) **solely for academic, research, and administrative purposes**.*
> 
> *Use of institutional IT resources for **commercial purposes, hacking, downloading pirated content, or accessing blocked websites** is strictly prohibited.*
> 
> *All users must abide by the **Information Technology Act, 2000/2008** and the **Copyright Act, 1957**.*
> 
> *Violation shall result in **suspension of network access, disciplinary action, and reporting to law enforcement** under Section 66 of the IT Act.*
> 
> *Policy Owner: Head, IT Services. Approved by: Principal. Effective: 01 Aug 2024. Next Review: 01 Aug 2025."*

---

#### **Model Answer — (b) CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022 (7 Marks)**

**1. Background (1 Mark)**
The **Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In)** functions under **Section 70B** of the IT Act, 2000 as the national agency for cyber incident response. On **28 April 2022**, CERT-In issued directions under **Section 70B(6)** to strengthen India's cyber defence.

**2. Key Provisions of the Directions (5 Marks — distribute 1 mark each for the 5 most-tested points):**

- **6-Hour Reporting Rule:** All service providers, intermediaries, data centres, and body corporates must report cyber security incidents to CERT-In **within 6 hours** of detection.

- **Synchronization of Clocks:** All IT/ICT systems must have their clocks synchronized with the **Network Time Protocol (NTP) server of the National Informatics Centre (NIC)** or **National Physical Laboratory (NPL)**, with a time-stamp accuracy of **±1 second UTC**.

- **Log Retention:** Logs of all ICT systems must be maintained for **180 days** (6 months) and made available to CERT-In on request.

- **KYC for VPN / VPS / Cloud Service Providers:** Service providers offering Virtual Private Network (VPN), Virtual Server, Cloud, Proxy, or Anonymiser services must maintain validated **KYC records** and the **originator's details** for at least **5 years**, even after the account is cancelled.

- **Data Centre, Virtual Server, Cloud Service Providers within India:** Such service providers must register with and maintain records of their customers and the services they provide.

**3. Categories of Reportable Incidents (1 Mark)**
Cyber attacks, data breaches, unauthorized access, malicious code attacks, identity theft, phishing attacks, denial-of-service attacks, and attacks on critical infrastructure.

**4. Penalties for Non-Compliance**
Non-compliance can attract **imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to ₹1 lakh** under Section 70B(7) of the IT Act, 2000.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in the "Policy" Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Policy with Procedure:** Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if a student writes procedural steps (e.g., "open SIEM dashboard, check logs") under a "policy" question. *Policy* = what & why; *Procedure* = how.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting statutory backing:** Every policy must be tied to a *law or standard*. A policy answer without mention of the **IT Act 2000, DPDPA 2023, or ISO 27001** loses generality marks.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring DPDPA 2023:** As of the 2024 Scheme, this is a *new* mandatory topic. Students who only mention the IT Act 2000 (without DPDPA 2023) are flagged as outdated.
> 
> 4. **Missing the "Communication" step:** A policy that is *not communicated* and *not acknowledged* is not enforceable. Always mention the LMS, read-receipt, or training step.
> 
> 5. **Numerical errors in the compliance formula:** Use $C_{\text{compliance}} = \frac{N_{\text{acknowledged}}}{N_{\text{total in-scope}}} \times 100\%$ — not the other way around. Reversing numerator and denominator is a common 2-mark error.
> 
> 6. **Writing "6 hours" without context:** Always say *"within 6 hours of noticing the incident"* — not just "6 hours", which is ambiguous.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Revision — Read Twice Before Exam**

- ✅ A **Policy** in cyber law = *authoritative written document* translating legal statutes into operational rules.
- ✅ Policy sits between **Law** (top) and **Standard/Procedure** (bottom) in the governance hierarchy.
- ✅ **National Cyber Security Policy 2013** = vision document of GoI under MeitY; *aspirational, not statutory*.
- ✅ **IT Act 2000/2008** = statutory backbone; **Section 43A, 69, 70B, 79** are the most-cited sections.
- ✅ **SPDI Rules, 2011** = first privacy sub-legislation under Section 43A.
- ✅ **DPDPA 2023** = modern data protection law; **Section 8(1)** mandates a *Privacy Notice*; penalty up to **₹250 crore** for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
- ✅ **CERT-In Directions 28 April 2022** = reportable incident in **6 hours** under **Section 70B(6)**.
- ✅ **Intermediary Rules 2021** = mandatory Grievance Officer + monthly compliance report.
- ✅ Ten essential components of a policy = **Identifier, Classification, Owner, Purpose, Scope, Definitions, Statements, R&R, Compliance, Review**.
- ✅ PDCA cycle = **Plan → Do → Check → Act** (ISO 27001 governance model).
- ✅ Risk formula = $R = P \times I \times C$; Compliance formula = $C_{\text{compliance}} = \frac{N_{\text{acknowledged}}}{N_{\text{total in-scope}}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ A **policy that is not communicated is not enforceable** in Indian law.
- ✅ The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **DPDPA 2023** and **CERT-In 2022 Directions** as *high-weightage* topics.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Policies and Information Technology Act - Need for an Information Security Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Security Policy?

A **Security Policy** is a formal, documented set of rules, principles, and procedures that govern how an organization manages, protects, and distributes its sensitive information and computing assets. It serves as the foundational blueprint for an organization's overall security posture and risk management strategy.

$$ \text{Security Policy} = f(\text{Rules} + \text{Procedures} + \text{Guidelines} + \text{Standards}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> A security policy is a written document that outlines the controls, procedures, practices, and guidelines by which an organization protects the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information assets and information systems.

## 1.2 What is an Information Security Policy?

An **Information Security Policy (ISP)** is a specialized, high-level sub-policy that specifically addresses the protection of **data, information, and information systems** from unauthorized access, disclosure, alteration, destruction, or disruption. It is the cornerstone policy from which all other security-related policies (network, email, BYOD, password, etc.) are derived.

The three pillars of Information Security (often called the **CIA Triad**) that every ISP must enforce are:

$$ \text{Information Security} = \{\text{Confidentiality}, \text{Integrity}, \text{Availability}\} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad - The Heart of Every Security Policy:**
> 1. **Confidentiality (C):** Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to have access.
> 2. **Integrity (I):** Ensuring the accuracy, consistency, and trustworthiness of information over its lifecycle.
> 3. **Availability (A):** Ensuring that authorized users have reliable, timely access to information and resources when needed.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The "Apartment Building Rulebook"**

Imagine a large apartment complex with 500 residents. Without a written rulebook:

- Anyone could enter any flat (**no confidentiality**).
- The plumber might accidentally break your expensive geyser (**no integrity**).
- The lift could break down because nobody funds its maintenance (**no availability**).

The *Apartment Rulebook* (our Information Security Policy) fixes this by stating:
- "Only residents with keycards may enter the building" → **Confidentiality**
- "Maintenance staff must log every repair in the register" → **Integrity**
- "Lift must be serviced every 3 months, power backup tested weekly" → **Availability**

Just as the rulebook governs the apartment, the **Information Security Policy governs the digital "apartment" of an organization** — its servers, databases, networks, and data.

## 1.4 Why the Information Technology Act, 2000 (Amended 2008) Matters Here

The **Information Technology Act, 2000 (IT Act)**, as amended by the **Information Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008**, is the primary cyber law of India. It provides the **legal backbone** that makes having an Information Security Policy not just a best practice, but a **legal necessity** for many organizations — especially those handling sensitive personal data, operating as **Intermediaries**, or functioning as **Body Corporates** handling digital information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key IT Act Provisions that Mandate Security Policies:**
> - **Section 43A** — Body Corporates handling sensitive personal data must implement **reasonable security practices**.
> - **Section 72 & 72A** — Penalties for breach of confidentiality and privacy.
> - **Section 79** — Safe harbor provisions for intermediaries (conditioned on having due diligence policies).
> - **Rules under Section 87** — Including the **Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011** (SPDI Rules).
> - **Section 69** — Government's interception, monitoring, and decryption powers (demands clear policy framework).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU Expects):**
> - **3 Pillars of InfoSec:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA).
> - **2 Legal Pillars in India:** IT Act 2000 + IT (Amendment) Act 2008 + SPDI Rules 2011.
> - **4 Key Policy Types:** Acceptable Use, Access Control, Network Security, Incident Response.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — The Need for an Information Security Policy

An Information Security Policy is **not optional** in the modern digital ecosystem. Below is the structured reasoning of why every organization — from a small startup to a government enterprise — needs one.

### 2.1.1 Protection of Sensitive Assets
- Organizations store **customer data, financial records, intellectual property (IP), trade secrets, and employee information**.
- A single breach can cause **irreparable damage** — both financial and reputational.
- The policy acts as the **first line of defense** by setting rules for data classification, handling, and disposal.

### 2.1.2 Legal and Regulatory Compliance
- The **IT Act 2000 (Section 43A)** and **SPDI Rules 2011** legally bind organizations to implement *"reasonable security practices"* when handling sensitive personal data.
- Non-compliance invites **penalties up to ₹5 crore** under Section 43A.
- Sectoral regulators (RBI for banking, IRDA for insurance, SEBI for capital markets) mandate specific security frameworks — a documented ISP is the **proof of compliance**.

### 2.1.3 Risk Management
- **Risk** = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact.
- A policy enables systematic **identification, assessment, and mitigation** of cyber risks.
- It ensures that security investment is **prioritized** based on actual exposure.

### 2.1.4 Defining Roles and Responsibilities
- Without a policy, no one knows **who is responsible** for what during a cyber-incident.
- The policy establishes the **chain of command** — CISO, IT Manager, System Admin, End User — each with clearly defined duties.

### 2.1.5 Standardization and Consistency
- A policy ensures **uniform application** of security controls across all departments, branches, and geographies.
- It eliminates the *"everyone does it their own way"* anti-pattern that creates security gaps.

### 2.1.6 Employee Awareness and Behavioral Change
- The **human factor** is the weakest link in security (over 90% of breaches involve human error per industry reports).
- A well-communicated policy trains employees to recognize phishing, social engineering, and insider threats.

### 2.1.7 Incident Response and Recovery
- When a breach occurs, a **pre-defined response plan** (part of the ISP) reduces downtime and damage.
- The policy specifies **who to call, what to disconnect, and how to report** — saving critical minutes.

### 2.1.8 Business Continuity
- An ISP integrates with the **Business Continuity Plan (BCP)** and **Disaster Recovery (DR)** strategy to ensure the organization survives major disruptions.

## 2.2 Key Characteristics of a Good Information Security Policy

| # | Characteristic | Explanation (KTU Board Expectation) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Comprehensive** | Covers all assets — physical, digital, human, and procedural. |
| 2 | **Enforceable** | Has clear disciplinary action for violations. |
| 3 | **Communicated** | Distinguishable to every employee, contractor, and third party. |
| 4 | **Reviewed Periodically** | Updated at least **annually** or when major changes occur. |
| 5 | **Aligned with Law** | Compliant with the IT Act 2000, SPDI Rules 2011, and other applicable laws. |
| 6 | **Endorsed by Top Management** | Signed and authorized by the CEO/Board — gives it legal weight internally. |
| 7 | **Practical & Realistic** | Achievable, measurable, and not overly restrictive. |
| 8 | **Risk-Based** | Controls proportionate to the organization's risk profile. |

## 2.3 Types of Security Policies (High-Yield)

| Policy Type | Purpose | Example Clause |
|---|---|---|
| **Organizational / Master Policy** | Sets the overall security tone and governance. | "XYZ Corp is committed to protecting its information assets at the highest standards." |
| **Issue-Specific Policies** | Address particular areas of concern. | Email Use Policy, Internet Use Policy, BYOD Policy, Social Media Policy. |
| **System-Specific Policies** | Govern specific systems or technologies. | Firewall Policy, Database Policy, Server Hardening Policy. |
| **Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)** | Defines what users may and may not do with company resources. | "No personal use of company laptops after 9 PM." |
| **Access Control Policy** | Specifies who can access what, and how. | "Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is enforced on all systems." |
| **Incident Response Policy** | Defines steps to take during a security incident. | "All breaches must be reported to CISO within 1 hour." |
| **Data Classification & Handling Policy** | Categorizes data and prescribes handling rules. | "Confidential data must be encrypted with AES-256 at rest." |

## 2.4 The Three Layers of Security Policy (Industry Standard)

A robust security policy framework is built in three tiers:

$$ \text{Tier 1} \rightarrow \text{Governing Policies (High-level, strategic)} $$

$$ \text{Tier 2} \rightarrow \text{Issue-Specific & System-Specific Policies (Tactical)} $$

$$ \text{Tier 3} \rightarrow \text{Standards, Guidelines, Procedures (Operational)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Banking Sector:** RBI mandates ISPs aligned with its Cyber Security Framework (2016).
> - **Healthcare:** Hospitals use ISPs to comply with the **Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act (DISHA)** draft and SPDI Rules.
> - **e-Governance:** Government portals need ISPs to satisfy the **National Cyber Security Policy 2013** and IT Act obligations.
> - **Startups:** ISPs are required to bid for enterprise contracts — clients demand proof of "reasonable security practices".

## 2.5 KTU Quick-Recall Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Term | Definition / Mnemonic |
|---|---|
| **CIA Triad** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability |
| **AAA** | Authentication, Authorization, Accounting |
| **Parkerian Hexad** | CIA + Possession, Authenticity, Utility (advanced model) |
| **IT Act 2000 Section 43A** | Liability for body corporates failing to protect sensitive data |
| **IT Act 2000 Section 72** | Penalty for breach of confidentiality (up to ₹2 lakh or 2 yrs imprisonment) |
| **IT Act 2000 Section 72A** | Punishment for disclosure of information in breach of contract |
| **IT Act 2000 Section 79** | Intermediary safe harbor (subject to due diligence) |
| **SPDI Rules 2011** | Reasonable Security Practices for Sensitive Personal Data |
| **3 Policy Tiers** | Governing → Issue/System Specific → Standards/Guidelines/Procedures |
| **4 Policy Types** | Organizational, Issue-Specific, System-Specific, Acceptable Use |
| **Penalties Range (IT Act)** | ₹25,000 to ₹5 crore (compensation); imprisonment up to 3 years (some sections) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & IT Act Mapping

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Process of Creating an Information Security Policy

The creation of an ISP is a **systematic 7-step process**. Every KTU answer that touches on *"how to develop an ISP"* should follow this flow.

**Step 1: Define Scope and Objectives**
- Identify what assets, departments, and geographies the policy will cover.
- State the goal: *"To ensure CIA of all information assets of XYZ Corporation."*

**Step 2: Conduct Risk Assessment**
- Identify **threats** (hackers, malware, insider, natural disaster).
- Identify **vulnerabilities** (unpatched systems, weak passwords).
- Calculate **impact and likelihood** of each risk.
- Output: A risk register.

**Step 3: Identify Legal and Regulatory Requirements**
- Map all applicable laws: **IT Act 2000, SPDI Rules 2011, Companies Act 2013 (Section 134/149)**, sectoral regulations (RBI, SEBI, IRDA), contractual obligations (NDAs, DPAs).

**Step 4: Draft the Policy**
- Use plain, simple language — avoid jargon.
- Cover all **4 policy types** (Organizational, Issue-Specific, System-Specific, AUP).
- Address **all 3 CIA elements**.

**Step 5: Obtain Management Approval**
- The Board of Directors / CEO must sign off.
- This gives the policy the **authority of the highest office** — critical for enforcement.

**Step 6: Communicate and Train**
- Distribute the policy to **every employee, contractor, and vendor**.
- Conduct mandatory **security awareness training** (with attendance records).

**Step 7: Monitor, Audit, and Review**
- Conduct periodic **internal audits** (at least annually).
- Update the policy when:
  - New threats emerge.
  - New laws are enacted.
  - Major business changes occur (mergers, new product lines).
  - After a security incident (lessons learned).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these steps matter legally:**
> The SPDI Rules 2011 under Section 43A of the IT Act define "Reasonable Security Practices" as those that are **"commensurate with the information being protected"** and are **"implemented in a manner that is consistent with the rules and procedures prescribed".** The 7-step process above is precisely how this "reasonable" standard is demonstrated in a court of law.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: IT Act Sections vs. Security Policy Requirements

| IT Act Section / Rule | Requirement | Corresponding Policy Clause |
|---|---|---|
| **Sec 43A + SPDI Rules** | Implement reasonable security practices for sensitive personal data | Data Protection & Encryption Policy |
| **Sec 69** | Government interception, monitoring, decryption powers | Government Requests Handling Policy |
| **Sec 72 & 72A** | Penalty for breach of confidentiality and privacy | Confidentiality & Non-Disclosure Policy |
| **Sec 79** | Intermediary safe harbor (needs due diligence) | Content Moderation & Grievance Redressal Policy |
| **Sec 65** | Tampering with computer source documents | Source Code Protection Policy |
| **Sec 66** | Computer-related offences (hacking, data theft) | Acceptable Use & Access Control Policy |
| **Sec 66B, 66C, 66D, 66E, 66F** | Specific cyber crimes (theft, cheating, obscenity, cyber terrorism) | Incident Response & Reporting Policy |
| **Sec 70B** | Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) | Incident Reporting to CERT-In within 6 hours (2022 directive) |
| **Sec 10A** | Validation of electronic contracts (Digital Signatures) | Digital Signature & E-Contract Policy |
| **Sec 67C** | Preservation of information by intermediaries | Data Retention & Log Preservation Policy |

## 3.3 The Need for ISP — Bullet-Justified Argument Matrix (KTU Board Style)

When asked *"Discuss the need for an Information Security Policy"* (a frequent 7-mark / 14-mark KTU question), structure the answer along the following **6-argument framework**:

**Argument 1: Legal Necessity under the IT Act 2000**
- Section 43A imposes a duty on body corporates.
- Penalties for non-compliance are statutory.

**Argument 2: Protection of Intellectual Property (IP) and Trade Secrets**
- IP is often the **most valuable intangible asset** of a tech company.
- An ISP defines IP classification, access, and protection rules.

**Argument 3: Prevention of Financial Loss**
- Average cost of a data breach in India (IBM 2023 report) is approximately **₹17.9 crore**.
- Prevention is **orders of magnitude cheaper** than remediation.

**Argument 4: Customer Trust and Brand Reputation**
- A breach destroys trust — once lost, **rebuilding it costs 5–10× more** than preventing the breach.
- Many enterprise contracts now require **ISO 27001 + documented ISP** as a pre-qualification.

**Argument 5: Regulatory and Contractual Compliance**
- RBI, SEBI, IRDA, GDPR (for companies dealing with EU data) all require documented security.
- ISP acts as the **single source of truth** for compliance auditors.

**Argument 6: Operational Discipline and Incident Management**
- Without an ISP, the organization is **"fighting blind"** during a crisis.
- A documented policy converts chaos into a **structured, executable playbook**.

## 3.4 Information Security Policy vs. Related Concepts (Differentiation Matrix)

| Concept | Focus | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Security Policy** | Protection of *information assets* | Specific to CIA of data |
| **Network Security Policy** | Protection of *network infrastructure* | Subset of ISP; focuses on firewalls, VPNs, IDS |
| **Privacy Policy** | How *personal data* is collected/used | Often public-facing (website); ISP is internal |
| **Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)** | What users *may* do with resources | Subset of ISP; addresses user behavior |
| **Disaster Recovery Policy (DRP)** | Recovery from *major incidents* | Reactive; ISP is broader (proactive + reactive) |
| **Business Continuity Plan (BCP)** | Continuation of *business operations* | Operates at organizational level; ISP feeds into it |
| **IT Governance Framework** (e.g., COBIT) | Alignment of IT with *business goals* | Macro-level; ISP is one component |
| **Risk Management Policy** | Identification and *mitigation* of risks | Risk management informs the ISP; ISP enforces controls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip (Examiner's Mindset):**
> Students often confuse **Privacy Policy** with **Information Security Policy**. Remember:
> - **Privacy Policy** = "What data we collect, why, and how we share it" (User-facing, often legal-driven).
> - **Information Security Policy** = "How we protect all data and systems" (Internal, security-driven).
> A Privacy Policy can be a **section within the larger ISP**, but they are not synonymous.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define an Information Security Policy. List any four key characteristics of a good ISP.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO: Understand]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Information Security Policy (ISP)** is a formal, documented set of rules, procedures, and guidelines that govern how an organization protects the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information assets and information systems. It is the master document that translates the organization's security goals into actionable controls.

The **four key characteristics** of a good ISP are:

1. **Comprehensive** — It must cover all assets (physical, digital, human, procedural).
2. **Enforceable** — It must specify clear disciplinary actions for non-compliance.
3. **Periodically Reviewed** — It must be updated at least annually or after major changes.
4. **Management-Approved** — It must be endorsed by the top management (Board/CEO) to have authority.

> *(Examiner's Note: Writing "CIA triad" as a characteristic without the above 4 is incomplete. Always give characteristics in bullet form.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. State the three pillars of information security. Mention the relevant section of the IT Act 2000 that mandates security practices for sensitive personal data.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO: Remember]`

**Model Answer:**

The **three pillars of information security** are the **CIA Triad**:

1. **Confidentiality (C)** — Ensuring that information is accessible only to authorized users.
2. **Integrity (I)** — Ensuring the accuracy, consistency, and trustworthiness of data.
3. **Availability (A)** — Ensuring that authorized users have reliable and timely access.

The relevant section of the **IT Act 2000** that mandates security practices for sensitive personal data is **Section 43A**, read with the **Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011 (SPDI Rules)**.

> *(Examiner's Note: Students often write only "IT Act" without specifying the section. Always name the section number for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 3 - Model Question A]**
> **Q3. (a) Discuss in detail the need for an Information Security Policy in a modern organization. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) Explain the different types of security policies with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** `[14 Marks Total]` `[CO: Analyze + Apply]`

### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Need for an Information Security Policy:**

An Information Security Policy is no longer a luxury; it is a **legal, operational, and strategic necessity**. The following points elaborate the need:

**1. Legal Compliance under the IT Act 2000:**
Section 43A of the IT Act 2000 imposes statutory liability on **body corporates** that handle sensitive personal data but fail to implement *"reasonable security practices."* The SPDI Rules 2011 further define what these practices entail. An ISP is the primary evidence of such compliance. Non-compliance can attract damages up to **₹5 crore**.

**2. Protection of Information Assets:**
Modern organizations possess vast digital assets — customer PII, financial data, intellectual property, and trade secrets. An ISP prescribes **data classification, encryption, access controls, and disposal procedures** to protect these assets from theft and leakage.

**3. Systematic Risk Management:**
A documented ISP allows the organization to **identify, assess, mitigate, and monitor** cyber risks in a structured manner. The Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact formula drives the design of controls.

**4. Clear Definition of Roles:**
The policy delineates the responsibilities of the **CISO, IT Manager, System Administrators, and End Users**. This eliminates confusion during incidents and ensures accountability.

**5. Effective Incident Response:**
The ISP contains a pre-approved **Incident Response Plan (IRP)** that specifies the steps to take during a breach — detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and lessons learned. This drastically reduces the **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)**.

**6. Building Customer Trust and Meeting Contractual Obligations:**
Enterprise clients and government tenders routinely demand proof of an implemented ISP. A robust policy, ideally certified to **ISO/IEC 27001**, becomes a **business enabler**, not just a cost center.

**Conclusion:** In an era of escalating cyber threats and tightening data protection laws, the Information Security Policy is the **backbone of organizational cyber resilience** and the first document any auditor, regulator, or court will examine.

### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Types of Security Policies:**

Security policies can be broadly classified into the following categories:

**1. Master / Organizational / Governing Policy:**
This is the top-level document that sets the tone, vision, and commitment of the organization towards information security. It is usually a one-to-two-page document signed by the CEO. *Example:* "XYZ Corporation Information Security Master Policy."

**2. Issue-Specific Policies:**
These address specific issues or areas of technology use. They are tactical in nature. *Examples:*
- Email Use Policy
- Internet Use Policy
- Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) Policy
- Social Media Policy
- Remote Work / Work-from-Home Policy

**3. System-Specific Policies:**
These govern the use and configuration of specific systems, hardware, or software. *Examples:*
- Firewall Configuration Policy
- Database Server Hardening Policy
- Wireless Network Access Policy
- Cloud Services Usage Policy

**4. Acceptable Use Policy (AUP):**
A user-facing policy that defines what is acceptable and unacceptable use of the organization's IT resources. *Example clauses:* "Use of company laptops for personal gambling is prohibited." "Sharing passwords is a disciplinary offence."

**5. Access Control Policy:**
Specifies the principles of access — *Least Privilege, Need-to-Know, Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)* — and the procedures for granting, modifying, and revoking access.

**6. Incident Response Policy:**
Defines the categorization of incidents, the escalation matrix, the incident response team, and the post-incident review process. Aligned with **CERT-In directives** (6-hour reporting under Section 70B).

**7. Data Classification and Handling Policy:**
Categorizes data as *Public, Internal, Confidential, Highly Confidential / Restricted* and prescribes labeling, transmission, storage, and destruction rules for each category.

> *(Examiner's Note: For full 7 marks, list at least 5–6 policy types and give one real-world example for each.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 3 - Model Question B (ALTERNATIVE)]**
> **Q4. (a) Explain the Information Technology Act 2000 provisions that necessitate an Information Security Policy. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) Describe the step-by-step process of developing and implementing an Information Security Policy in an organization. (7 Marks)** `[14 Marks Total]` `[CO: Understand + Apply]`

### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**IT Act 2000 Provisions Necessitating an Information Security Policy:**

The Information Technology Act 2000, amended in 2008, and the SPDI Rules 2011 together create a comprehensive legal framework that compels organizations to maintain an Information Security Policy. The key provisions are:

**1. Section 43A — Compensation for Failure to Protect Data:**
A body corporate that is negligent in implementing *reasonable security practices* while handling sensitive personal data is liable to pay **damages up to ₹5 crore** to the affected person. The only defense is to prove that *"reasonable security practices"* were in place — which requires a documented ISP.

**2. Section 72 & 72A — Penalties for Breach of Confidentiality:**
Any person (including an employee) who discloses personal information in breach of a lawful contract is punishable with **imprisonment up to 3 years or a fine up to ₹5 lakh**, or both. An ISP with strict **confidentiality clauses** mitigates this risk.

**3. Section 79 — Safe Harbor for Intermediaries:**
Intermediaries (e.g., social media platforms, e-commerce sites) are exempt from liability for third-party content **only if** they observe due diligence and follow prescribed **rules and policies** — mandating the existence of a robust ISP covering content moderation, grievance redressal, and data retention.

**4. Section 65 — Tampering with Computer Source Documents:**
Punishable with **imprisonment up to 3 years or a fine up to ₹2 lakh**. Necessitates a **Source Code Protection Policy** within the ISP.

**5. Section 66 — Computer-Related Offences (Hacking, Data Theft):**
Punishable with **imprisonment up to 3 years or a fine up to ₹5 lakh**. Drives the **Access Control Policy** and **Acceptable Use Policy**.

**6. Section 70B — Indian CERT and Section 67C — Data Preservation:**
CERT-In's 2022 directive requires intermediaries, data centres, and body corporates to report cyber incidents within **6 hours**. Section 67C requires intermediaries to preserve information for **180 days**. Both necessitate an **Incident Response Policy** with defined reporting timelines.

**7. SPDI Rules 2011 (under Section 43A):**
Define *"sensitive personal data"* (passwords, financial info, health data, sexual orientation, biometric data) and require explicit consent, purpose limitation, and reasonable security practices — operationalized through the ISP.

### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step-by-Step Process of Developing an Information Security Policy:**

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Objectives:**
The first step is to clearly state **what** the policy will cover (all information systems, all employees, all geographies) and **what** it aims to achieve (e.g., *"To ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all information assets of ABC Ltd."*).

**Step 2 — Conduct a Risk Assessment:**
Identify all **information assets**, the **threats** they face (hackers, malware, insider, natural disaster), their **vulnerabilities** (unpatched systems, weak passwords, lack of training), and the **impact** of a breach. Build a **risk register** and prioritize risks.

**Step 3 — Identify Legal, Regulatory, and Contractual Requirements:**
Map all applicable laws: **IT Act 2000, SPDI Rules 2011, Companies Act 2013, sectoral regulations (RBI, SEBI, IRDA), and contractual obligations (NDAs, DPAs, GDPR for EU data).**

**Step 4 — Draft the Policy:**
Using a structured template, draft the policy in clear, simple language. Cover all **4 policy types** (Master, Issue-Specific, System-Specific, AUP) and address all **3 CIA elements**. Include a **glossary** of terms.

**Step 5 — Stakeholder Review:**
Circulate the draft to **legal, HR, IT, operations, and senior management** for feedback. Address concerns and incorporate changes.

**Step 6 — Obtain Top Management Approval:**
The final draft must be **approved and signed by the CEO or Board of Directors**. This gives the policy the **authority of the highest office** and is critical for enforcement and legal defense.

**Step 7 — Communicate, Train, and Implement:**
Distribute the policy to **all employees, contractors, and third parties**. Conduct **mandatory training sessions** with attendance records. Use e-learning modules, posters, and periodic drills.

**Step 8 — Monitor, Audit, and Review:**
Conduct **periodic internal audits** (at least annually) and **external audits** (every 2–3 years) to measure compliance. Update the policy when:
- New threats or technologies emerge.
- New laws are enacted (e.g., the upcoming **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023**).
- Major organizational changes occur (mergers, acquisitions, new product lines).
- After any significant security incident (**lessons learned**).

> *(Examiner's Note: Always conclude Part (b) with a continuous-review loop diagram or a statement that *"the policy is a living document"*. This shows practical understanding.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IT Act Section Numbers:** Students frequently misquote Section 43 as 43A. Always write "**Section 43A**" precisely — examiners deduct 1 mark for incorrect section numbers.
> 2. **Writing "Privacy Policy" instead of "Information Security Policy":** These are **not the same**. Privacy is a subset; ISP is broader. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Forgetting the Legal Anchor:** Any answer on ISP that omits the **IT Act 2000 and SPDI Rules 2011** is considered incomplete. Always include the legal basis.
> 4. **Listing policy types without examples:** Saying "Acceptable Use Policy exists" earns 1 mark; saying "Acceptable Use Policy — e.g., *No personal use of company laptops after 9 PM*" earns full credit.
> 5. **No Conclusion:** A 14-mark answer without a concluding paragraph is marked down by 1–2 marks. End with a forward-looking statement linking to the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023** or **ISO 27001**.
> 6. **Skipping the CIA Triad:** Even if the question is not directly on CIA, briefly mentioning it earns goodwill marks and shows foundational understanding.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Bullets — Pin These for the Exam:**

- **Definition (Memorize):** *"An Information Security Policy is a formal, documented set of rules and procedures that govern the protection of an organization's information assets to ensure Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability."*

- **CIA Triad** = **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability — the **non-negotiable core** of every ISP.

- **3 Policy Tiers:**
  1. **Governing Policies** (Strategic, high-level).
  2. **Issue-Specific & System-Specific Policies** (Tactical).
  3. **Standards, Guidelines, Procedures** (Operational, detailed).

- **4 Major Policy Types:** Master / Organizational, Issue-Specific, System-Specific, Acceptable Use Policy (AUP).

- **5 Other Key Policies:** Access Control, Incident Response, Data Classification, Email/Internet, BYOD.

- **7-Step Development Process:** Scope → Risk Assessment → Legal Mapping → Draft → Approval → Communicate/Train → Monitor/Audit/Review.

- **6 Reasons Why ISP is Needed:** Legal Compliance, Asset Protection, Risk Management, Role Clarity, Incident Response, Customer Trust.

- **Key IT Act Sections (Memorize Section Numbers):**
  - **Sec 43A** — Body corporate liability for sensitive data (damages up to **₹5 crore**).
  - **Sec 72 / 72A** — Breach of confidentiality (up to 3 yrs imprisonment / ₹5 lakh fine).
  - **Sec 79** — Intermediary safe harbor (with due diligence).
  - **Sec 70B** — CERT-In 6-hour incident reporting directive.
  - **Sec 67C** — 180-day data preservation by intermediaries.
  - **Sec 65** — Source code tampering.
  - **Sec 66** — Hacking & data theft.

- **SPDI Rules 2011:** Define *"sensitive personal data"* (password, financial info, health, sexual orientation, biometric, genetic) — the operational backbone of Section 43A.

- **Risk Formula:** $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$.

- **AAA Framework:** **A**uthentication, **A**uthorization, **A**ccounting — complements CIA in policy design.

- **Parkerian Hexad (Advanced):** CIA + Possession + Authenticity + Utility (mention only if asked for depth).

- **Statutory Penalty Maximums:**
  - Section 43A damages: Up to **₹5 crore**.
  - Section 72/72A imprisonment: Up to **3 years**.
  - Section 66 imprisonment: Up to **3 years** (or 7 years for **Sec 66F — Cyber Terrorism**).

- **Key Industry Standards to Reference:** **ISO/IEC 27001**, **NIST Cybersecurity Framework**, **COBIT 2019**, **PCI-DSS** (for payments).

- **Future-Ready Note:** Always mention the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** as the upcoming law that will further strengthen ISP requirements — examiners love this forward-looking reference.

- **Golden Exam Line (Use as a Closer):** *"In the modern threat landscape, an Information Security Policy is not merely a compliance document — it is a strategic asset that protects the organization's information, reputation, and future, while fulfilling its statutory obligations under the IT Act 2000 and the SPDI Rules 2011."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Security Standards: ISO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Information Security Standards: ISO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are Information Security Standards?

An **Information Security Standard** is a formally documented specification, framework, or set of guidelines established by recognized international standardization bodies (such as **ISO** — the International Organization for Standardization, and **IEC** — the International Electrotechnical Commission) that defines the processes, controls, policies, and best practices an organization must adopt to protect the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of its information assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (PECCT861 / Module 3):**
> *Information Security Standards are globally recognized, consensus-based frameworks (primarily the ISO/IEC 27000 family) that provide a structured methodology for establishing, implementing, monitoring, reviewing, and continually improving an organization's Information Security Management System (ISMS), ensuring the systematic protection of information assets against evolving cyber threats.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Passport & Insurance Policy"

Imagine you are running a **bank vault business** (the organization holding sensitive data). Customers trust you with their money (their confidential information). To prove to the world that you are a *trustworthy* and *secure* bank, you cannot simply say "Trust us." You need an independent, internationally recognized auditor to inspect your vaults, your locks, your cameras, and your safety drills.

That is exactly what **ISO 27001 certification** does in the digital world.

| Real-World Banking Analogy | ISO Information Security Equivalent |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Bank vault with reinforced walls | Encrypted servers and access controls |
| CCTV cameras and alarm systems | Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), SIEM |
| Trained security guards | Cybersecurity awareness and training program |
| Insurance policy covering losses | Business Continuity & Risk Management Plan |
| Bank's licence to operate | ISO/IEC 27001 Certification |
| Annual safety audit by external agency | Surveillance audit by accredited certification body |
| Bank's internal safety manual | **Statement of Applicability (SoA)** |

> [!NOTE]
> Think of **ISO Standards as the "rulebook" for playing the cybersecurity game fairly and safely**. Without them, every company would invent its own rules, leading to chaos and vulnerabilities.

---

## 1.3 Why Do These Standards Exist?

Information security is a **multi-dimensional, cross-disciplinary problem**. A single company cannot develop its own ad-hoc security and expect global recognition. Standards serve to:

1. **Harmonize** security practices across geographies and industries.
2. **Provide a benchmark** against which organizations can be audited and certified.
3. **Reduce ambiguity** in legal, contractual, and regulatory obligations (e.g., GDPR, DPDP Act, HIPAA compliance often leverages ISO controls).
4. **Enable trust** in B2B and B2G (Government) transactions.
5. **Facilitate trade and digital commerce** across borders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardization Body Trivia for KTU Exams:**
> - **ISO** = International Organization for Standardization (founded **23 February 1947** in Geneva, Switzerland).
> - **IEC** = International Electrotechnical Commission.
> - **Joint ISO/IEC** standards are denoted as **"ISO/IEC XXXXX"** (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001:2022).
> - "ISO" is **not** an acronym — it is derived from the Greek word **"isos"** meaning "equal," reflecting standardization.

---

## 1.4 The ISO/IEC 27000 Family — An Overview

The ISO/IEC 27000 family is the **premier global benchmark** for information security management. It is often referred to as the **"ISMS Family"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **ISO/IEC 27001** = The **certifiable** standard (tells you *what* to do). It specifies the requirements for establishing, operating, monitoring, reviewing, maintaining, and improving an ISMS.
> - **ISO/IEC 27002** = The **supporting** standard (tells you *how* to do it). It provides implementation guidance and best practices for the controls listed in Annex A of ISO 27001.
> - **ISO/IEC 27000** = The **vocabulary/glossary** standard.

| Standard | Focus | Certifiable? |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| ISO/IEC 27000 | Terms and definitions | No |
| **ISO/IEC 27001** | ISMS requirements | **Yes** ✓ |
| ISO/IEC 27002 | Controls implementation guidance | No |
| ISO/IEC 27003 | ISMS implementation guidance | No |
| ISO/IEC 27004 | ISMS measurement and metrics | No |
| ISO/IEC 27005 | Information security risk management | No |
| ISO/IEC 27017 | Cloud security controls | Yes (extension) |
| ISO/IEC 27018 | Privacy in cloud (PII protection) | Yes (extension) |
| ISO/IEC 27701 | Privacy Information Management (PIMS) | Yes (extension) |
| ISO/IEC 27035 | Incident management | No |
| ISO 31000 | Enterprise risk management (generic) | No |
| ISO 22301 | Business continuity management | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Core of ISO 27001 — The Information Security Management System (ISMS)

An **ISMS** is a systematic, risk-based, top-down approach to managing an organization's information security. It is **not a product, software, or firewall** — it is a **management framework** involving people, processes, and technology.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "The 3 Pillars of ISMS":**
> - **P**eople (training, awareness, governance)
> - **P**rocess (policies, procedures, audits)
> - **P**roducts (firewalls, encryption, anti-malware)

### 2.1.1 CIA Triad — The Foundational Security Model

The entire ISO 27000 ecosystem is built around protecting the **CIA Triad**:

- **Confidentiality (C):** Ensuring information is accessible only to those authorized.
- **Integrity (I):** Ensuring information is accurate and has not been tampered with.
- **Availability (A):** Ensuring information is accessible when needed by authorized users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sometimes extended to the **Parkerian Hexad** (added: Possession, Authenticity, Utility) or the **STRIDE model** (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) by Microsoft.

---

## 2.2 The PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act) — The Engine of ISO 27001

ISO 27001:2022 mandates that an ISMS follow the **PDCA cycle**, also known as the **Deming Cycle** or **Shewhart Cycle**. This is a continuous improvement model.

| Phase | Full Form | Core Activities | KTU-Friendly Explanation |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Plan** | Establish the ISMS | Define scope, policy, risk assessment, risk treatment, SoA | "Make a plan — what to protect, how to protect, who protects." |
| **Do** | Implement and operate the ISMS | Deploy controls, train staff, manage operations, run BCP | "Execute the plan — install firewalls, train people, run drills." |
| **Check** | Monitor and review the ISMS | Internal audits, KPI measurement, management review | "Check if it works — audit, measure, evaluate." |
| **Act** | Maintain and improve the ISMS | Corrective actions, preventive actions, continual improvement | "Fix what's broken and keep getting better." |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw the **PDCA cycle as a circular diagram** in your answer. The four arrows must be clockwise, and you must mention that the cycle is **iterative and continuous** — not one-time.

---

## 2.3 The ISO 27001:2022 Control Structure (Annex A)

The **2022 revision** of ISO 27001 reduced the number of controls from **114 (in 2013 version)** to **93 controls**, reorganized into **4 themes** (instead of 14 domains):

### 2.3.1 The Four Themes of Annex A Controls

| Theme Number | Theme Name | Number of Controls | Examples |
|--------------|------------|--------------------|----------|
| **A.5** | **Organizational Controls** | 37 | Policies, roles, information classification, supplier relationships |
| **A.6** | **People Controls** | 8 | Screening, awareness, training, disciplinary process |
| **A.7** | **Physical Controls** | 14 | Physical entry, secure areas, equipment protection |
| **A.8** | **Technological Controls** | 34 | Encryption, malware protection, backup, logging, network security |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total = 37 + 8 + 14 + 34 = 93 controls** (Confirmed count for KTU 2024 syllabus).

### 2.3.2 Sample Critical Controls (High-Yield for KTU)

| Control ID | Control Name | Purpose |
|------------|--------------|---------|
| A.5.1 | Policies for information security | Management direction |
| A.5.10 | Acceptable use of information | User behavior rules |
| A.5.12 | Classification of information | Data labeling |
| A.5.15 | Access control | Authorization rules |
| A.5.19 | Information security in supplier relationships | Third-party risk |
| A.5.23 | Information security for use of cloud services | Cloud-specific risks |
| A.6.3 | Information security awareness, education & training | Human firewall |
| A.6.6 | Confidentiality / non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) | Legal protection |
| A.7.2 | Physical entry | Server room access |
| A.7.10 | Storage media | USB and disk handling |
| A.8.1 | User endpoint devices | Laptops, mobiles |
| A.8.2 | Privileged access rights | Admin account management |
| A.8.5 | Secure authentication | MFA, strong passwords |
| A.8.8 | Management of technical vulnerabilities | Patch management |
| A.8.12 | Data leakage prevention (DLP) | Prevent data exfiltration |
| A.8.16 | Monitoring activities | SIEM, log monitoring |
| A.8.24 | Use of cryptography | Encryption standards |
| A.8.28 | Secure coding | SDLC security |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description | Application |
|---------|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Risk Magnitude** | $R = L \times I$ | Risk = Likelihood × Impact | Core of ISO 27005 risk assessment |
| **Residual Risk** | $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} - R_{controls}$ | Risk remaining after controls | Used in Risk Treatment Plan |
| **Risk Appetite Threshold** | $R_{appetite} \geq R_{residual}$ | Management-accepted risk level | Acceptance criteria |
| **Control Effectiveness Ratio** | $E = \dfrac{C_{mitigated}}{C_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % of risk mitigated by a control | Used in audit reporting |
| **Asset Value** | $V_{asset}$ | Quantitative or qualitative valuation | Step 1 of risk assessment |
| **Threat × Vulnerability** | $T \times V \to Risk$ | Threat exploiting a weakness yields risk | ISO 27005 risk identification |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected annual monetary loss | Quantitative risk analysis |
| **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Loss from one incident | Quantitative risk analysis |
| **Return on Security Investment** | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost_{control}}{Cost_{control}}$ | Financial justification of security investment | Business case for controls |
| **Maturity Level** | $L \in \lbrace 1,2,3,4,5 \rbrace$ | CMMI-style maturity scale | ISO 27004 measurement |
| **PDCA Iterations** | $N \to \infty$ | Continuous improvement is endless | ISMS lifecycle |
| **Total Controls (2022)** | $\sum C = 93$ | Sum across 4 themes | Frequently asked in KTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Probability Symbols:**
> In risk matrices, **L (Likelihood)** and **I (Impact)** are usually rated on a scale of **1 to 5**, where:
> - **1 = Very Low / Negligible**
> - **2 = Low**
> - **3 = Medium**
> - **4 = High**
> - **5 = Very High / Catastrophic**
>
> So $R = L \times I$ yields a risk score from 1 to 25.

---

## 2.5 The Statement of Applicability (SoA) — A Critical Mandatory Document

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SoA** is the single most important document required for ISO 27001 certification. It is mandatory.

**SoA** is a document that lists:
- All **93 Annex A controls**.
- Whether each control is **applicable** or **not applicable** to the organization.
- The **justification** for inclusion or exclusion.
- The **implementation status** (implemented, partially implemented, planned).
- The **control objective** it addresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** The SoA is like the **"ingredients list"** on a food packet — it tells the auditor (and customers) exactly what security ingredients (controls) are inside the "security recipe" (ISMS).

---

## 2.6 The Risk Management Process (Per ISO 27005)

ISO 27005 provides a structured risk management methodology aligned with ISO 27001.

### 2.6.1 Steps in ISO 27005 Risk Management

1. **Context Establishment** — Define scope, boundaries, criteria.
2. **Risk Assessment**
   - **Risk Identification** — Identify assets, threats, vulnerabilities.
   - **Risk Analysis** — Assess likelihood and impact.
   - **Risk Evaluation** — Compare against risk acceptance criteria.
3. **Risk Treatment** — Choose one of four options per risk:
   - **Risk Modification** (apply controls)
   - **Risk Retention** (accept)
   - **Risk Avoidance** (eliminate the activity)
   - **Risk Sharing** (transfer — e.g., insurance, outsourcing)
4. **Risk Acceptance** — Formal acceptance by management.
5. **Risk Communication & Consultation** — Stakeholder engagement.
6. **Risk Monitoring & Review** — Continuous tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "4 Ts" of Risk Treatment — Frequently asked in KTU:**
> - **Modify, Tolerate, Terminate, Transfer.**

### 2.6.2 The Risk Treatment Equation

$$
R_{residual} = R_{inherent} - (C_{effectiveness} \times R_{inherent})
$$

$$
R_{residual} = R_{inherent} \times (1 - E_{control})
$$

Where $E_{control}$ is the effectiveness of the applied controls (0 ≤ E ≤ 1).

> **Example:** If inherent risk score is 20, and the control is 60% effective, then:
> $R_{residual} = 20 \times (1 - 0.60) = 20 \times 0.40 = 8$

---

## 2.7 Other Important ISO Standards in the Information Security Ecosystem

### 2.7.1 ISO 31000 — Enterprise Risk Management

- A **generic, non-certifiable** standard for risk management.
- Can be applied to **any** type of risk: financial, operational, strategic, cybersecurity.
- Provides a framework (not a certifiable management system).

### 2.7.2 ISO 22301 — Business Continuity Management System (BCMS)

- **Certifiable** standard.
- Focuses on organizational resilience, disaster recovery, and continuity.
- Heavily integrated with ISO 27001 for business continuity controls.

### 2.7.3 ISO/IEC 27701 — Privacy Information Management System (PIMS)

- **Extension** to ISO 27001 and ISO 27002.
- Adds privacy-specific controls for **Personally Identifiable Information (PII)**.
- Helps comply with **GDPR, DPDP Act 2023 (India), CCPA**.

### 2.7.4 ISO/IEC 27017 — Cloud Security

- Code of practice for information security controls applicable to cloud services.
- Jointly with ISO 27018, addresses **shared responsibility model** in cloud.

### 2.7.5 ISO/IEC 27035 — Incident Management

- Provides guidelines for **incident response**, including:
  - Planning and preparation
  - Detection and reporting
  - Assessment and decision
  - Responses (containment, eradication, recovery)
  - Lessons learned

---

## 2.8 ISO 27001 Certification Lifecycle

| Step | Activity | Description |
|------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Gap Analysis** | Compare current state with ISO 27001 requirements |
| 2 | **Scope Definition** | Define boundaries of the ISMS |
| 3 | **ISMS Design & Documentation** | Policies, SoA, risk register, procedures |
| 4 | **Implementation** | Deploy controls, train staff |
| 5 | **Internal Audit** | Self-audit by trained internal auditors |
| 6 | **Management Review** | Top management formally reviews ISMS |
| 7 | **Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review)** | External auditor checks documents |
| 8 | **Stage 2 Audit (On-site Certification)** | External auditor verifies implementation |
| 9 | **Certification Issued** | Valid for **3 years** |
| 10 | **Surveillance Audits** | Annual (Year 1 and Year 2) |
| 11 | **Recertification** | Every 3 years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Highlight:** ISO 27001 certification is **valid for 3 years**, with **surveillance audits annually** in years 1 and 2, and a **full recertification audit in year 3**.

---

## 2.9 Mapping of ISO 27001 with Other Frameworks

| Framework | Full Name | Issuer | Relationship with ISO 27001 |
|-----------|-----------|--------|------------------------------|
| **NIST CSF** | Cybersecurity Framework | US NIST | Complementary; can be mapped |
| **COBIT** | Control Objectives for Information and Related Technologies | ISACA | Governance layer, maps to ISO 27001 |
| **PCI DSS** | Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard | PCI SSC | Specific to card data, maps subset of ISO 27001 |
| **HIPAA** | Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act | US | Sector-specific, ISO 27001 helps compliance |
| **GDPR / DPDP Act** | Data protection laws | EU / India | ISO 27701 supports compliance |
| **CIS Controls** | Center for Internet Security Controls | CIS | Operational, maps to ISO 27001 controls |
| **SOC 2** | Service Organization Control 2 | AICPA | Audit report; ISO 27001 is more prescriptive |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to compare, emphasize that **ISO 27001 is a MANAGEMENT SYSTEM standard**, while **NIST CSF is a FRAMEWORK** and **PCI DSS is a REGULATION**.
