# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Case-Based Reasoning

## 3.1 Legal Framework — Flow of Digital Signature Verification

The following analytical framework demonstrates how a digital signature operates within the IT Act 2000 legal regime.

**Step 1: Key Pair Generation**
The subscriber generates an **asymmetric key pair** consisting of:
- A **Private Key** (kept secret by the subscriber)
- A **Public Key** (published freely, often in the DSC itself)

> Mathematical representation:

$$
K_{subscriber} = \{ K_{private}, K_{public} \}
$$

where $K_{private} \neq K_{public}$, and both are mathematically related via a **one-way trapdoor function** (typically **RSA** or **ECC**).

**Step 2: Document Hashing**
Before signing, the electronic record (document) is passed through a **hash function** (e.g., **SHA-256**):

$$
H = \text{Hash}(D) \quad \text{where } D = \text{Electronic Document}
$$

The hash $H$ is a fixed-length alphanumeric string (256 bits for SHA-256) that uniquely represents the document content.

**Step 3: Signature Creation**
The hash is encrypted using the subscriber's private key:

$$
S = E_{K_{private}}(H)
$$

The result $S$ is the **Digital Signature**, which is appended to the document.

**Step 4: Signature Verification (Receiver Side)**
The receiver decrypts $S$ using the subscriber's **public key** to retrieve $H'$, and independently hashes the document to get $H$. If they match, the signature is authentic:

$$
D_{K_{public}}(S) = H' \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Hash}(D) = H
$$

$$
\text{Verification} \iff H' = H
$$

**Step 5: Certifying Authority Issuance**
The CA verifies the subscriber's identity, binds the public key $K_{public}$ to the subscriber's identity (Name, PAN, Email, etc.), and issues a **Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)**.

> KTU Interpretation:
> A digital signature is **NOT merely a scanned image of a signature**. It is a **cryptographic mechanism** that ensures:
> - **Authenticity** — The sender is who they claim to be.
> - **Integrity** — The document was not tampered with.
> - **Non-repudiation** — The sender cannot later deny signing.

## 3.2 Penalty Framework — Analytical Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Penalty Computation under the IT Act:**

| Section | Offence | Nature | Maximum Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sec 43** | Damage to computer / computer system | Civil (Compensation) | Up to **₹1 crore** by Adjudicating Officer |
| **Sec 43A** | Negligence in handling sensitive personal data (Body Corporate) | Civil | Compensation to affected person |
| **Sec 65** | Tampering with computer source documents | Criminal | Up to **3 years** imprisonment or fine up to **₹2 lakh** or both |
| **Sec 66** | Hacking (Computer-related offence) | Criminal (Cognizable, Bailable) | Up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹2 lakh** or both |
| **Sec 66A** *(Struck down in Shreya Singhal v. UoI, 2015)* | Sending offensive messages | Struck down | N/A |
| **Sec 66B** | Receiving stolen computer or communication device | Criminal | Up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 66C** | Identity theft (using password, unique ID fraudulently) | Criminal | Up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 66D** | Cheating by personation using computer | Criminal | Up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 66E** | Publishing private images of others (Voyeurism) | Criminal | Up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2 lakh** |
| **Sec 66F** | Cyber terrorism | Criminal (Non-bailable) | **Imprisonment up to life** |
| **Sec 67** | Publishing obscene material in electronic form | Criminal | First conviction: up to **5 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh**; Second: up to **7 years** + fine up to **₹10 lakh** |
| **Sec 67A** | Publishing sexually explicit material | Criminal | First: up to **5 years** + fine; Second: up to **7 years** + fine |
| **Sec 67B** | Publishing child pornography | Criminal | First: up to **5 years** + fine; Second: up to **7 years** + fine |
| **Sec 69** | Government's power to intercept / monitor | Administrative | — |
| **Sec 69B** | Government's power to block websites | Administrative | — |
| **Sec 72** | Breach of confidentiality by intermediary | Criminal | Up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 79** | Intermediary liability / safe harbour | Conditional | Safe harbour if intermediary follows due diligence |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Alert:** The famous **Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)** case struck down **Section 66A** as unconstitutional (violation of **Article 19(1)(a)** — Freedom of Speech). This is a **favorite KTU question** linking the IT Act to constitutional law.

## 3.3 Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT) — Procedural Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Working of the Cyber Appellate Tribunal (Sections 48–64):**

1. **Establishment** — Section 48 authorizes the Central Government to establish CAT.
2. **Composition** — Presiding Officer (a person qualified to be a **High Court Judge**).
3. **Tenure** — Presiding Officer holds office for **5 years** or until age of **65**, whichever is earlier.
4. **Jurisdiction** — Hears appeals against orders of the **Adjudicating Officer** under Sections 46, 47, and 65–77.
5. **Appeals to High Court** — Under Section 62, any person aggrieved by CAT's order can appeal to the **High Court** within **60 days**.
6. **Civil Court Bar** — Section 58 states that no court (other than the Supreme Court and High Court under Article 226/227) shall have jurisdiction to entertain any suit or proceeding related to matters under CAT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Update:** The **Finance Act 2017** abolished the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** and transferred its jurisdiction to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)**. However, in practice, the **IT Act still references CAT**, and KTU exams continue to test this provision.

## 3.4 The IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 — Key Additions

> [!NOTE]
> **Major Changes Introduced in 2008:**

The 2008 Amendment was a watershed moment. It added **cyber terrorism (Sec 66F)**, expanded the definition of offences, included **data protection (Sec 43A)**, introduced the concept of **intermediaries (Sec 79)**, and brought in **Section 69** for government interception powers.

**Key additions:**
- **Section 66F** — Cyber terrorism (life imprisonment)
- **Section 69** — Interception / monitoring powers
- **Section 69B** — Blocking of websites
- **Section 79** — Safe harbour for intermediaries
- **Section 43A** — Body corporate liability for data breaches
- **Section 10A** — Intermediary registration with government
- **Section 77B** — Compounding of offences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Sec 66F — Cyber Terrorism):**
> *"Whoever commits cyber terrorism shall be punishable with imprisonment which may extend to imprisonment for life."*
> Cyber terrorism = unauthorized access with intent to threaten unity, integrity, security, sovereignty, or strike terror in the people.

## 3.5 KTU Analysis: Why is the IT Act 2000 called "World's First Cyber Law"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Reasoning for KTU Answers:**

1. **Chronological primacy** — Enacted in 2000, before most countries had dedicated e-commerce laws.
2. **Comprehensive scope** — It was not a sectoral law (e.g., banking); it covered **all electronic transactions**.
3. **UNCITRAL alignment** — The Act was a direct implementation of the **UNCITRAL Model Law 1996**.
4. **Single statute for digital signatures** — Unlike the **US E-SIGN Act 2000** (signed October 2000), India's IT Act was passed earlier in the legislative process.
5. **Cyber Appellate Tribunal** — A novel institution for adjudicating digital disputes.

## 4.2 Process Flow — How a Digital Signature Works (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER_SIDE
        D1[Electronic Document] --> H1[SHA-256 Hash Function]
        H1 --> HV[Hash Value H]
        PK1[Private Key of Sender] --> ENC1[Encryption Algorithm]
        HV --> ENC1
        ENC1 --> SIG[Digital Signature S]
    end
    
    SIG --> TX[Transmitted to Receiver]
    
    subgraph RECEIVER_SIDE
        TX --> DEC[Decrypt with Public Key]
        PK2[Senders Public Key] --> DEC
        DEC --> HV2[Retrieved Hash H Prime]
        TX --> H2[Hash Function on Document]
        H2 --> HV3[Computed Hash H Double Prime]
    end
    
    HV2 --> CMP{Match H Prime equals H Double Prime}
    HV3 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Yes| VALID[Signature Valid - Authentic and Untampered]
    CMP -->|No| INVALID[Signature Rejected - Tampered or Forged]
```

## 4.3 Penalty & Adjudication Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OFF[Cyber Offence Occurs] --> FIR[FIR / Complaint to Police]
    FIR --> AO[Adjudicating Officer - Sec 47]
    AO --> PEN[Order of Penalty under Sec 43 / 43A]
    PEN --> CAT[Cyber Appellate Tribunal - Sec 48]
    CAT --> HC[High Court Appeal - Sec 62]
    HC --> SC[Supreme Court under Article 136]
    
    OFF --> ALT[Alternative - Criminal Trial]
    ALT --> MAG[Magistrate Court]
    MAG --> SES[Sessions Court for Serious Offences]
    SES --> HC2[High Court]
    HC2 --> SC2[Supreme Court]
    
    PEN -.Appeals Period.-> AP[30 days for CAT, 60 days for High Court]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Digital Signature Certificate (DSC) Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AP[Applicant Submits Documents to CA] --> VER[CA Verifies Identity]
    VER --> KG[CA Generates Key Pair or Asks Subscriber]
    KG --> ISS[CA Issues Digital Signature Certificate]
    ISS --> USE[Subscriber Uses DSC to Sign Documents]
    USE --> SUS{CA Suspends or Revokes}
    SUS -->|Suspended| TEMP[Temporary Hold for Investigation]
    SUS -->|Revoked| PERM[Permanent Cancellation]
    TEMP --> REIN{Reinstate or Revoke}
    REIN -->|Reinstate| USE
    REIN -->|Revoke| PERM
    PERM --> EXP[Certificate Expired after Validity Period]
    EXP --> REN[Renew DSC if Required]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

### **Q1. Define "Digital Signature" and "Digital Signature Certificate" under Section 2 of the IT Act 2000. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Signature (Section 2(1)(p)):**
> *"Digital Signature means authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber of asymmetric crypto system and hash function."*
>
> In simple terms, a digital signature is a cryptographic code that uniquely identifies the sender of an electronic document and ensures the document has not been tampered with. It uses a pair of keys — a **private key** (kept secret by the signer) and a **public key** (made available to all).
>
> **Digital Signature Certificate (Section 2(1)(q)):**
> *"Digital Signature Certificate means a certificate issued by a Certifying Authority (CA) to verify the authenticity of a digital signature."*
>
> A DSC is an electronic document that binds the **identity of a subscriber** (name, email, PAN, etc.) with their **public key**. It is issued by a licensed **Certifying Authority** under Section 21 of the Act and is valid for **1 or 2 years** depending on the class.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### **Q2. State any three salient features of the Information Technology Act 2000. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The three salient features of the IT Act 2000 are:
> 1. **Legal recognition of electronic records** — Section 4 gives legal status to electronic documents, treating them at par with paper-based documents.
> 2. **Legal recognition of digital signatures** — Section 5 declares that digital signatures shall be deemed to satisfy the requirements of law relating to signatures.
> 3. **Establishment of the Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** — Section 48 establishes CAT to hear appeals against orders of the Adjudicating Officer, ensuring speedy redressal of digital disputes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Scheme, Model Question]**

### **Question A: "Discuss the salient features, objectives, and major provisions of the Information Technology Act 2000. Explain in detail the role of Certifying Authorities under the Act." (14 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### **Part (a) — Salient Features and Objectives of the IT Act 2000 (7 Marks)**

The Information Technology Act 2000 is the cornerstone of Indian cyber law. Its salient features and objectives are as follows:

1. **Legal Recognition of Electronic Records (Section 4):** Before 2000, the Indian Evidence Act 1872 required documents to be in paper form. Section 4 changed this by giving legal recognition to electronic records, paving the way for **e-commerce and e-governance**.

2. **Legal Recognition of Digital Signatures (Section 5):** Section 5 declares that a digital signature affixed in the prescribed manner shall be deemed to be a valid signature under any law. This is the foundation for **paperless transactions**.

3. **Regulation of Certifying Authorities (Sections 17–34):** The Act establishes the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** under the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) and prescribes licensing requirements for entities that wish to issue Digital Signature Certificates (DSC).

4. **Penalties for Computer-Related Offences (Section 43):** Section 43 imposes **civil liability** on persons who damage computers, computer systems, or computer networks. The maximum compensation is **₹1 crore**, awarded by the **Adjudicating Officer** appointed under Section 46.

5. **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (Sections 48–64):** Section 48 establishes CAT for speedy redressal. The Presiding Officer must be qualified to be a **High Court Judge**. The Tribunal has powers equivalent to a Civil Court under the **Code of Civil Procedure, 1908**.

6. **Offences and Penalties (Sections 65–78):** The Act defines specific cybercrimes like **hacking (Sec 66)**, **publishing obscene material (Sec 67)**, **breach of confidentiality (Sec 72)**, and **cyber terrorism (Sec 66F, post-2008 amendment)**.

7. **Extra-territorial Application (Section 1(2)):** The Act extends to offences committed by **any person anywhere in the world**, if the computer, computer system, or network involved is located in India.

#### **Part (b) — Role of Certifying Authorities (7 Marks)**

A **Certifying Authority (CA)** is a trusted third party that issues **Digital Signature Certificates (DSC)** to subscribers. The role of CAs is governed by **Chapter IV (Sections 17–34)** of the IT Act 2000.

**Key Functions of a CA:**

1. **Issuing DSC (Section 35):** A CA verifies the identity of the applicant (subscriber), generates or accepts their key pair, and issues a DSC binding the public key to the subscriber's identity (name, email, PAN, etc.).

2. **Compliance with Rules (Section 28):** The CA must comply with the **Information Technology (Certifying Authorities) Rules, 2000**, which include:
   - Maintaining a **secure hardware and software system**.
   - Ensuring that subscribers' private keys are protected.
   - Maintaining records of all DSCs issued.
   - Publishing a **revocation list** of suspended or revoked certificates.

3. **Standards for Digital Signatures (Section 20):** The Central Government prescribes the algorithms (such as **RSA, DSA, ECC**) that CAs must use to ensure interoperability and security.

4. **Suspension and Revocation of DSC (Section 37):** A CA can suspend a DSC if it suspects compromise, and revoke it permanently if confirmed. The subscriber must be informed.

5. **Submission of Compliance Reports (Section 30):** Every CA must submit annual compliance reports to the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**.

**Examples of Licensed CAs in India:** eMudhra, (n)Code Solutions, Tata Consultancy Services, IDGRL, Sify Technologies.

---

### **Question B: "Explain the various penalties and offences under the Information Technology Act 2000. Distinguish between civil and criminal liability under the Act with relevant case law." (14 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### **Part (a) — Civil Liability under the IT Act (7 Marks)**

**Civil liability** is imposed under **Sections 43 and 43A** and is adjudicated by the **Adjudicating Officer** (not a criminal court).

**Section 43 — Damage to Computer / Computer System:**
If any person, without permission of the owner:
- Accesses or secures access to a computer
- Downloads, copies, or extracts data
- Introduces or causes introduction of a computer contaminant (like a virus)
- Damages or disrupts the computer
- Provides assistance to another person to do any of the above

→ The aggrieved party can claim **compensation up to ₹1 crore** from the Adjudicating Officer under Section 46.

**Section 43A — Compensation for Negligence by Body Corporate:**
If a **body corporate** (company) that possesses, deals with, or handles **sensitive personal data** is negligent in implementing reasonable security practices and causes wrongful loss or wrongful gain, it is liable to pay **damages to the affected person**.

**Section 45 — Jurisdiction:**
The Adjudicating Officer has jurisdiction over the area where the computer, computer system, or computer network is located.

**Advantages of Civil Liability:**
- Faster redressal (no long criminal trial).
- Compensatory in nature.
- Direct adjudication by expert officer.

#### **Part (b) — Criminal Liability under the IT Act (7 Marks)**

**Criminal liability** is imposed under **Chapter XI (Sections 65–78)** and tried by criminal courts (Magistrate/Sessions Court).

| Section | Offence | Punishment |
|---|---|---|
| 65 | Tampering with computer source documents | 3 years or ₹2 lakh fine or both |
| 66 | Hacking (with intent or knowledge) | 3 years or ₹2 lakh fine or both |
| 66B | Receiving stolen computer | 3 years or ₹1 lakh fine |
| 66C | Identity theft | 3 years + ₹1 lakh fine |
| 66D | Cheating by personation | 3 years + ₹1 lakh fine |
| 66E | Voyeurism | 3 years + ₹2 lakh fine |
| 66F | Cyber terrorism | Imprisonment up to **life** |
| 67 | Publishing obscene material | 5 years + ₹1 lakh (1st); 7 years + ₹10 lakh (2nd) |
| 69 | Government interception (administrative) | — |
| 72 | Breach of confidentiality | 2 years + ₹1 lakh |
| 77 | Most offences are **cognizable, bailable, non-compoundable** | — |

**Relevant Case Law:**

1. **Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) — Supreme Court:**
   The Supreme Court struck down **Section 66A** of the IT Act as unconstitutional, holding it violated **Article 19(1)(a)** of the Constitution (Freedom of Speech and Expression). The Court held that the provision was vague and gave excessive discretion to police.

2. **State of Tamil Nadu v. Suhas Katti (2004):**
   Landmark case where a person was convicted under **Section 67** of the IT Act for posting defamatory and obscene messages in a Yahoo group. This was one of the first convictions under the IT Act.

3. **Avnish Bajaj v. State (NCT of Delhi) (2005):**
   In the **Bazee.com** case, the Delhi High Court held that an **intermediary** (online marketplace) could be held liable if it had knowledge of illegal content and failed to act.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing civil vs criminal** — Students often fail to distinguish Section 43 (civil) from Section 66 (criminal).
> 2. **Wrong sections** — Mixing up Section 65 (source document tampering) with Section 66 (hacking).
> 3. **Forgetting amendments** — Section 66A was **struck down in 2015**. Do not cite it as current law.
> 4. **Ignoring case law** — KTU expects at least **one relevant case law citation** in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Missing Section 79** — Intermediaries are protected under Section 79 only if they follow due diligence. Mere hosting does not mean liability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — IT Act 2000:**

- ⏰ **Enacted:** 9th June 2000 (Presidential assent); Effective from 17th October 2000.
- 📜 **Citation:** Act No. 21 of 2000; passed by Parliament.
- 🌍 **World's first** comprehensive cyber law on e-commerce.
- 🏛️ **Administered by:** Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY).
- 🔐 **Section 4:** Legal recognition of **electronic records**.
- ✍️ **Section 5:** Legal recognition of **digital signatures**.
- 🏢 **Section 17:** **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** — headquartered in New Delhi.
- 📋 **Section 21:** License to issue DSC.
- 💰 **Section 43:** Civil liability for damage to computer — up to **₹1 crore**.
- 🏢 **Section 43A:** Body corporate liability for data negligence.
- ⚖️ **Section 46:** Adjudicating Officer appointed.
- 🏛️ **Section 48:** **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** — Presiding Officer tenure 5 years, age 65.
- 📑 **Section 62:** Appeal to High Court within **60 days**.
- 💻 **Section 65:** Tampering with source documents — 3 years.
- 🕵️ **Section 66:** Hacking — 3 years or ₹2 lakh.
- 👤 **Section 66C:** Identity theft — 3 years.
- 🆘 **Section 66D:** Cheating by personation — 3 years.
- 💣 **Section 66F:** Cyber terrorism — **life imprisonment**.
- 🚫 **Section 66A:** **Struck down** in *Shreya Singhal v. UoI (2015)*.
- 🔞 **Section 67:** Obscene material — 5 years (first); 7 years (second).
- 🔐 **Section 72:** Breach of confidentiality — 2 years.
- 🛡️ **Section 79:** Safe harbour for intermediaries (conditional).
- 📡 **Section 69 / 69B:** Government interception / website blocking powers.
- 🛠️ **2008 Amendment:** Added cyber terrorism, data protection, intermediary provisions.
- 🏛️ **Finance Act 2017:** Abolished CAT, transferred jurisdiction to **TDSAT**.
- 🌐 **Extra-territoriality:** Section 1(2) — applies to offences outside India.
- 🔑 **Digital Signature = Asymmetric crypto + hash function**, NOT a scanned image.
- 📜 **Hash functions commonly used:** SHA-256, SHA-3, MD5 (legacy).
- 🧾 **DSC Classes:** Class 1 (low), Class 2 (medium), Class 3 (high assurance).
- ⚠️ **Most offences:** Cognizable, bailable, non-compoundable (Sec 77).

> **Best of luck for your KTU ESE Exam!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Penalties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Penalties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) and Cyber Law, **Penalties** refer to the legally prescribed punishments — including **monetary fines (compensation/damages)**, **imprisonment (terms ranging from months to years)**, **forfeiture of infringing goods**, and **cancellation/surrender of statutory IP rights** — imposed upon individuals, companies, or bodies corporate who are found guilty of committing statutory offences under the respective IP and cyber legislations of India.

The primary statutes that prescribe penalties in this domain are:

- The **Copyright Act, 1957** (Sections 62, 63, 63-A, 65, 66, 66-A, 66-B, 67, 68, 71)
- The **Patents Act, 1970** (Section 118–124)
- The **Trademarks Act, 1999** (Sections 103–115)
- The **Designs Act, 2000** (Section 22–26)
- The **Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 43–46, 65–67, 69–71)
- The **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999** (Sections 37–41)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT861 – Module 3):**
> The module expects students to internalize the **quantum of punishment**, the **nature of offence** (cognizable / non-cognizable, bailable / non-bailable), and the **jurisdiction of trial** for every major statutory violation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the legal system as a **school**, and statutes as **rules of the classroom**:

- If a student (an infringer) is caught **cheating in an exam** (committing an offence), the school does not just ask them to "say sorry." It imposes a **graded penalty** — a warning, a fine, suspension, or even expulsion.
- Similarly, when someone **pirates software, counterfeits a trademark, leaks a patent, or hacks a computer**, the law responds with a **predetermined, objective punishment** — not a subjective opinion.
- The **"penalty schedule"** is like the **"detention slip"** — it tells the judge (the principal) exactly what to do for each rule broken.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Layer Deterrent Model (KTU Favourite):**
> 1. **Civil Remedies** (injunctions, damages) — civil court, lower threshold.
> 2. **Criminal Penalties** (imprisonment + fine) — magistrate's court, higher threshold.
> 3. **Administrative Penalties** (seizure, destruction of goods) — customs/executive action.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Statutory Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Statutory Value |
|---|---|
| Statutory minimum fine (general) | **₹ 25,000** |
| Maximum imprisonment (general IP) | **3 years** (extendable to **6 years** for repeat offences) |
| Maximum fine (general IP) | **₹ 2,00,000** (extendable on subsequent conviction) |
| IT Act max fine (specific) | **₹ 1 Crore** (for certain cyber terrorism offences) |
| Cognizable threshold | Offences punishable with **≥ 2 years** imprisonment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of Statutory Penalties

Statutory penalties are structured along **three orthogonal axes**, which the KTU examiner frequently tests:

1. **Severity Axis** — Fines vs. Imprisonment vs. Both.
2. **Procedure Axis** — Cognizable / Non-cognizable; Bailable / Non-bailable; Compoundable / Non-compoundable.
3. **Subject Axis** — Individual / Company / Director / Officer-in-charge.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following compact table is the **single most important revision asset** for this topic. Memorize section numbers + minimum + maximum quantum.

**Table 2.1: Penalties under Major IP Statutes (India)**

| Statute | Section | Nature of Offence | Punishment (Imprisonment) | Punishment (Fine) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Copyright Act, 1957 | 63 | Infringement of copyright / other rights | Min **6 months**, Max **3 years** | Min **₹ 50,000**, Max **₹ 2,00,000** |
| Copyright Act, 1957 | 63-A | Second / subsequent conviction | Min **1 year**, Max **3 years** | Min **₹ 1,00,000**, Max **₹ 2,00,000** |
| Copyright Act, 1957 | 65 | Knowingly using infringing copies on a commercial scale | Min **1 year**, Max **3 years** | Min **₹ 50,000**, Max **₹ 2,00,000** |
| Copyright Act, 1957 | 65-A (now 69) | Circumvention of TPMs / Rights Management Info | Min **1 year**, Max **3 years** | Fine not less than **₹ 50,000** |
| Copyright Act, 1957 | 66 | Internet / digital piracy (incl. streaming) | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹ 5,00,000** |
| Patents Act, 1970 | 118 | Wrongful use of "Patent Office" word | Up to **2 years** | Up to **₹ 10,00,000** |
| Patents Act, 1970 | 119 | Falsification of register | Up to **2 years** | Up to **₹ 10,00,000** |
| Patents Act, 1970 | 120 | Unauthorised claim of patent rights | Up to **2 years** | Up to **₹ 5,00,000** |
| Trademarks Act, 1999 | 103 | False trade descriptions (general) | Min **6 months**, Max **3 years** | Min **₹ 50,000**, Max **₹ 2,00,000** |
| Trademarks Act, 1999 | 104 | Falsely representing a trademark as registered | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹ 2,00,000** |
| Designs Act, 2000 | 22 | Piracy of registered design | Min **1 month**, Max **3 years** | Min **₹ 10,000**, Max **₹ 2,00,000** |
| IT Act, 2000 | 43 / 43-A | Damage to computer / data / network | — | Compensation up to **₹ 5 Crore** (civil) |
| IT Act, 2000 | 65 | Tampering with computer source documents | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹ 2,00,000** |
| IT Act, 2000 | 66 | Computer-related offences (hacking) | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹ 5,00,000** |
| IT Act, 2000 | 66-C | Identity theft | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹ 1,00,000** |
| IT Act, 2000 | 66-D | Cheating by personation using computer resource | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹ 1,00,000** |
| IT Act, 2000 | 66-E | Violation of privacy (publishing obscene images) | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹ 2,00,000** |
| IT Act, 2000 | 66-F | Cyber terrorism | Imprisonment which **may extend to life** | — |
| IT Act, 2000 | 67 | Publishing obscene / lascivious material in electronic form | Min **3 years**, Max **5 years** (1st offence) ; Min **5 years**, Max **7 years** (subsequent) | Min **₹ 5,00,000**, Max **₹ 10,00,000** |
| IT Act, 2000 | 69 | Failure to intercept / monitor / decrypt on government order | Up to **7 years** + fine | — |
| GI Act, 1999 | 37 | Infringement / falsification of GI | Min **6 months**, Max **3 years** | Min **₹ 50,000**, Max **₹ 2,00,000** |

### 2.3 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **Cybersecurity professionals** must know IT Act penalties to advise clients on **incident-response disclosure obligations**.
- **Software vendors and SaaS companies** rely on Copyright Act §§ 65 & 66 to pursue **software pirates** in criminal court.
- **Pharma and biotech firms** invoke Patent Act § 120 against generic competitors who falsely mark products as "patented."
- **Brand-protection teams** in FMCG / luxury sectors use Trademark Act §§ 103–104 against counterfeiters, with the **bifurcated fine structure** (minimum + maximum) being a strong deterrent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Repeat Offence Doubling Rule"** — A central feature of Indian IP penalties is that on a **second or subsequent conviction**, both the **minimum imprisonment** and the **minimum fine are doubled** (e.g., Copyright Act § 63-A). This is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Determining the Applicable Penalty

Whenever a KTU question asks "What is the penalty for offence X?", apply this **5-step decision tree**:

**Step 1 — Identify the Statute**
Determine whether the act falls under the Copyright Act, Patent Act, Trademark Act, IT Act, Designs Act, or GI Act. Each statute is **mutually exclusive** in the specific offence definitions, though overlap can occur (e.g., digital music piracy → Copyright Act § 65 + IT Act § 66).

**Step 2 — Identify the Specific Section**
Pin down the exact statutory section that defines the offence. **Never quote the section number from memory**; cross-verify with the bare act.

**Step 3 — Classify the Offence**
Determine three binary attributes:
- Cognizable or Non-cognizable?
- Bailable or Non-bailable?
- Compoundable or Non-compoundable?

**Step 4 — Extract the Quantum**
- Identify the **minimum imprisonment** (if any).
- Identify the **maximum imprisonment** (if any).
- Identify the **minimum fine** (if any).
- Identify the **maximum fine** (if any).
- Check for **doubling on second conviction**.

**Step 5 — Identify the Court of Trial**
- Offences carrying imprisonment up to **3 years** → **Metropolitan Magistrate / Judicial Magistrate First Class**.
- Offences carrying imprisonment **> 3 years** → **Sessions Court**.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Software Piracy in a B-School

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> "Ram, a student, downloads a licensed copy of a design software and distributes it on a Telegram channel to 1,000 users. Identify the statutory penalty."

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statute Identification**
The act involves **reproduction + distribution of a copyrighted work on a commercial scale** in digital form. → **Copyright Act, 1957** is the primary statute. (IT Act § 66 may apply additionally for hacking, but the core act is infringement of a copy of a computer program.)

**Step 2 — Section Identification**
Under the Copyright Act, **Section 65** (inserted by the 2012 Amendment) specifically criminalizes **knowing use** of an infringing copy of any work on a **commercial scale**. The definition of "commercial scale" (Section 2(caa)) covers acts that are not merely private — distribution to 1,000 users is commercial.

**Step 3 — Classify the Offence**
Section 65 offence is **cognizable, non-bailable, and non-compoundable**.

**Step 4 — Quantum of Punishment**
- Imprisonment: **Minimum 1 year, Maximum 3 years**.
- Fine: **Minimum ₹ 50,000, Maximum ₹ 2,00,000**.

**Step 5 — Court of Trial**
Since the maximum imprisonment is 3 years, the trial is by a **Judicial Magistrate First Class (JMFC)** under Section 62 of the Copyright Act.

**Additional Trigger — Repeat Offence:**
If Ram is convicted and later repeats the act, **Section 63-A** applies:
- Imprisonment: **Minimum 1 year (doubled), Maximum 3 years**.
- Fine: **Minimum ₹ 1,00,000 (doubled), Maximum ₹ 2,00,000**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Offences by Companies (Section 11 of the Copyright Act / Equivalent Provisions)

A frequently tested KTU concept is: **"Who is liable when the offender is a company?"**

Under **Section 11 of the Copyright Act, 1957**, where any offence is committed by a company, **every person who at the time of the offence was in charge of and responsible to the company for the conduct of the business** is deemed guilty and is liable to be prosecuted.

The **penal liability matrix** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{company}}(O) &= P_{\text{responsible director}}(O) + P_{\text{any officer in default}}(O) \\
\text{where } P(O) &= (I_{\min}, I_{\max}, F_{\min}, F_{\max}) \text{ for offence } O.
\end{aligned}
$$

In plain text:

> For any offence **O** committed by a **company**, the **director / person in-charge** AND the **officer in default** are **jointly and severally liable** to the same penalty as the company itself, **unless** they can prove that the offence was committed **without their knowledge** or that **they exercised all due diligence** to prevent it.

The defence available to the director is the **"due diligence exception"**:

$$
\text{Defence available} \iff \text{Offence committed without knowledge of director} \lor \text{Director exercised all due diligence}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example: Cyber Terrorism under IT Act § 66-F

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> "A hacker group infiltrates the SCADA system of a national power grid, intending to cause widespread fear. Discuss the applicable penalty."

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statute:** **Information Technology Act, 2000** (Amended 2008).

**Step 2 — Section:** **Section 66-F** (Cyber Terrorism) — applies when a person accesses a computer resource with intent to threaten the unity, integrity, sovereignty, or security of India.

**Step 3 — Classification:** Cognizable, non-bailable, non-compoundable.

**Step 4 — Quantum of Punishment:**
- Imprisonment: **May extend to imprisonment for life** (i.e., up to life imprisonment).
- Fine: **As the court may deem fit** (no statutory cap).

**Step 5 — Court:** **Sessions Court** (life imprisonment falls outside Magistrate's jurisdiction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students write "imprisonment up to 7 years" for § 66-F, confusing it with § 69. **Life imprisonment is the correct upper limit.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Penalty Architecture Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Statutory Violation Detected] --> B{Which Statute?}
    B -->|Copyright Act 1957| C1[Sec 63 / 63-A / 65 / 66 / 69]
    B -->|Patents Act 1970| C2[Sec 118 / 119 / 120]
    B -->|Trademarks Act 1999| C3[Sec 103 / 104]
    B -->|Designs Act 2000| C4[Sec 22]
    B -->|IT Act 2000| C5[Sec 43 / 65 / 66 / 66-F / 67]
    B -->|GI Act 1999| C6[Sec 37]

    C1 --> D[Identify Nature of Offence]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D

    D --> E{First Conviction?}
    E -->|Yes| F1[Apply Base Penalty]
    E -->|No| F2[Apply Doubled Penalty]

    F1 --> G[Determine Court of Trial]
    F2 --> G

    G --> H{Imprisonment up to 3 years?}
    H -->|Yes| I1[Metropolitan Magistrate or JMFC]
    H -->|No| I2[Sessions Court]

    I1 --> J[Execute Sentence and Order Forfeiture of Infringing Goods]
    I2 --> J
```

### 4.2 Comparative Penalty Severity Map (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 4
    block:gr1
        columns 2
        A1["Sec 120 Patents Act\n2 yrs / Rs 5 Lakh"]
        A2["Sec 104 TM Act\n3 yrs / Rs 2 Lakh"]
    end
    block:gr2
        columns 2
        B1["Sec 63 Copyright Act\n6m to 3 yrs /\nRs 50k to 2 Lakh"]
        B2["Sec 22 Designs Act\n1m to 3 yrs /\nRs 10k to 2 Lakh"]
    end
    block:gr3
        columns 2
        C1["Sec 66 IT Act\n3 yrs / Rs 5 Lakh"]
        C2["Sec 67 IT Act\n3 to 7 yrs /\nRs 5 Lakh to 10 Lakh"]
    end
    block:gr4
        columns 1
        D1["Sec 66-F IT Act\nUp to LIFE IMPRISONMENT\nplus discretionary fine"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#ff5050,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#ffa500,color:#000000
    style B1 fill:#ffff66,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **red block** (Sec 66-F Cyber Terrorism) is the **maximum severity**; the **yellow blocks** represent **medium severity** IP offences; and **green/light blocks** are the **lower-bound** administrative-misconduct offences (e.g., falsely marking a product as "patented").

### 4.3 Liability Cascade for Corporate Offenders

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Company Commits Offence] --> B[Every Director In-Charge]
    A --> C[Every Officer In-Default]
    B --> D{Defence Available?}
    C --> D
    D -->|No Knowledge| E[Acquittal]
    D -->|Due Diligence| E
    D -->|Neither| F[Joint and Several Criminal Liability]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> What is meant by the term "cognizable offence" in the context of penalties under IP statutes? Give one example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **cognizable offence** is one in which a police officer can arrest the accused without a warrant from a magistrate and can investigate the case without prior permission of the court.
**Example:** Offence under **Section 65 of the Copyright Act, 1957** (knowing use of infringing copy on a commercial scale) is cognizable because it carries imprisonment exceeding two years (minimum 1 year, maximum 3 years).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> State the punishment prescribed for a first-time offender under Section 67 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 for publishing obscene material in electronic form.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Under **Section 67 IT Act**, on a **first conviction**:
- Imprisonment: **Minimum 3 years, Maximum 5 years**.
- Fine: **Minimum ₹ 5,00,000, Maximum ₹ 10,00,000**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> (a) Discuss in detail the penalties prescribed under the Copyright Act, 1957 for infringement of copyright. Distinguish between first and second convictions. (7 marks)
> 
> (b) Explain the provisions relating to offences by companies and the liability of directors and officers-in-charge under the Copyright Act. (7 marks)

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

The Copyright Act, 1957 prescribes the following key penalties:

1. **Section 63 – General Infringement:** Imprisonment from **6 months to 3 years** and a fine from **₹ 50,000 to ₹ 2,00,000**.

2. **Section 63-A – Second / Subsequent Conviction:** Both the **minimum imprisonment and minimum fine are doubled**. The minimum imprisonment becomes **1 year** and the minimum fine becomes **₹ 1,00,000**.

3. **Section 65 – Commercial-Scale Piracy:** Knowingly using an infringing copy of any work on a commercial scale invites imprisonment of **1 to 3 years** plus a fine of **₹ 50,000 to ₹ 2,00,000**.

4. **Section 66 – Internet Piracy:** Recording / distributing infringing copies of any cinematograph film or sound recording via the internet attracts imprisonment up to **3 years** and a fine up to **₹ 5,00,000**.

5. **Section 69 – Technological Protection Measures (TPMs):** Circumventing technological protection or altering rights-management information is punishable with imprisonment from **1 to 3 years** plus a fine not below **₹ 50,000**.

| Conviction Type | Min. Imprisonment | Min. Fine |
|---|---|---|
| First conviction (Sec 63) | 6 months | ₹ 50,000 |
| Second / subsequent (Sec 63-A) | 1 year | ₹ 1,00,000 |

**(b) Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Section 11 of the Copyright Act** deals with offences by companies. The provision operates as follows:

- **Primary Liability of the Company:** Where an offence is committed by a company, the **company itself is liable** and shall be proceeded against.

- **Deemed Liability of Natural Persons:** Every person who at the time of the offence was **in charge of, and was responsible to** the company for the conduct of the business of the company, **shall be deemed to be guilty** of the offence and shall be **liable to be proceeded against and punished**.

- **Liability of Officers-in-Default:** Any director, manager, secretary, or other officer of the company who the court finds was **"in default"** is also liable.

- **Defence Available:** A director / officer can escape liability only if he proves either:
  (i) the offence was committed **without his knowledge**, OR
  (ii) he had exercised **all due diligence** to prevent its commission.

- **Trial Procedure:** No such officer shall be punishable with imprisonment **unless** the court finds that the offence was committed with his **knowledge or connivance**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the "due diligence" defence** and lose 2 marks. Always mention both limbs — *absence of knowledge* AND *exercise of due diligence* — as defences available to the director.
> 2. When stating quantum of fine, **always specify "minimum to maximum"** (e.g., ₹ 50,000 to ₹ 2,00,000). Writing only "₹ 2 lakh" will fetch only 1 out of 3 marks.
> 3. For Sec 63-A, write the **doubling effect** explicitly: "On second conviction, the minimum imprisonment is raised from 6 months to 1 year AND the minimum fine is raised from ₹ 50,000 to ₹ 1,00,000." Examiners allot separate marks for the comparison.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> (a) Explain the penalties prescribed under the Information Technology Act, 2000 for (i) hacking, (ii) identity theft, and (iii) cyber terrorism. (7 marks)
> 
> (b) "Penalties under the IT Act have a graded structure corresponding to the severity of the cyber-offence." Critically analyse this statement with case laws. (7 marks)

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Hacking — Section 66 IT Act:**
Whoever, with intent to cause or knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or damage, dishonestly or fraudulently destroys or deletes or alters any information residing in a computer resource, shall be punishable with imprisonment up to **3 years** or with fine up to **₹ 5,00,000**, or both.

**(ii) Identity Theft — Section 66-C IT Act:**
Whoever, fraudulently or dishonestly makes use of the **electronic signature, password, or any other unique identification feature** of any other person, shall be punishable with imprisonment up to **3 years** and a fine up to **₹ 1,00,000**.

**(iii) Cyber Terrorism — Section 66-F IT Act:**
Whoever commits a cyber-attack with intent to threaten the **unity, integrity, sovereignty, or security of India**, or to strike terror in the people, shall be punishable with imprisonment which **may extend to life imprisonment** along with a fine as the court deems fit.

The graded severity is summarized below:

| Offence | Section | Maximum Imprisonment | Maximum Fine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hacking | 66 | 3 years | ₹ 5,00,000 |
| Identity theft | 66-C | 3 years | ₹ 1,00,000 |
| Cyber terrorism | 66-F | Life | Discretionary |

**(b) Model Answer (7 marks):**

The IT Act 2000 (as amended in 2008) demonstrates a deliberate **gradient of severity**:

- **Lower Tier (Civil Penalties — Section 43 / 43-A):** Damages to computer systems attract only **civil compensation up to ₹ 5 Crore**, no criminal liability.
- **Middle Tier (Sections 65, 66, 66-A to 66-E):** Criminal offences carrying **3 years imprisonment** and **fines of ₹ 1 to 5 Lakh**.
- **Upper Tier (Section 67):** Obscenity / pornography — **3 to 5 years (first) and 5 to 7 years (subsequent)** — reflecting the grave social harm.
- **Apex Tier (Section 66-F Cyber Terrorism):** **Life imprisonment** — reflecting threats to national security.

**Critical Case Laws:**

1. ***Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)*** — The Supreme Court struck down **Section 66-A** (criminalising "offensive" online messages) as violative of Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of speech). This is a landmark showing that **penalty statutes must satisfy the proportionality test**.

2. ***Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014)*** — Electronic records must be proved in accordance with **Section 65-B of the Indian Evidence Act**; otherwise, the penalty proceedings may fail for want of evidence.

3. ***Avnish Bajaj v. State (NCT of Delhi) (2005)*** — The Delhi High Court held that an **intermediary** like Baazee.com was not directly liable for hosting infringing content unless it had specific knowledge. This shaped the **"knowledge threshold"** for criminal liability under IT Act penalties.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Part B Question B:**
> 1. Writing only the section numbers without **citing the case laws** loses 2–3 marks. KTU valuation key **explicitly awards 1 mark per case law** with proper citation.
> 2. Avoid generic statements like "hacking is bad." Always quote the **statutory phrase** ("with intent to cause or knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or damage").
> 3. For the **graded structure**, you **must** use a table or a tier-based list; examiners award marks for **structured presentation**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Penalties (PECCT861 Module 3)**

- **Three-Layer Deterrent Model:** Civil → Criminal → Administrative.
- **Section 63, Copyright Act** = base infringement penalty (6 months – 3 years / ₹ 50,000 – ₹ 2,00,000).
- **Section 63-A** = **doubling on second conviction** — guaranteed KTU question.
- **Section 65 Copyright Act** = commercial-scale piracy (1–3 years).
- **Section 66 IT Act** = hacking (3 years / ₹ 5 Lakh).
- **Section 66-F IT Act** = cyber terrorism (up to **life imprisonment**).
- **Section 67 IT Act** = first conviction = **3–5 years**; subsequent = **5–7 years**.
- **Section 103 TM Act** = false trade descriptions (6 months – 3 years).
- **Section 120 Patents Act** = false claim of patent rights (2 years / ₹ 5 Lakh).
- **Section 11 Copyright Act** = offences by companies; **deemed liability** of directors.
- **Defence of Director:** either *no knowledge* OR *all due diligence* — both limbs must be stated.
- **Court of Trial:** ≤ 3 years → JMFC; > 3 years → Sessions Court.
- **Forfeiture:** Court **must** order seizure/forfeiture of infringing goods (Sections 62, 66-A, etc.).
- **Compounding:** Most IP offences are **non-compoundable**; only specified ones under IT Act Rules can be compounded.
- **Shreya Singhal (2015)** = struck down Sec 66-A — must remember for **judicial scrutiny** questions.
- **Anvar P.V. (2014)** = Section 65-B Evidence Act compliance for electronic records.
- **Repeat-offence doubling** is the **single most repeated** KTU question on this topic — memorize it first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adjudication and Appeals under the IT Act 2000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adjudication and Appeals under the IT Act 2000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Adjudication under the IT Act 2000?

**Adjudication** under the Information Technology Act, 2000 (as amended in 2008) refers to the **quasi-judicial process** by which a designated authority (the Adjudicating Officer) hears disputes, determines liability, and awards compensation or damages for violations of the Act that are not classified as criminal offenses punishable by imprisonment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Adjudication is the statutory mechanism established under **Chapter IX (Sections 46–51)** of the IT Act 2000, whereby the Central Government appoints an officer not below the rank of **Joint Secretary** to adjudicate claims for **damages and compensation** arising from contravention of provisions of the Act, particularly those related to **data protection, privacy, electronic records, digital signatures, and Section 79 (Intermediary Liability)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you order a product online, but the company **leaks your credit card details**, causing you financial loss. You cannot file a criminal FIR for this (because it's a *civil wrong*, not a crime like hacking). So who settles this dispute? 

👉 **The Adjudicating Officer acts like a "Cyber Civil Judge"** — a specialized, government-appointed referee who listens to both parties, examines digital evidence, and orders the guilty party to pay you compensation (money) for your loss. It is faster, cheaper, and simpler than going to a regular civil court.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Adjudication** = Quasi-judicial (administrative authority acting as judge) → deals with **CIVIL wrongs** → awards **COMPENSATION**.
> - **Criminal Trial** = Judicial (regular court with magistrate/judge) → deals with **CRIMES** → awards **IMPRISONMENT / FINE** (e.g., Sections 65, 66, 67 of IT Act).

## 1.2 What is an Appeal under the IT Act 2000?

An **appeal** is the legal right of an aggrieved party to challenge the order passed by the Adjudicating Officer before a higher judicial authority, in order to seek **justice, reversal, or modification** of that order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Hierarchy (KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus):**
> $$\text{Adjudicating Officer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{High Court}$$
>
> **Note:** Post the **Information Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008**, the Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT) was dissolved, and its jurisdiction was transferred to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)**, notified via Gazette Notification dated **22 September 2010**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Adjudicating Officer (AO)** | Officer appointed by Central Govt. (not below Joint Secretary rank) to settle civil disputes under IT Act |
| **Compensation** | Monetary relief awarded to a person who has suffered damage due to a contravention |
| **Damage** | Any loss caused to a person — financial, reputational, or otherwise |
| **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** | Quasi-judicial body for hearing appeals (now subsumed under TDSAT) |
| **High Court** | Final appellate authority under Section 58 of the IT Act |
| **Intermediary** | Any entity that handles electronic records on behalf of another (e.g., ISPs, social media) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constitutional & Statutory Foundation

The adjudication framework draws its power from **Article 323-A of the Indian Constitution**, which allows Parliament to establish administrative tribunals for adjudication of disputes. The IT Act 2000 operationalizes this through **Chapter IX (Sections 46–51)** and **Chapter X (Sections 57–58)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adjudication?**
> The IT Act creates new digital-age liabilities (e.g., failure to protect sensitive personal data). Traditional civil courts are overburdened and lack technical expertise. Adjudication provides a **specialized, expedited, and cost-effective** remedy.

## 2.2 The Adjudicating Officer — Appointment & Qualifications

### 2.2.1 Statutory Provision: Section 46

The **Central Government** appoints one or more officers, not below the rank of a **Joint Secretary to the Government of India or Secretary to a State Government**, to be the Adjudicating Officer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplicity Principle:** The Central Government **may appoint multiple** Adjudicating Officers for different territorial jurisdictions or subject matters. This ensures **specialization** and **workload distribution**.

### 2.2.2 Statutory Provision: Section 47 — Qualifications

A person shall **not be qualified** for appointment as an Adjudicating Officer unless:
1. He is qualified to be a **District Judge**; OR
2. He has held the post of **Joint Secretary to the Government of India** or any equivalent post.

### 2.2.3 Salary & Conditions

The salary, allowances, and other conditions of service of the Adjudicating Officer are **not varied to his disadvantage** after his appointment — this ensures **independence and impartiality**, akin to constitutional protection given to judges.

## 2.3 Powers of the Adjudicating Officer (Section 48)

The Adjudicating Officer has the powers of a **Civil Court** under the **Code of Civil Procedure, 1908**, while trying a suit. These include:

| Power | Practical Use in Cyber Disputes |
|-------|-------------------------------|
| Summoning & enforcing attendance of witnesses | Compelling tech experts or company representatives to testify |
| Requiring discovery and production of documents | Accessing server logs, email records, transaction trails |
| Receiving evidence on affidavits | Accepting sworn digital evidence (e.g., e-signed affidavits) |
| Issuing commissions for examination of witnesses | Cross-jurisdictional evidence gathering (e.g., server in USA) |
| Reviewing decisions | Correcting errors in earlier orders |
| Dismissing applications for default | Handling non-appearance of parties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** All proceedings before the AO are deemed to be **judicial proceedings** within the meaning of **Sections 193 and 228 of the Indian Penal Code (now Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023)**. This means giving **false evidence** before the AO is a **punishable offense**.

## 2.4 Who Can File a Claim? (Section 49 + Section 46)

A claim for compensation can be filed by:
- **Any person** who has suffered damage due to a contravention of any provision of the IT Act;
- The AO, **suo motu (on his own motion)**, can also initiate inquiry.

## 2.5 Order of the Adjudicating Officer

After hearing both sides, the AO passes a **reasoned order**:
1. Determining whether a contravention has occurred;
2. Directing the guilty party to pay **compensation** (no fixed upper limit in original Act; capped at **₹5 crore** by the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 for certain contraventions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The AO's order **shall be executable as a decree** of a civil court, and recovery can be made as arrears of **land revenue** (Section 50).

## 2.6 Appeals Framework — Statutory Map

### 2.6.1 Section 51 — Appeal to Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)

- Any person aggrieved by an order of the AO may prefer an appeal to the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)**.
- Time limit: **45 days** from the date of receipt of the order.
- The Tribunal is established by the Central Government under **Section 48 of the IT Act 2000** (original).

> [!NOTE]
> **Post-2008 Amendment Status:** Following the **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008**, the CAT was **never constituted**. Its functions were transferred to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)** under the **Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI) Act, 1997**, by an ordinance dated **22 September 2010**.

### 2.6.2 Section 58 — Appeal to High Court

- Any person dissatisfied with the order of CAT (now TDSAT) may appeal to the **High Court** within **60 days**.
- The High Court's decision is **final** — no further statutory appeal is provided under the IT Act.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Statutory Reference | Key Parameter | Value / Detail |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Appointment of AO | Section 46 | Rank | Not below **Joint Secretary (GoI)** or **Secretary (State Govt.)** |
| Qualifications of AO | Section 47 | Standard | Qualified to be **District Judge** or held equivalent post |
| Powers of AO | Section 48 | Reference Law | **Code of Civil Procedure, 1908** |
| Who can file claim | Section 49 read with Section 46 | Claimant | **Any person** who suffered damage |
| Maximum compensation (general) | Section 47 (post-2008) | Cap | **₹5 crore** (for contraventions not specifically limited) |
| Appeal deadline (to CAT/TDSAT) | Section 51 | Time | **45 days** |
| Tribunal to hear appeal | Section 57 (amended) | Body | **TDSAT** (after 2010) |
| Appeal to High Court | Section 58 | Time | **60 days** |
| Recovery mechanism | Section 50 | Mode | As **arrears of land revenue** |
| Statement in pleading | Section 48(2) | Form | **Verification in form of affidavit** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Time Limits — Remember these for KTU exams:**
> $$\text{AO Order} \xrightarrow{\,45\,\text{days}\,} \text{CAT/TDSAT} \xrightarrow{\,60\,\text{days}\,} \text{High Court (Final)}$$

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

For **B.Tech students**, understanding this framework is critical for:
- **Cybersecurity Risk Assessment:** Determining the legal exposure of a startup/data-handling company.
- **Compliance Engineering:** Designing systems (encryption, access logs) to defend against potential adjudication claims.
- **IPR-cyber overlap:** Linking Section 79 (intermediary liability) with Section 46 (adjudication) — e.g., when an e-commerce platform leaks user data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: How a Cyber Civil Dispute Flows

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step procedural derivation** of how an adjudication case moves through the IT Act's dispute resolution machinery.

### Stage 1: Initiation of the Claim

**Step 1.1 — Occurrence of a Wrongful Act**
A person (Claimant) suffers damage due to an act/omission that contravenes a provision of the IT Act. For example:
- A hospital's unsecured database leaks a patient's medical records (violation of reasonable security practices under Section 43A).
- A person receives defamatory messages via email (violation of privacy).

**Step 1.2 — Filing the Application**
The Claimant files a written application before the Adjudicating Officer, **stating**:
1. The name and address of the person who caused the damage;
2. The particulars of the damage suffered;
3. The relief (compensation amount) claimed.

> The application **must be verified** in the form of an affidavit (Rule 3 of the IT (Qualification and Experience of Adjudicating Officers and Manner of Holding Enquiry) Rules, 2003).

### Stage 2: Pre-Hearing Procedure

**Step 2.1 — Issuance of Notice (Section 48)**
The AO issues notice to the **Respondent** (the person alleged to have caused the damage) to appear and show cause within the prescribed time.

**Step 2.2 — Filing of Reply**
The Respondent submits a written reply with supporting evidence (digital records, logs, contracts).

**Step 2.3 — Discovery of Documents**
The AO may direct both parties to disclose documents in their possession. Since the AO has the powers of a Civil Court under **CPC 1908**, **Order XI** (Discovery and Inspection) applies.

### Stage 3: The Hearing

**Step 3.1 — Recording of Evidence**
The AO records evidence, which may be oral, documentary, or **affidavit-based** (Section 48(2)).

**Step 3.2 — Examination of Witnesses**
Witnesses may be summoned under **Order XVI of CPC, 1908**. For cross-border disputes, commissions may be issued under **Order XXVI CPC**.

### Stage 4: Determination and Order

**Step 4.1 — Application of Mind**
The AO applies the **principles of natural justice** — *audi alteram partem* (hear the other side) and *nemo judex in causa sua* (no one should be a judge in their own cause).

**Step 4.2 — Determination of Compensation**
The compensation is calculated considering:

$$
\text{Compensation} = \text{Direct Loss} + \text{Consequential Loss} + \text{Loss of Reputation} - \text{Contributory Negligence}
$$

The AO passes a **reasoned written order** — either **allowing** or **dismissing** the claim.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order must be speaking:** A non-speaking order is liable to be set aside in appeal (principle from *Mahabir Auto Stores v. IOC*, 1990 Supp SCC 404).

### Stage 5: Enforcement

**Step 5.1 — Recovery (Section 50)**
If the Respondent fails to pay the compensation, the order is executed as a **decree of a civil court**. The recovery is made as **arrears of land revenue** under the relevant State Revenue Act.

### Stage 6: Appeals (Exhaustive Path)

**Step 6.1 — First Appeal: Section 51**
- File a **memorandum of appeal** to CAT/TDSAT within **45 days**.
- The appeal lies on **questions of law and fact**.

**Step 6.2 — Second Appeal: Section 58**
- File a **second appeal** to the **High Court** within **60 days**.
- The High Court entertains questions of **law** primarily, though facts can also be re-examined in interest of justice.

**Step 6.3 — Conclusion**
The decision of the High Court is **final and binding**. No further appeal lies under the IT Act. However, constitutional remedies under **Article 226 / Article 32** of the Constitution (via the Supreme Court) remain available in extreme cases.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Powers & Limitations Comparison

| Parameter | Adjudicating Officer (AO) | Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT) / TDSAT | High Court |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|------------|
| **Statutory Basis** | Section 46 | Section 57 | Section 58 |
| **Nature of Body** | Quasi-judicial (single officer) | Quasi-judicial (tribunal) | Constitutional court |
| **Powers** | Powers of Civil Court under CPC | Same as AO + appellate review | Constitutional & statutory appellate |
| **Penalty / Award** | Compensation up to ₹5 crore | Modify, confirm, or reverse AO's order | Final adjudication; reverse/modify |
| **Time Limit for Filing Appeal** | N/A (original order) | **45 days** | **60 days** |
| **Nature of Review** | First-instance adjudication | First appeal | Second / final appeal |
| **Precedent Value** | Binding on parties | Binding on parties | Binding precedent for lower bodies |

## 3.3 Distinguishing Adjudication from Criminal Prosecution

| Criterion | Adjudication (Chapter IX) | Criminal Prosecution (Chapter XI) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Triggered by** | Civil damage claim | Cognizance of offense |
| **Authority** | Adjudicating Officer (Joint Secretary rank) | Magistrate (Sessions/Metropolitan) |
| **Relief** | Monetary compensation | Imprisonment + fine |
| **Procedure** | Summary inquiry, CPC-based | CrPC (now BNSS)-based, full trial |
| **Burden of Proof** | Preponderance of probabilities | Beyond reasonable doubt |
| **Sections involved** | 43, 43A, 79, etc. | 65, 66, 66A (struck down), 67, 69, etc. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Adjudication & Appeals Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Person suffers damage due to IT Act contravention] --> B[Files claim before Adjudicating Officer AO]
    B --> C{AO issues notice to Respondent}
    C --> D[Respondent files reply with evidence]
    D --> E[Discovery of documents Order XI CPC]
    E --> F[Oral / Affidavit evidence recorded]
    F --> G{AO passes reasoned order}
    G -- Compensation awarded --> H[Order executable as decree of civil court]
    G -- Claim dismissed --> I[Claimant receives order]
    H --> J{45 days to appeal}
    I --> J
    J -- Yes --> K[Appeal to Cyber Appellate Tribunal / TDSAT]
    J -- No --> L[Order becomes final]
    K --> M{TDSAT decides}
    M -- Confirm/Modify/Reverse --> N{60 days to appeal}
    N -- Yes --> O[Appeal to High Court Section 58]
    N -- No --> L
    O --> P[High Court final decision]
    P --> Q[Constitutional remedy Article 226/32 if needed]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Statutory Architecture (Sections 46–58)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ch9[Chapter IX - Adjudication of Penalties]
        S46[Section 46 Appointment of AO]
        S47[Section 47 Qualifications of AO]
        S48[Section 48 Powers of AO like Civil Court]
        S49[Section 49 Claims by aggrieved persons]
        S50[Section 50 Recovery of Penalty]
    end
    subgraph Ch10[Chapter X - Appellate Tribunal]
        S51[Section 51 Appeals to CAT]
        S57[Section 57 Establishment of CAT]
        S58[Section 58 Appeal to High Court]
    end
    S46 --> S47 --> S48 --> S49 --> S50
    S50 --> S51
    S51 --> S57
    S57 --> S58
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Post-2008 Institutional Reform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original IT Act 2000: Chapter X provided for CAT] --> B[IT Amendment Act 2008: CAT not to be constituted]
    B --> C[Ordinance dated 22 Sept 2010]
    C --> D[Jurisdiction transferred to TDSAT under TRAI Act 1997]
    D --> E[TDSAT hears appeals from Adjudicating Officer Orders]
    E --> F[Further appeal lies to High Court under Section 58]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Two-Track Justice System under IT Act

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cyber Wrong] --> B{Type of Wrong}
    B -- Civil damage, compensation --> C[Adjudication Track]
    B -- Criminal act, imprisonment --> D[Criminal Prosecution Track]
    C --> E[Adjudicating Officer]
    E --> F[CAT / TDSAT]
    F --> G[High Court]
    D --> H[Magistrate / Sessions Court]
    H --> I[High Court]
    I --> J[Supreme Court]
    G --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
**Define the term "Adjudicating Officer" as per the Information Technology Act, 2000. What is the minimum rank of an officer who can be appointed as an Adjudicating Officer?**

**Model Answer:**
An Adjudicating Officer is a quasi-judicial authority appointed by the Central Government under **Section 46** of the IT Act, 2000 to adjudicate claims for compensation arising from contravention of provisions of the Act. The minimum rank of an Adjudicating Officer is that of a **Joint Secretary to the Government of India** or **Secretary to a State Government**. The officer should also be qualified to be a **District Judge** (Section 47).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the powers of an Adjudicating Officer and a regular Civil Court.**

**Model Answer:**
The Adjudicating Officer, while exercising powers, is **bound by the principles of natural justice** and has the same powers as a Civil Court under the **Code of Civil Procedure, 1908**, including summoning witnesses, discovery of documents, and receiving evidence on affidavits (Section 48). However, unlike a regular Civil Court, the AO is **not a full-fledged court of record** and the proceedings are **summary in nature** aimed at quick redressal of cyber civil disputes. Appeals lie to the Cyber Appellate Tribunal (now TDSAT) and then to the High Court.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option A

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **appointment, qualifications, and powers of the Adjudicating Officer** under the IT Act, 2000. Discuss whether the Adjudicating Officer's order is executable as a decree of a civil court. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Outline the **appeals mechanism** under the IT Act, 2000. What is the present status of the Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT) post the 2008 amendment? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Appointment of the Adjudicating Officer (Section 46):**
The Central Government, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoints one or more officers, not below the rank of **Joint Secretary to the Government of India** or **Secretary to a State Government**, to be the Adjudicating Officer. The objective is to ensure that the adjudicating function is performed by a senior administrative authority with legal and technical understanding.

**Qualifications (Section 47):**
A person is qualified to be appointed as an Adjudicating Officer only if he:
1. Is qualified to be appointed as a **District Judge**; OR
2. Has held the post of **Joint Secretary to the Government of India** or any equivalent post.

The salary and allowances of the AO shall not be varied to his **disadvantage** after appointment, ensuring his **functional independence** akin to judicial officers.

**Powers of the Adjudicating Officer (Section 48):**
The AO has all the powers of a **Civil Court** under the **Code of Civil Procedure, 1908**, while trying a suit, in respect of:
- Summoning and enforcing attendance of witnesses;
- Requiring discovery and production of documents;
- Receiving evidence on affidavits;
- Issuing commissions for examination of witnesses;
- Reviewing its own decisions;
- Dismissing applications for default of appearance.

All proceedings before the AO are deemed **judicial proceedings** within the meaning of Sections 193 and 228 of the IPC (now BNS 2023).

**Execution of Order as a Decree:**
Yes. As per **Section 50** of the IT Act, 2000, the order of the AO directing payment of compensation is executable as a **decree of a civil court**, and the recovery can be made as **arrears of land revenue**. This ensures the enforceability of the AO's orders without requiring a fresh civil suit.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Appeals Mechanism under the IT Act, 2000:**

The IT Act provides a **two-tier appeal mechanism**:

**1. First Appeal — Section 51: To the Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT) / TDSAT**
- Any person aggrieved by an order of the Adjudicating Officer may file an appeal to the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal**.
- The appeal must be filed within **45 days** from the date of receipt of the order.
- The Tribunal may **confirm, modify, or reverse** the order of the AO.

**2. Second Appeal — Section 58: To the High Court**
- Any person aggrieved by the order of CAT (now TDSAT) may appeal to the **High Court** within **60 days**.
- The High Court's decision is **final**, subject only to the **constitutional remedy** under Article 226/Article 32.

**Present Status of the Cyber Appellate Tribunal (Post 2008 Amendment):**
The IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 proposed a complete restructuring of the appellate framework. Section 48 of the amended Act empowers the Central Government to establish a **Cyber Appellate Tribunal** for hearing appeals. However, **CAT was never constituted**. The **Ordinance dated 22 September 2010** transferred its jurisdiction to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)** under the TRAI Act, 1997. TDSAT now hears all appeals from Adjudicating Officers under the IT Act.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option B

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **procedure for adjudication** of claims for damages under the IT Act, 2000. What are the powers of the Adjudicating Officer in the conduct of such proceedings? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **hierarchy and jurisdiction** of the various appellate bodies under the IT Act, 2000, with specific reference to the role of **TDSAT** and the **High Court** in adjudicating cyber disputes. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Procedure for Adjudication (Sections 46, 48, 49, 50):**

The adjudication of claims for damages under the IT Act, 2000 follows a **structured, quasi-judicial procedure**:

1. **Filing of Claim (Section 49):** A person who has suffered damage due to contravention of any provision of the IT Act may file a claim for compensation before the Adjudicating Officer. The claim must be in the form of an application specifying the damage suffered and the relief claimed, **verified in the form of an affidavit** (Rule 3, IT (Adjudication) Rules, 2003).

2. **Notice to Respondent:** The AO issues notice to the person alleged to have caused the damage, calling upon him to show cause within a specified time as to why relief should not be granted.

3. **Filing of Reply and Evidence:** The Respondent files a written reply along with supporting evidence — digital records, system logs, contractual terms, etc.

4. **Discovery and Inspection:** The AO, exercising powers under Order XI of CPC, may direct production and inspection of documents.

5. **Recording of Evidence:** Evidence is recorded — oral testimony, documentary evidence, and affidavits under Section 48(2).

6. **Hearing:** The AO conducts a fair hearing, ensuring both sides are given an opportunity to present their case (principle of *audi alteram partem*).

7. **Passing of Order:** After considering the evidence, the AO passes a **reasoned, speaking order** either allowing or dismissing the claim and determining the compensation amount (capped at **₹5 crore** for certain contraventions).

8. **Recovery:** If the Respondent fails to pay, the order is executed as a **decree of a civil court**, and recovery is made as **arrears of land revenue** (Section 50).

**Powers of the AO in the Proceedings:**
The AO exercises the **powers of a Civil Court** under the CPC, 1908, in respect of:
- Summoning and enforcing the attendance of any person;
- Examining him on oath;
- Requiring discovery and production of documents;
- Receiving evidence on affidavits;
- Issuing commissions for examination of witnesses;
- Reviewing its own decisions;
- Dismissing applications for default.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Hierarchy and Jurisdiction of Appellate Bodies:**

The IT Act, 2000 (as amended in 2008) provides for a **two-tier appellate hierarchy** with the following jurisdictional framework:

**Tier 1 — Adjudicating Officer (AO) [Section 46]:**
- **Jurisdiction:** Adjudicates claims for compensation arising from civil wrongs under the IT Act.
- **Territorial:** All-India jurisdiction, with the AO appointed for specific zones/subject matters.
- **Pecuniary:** Compensation up to **₹5 crore** (post 2008 amendment).
- **Nature:** Quasi-judicial, **first-instance** authority.

**Tier 2 — Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT) / TDSAT [Section 51 read with TRAI Act]:**
- **Jurisdiction:** Hears appeals from orders of the AO on **questions of law and fact**.
- **Composition:** Originally a **Presiding Officer** qualified to be a **High Court Judge**; under TDSAT, the bench is led by a **Chairperson** of TDSAT.
- **Time Limit:** Appeal must be filed within **45 days** of receipt of the AO's order.
- **Current Status:** Although the IT Act 2000 contemplates a CAT, it was **never constituted** post-2008. The **Ordinance of 22 September 2010** transferred all CAT jurisdiction to **TDSAT** under Section 14 of the TRAI Act, 1997.
- **Powers:** Same as AO + appellate review; can **confirm, modify, or set aside** the AO's order.

**Tier 3 — High Court [Section 58]:**
- **Jurisdiction:** Hears second appeals from orders of CAT/TDSAT.
- **Time Limit:** Appeal within **60 days** of receipt of the order.
- **Scope:** Primarily **questions of law**; however, in interest of justice, facts may also be re-examined.
- **Finality:** The decision of the High Court is **final** under the IT Act.
- **Constitutional Remedy:** Aggrieved parties may approach the **Supreme Court** under **Article 136** (Special Leave Petition) of the Constitution.

**Critical Analysis:**
The current framework suffers from a **jurisdictional ambiguity** post-2010. TDSAT is primarily a **telecom tribunal** — placing IT Act appeals before it raises concerns about **specialization and expertise**. Many academic commentaries and the Law Commission of India's **156th Report on "Identification of Police Personnel in Interrogation" and 245th Report on "Obsolete Statutes"** have recommended the **reconstitution of a dedicated Cyber Appellate Tribunal** to handle the growing volume of cyber disputes, especially under the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CAT and TDSAT:** Many students write that CAT is functional. **KTU's expected answer** requires you to state that **CAT was never constituted** post-2008 and **TDSAT now hears the appeals**. Losing 2–3 marks for missing this.
> 2. **Forgetting time limits:** The **45-day** (to CAT/TDSAT) and **60-day** (to High Court) limits are **frequently tested**. Memorize them.
> 3. **Section numbers:** Examiners expect exact section references: **Section 46 (AO)**, **Section 47 (Qualifications)**, **Section 48 (Powers)**, **Section 50 (Recovery)**, **Section 51 (Appeal)**, **Section 58 (HC Appeal)**.
> 4. **Confusing "Adjudication" with "Arbitration":** Adjudication is **state-driven**; arbitration is **private dispute resolution**.
> 5. **Writing "Supreme Court" as the final appellate authority:** The IT Act explicitly designates the **High Court** as final. Supreme Court jurisdiction is **constitutional**, not statutory under the IT Act.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Adjudication & Appeals**

- ✅ **Adjudicating Officer (AO)** is appointed by the **Central Government** under **Section 46**, rank not below **Joint Secretary (GoI)** or **Secretary (State)**.
- ✅ **Qualifications** under **Section 47**: qualified to be **District Judge** OR held post of Joint Secretary.
- ✅ AO has powers of a **Civil Court under CPC, 1908** (Section 48).
- ✅ **Order is executable as a decree**; recovery is as **arrears of land revenue** (Section 50).
- ✅ **First Appeal:** **45 days** to **CAT/TDSAT** (Section 51).
- ✅ **Second Appeal:** **60 days** to **High Court** (Section 58). High Court is **final**.
- ✅ **CAT was never constituted** post-2008; jurisdiction lies with **TDSAT** (w.e.f. **22 Sept 2010**).
- ✅ All proceedings before AO are deemed **judicial proceedings** (Sections 193, 228 IPC/BNS).
- ✅ **Compensation cap** for certain contraventions is **₹5 crore** (post-2008 amendment).
- ✅ **Distinguish Adjudication (Civil/Compensation) from Criminal Prosecution (Imprisonment)** — frequently asked.
- ✅ **Intermediary liability** (Section 79) claims are also adjudicated through this AO framework.
- ✅ **Salary protection clause** under Section 47 ensures **AO's independence** — akin to a judicial officer.
- ✅ **Suo motu power:** AO can initiate inquiry on its own motion (not just on application).
- ✅ The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** will significantly expand the **adjudication load** on this framework, and a dedicated **Data Protection Board** will parallel the AO mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Offences under IT Act 2000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Offences under IT Act 2000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Offences under IT Act 2000 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Information Technology Act, 2000** (amended in 2008 via the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008) is the primary legislation in India that defines and penalises offences relating to computers, computer networks, electronic records, digital signatures, and cyberspace. **Chapter XI (Sections 65–78)** of the Act is titled *"Offences and Penalties"* and it constitutes the **substantive criminal law backbone** of Indian cyber jurisprudence.
