An **"offence"** under the IT Act is any act or omission that is made punishable by a fine, imprisonment, or both under the provisions of Sections 65 to 78 read with Schedule I (which contains 10 classes of offences triable by the Cyber Appellate Tribunal / designated courts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 3 explicitly requires understanding the **classification of offences**, the **penalties prescribed**, and the **jurisdictional reach** of the IT Act. Every B.Tech examiner expects students to memorise *Section number + Section title + Quantum of punishment*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Penal Code"

Think of the IT Act 2000 as the **"Penal Code of Cyberspace"**, in the same way the Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860 is the penal code of physical India. Just as IPC Section 302 punishes murder, IT Act Section 66F punishes cyber terrorism. The "weapon" has changed from a knife to a keyboard, but the *intent* and the *harm* are analogous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Pillar Analogy:**
> 1. **Weapon Analogy** → A computer / mobile / network is the "weapon" used.
> 2. **Victim Analogy** → A person, organisation, data, or the sovereignty of the State.
> 3. **Punishment Analogy** → Fine + Imprisonment, but **bailable** in most cases (non-bailable for cyber terrorism, child pornography, and a few others).

---

## 1.3 Why is the Study of Offences Critical for Engineers?

- A B.Tech graduate often handles **source code, databases, networks, and confidential client data** — every one of these can become the *corpus delicti* (body of the crime).
- KTU frequently tests **professional liability** under Sections 65, 66, 72, and 73.
- Many offences have **imprisonment up to 3 / 7 / 10 years or life** — non-trivial legal exposure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic for Context:** India recorded over **15 lakh (1.5 million) cybercrime cases** in 2023 (NCRB + I4C data). Offences under the IT Act form a substantial chunk of these, especially **Section 66 (IT Act), 66C (Identity Theft), 66D (Cheating by Personation)**.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Offences (Roadmap)

The offences are logically grouped into **five (5) functional categories**, which KTU examiners often test:

| S.No. | Category | Sections | Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Offences against **Computer Systems & Data** | 65, 66, 66A (struck down), 66B, 66C, 66D, 66E | Hacking, Theft, Identity Theft |
| 2 | Offences against **Public Order / State Sovereignty** | 66F, 69, 69A, 69B | Cyber Terrorism, Interception |
| 3 | Offences against **Public Decency & Morality** | 67, 67A, 67B | Obscenity, Child Pornography |
| 4 | Offences against **Trust & Electronic Records** | 71, 72, 72A, 73, 74 | Breach of Confidentiality, Digital Sig. Fraud |
| 5 | **Procedural & Jurisdictional** Offences | 75, 76, 77, 78, 79, 80 | Extra-territorial reach, Search & Seizure |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The "Offence Anatomy" — Four Mandatory Legal Ingredients

For ANY act to constitute an offence under the IT Act, the prosecution must establish **four ingredients (legal elements)**:

1. **Actus Reus** (Guilty Act) — A physical/digital act (e.g., hacking, publishing).
2. **Mens Rea** (Guilty Mind) — Intent, knowledge, or recklessness (except for strict liability offences).
3. **Causation** — A direct link between the act and the harm to the *computer resource* or *person*.
4. **Harm / Injury** — Damage to data, privacy, property, sovereignty, or decency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks *"Is X an offence under IT Act?"*, your answer MUST reference all four ingredients in the structure: *"Yes, the act of X satisfies the four legal ingredients of an offence under Section Y of the IT Act, 2000 because…"*

---

## 2.2 Section-by-Section High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORISATION FORMULA:** *Section No. → Offence Title → Punishment → Cognizable / Bailable / Triable By*.

| Section | Offence Title (Short) | Punishment | Cognizable? | Bailable? | Triable By |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **65** | Tampering with Computer Source Documents | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** OR fine up to **₹2 lakh** | Yes | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **66** | Computer-Related Offences (Hacking, Data Theft, Damage) | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** OR fine up to **₹5 lakh** | Yes | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **66B** | Receipt of Stolen Computer Resource / Communication Device | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** OR fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Yes | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **66C** | Identity Theft (Using Password/Unique ID fraudulently) | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Yes | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **66D** | Cheating by Personation using Computer Resource | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Yes | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **66E** | Violation of Privacy (Capturing/publishing private images) | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** OR fine up to **₹2 lakh** | Yes | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **66F** | Cyber Terrorism | Imprisonment up to **Life Imprisonment** | Yes | **No** | Court of Session (Special Court) |
| **67** | Publishing Obscene Material in Electronic Form | First offence: 5 yrs + ₹1 lakh; Repeat: 10 yrs + ₹2 lakh | Yes | **No** | Court of Session |
| **67A** | Publishing Sexually Explicit Material | First: 7 yrs + ₹10 lakh; Repeat: 14 yrs + ₹20 lakh | Yes | **No** | Court of Session |
| **67B** | Publishing Child Pornography | First: 5 yrs + ₹10 lakh; Repeat: 7 yrs + ₹20 lakh | Yes | **No** | Court of Session |
| **68** | Failure to Comply with Directions of Controller (Decryption order) | Imprisonment up to **2 yrs** OR fine up to **₹1 lakh** (continuing: ₹25k/day) | No | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **69** | Failure to Intercept / Monitor / Decrypt Information | Imprisonment up to **7 yrs** + fine | Yes | Yes | Court of Session |
| **69A** | Failure to Block Website on Government Order | Imprisonment up to **7 yrs** + fine | No | Yes | Court of Session |
| **69B** | Failure to Monitor / Collect / Retain Traffic Data | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** + fine | No | Yes | Court of Session |
| **70** | Unauthorised Access to Protected Systems (Critical Infra) | Imprisonment up to **10 yrs** + fine | Yes | **No** | Court of Session |
| **71** | Misrepresentation (Suppressing material fact to obtain Licence/DSC) | Imprisonment up to **2 yrs** OR fine up to **₹1 lakh** | No | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **72** | Breach of Confidentiality & Privacy (by Service Provider/Employee) | Imprisonment up to **2 yrs** OR fine up to **₹1 lakh** | No | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **72A** | Disclosure of Information in Breach of Lawful Contract | Imprisonment up to **3 yrs** OR fine up to **₹5 lakh** | Yes | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **73** | Publishing False Digital Signature Certificates | Imprisonment up to **2 yrs** OR fine up to **₹1 lakh** | No | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **74** | Publication of DSC for Fraudulent Purposes | Imprisonment up to **2 yrs** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | No | Yes | Magistrate of the First Class |
| **75** | Extra-Territorial Application (offence committed outside India) | Same as prescribed for the corresponding section | Same | Same | Same |
| **76** | Confiscation of Computer Resource, etc. | Property of the accused confiscated | — | — | Designated Adjudicating Officer |
| **77** | Compensation, Penalties or Damages not to be Interfered by Court | No civil court interference | — | — | — |
| **78** | Compounding of Offences (with permission of Court) | Offences compoundable as per Schedule I | — | — | Court of competent jurisdiction |
| **79** | Penalties for Contraventions (residual clause) | Compensation up to **₹25 crore** | — | — | Adjudicating Officer |
| **80** | Power of Police Officer to Enter, Search, Arrest | — | — | — | Police Officer not below **Inspector** rank |

---

## 2.3 Why and How — The Legislative Logic

The IT Act does not punish every cyber-deviance. It punishes those acts that:
- **Cause economic loss** (66, 66B, 66C, 66D, 72A, 79),
- **Threaten sovereignty & security** (66F, 70),
- **Injure morality & child welfare** (67, 67A, 67B),
- **Destroy trust in digital infrastructure** (65, 71, 73, 74).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Liability vs. Mens Rea:** Sections **67A, 67B, and 66F** impose **strict liability** — the prosecution does NOT need to prove intent. Mere commission of the act is enough. This is exam-favourite material.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Practical Application of Offence Provisions |
|---|---|
| **Software Engineering** | Avoid liability under **Sec 65** (never tamper with open-source licence attributions), **Sec 72** (enforce data confidentiality in code), **Sec 66** (write secure code to avoid being the *instrumentum* of crime). |
| **Network Engineering** | Liability awareness for **Sec 69/69A/69B** — telecom and ISP engineers must comply with lawful interception orders. |
| **AI / Data Science** | **Sec 66E (Privacy)** and **Sec 72A (Breach of Contract)** directly affect training data handling and dataset publication. |
| **Hardware / IoT** | **Sec 70 (Protected Systems)** — IoT devices in critical infrastructure (power grids, hospitals) trigger this provision. |
| **E-Commerce** | **Sec 66C, 66D, 72A** — most common prosecution grounds against e-fraud. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs (The "How" of Each Offence)

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **legal subject**, the "derivations" below are **interpretive legal analyses** of how a lawyer or KTU examiner breaks down an offence into its actionable elements. No programming code is used.

---

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Breakdown: Section 66 (Computer-Related Offences) — The Most Tested Section

**Step 1 — Read the Bare Statute:**

> *"Whoever, with the intent to cause or knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person, destroys or deletes or alters any information residing in a computer resource, or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously by any means, commits the offence of computer-related offence."*

**Step 2 — Identify the Actor (Subject):** *"Whoever"* → any natural person. (Legal persons / companies also covered via the 2008 amendment.)

**Step 3 — Identify the Mental State (Mens Rea):**
- **Option A:** Intent to cause wrongful loss/damage, OR
- **Option B:** Knowledge that wrongful loss/damage is likely.

**Step 4 — Identify the Prohibited Acts (Actus Reus):** Any one of the following —

$$A_{66} = \{\text{destroy}, \text{delete}, \text{alter}, \text{suppress}, \text{diminish utility}, \text{injure}\}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Object (Target):** "Any information residing in a computer resource" — this includes **data, code, metadata, logs, configuration files**.

**Step 6 — Identify the Harm:** *"Wrongful loss or damage"* — measured in **monetary, reputational, or functional terms**.

**Step 7 — Match the Punishment:** Up to **3 years imprisonment OR fine up to ₹5 lakh, OR both**.

> **Why this matters for KTU:** Section 66 is the **"gateway" cyber-offence**. Most cyber-crimes (phishing, data breach, ransomware) are charged under Section 66 along with the specialised sub-sections (66B–66F).

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Breakdown: Section 66F (Cyber Terrorism) — The Highest Punishment Section

**Step 1 — Bare Statute (Simplified):**
A person commits cyber terrorism if he / she:
- **Accesses** a computer resource without authorisation, OR
- **Introduces** a virus / contaminant, OR
- **Denies** access to a person authorised to use the resource, OR
- **Damages / disrupts** the resource,

with the **intent to threaten the unity, integrity, security, or sovereignty of India**, or to **strike terror in the people**.

**Step 2 — Mental State Required (CRITICAL):**

$$M_{66F} = \{\text{Intent to threaten unity, integrity, security, sovereignty}\} \cup \{\text{Intent to strike terror}\}$$

**Step 3 — Punishment Matrix:**

$$P_{66F} = \text{Imprisonment which may extend to LIFE}$$

**Step 4 — Why It Is The Most Severe:**
- Cognizable ✓
- **Non-bailable** ✓
- Triable by Special Court (Sessions) ✓
- **No statutory minimum** — life imprisonment is the **maximum**, not the minimum.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Section 66 (Computer-related offence)** with **Section 66F (Cyber terrorism)**. Remember: **66F requires the *terror / sovereignty* angle**. Hacking a bank server is Section 66; hacking a defence server to wage an ideological war is Section 66F.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Breakdown: Section 67 vs 67A vs 67B — The Obscenity Triad

These three sections form a **graduated hierarchy** based on the **severity of the material**:

$$\text{Severity}_{67} < \text{Severity}_{67A} < \text{Severity}_{67B}$$

**Step 1 — Section 67 (Obscene Material):**
- Targets: *Any obscene material* in electronic form.
- Punishment: First offence = **5 years + ₹1 lakh**; Repeat = **10 years + ₹2 lakh**.

**Step 2 — Section 67A (Sexually Explicit Material):**
- Targets: Material which is *sexually explicit* (a higher standard than "obscene").
- Punishment: First = **7 years + ₹10 lakh**; Repeat = **14 years + ₹20 lakh**.
- **Strict liability** — intent to publish is enough.

**Step 3 — Section 67B (Child Pornography):**
- Targets: Depicts children (under 18) in sexually explicit acts.
- Punishment: First = **5 years + ₹10 lakh**; Repeat = **7 years + ₹20 lakh**.
- **Strict liability** applies.

**Step 4 — Comparative Punishment Table:**

| Section | Material Type | First Offence | Repeat Offence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 67 | Obscene | 5 yrs + ₹1 L | 10 yrs + ₹2 L |
| 67A | Sexually Explicit | 7 yrs + ₹10 L | 14 yrs + ₹20 L |
| 67B | Child Pornography | 5 yrs + ₹10 L | 7 yrs + ₹20 L |

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Breakdown: Section 75 — Extra-Territorial Jurisdiction (A 2024-Realm Favourite)

**Step 1 — Bare Principle:** The IT Act applies to **any offence or contravention committed outside India** by *any person*, irrespective of nationality, if the **act affects a computer resource located in India**.

**Step 2 — Two-Conditional Test:**

$$\text{S.75 applies iff} \left[ (\text{Offence committed outside India}) \wedge (\text{Computer resource in India is affected}) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Example Worked-Out Case:**

*Scenario:* A hacker based in **Berlin, Germany**, launches a ransomware attack on a server located in **Bengaluru, India**. The ransom is paid in crypto to a wallet in Singapore.

*Application of S.75:* Yes, the IT Act applies because:
- The offence (data encryption / extortion) was committed *outside* India (Berlin).
- The *target computer resource* (server) is in India.

*Result:* The Indian courts have jurisdiction even though the accused and the modus operandi are foreign.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why S.75 matters in 2024:** With cloud computing, the "location" of a computer resource is fluid. S.75 ensures **India is not a safe harbour for cyber-criminals** simply because the keyboard was pressed abroad.

---

## 3.5 Procedural Note: Compounding of Offences (Section 78)

| Aspect | Legal Position |
|---|---|
| **Compoundable?** | Yes, but **with permission of the Court**. |
| **Schedule I Reference** | The 10 categories of offences listed in Schedule I (mostly Sections 65, 66, 66B, 66C, 66D, 66E, 67, 67A, 67B, 72, 72A, 73, 74) are compoundable. |
| **Non-Compoundable** | **Section 66F (Cyber Terrorism) and Section 67B (Child Pornography) are NOT compoundable** — they must go to trial. |

> **Why Procedural Awareness:** Engineers working in-house as compliance officers must know which offences can be settled vs. which must face trial. KTU tests this under "Awareness" COs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Block-Level Architecture)

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification Tree of Offences under IT Act 2000

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Offences under IT Act 2000] --> B[Offences vs Computer Systems]
    A --> C[Offences vs Public Order]
    A --> D[Offences vs Public Decency]
    A --> E[Offences vs Trust in Digital Records]
    A --> F[Procedural Jurisdictional Offences]

    B --> B1[S 65 Tampering Source Code]
    B --> B2[S 66 Computer Related Offence]
    B --> B3[S 66B Stolen Computer Resource]
    B --> B4[S 66C Identity Theft]
    B --> B5[S 66D Cheating by Personation]
    B --> B6[S 66E Violation of Privacy]

    C --> C1[S 66F Cyber Terrorism]
    C --> C2[S 69 Failure to Intercept]
    C --> C3[S 69A Failure to Block Website]
    C --> C4[S 69B Failure to Monitor Traffic]
    C --> C5[S 70 Unauthorised Access Protected System]

    D --> D1[S 67 Obscene Material]
    D --> D2[S 67A Sexually Explicit Material]
    D --> D3[S 67B Child Pornography]

    E --> E1[S 71 Misrepresentation to Authority]
    E --> E2[S 72 Breach of Confidentiality by Employee]
    E --> E3[S 72A Disclosure Breaching Contract]
    E --> E4[S 73 False Digital Signature Cert]
    E --> E5[S 74 Fraudulent Publication of DSC]

    F --> F1[S 75 Extra Territorial Jurisdiction]
    F --> F2[S 76 Confiscation of Property]
    F --> F3[S 77 No Civil Court Interference]
    F --> F4[S 78 Compounding of Offences]
    F --> F5[S 79 Residual Penalties upto 25 Crore]
    F --> F6[S 80 Power of Police to Enter and Search]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Punishment Severity Tiers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Low Severity up to 2 Years] --> L1A[S 68, S 71, S 72, S 73, S 74]
    L2[Medium Severity up to 3 Years] --> L2A[S 65, S 66, S 66B, S 66C, S 66D, S 66E, S 69B, S 72A]
    L3[High Severity 5 to 10 Years] --> L3A[S 67, S 67A, S 67B, S 69, S 69A, S 70]
    L4[Maximum Severity Life Imprisonment] --> L4A[S 66F Cyber Terrorism]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Offence Anatomy: How a Cyber-Offence Is Established

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Suspected Cyber Act] --> Q1{Is there a Computer Resource Involved?}
    Q1 -- No --> No1[Not an IT Act Offence. May be IPC]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does the Act fall under S 65 to S 78?}
    Q2 -- No --> No2[May be adjudicated under S 79 Residual]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is Mens Rea or Strict Liability satisfied?}
    Q3 -- No --> No3[No Offence]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is there Wrongful Loss or Damage?}
    Q4 -- No --> No4[No Offence]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Convict[Offence Established. Prosecution under IT Act]
    Convict --> Court[Trial by Magistrate First Class or Court of Session]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Special vs General Offences Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Offence Type] --> B[Compoundable]
    A --> C[Non Compoundable]
    A --> D[Bailable]
    A --> E[Non Bailable]
    A --> F[Cognizable]
    A --> G[Non Cognizable]

    B --> B1[S 65, S 66, S 66B, S 66C, S 66D, S 66E, S 67, S 67A, S 72, S 72A, S 73, S 74]
    C --> C1[S 66F Cyber Terrorism]
    C --> C2[S 67B Child Pornography]

    D --> D1[All Sections EXCEPT S 66F, S 67, S 67A, S 67B, S 70]
    E --> E1[S 66F, S 67, S 67A, S 67B, S 70]

    F --> F1[S 65, S 66, S 66B, S 66C, S 66D, S 66E, S 66F, S 67, S 67A, S 67B, S 69, S 70, S 72A]
    G --> G1[S 68, S 69A, S 69B, S 71, S 72, S 73, S 74]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit the Section number** while writing the offence title. Always quote: *"Section XX of the IT Act, 2000"*.
> - Many confuse the **IT Act 2000 (original) and the IT (Amendment) Act 2008**. The amended version inserted 66A–66F, 67A, 67B, 69A, 69B, etc. Always state *"as amended in 2008"*.
> - Forgetting the **cognizable / bailable / triable-by** classification causes loss of 1–2 marks.
> - **Section 66A was struck down by the Supreme Court in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)***. Examiners LOVE testing this. Do NOT cite S.66A as a current offence.

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Direct)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is meant by "tampering with computer source documents" under the IT Act, 2000? State the punishment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Section 65 of the IT Act, 2000 (as amended in 2008) penalises *"tampering with computer source documents"*. Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys, alters, or causes another to conceal, destroy, or alter any computer source code, computer programme, computer database, or any part thereof, which is required to be kept or maintained by law, commits this offence.
**Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹2 lakh**, or both. The offence is **cognizable, bailable, and triable by a Magistrate of the First Class.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between "Identity Theft" (Sec 66C) and "Cheating by Personation" (Sec 66D).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Section 66C — Identity Theft | Section 66D — Cheating by Personation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Fraudulently or dishonestly using the unique identification feature (password, signature, biometric, etc.) of another person. | Communicating any information which is false and which the sender knows to be false, with intent to cause damage or harm, by impersonating another person. |
| **Mental State** | Dishonest / fraudulent intent | Knowledge of falsehood + intent to cause damage |
| **Punishment** | Up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh | Up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh |
| **Distinct Feature** | Targets the *credential* itself | Targets the *impersonated communication* |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a) Explain in detail the offence of "Cyber Terrorism" under Section 66F of the IT Act, 2000. Discuss its ingredients, punishment, and how it differs from the general computer-related offence under Section 66. (7 marks)**

**(b) Critically analyse the offences relating to publication of obscene, sexually explicit, and child pornography material under Sections 67, 67A, and 67B. Highlight the legal distinction and punishment tiers. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — (a) Cyber Terrorism under Section 66F (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Section 66F was inserted by the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 to address the emerging threat of using cyberspace as a weapon against the State. It is the most severe cyber-offence under Indian law.

**Ingredients of the Offence (3 marks):**
The prosecution must establish the following:
1. The accused **accessed** a computer resource without authorisation OR **introduced a virus/contaminant** OR **caused denial of access** OR **damaged/disrupted** the resource.
2. The act was done **with the intent** to threaten the **unity, integrity, security, or sovereignty of India**, OR to **strike terror in the people** OR to **destabilise the economic security** of the State.

**Punishment (1 mark):**
Imprisonment which **may extend to life imprisonment**, along with a fine. The offence is **cognizable, non-bailable**, and triable by the **Court of Session (Special Court)**.

**Distinction from Section 66 (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Section 66 (Computer-Related Offence) | Section 66F (Cyber Terrorism) |
|---|---|---|
| Object | Any person / public | Sovereignty & security of the State |
| Mens Rea | Intent to cause wrongful loss / damage | Intent to threaten / strike terror |
| Maximum Punishment | 3 years + ₹5 lakh fine | **Life imprisonment** + fine |
| Bailable | Yes | **No** |
| Severity | Medium | Highest |

---

#### **Model Answer — (b) Sections 67, 67A, 67B — Obscenity Offences (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Sections 67, 67A, and 67B form a graduated framework targeting three levels of sexually offensive content in electronic form, with progressively stricter punishments reflecting greater social harm.

**Section 67 — Obscene Material (2 marks):**
- Targets material which is *"lascivious or appeals to the prurient interest"* and is *"obscene"* under Section 292 IPC standards.
- Punishment: First offence — up to **5 years + ₹1 lakh fine**; Repeat offence — up to **10 years + ₹2 lakh fine**.
- Cognizable and **non-bailable**.

**Section 67A — Sexually Explicit Material (2 marks):**
- Targets material depicting *"sexually explicit acts"* — a higher threshold than "obscene".
- Punishment: First offence — up to **7 years + ₹10 lakh fine**; Repeat — up to **14 years + ₹20 lakh fine**.
- **Strict liability** — no need to prove intent to publish for a first offence.

**Section 67B — Child Pornography (2 marks):**
- Targets material depicting **children under 18** in sexually explicit acts.
- Punishment: First offence — up to **5 years + ₹10 lakh fine**; Repeat — up to **7 years + ₹20 lakh fine**.
- **Strict liability** and **non-compoundable**.

**Conclusion (No separate marks, but adds depth):**
The 2008 Amendment reflects the international movement against online child exploitation (aligned with the **Budapest Convention 2001** and the **ITU Child Online Protection framework**).

---

### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a) Discuss the offence of "Tampering with Computer Source Documents" (S 65) and the "Computer-Related Offence" (S 66). Compare the two with reference to the nature of the act and punishment. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the extra-territorial application of the IT Act under Section 75. Why is this provision significant in the era of cloud computing? (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — (a) Section 65 vs Section 66 (7 marks)**

**Section 65 — Tampering with Computer Source Documents (3 marks):**
- **Nature of the Act:** Static / documentary — destroying, altering, or concealing the *source code, programme, or database* that is mandated to be maintained by law.
- **Target:** The *integrity of the document* (not the data being processed).
- **Mens Rea:** Knowingly or intentionally.
- **Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **3 years OR fine up to ₹2 lakh** OR both.
- **Example:** A software company employee deletes the source code of a banking application before a regulatory audit.

**Section 66 — Computer-Related Offence (3 marks):**
- **Nature of the Act:** Dynamic / data-level — destroying, deleting, altering, suppressing, or diminishing the utility of *information residing in a computer resource*.
- **Target:** The *information / data* and its operational utility.
- **Mens Rea:** Intent to cause or knowledge of likelihood of causing wrongful loss or damage.
- **Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **3 years OR fine up to ₹5 lakh** OR both.
- **Example:** A hacker deploys ransomware encrypting a hospital's patient database.

**Comparative Analysis (1 mark):**
- Both punishable up to 3 years; however, **S.66 carries a higher fine ceiling (₹5 lakh vs ₹2 lakh)** reflecting the greater commercial and social damage of data-level attacks.
- S.65 is **document-centric**; S.66 is **data- and intent-centric**.

---

#### **Model Answer — (b) Extra-Territorial Jurisdiction — Section 75 (7 marks)**

**Statutory Provision (1 mark):**
Section 75 of the IT Act, 2000 provides that the provisions of the Act apply to **any offence or contravention committed outside India** by any person, irrespective of his/her nationality, **if the act involves a computer, computer network, or computer resource located in India.**

**Two-Part Test (2 marks):**
1. The offence must have been committed **outside the territory of India**.
2. The **target computer resource must be located in India** (or the act must have an effect in India).

**Significance in the Cloud Era (4 marks):**
1. **Borderless Computing:** With cloud servers distributed globally (AWS, Azure, GCP), data is rarely confined to one jurisdiction. S.75 ensures that **an Indian data subject is protected** even if the attacker is abroad.
2. **Combating Transnational Crime:** Cybercrime networks operate across multiple countries. S.75 enables Indian agencies (CBI, I4C, state cyber cells) to **investigate and prosecute foreign actors** through MLATs (Mutual Legal Assistance Treaties).
3. **Data Sovereignty:** Aligns with the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** principles that data of Indian residents has Indian jurisdiction.
4. **Preventing Safe Havens:** Ensures India is not used as a launchpad by international cyber-criminals targeting Indian resources.

**Landmark Reference:** The principle of S.75 was judicially affirmed in *State of Tamil Nadu v. Suhas Katti* (2004) and reinforced in subsequent judgments on jurisdiction in cyberspace.

**Conclusion:** S.75 is the **jurisdictional backbone** that gives the IT Act its **global reach** and is critical for the enforcement architecture of Indian cyber-law.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Notes — Read This Twice Before the Exam**

- **IT Act 2000 + IT (Amendment) Act 2008** is the operative legislation; Chapter XI (S.65–S.78) is the offences chapter.
- **Section 65** → Tampering with Source Code (3 yrs / ₹2 L). **Memorise the keyword: "Document / Source code".**
- **Section 66** → Computer-related offence (3 yrs / ₹5 L). **Memorise the keyword: "Data / Information".** It is the **gateway section**.
- **Section 66B** → Stolen Computer Resource (3 yrs / ₹1 L). Simple possession of stolen IT asset.
- **Section 66C** → Identity Theft (3 yrs + ₹1 L). Targets *credentials*.
- **Section 66D** → Cheating by Personation (3 yrs + ₹1 L). Targets *impersonated communication*.
- **Section 66E** → Violation of Privacy (3 yrs / ₹2 L). Capturing/publishing private images.
- **Section 66F** → Cyber Terrorism (**LIFE** imprisonment). **The most severe cyber-offence.** Non-bailable. Sovereignty / terror angle.
- **Section 67** → Obscene material (5/10 yrs + ₹1L/2L). Bailable after first arrest in some cases but **non-bailable per statute**.
- **Section 67A** → Sexually explicit (7/14 yrs + ₹10L/20L). Strict liability.
- **Section 67B** → Child Pornography (5/7 yrs + ₹10L/20L). Strict liability. Non-compoundable.
- **Section 68** → Non-compliance with Controller's directions (2 yrs / ₹1 L).
- **Section 69** → Failure to intercept (7 yrs + fine).
- **Section 69A** → Failure to block website (7 yrs + fine).
- **Section 69B** → Failure to monitor traffic (3 yrs + fine).
- **Section 70** → Unauthorised access to *protected systems* (10 yrs + fine). Critical infrastructure.
- **Section 71** → Misrepresentation to authority (2 yrs / ₹1 L).
- **Section 72** → Breach of confidentiality by employee (2 yrs / ₹1 L).
- **Section 72A** → Disclosure in breach of lawful contract (3 yrs / ₹5 L). **Cognizable** unlike S.72.
- **Section 73** → False Digital Signature Certificate (2 yrs / ₹1 L).
- **Section 74** → DSC for fraudulent purpose (2 yrs + ₹1 L).
- **Section 75** → **Extra-territorial jurisdiction** — every question on "foreign cyber-crime" expects this answer.
- **Section 76** → Confiscation of property.
- **Section 78** → Compounding of offences (Schedule I).
- **Section 79** → Residual penalty up to **₹25 crore**.
- **Section 80** → Power of police (Inspector rank) to enter, search, seize, and arrest.
- **Section 66A was STRUCK DOWN** in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India, 2015*. Do NOT cite it.
- **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** — currently the designated appellate forum (though its functioning was affected by a 2017 Ordinance; jurisdiction lies with the High Court under Section 29 of the Administrative Tribunals Act read with the IT Act).
- **Cognizable + Bailable + Magistrate 1st Class** is the default for *most* offences; exceptions are **66F, 67, 67A, 67B, 70 (non-bailable)** and **66F, 67, 67A, 67B, 70 (Sessions Court)**.
- **Schedule I** lists 10 classes of compoundable offences.
- **Fine ceilings are in INR (₹)** — not dollars. Examiners check this.
- **Trial procedure** → Inquiry under CrPC 1973 applies; designated **Special Courts** under the IT Act.
- **"Computer resource"** is defined in S.2(1)(k) — remember: includes *any device, software, network, data, database*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Answer Writing:**
> Always structure your offence-related answer as:
> 1. **Section + Title** (1 line)
> 2. **Ingredients** (3–4 lines, in bullet form)
> 3. **Punishment** (1 line — number, fine, nature)
> 4. **Class of offence** — Cognizable / Bailable / Triable by (1 line)
> 5. **One Real-Life Example** (1 line — to score the "Application" marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Right to Information Act 2005
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Right to Information Act 2005

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — RIGHT TO INFORMATION ACT, 2005

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Importance):**
> The **Right to Information Act, 2005 (RTI Act)** is a landmark piece of Indian legislation enacted by the **Parliament of India** on **15th June 2005** and came into force on **12th October 2005**. It provides a statutory framework for citizens to secure access to information held by **Public Authorities**, thereby promoting transparency, accountability, and good governance.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
The RTI Act, 2005 is **An Act to provide for setting out the practical regime of right to information for citizens to secure access to information under the control of public authorities, in order to promote transparency and accountability in the working of every public authority, the constitution of a Central Information Commission and State Information Commissions and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto.**

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| Act Number | No. 22 of 2005 |
| Enactment Date | 15th June 2005 |
| Date of Commencement | 12th October 2005 (Full Act) |
| Applicable Territory | Whole of India (except J&K prior to 2019) |
| Applicable To | All constitutional authorities, bodies owned/controlled/financed by the Government, NGOs substantially financed by Government |
| Citizen Requirement | Only Indian Citizens (Section 3) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — "The Glass House of Government":**
> Imagine the Government as a massive house where decisions about your money, your roads, your schools, and your safety are made. Before RTI, this house had thick curtains — citizens could only see what the Government *chose* to show. The **RTI Act, 2005** essentially pulled back those curtains. Now, every Indian citizen has the legal right to **walk up to a designated officer (PIO), pay ₹10, and ask "How did you spend my tax money?"** — and the officer is legally bound to answer within **30 days**. If they refuse, lie, or delay, they face personal penalties up to **₹25,000 per day** from an independent Information Commission.

### 1.3 Constitutional Backing & Pre-RTI Position
- **Article 19(1)(a)** of the Constitution guarantees the freedom of speech and expression, which the Supreme Court interpreted in *Raj Narain vs. Indira Gandhi (1975)* and *S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India (1982)* as encompassing the right to know.
- Prior to 2005, India had the **Freedom of Information Act, 2002**, which had limited applicability and weak enforcement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always begin any RTI answer by mentioning that the Act operationalizes the fundamental right to information implicit in **Article 19(1)(a)**. This fetches easy marks.

### 1.4 Statement of Objects and Reasons — Key Objectives
The Statement of Objects and Reasons lists **three primary objectives**:
1. To ensure transparency in the functioning of public authorities.
2. To bring accountability in decision-making processes.
3. To contain corruption and promote good governance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Act does NOT apply to:**
> - **J&K** (was exempted, but merged in 2019 — now applies)
> - **Intelligence and security organizations** specified in the Second Schedule (Section 24)
> - **Sovereignty and Integrity of India**, scientific/economic interests, etc., (Section 8)

---

### 1.5 Important Definitions under Section 2

| Term (Section 2) | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Appropriate Government** (2(a)) | Central Government for Central Public Authorities; State Government for State Public Authorities |
| **Central Information Commission (CIC)** (2(b)) | The Commission constituted under Section 12 |
| **Central Public Information Officer (CPIO)** (2(c)) | Officer designated under Section 5(1) |
| **Chief Information Commissioner (CIC)** (2(d)) | Head of the Central Information Commission |
| **Competent Authority** (2(e)) | Minister in-charge of a Ministry/Department |
| **Information** (2(f)) | Any material in any form — records, documents, memos, e-mails, opinions, advices, press releases, circulars, orders, logbooks, contracts, reports, papers, samples, models, data material held in any electronic form |
| **Public Authority** (2(h)) | Any body established/constituted by/under the Constitution; any body owned, controlled or substantially financed by the Government; any NGO substantially financed by Government |
| **Record** (2(i)) | Includes documents, manuscripts, files, microfilms, microfiche, facsimile copies of documents, etc. |
| **Right to Information** (2(j)) | The right to obtain information held by a public authority which is accessible under the Act |
| **State Information Commission (SIC)** (2(k)) | Commission constituted under Section 15 |
| **State Public Information Officer (SPIO)** (2(l)) | Officer designated under Section 5(2) |
| **Third Party** (2(n)) | Any person other than the citizen making the request |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Structural Framework of RTI Act

## 2.1 Salient Features of the Act (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> The following **14 Salient Features** are most frequently asked in KTU examinations as 7-mark and 14-mark questions:

1. **Universal Applicability** — Applies to all Public Authorities (Central, State, Local).
2. **Citizens-only Right** — Only Indian citizens can seek information (Section 3).
3. **Three-Tier Government Mechanism** — Central Information Commission, State Information Commissions, and departmental appellate authorities.
4. **Mandatory Disclosure** — Section 4 mandates suo motu disclosure of 17 categories of information.
5. **Time-Bound Delivery** — Information to be provided within **30 days**, **48 hours** if it concerns life or liberty.
6. **Nominal Fee** — ₹10 application fee + actual cost of information (Section 6 & 7).
7. **Independent Oversight** — Information Commissions function as independent quasi-judicial bodies.
8. **Penalty Provisions** — Personal penalty on PIOs up to **₹25,000** (Section 20).
9. **Appeals Mechanism** — Two-tier appeal: First to Departmental Officer, Second to Information Commission (Section 19).
10. **Partial Disclosure** — Severability clause (Section 10) allows partial disclosure.
11. **Third-Party Protection** — Section 11 protects commercial confidence, trade secrets, etc.
12. **Bilingual Coverage** — Requests can be made in English, Hindi, or the official language of the area.
13. **No Formal Requisition Needed** — Application does not require stating reasons.
14. **Penal Consequences** — Penalty on PIO for refusal, delay, or providing false information.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Framework Table (Cheat Sheet)

| Section | Subject Matter | Key Provision (Mandatory for Exams) |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 3** | Right to Information | All citizens have the right to information subject to the Act |
| **Section 4** | Obligations of Public Authorities | Suo motu disclosure of 17 categories (mandatory) |
| **Section 5** | Designation of PIOs, APIOs, PIOs | Designation mandatory for all Public Authorities |
| **Section 6** | Request for Information | Application in writing/electronic, ₹10 fee |
| **Section 7** | Disposal of Request | Reply within 30 days; 48 hours if life/liberty |
| **Section 8** | Exemption from Disclosure | 10 grounds for rejection (sovereignty, contempt, parliamentary privilege, trade secrets, fiduciary, foreign govt, life/safety, investigation, cabinet papers, personal info) |
| **Section 9** | Grounds for Rejection | Access may be refused if it would involve infringement of copyright |
| **Section 10** | Severability | Part exempt → partial disclosure of rest |
| **Section 11** | Third Party Information | Notice to third party before disclosure |
| **Section 12** | Constitution of CIC | Chief + up to 10 Information Commissioners |
| **Section 13** | Term of Office | 5 years or until 65 years of age (whichever earlier) |
| **Section 14** | Service Conditions | Salary, allowances equivalent to CEC/CAG (Chief); Secretary (others) |
| **Section 15** | Constitution of SIC | Parallel State body |
| **Section 16** | Term of SIC | Same as CIC |
| **Section 17** | Service Conditions of SIC | Same as CIC |
| **Section 18** | Powers & Functions of CIC | Inquiry, recommend, require production |
| **Section 19** | Appeals | First appeal: Departmental Officer; Second: Commission |
| **Section 20** | Penalties | ₹250/day up to ₹25,000; disciplinary recommendation |
| **Section 21** | Action Against CIC/SIC | Only by President/Governor on grounds of misbehaviour |
| **Section 22** | Act to Override Other Laws | Overrides Official Secrets Act, 1923 |
| **Section 23** | No Court Jurisdiction | No civil court has jurisdiction |
| **Section 24** | Exempted Organizations | Intelligence/security bodies in Second Schedule |
| **Section 25** | Monitoring & Reporting | Annual reports tabled in Parliament/Legislature |

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Key Sections

### 2.3.1 Section 3 — Right to Information (The Cornerstone)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 3 — Operative Provision:**
> *Subject to the provisions of this Act, all citizens shall have the right to information.* This right is available only to **citizens of India**. The right is subject to the limitations contained in Sections 8, 9, 10, and 11.

**Key Points:**
- Right is conferred on the citizen, not on non-citizens, corporations, NGOs.
- Right is subject to exemptions (Section 8) and third-party protections (Section 11).
- The State cannot confer the right in a manner inconsistent with the Act.

### 2.3.2 Section 4 — Obligations of Public Authorities (Mandatory Disclosure)

This is the **proactive transparency provision** — every Public Authority must maintain and proactively disclose **17 categories of information**, including:

| S.No | Mandatory Disclosure |
|---|---|
| 1 | Particulars of organization, functions, and duties |
| 2 | Powers and duties of officers and employees |
| 3 | Procedure followed in decision-making |
| 4 | Norms for discharge of functions |
| 5 | Rules, regulations, instructions, manuals used |
| 6 | Statement of categories of documents held |
| 7 | Statement of boards, councils, committees, etc. |
| 8 | Directory of officers and employees |
| 9 | Monthly remuneration of officers and employees |
| 10 | Budget allocated to each agency |
| 11 | Manner of execution of subsidy programs |
| 12 | List of beneficiaries of concessional permits/authorizations |
| 13 | Particulars of recipients of concessions/permits |
| 14 | Information available in electronic form |
| 15 | Facilities for citizens to obtain information |
| 16 | Names, designations, and addresses of PIOs |
| 17 | Other information prescribed by the Government |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "B-S-D-R-P"**: *"Buildings, Staff, Decisions, Rules, Publications"* — use this to recall Section 4 categories.

### 2.3.3 Section 5 — Designation of Officers (Implementation Mechanism)

**3-Tier Officer Structure:**

1. **Public Information Officer (PIO)** — Central point for receiving applications; each Public Authority must designate a PIO in all administrative units/offices.
2. **Assistant Public Information Officer (APIO)** — At sub-divisional level for ease of access.
3. **Departmental Appellate Authority** — Officers senior to PIO to hear first appeals.

### 2.3.4 Section 6 — Making the Request

**Procedural Requirements:**
- Written application (English/Hindi/Official Language).
- Payment of **₹10** as application fee (in cash, DD, money order, or court fee stamp — electronic payment also accepted).
- No need to state reasons.
- Should specify format of information (inspection, copies, certified samples).

### 2.3.5 Section 7 — Disposal of Request

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 30-30-48 Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **30 days** from receipt of request.
> - **30 days** where request is transferred to another public authority.
> - **48 hours** where information concerns **life or liberty** of a person.
> - **5 days** if request received after transfer.
> - **35 days** for third-party information (after notice).

**Rejection Grounds (Section 7(9)):**
- Fee not paid.
- Information is exempted under Sections 8 and 9.
- Request is frivolous or vexatious.

### 2.3.6 Section 8 — Exemptions (10 Grounds)

The information can be withheld if it falls within these 10 categories:

1. **Sovereignty & Integrity of India** — Information disclosure prejudicially affecting sovereignty, security, strategic, scientific, or economic interests.
2. **Infringement of Parliamentary/State Legislature Privileges**.
3. **Breach of Privileged Communication** — Information that would constitute contempt.
4. **Court Records** — Information that would cause contempt of court.
5. **Breach of Parliamentary Privileges**.
6. **Commercial Confidence, Trade Secrets, Intellectual Property** — unless larger public interest warrants disclosure.
7. **Fiduciary Relationships** — information received in confidence from a person.
8. **Foreign Government Information**.
9. **Life and Physical Safety** — disclosure endangering life.
10. **Investigation Records** — would impede prosecution.
11. **Cabinet Papers** — subject to decisions of competent authority.
12. **Personal Information** — unconnected with public activity, invades privacy.

### 2.3.7 Section 19 — Appeals Mechanism (Two-Tier)

```
FIRST APPEAL  →  Departmental Appellate Authority (senior officer)
                   ↓ (30 days)
SECOND APPEAL →  Information Commission (CIC/SIC)
                   ↓ (Decided within 90-180 days)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **No Court Intervention:** Under **Section 23**, no civil court has jurisdiction to entertain any suit regarding the Act. However, the **Supreme Court (Article 136)** and **High Court (Article 226)** retain writ jurisdiction.

### 2.3.8 Section 20 — Penalties and Disciplinary Action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penalty Schedule (Mandatory Memorization):**
> - **₹250 per day** of delay, subject to a maximum of **₹25,000**.
> - Penalty imposed on the **PIO personally**, not the government.
> - Penalty is recovered from the officer's salary.
> - Disciplinary action recommended for willful delays/denials.
> - No penalty if PIO proves the request was processed in good faith.

### 2.3.9 Section 22 — Override Effect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The provisions of this Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in the Official Secrets Act, 1923, and any other law for the time being in force.**

This is the **Single Most Important Provision** — it establishes the supremacy of RTI over the **Official Secrets Act, 1923**.

### 2.3.10 Section 24 — Exempted Organizations

Intelligence and security organizations listed in the Second Schedule are **exempt**, BUT:
- Information about **corruption and human rights violations** IS still accessible.
- Cabinet Secretariat, PMO, PM's residence are NOT exempted.

### 2.4 Comparison: RTI Act vs Official Secrets Act, 1923

| Aspect | RTI Act, 2005 | Official Secrets Act, 1923 |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Era** | Modern (Information Age) | Colonial Era (British Era) |
| **Objective** | Transparency & accountability | Secrecy of government information |
| **Operative Principle** | Disclosure is the norm; secrecy is exception | Secrecy is the default; disclosure is exception |
| **Authority** | Citizen-centric | State-centric |
| **Burden of Proof** | On Public Authority to justify denial | N/A (denial is automatic) |
| **Legal Status Today** | **Supersedes OSA** (Section 22 of RTI) | Effective but subordinate to RTI |
| **Penalty** | On PIO for non-disclosure | On citizen for seeking/obtaining secrets |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of RTI

For B.Tech Engineering students:
- **Project Funding Disclosure** — Students can RTI the breakdown of research grants from DST, AICTE, KTU, etc.
- **Municipal Works Transparency** — Query tender processes for public infrastructure projects.
- **Educational Institution Audit** — Audit fee structures, procurement, faculty appointments in aided institutions.
- **Right to Information in Cyber Laws** — Used to access FIR copies, investigation status of cyber crimes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: How to Answer a 14-Mark RTI Question

> [!NOTE]
> Since RTI Act is a **legal-procedural topic**, this section explains **structured analytical methodology** for answering exam questions, breaking down every section, sub-section, and procedural step-by-step logic.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logic: From Filing RTI to Final Resolution

### STEP 1 — Citizen Identifies Information Need
The citizen (only Indian citizen) identifies that the information sought is **held by a Public Authority** and is **not exempted under Section 8 or 9**.

> [!NOTE]
> *Logic Step:* The right is not absolute — Sections 8/9/10/11 act as filters.

### STEP 2 — Identify the Correct Public Authority
The citizen must direct the request to the **relevant Public Authority** holding the information. If unsure, the citizen can approach the **APIO** of the area.

### STEP 3 — Submit Application under Section 6
The application must contain:
- Name and contact details of the applicant.
- Specific information sought (clear and precise).
- Format of information required (copy, inspection, certified sample).
- Application fee of **₹10**.
- BPI (Below Poverty Line) applicants are **exempted from fees** (Section 7(5)).

### STEP 4 — Receipt & Registration by PIO
The PIO receives the request, registers it, and either:
- **(a)** Processes it directly, OR
- **(b)** Transfers it under Section 6(3) to another public authority within **5 days** if the information is held by another authority.

### STEP 5 — Timeline Evaluation
- **Standard Request:** PIO must respond within **30 days**.
- **Life/Liberty Matters:** PIO must respond within **48 hours**.
- **Third-Party Information:** PIO must issue notice to third party; total time **35 days** (within 40 days).
- **BPL Applicants:** Free of charge.

### STEP 6 — Information Provided or Rejected
- **Provided:** Format as requested (copy, inspection, etc.).
- **Rejected:** PIO must state reasons in writing under **Section 7(9)**, citing exemption.

### STEP 7 — First Appeal (Section 19(1))
If aggrieved, applicant files appeal within **30 days** to the **Departmental Appellate Authority** (senior officer).

### STEP 8 — Second Appeal (Section 19(3))
If still aggrieved, applicant files second appeal within **90 days** to the **Information Commission (CIC/SIC)**.

### STEP 9 — Penalty Imposition (Section 20)
The Commission may impose penalty of ₹250/day (max ₹25,000) on the PIO, after inquiry.

### STEP 10 — Disciplinary Recommendation
If PIO has acted maliciously, Commission can recommend disciplinary action to the competent authority.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Section-Wise Decision Tree

```
CITIZEN WANTS INFORMATION
          │
          ▼
  IS THE HOLDER A PUBLIC AUTHORITY?
          │
   ┌──────┴──────┐
   YES           NO → RTI Not Applicable
   │
   ▼
IS IT EXEMPTED UNDER SECTION 8?
   │
   ┌──────┴──────┐
   YES           NO → INFORMATION DISCLOSED
   │
   ▼
IS IT PERSONAL INFO WITHOUT PUBLIC INTEREST?
   │
   ┌──────┴──────┐
   YES           NO → PUBLIC INTEREST PREVAILS → DISCLOSED
   │
   ▼
PARTIAL EXEMPTION UNDER SECTION 10?
   │
   ┌──────┴──────┐
   YES           NO → FULLY REJECTED
   │
   ▼
SEVERABILITY APPLIED → PARTIAL DISCLOSURE
```

### 3.3 Mathematical Limit Calculations (For Numerical Problems)

While RTI is mostly legal, certain numerical limits appear in KTU questions. These are the **standard constants** of the Act:

$$\text{Application Fee} = ₹10$$

$$\text{Penalty Rate} = ₹250 \text{ per day}$$

$$\text{Maximum Penalty} = ₹25{,}000$$

$$\text{Standard Response Time} = 30 \text{ days}$$

$$\text{Life/Liberty Response Time} = 48 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Third-Party Total Time} = 40 \text{ days (with notice)}$$

$$\text{First Appeal Time Limit} = 30 \text{ days}$$

$$\text{Second Appeal Time Limit} = 90 \text{ days}$$

$$\text{Appeal Disposal Time} = 90 \text{ to } 180 \text{ days}$$

#### 3.3.1 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Style)

> **Problem:** A citizen files an RTI application on 1st January. The PIO delays reply by 50 days. The Information Commission imposes penalty. Calculate the penalty amount and explain whether PIO can challenge it.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

> *Step 1:* Standard response time = 30 days.

> *Step 2:* Delay = 50 days (already includes the standard period).

> *Step 3:* Penalty rate = ₹250/day.

> *Step 4:* 
$$\text{Penalty} = 50 \times ₹250 = ₹12{,}500$$

> *Step 5:* Check maximum cap:
$$₹12{,}500 < ₹25{,}000 \text{ (No cap applied)}$$

> *Step 6:* Penalty is recoverable from PIO's salary.

> *Step 7:* PIO can challenge the penalty order by filing a **review application** before the same Commission within **30 days**.

> **Final Answer:** Penalty = ₹12,500, recoverable from PIO's salary, and reviewable by the Commission.

#### 3.3.2 Advanced Numerical: Life/Liberty Cases

> **Problem:** A journalist files an RTI seeking information about hospital negligence causing death. Calculate the deadline and consequence of 5-day delay by PIO.

**Solution:**

> *Step 1:* Information concerns **life or liberty** → Section 7(1) proviso applies.

> *Step 2:* Deadline = **48 hours**.

> *Step 3:* Delay = 5 days = 120 hours.

> *Step 4:* Penalty = (5 × ₹250) = **₹1,250** only (delay capped at minimal for life/liberty matters).

> *Step 5:* PIO also faces disciplinary action for endangering life.

---

### 3.4 The Section-Wise Procedural Flowchart (Textual Form)

```
Section 6  →  Section 7  →  Section 8/9/10  →  Section 11
(REQUEST)    (PROCESSING)  (EXEMPTIONS)        (THIRD PARTY)
              (30 days)      (Filter Apply)       (Notice 5+30)
                              │
                              ▼
                          Section 19
                         (APPEAL - 2 Tiers)
                              │
                              ▼
                          Section 20
                         (PENALTY)
                              │
                              ▼
                          Section 21/22
                      (OVERSIGHT & OVERRIDE)
```

### 3.5 The Hierarchy of Officers (Important for KTU Diagram Questions)

```
                    CITIZEN
                       │
                       ▼
              ASSISTANT PIO (APIO)
                  (Sub-divisional level)
                       │
                       ▼
              PUBLIC INFORMATION OFFICER (PIO)
              (Departmental level)
                       │
                       ▼
          FIRST APPELLATE AUTHORITY
          (Senior Officer of the Department)
                       │
                       ▼
           INFORMATION COMMISSION
             (CIC / SIC)
                       │
                       ▼
            HIGH COURT (Article 226)
        SUPREME COURT (Article 136)
```

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTI Act — Complete Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B -- No --> B1[Transfer to Correct Authority Section 6 3 - 5 days]

    B -- Yes --> C{Exempted Under Section 8 9?}

    C -- No --> E{Third Party Involved Section 11?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Issue Notice to Third Party - 5 Days]
    F --> G[Third Party Reply - 30 Days]
    G --> H{Severability Possible Section 10?}
    E -- No --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Partial Disclosure]
    H -- No --> J[Full Disclosure Section 7]
    I --> K[Provide Information]
    J --> K

    R --> P
    S --> P
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1: Define "Public Authority" under the RTI Act, 2005. **[3 Marks]**
**Model Answer (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> **Definition (Section 2(h)):** Public Authority means:
> - (i) Any authority, body, or institution of self-government established/constituted by/under the Constitution;
> - (ii) Any other body owned, controlled, or substantially financed by the Government;
> - (iii) Any non-Government organization substantially financed directly or indirectly by the Government.
>
> **Examples:** Municipalities, Panchayats, Universities receiving Government grants, LIC, ONGC, BSNL, etc.

### Question 2: What is the time limit for providing information under Section 7? **[3 Marks]**
**Model Answer:**
> Under Section 7, the Public Information Officer (PIO) is bound to provide information:
> - **30 days** from the receipt of request (general rule);
> - **48 hours** if the information concerns the **life or liberty** of any person;
> - **35 days** if the request involves third-party information (after notice);
> - **5 days** for transfer of request to another public authority.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: **Choice 1** — Right to Information Act, 2005 — Comprehensive Analysis

**[14 Marks]** *Split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks*

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Q: Discuss the salient features and objectives of the Right to Information Act, 2005. Explain how Section 4 enforces mandatory disclosure.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The RTI Act, 2005 enacted on 15th June 2005 and brought into force on 12th October 2005 is a landmark legislation empowering Indian citizens to access information held by Public Authorities. It operationalizes the fundamental right to information implicit in **Article 19(1)(a)** of the Constitution.

**Objectives (2 marks):**
1. To promote transparency and accountability in governance.
2. To contain corruption and promote good governance.
3. To secure citizens' right to information as a part of participatory democracy.

**Salient Features (2 marks):**
- Applicable to all Public Authorities (Central/State).
- Citizens-only right (Section 3).
- Three-tier institutional mechanism (PIO, Departmental Appellate Authority, CIC/SIC).
- Mandatory disclosure (Section 4).
- Time-bound delivery (30 days; 48 hours for life/liberty).
- Penalty for non-compliance (Section 20).
- Override effect over Official Secrets Act, 1923 (Section 22).
- Bilingual application, BPL fee exemption.

**Section 4 — Mandatory Disclosure (2 marks):**
Section 4 mandates every Public Authority to proactively disclose **17 categories** of information, including:
- Particulars of organization, functions, and duties (Section 4(1)(b)(i)).
- Powers and duties of officers (Section 4(1)(b)(ii)).
- Procedure for decision-making (Section 4(1)(b)(iii)).
- Norms for discharge of functions.
- Rules, regulations, and manuals used.
- Statement of categories of documents held.
- Directory of officers and employees.
- Monthly remuneration of officers/employees.
- Budget allocation for each agency.
- Manner of execution of subsidy programs.
- List of beneficiaries of concessional permits.
- Particulars of recipients of concessions.
- Information in electronic form.
- Facilities for citizen information.
- Names, designations, addresses of PIOs/APIOs.

This proactive disclosure reduces the need for citizens to file RTIs for routine information, thereby reducing administrative burden.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Q: Explain the structure, powers, and functions of the Central Information Commission (CIC) and State Information Commissions (SICs). Discuss the penalty mechanism under Section 20.**

**Model Answer:**

**Constitution of CIC (Section 12) — 2 marks:**
- Constituted by the Central Government through a Gazette notification.
- Comprises a **Chief Information Commissioner (CIC)** and **up to 10 Information Commissioners (ICs)**.
- Appointed by the **President of India** on the recommendation of a committee consisting of:
  - The Prime Minister (Chairperson)
  - The Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha
  - A Union Minister nominated by the Prime Minister.

**Constitution of SIC (Section 15) — 1 mark:**
- Constituted by the State Government.
- Comprises a **State Chief Information Commissioner (SCIC)** and **up to 10 State Information Commissioners (SICs)**.
- Appointed by the **Governor** on the recommendation of a similar committee chaired by the Chief Minister.

**Term and Service Conditions (Sections 13, 14, 16, 17) — 1 mark:**
- Term: **5 years** from the date of assuming office OR until **65 years of age**, whichever is earlier.
- Salary: Equivalent to that of Chief Election Commissioner (for Chief); Secretary to Government (for others).

**Powers and Functions (Section 18) — 2 marks:**
- Receive and inquire into complaints from any person.
- Issue directions to Public Authorities.
- Order disclosure of information.
- Impose penalties and recommend disciplinary action.
- Require production of records.
- Recommend steps for compliance.
- Submit annual reports to Parliament/State Legislature.

**Penalty Mechanism (Section 20) — 1 mark:**
- **₹250 per day** of delay.
- Maximum: **₹25,000**.
- Penalty recovered from PIO's salary.
- Review possible within **30 days** before the Commission.
- Disciplinary action against PIO for willful delays/denials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting BPL exemption** — BPL applicants are exempt from all fees under Section 7(5). Many students miss this.
> 2. **Confusing CIC with SIC appointment authority** — CIC is appointed by the **President**; SIC by the **Governor**. Marks are deducted if swapped.
> 3. **Missing the override clause (Section 22)** — Section 22 overrides the **Official Secrets Act, 1923**, but NOT the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** or the **Bankers' Books Evidence Act**. Read the section carefully.
> 4. **Time limits confusion** — 30 days standard; 48 hours life/liberty; 35+5=40 days third party; 30 days first appeal; 90 days second appeal. Memorize these precisely.
> 5. **Third Party Protection** — Under Section 11, third party must be given notice and opportunity to be heard BEFORE disclosure. Many students skip this.

---

### Question B: **Choice 2** — Alternate 14-Mark Question

**[14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Q: Define "Information" and "Right to Information" under Section 2 of the RTI Act, 2005. Explain the exemptions under Section 8 with appropriate illustrations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Information (Section 2(f)) — 2 marks:**
"Information" means any material in any form, including:
- Records, documents, memos, e-mails, opinions, advices, press releases, circulars, orders, logbooks, contracts, reports, papers, samples, models.
- Data material held in any electronic form.
- Information relating to any private body which can be accessed by a Public Authority under any other law.

**Definition of Right to Information (Section 2(j)) — 1 mark:**
"Right to Information" means the right to information accessible under this Act which is held by or under the control of any Public Authority and includes the right to:
- Inspection of work, documents, records.
- Taking notes, extracts, or certified copies.
- Taking certified samples of materials.
- Obtaining information in the form of diskettes, floppies, tapes, video cassettes, or in any other electronic mode.

**Section 8 — Exemptions (4 marks):**

| Section | Exemption | Illustration |
|---|---|---|
| 8(1)(a) | Sovereignty & Integrity of India | Defence deployment locations |
| 8(1)(b) | Information forbidden by any court/tribunal | Sub judice matters |
| 8(1)(c) | Parliamentary/State Legislature privilege | Legislative proceedings |
| 8(1)(d) | Commercial confidence, trade secrets, IP | Coca-Cola's secret formula |
| 8(1)(e) | Fiduciary relationship | Doctor-patient privileged communication |
| 8(1)(f) | Foreign Government information | Diplomatic correspondence |
| 8(1)(g) | Life and physical safety | Identity of rape victim |
| 8(1)(h) | Investigation/prosecution records | Identity of confidential informant |
| 8(1)(i) | Cabinet papers (subject to competent authority) | Cabinet notes before decision |
| 8(1)(j) | Personal information unrelated to public activity | Medical history of a citizen |

> **Important:** Exemptions under 8(1)(j) — personal information — is **subject to larger public interest**. If disclosure serves larger public interest, personal information may be disclosed.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Q: Discuss the appeal mechanism under Section 19. How does the penalty provision under Section 20 act as a deterrent against non-compliance? Illustrate with case laws if applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

**Two-Tier Appeal Structure (3 marks):**

**First Appeal — Section 19(1):**
- Filed before the **Departmental Appellate Authority** (officer senior in rank to the PIO).
- Time limit: Within **30 days** from the date of PIO's order.
- Disposal: Within **30 days** or such extended period (max 45 days total).

**Second Appeal — Section 19(3):**
- Filed before the **Information Commission (CIC/SIC)**.
- Time limit: Within **90 days** from the date of first appellate order.
- Disposal: Commission may dispose of appeal within **90 to 180 days**.

**Section 20 — Penalty Mechanism (3 marks):**

The penalty provision is the **cornerstone of enforcement** under the Act. Key features:

1. **Penalty Rate:** ₹250 per day of delay, subject to a maximum of ₹25,000.
2. **Recovery:** Recovered from the **PIO's personal salary** — not from the public authority.
3. **Inquiry:** The Commission holds an inquiry before imposing penalty.
4. **Mitigation:** No penalty if PIO proves he acted in good faith.
5. **Review:** PIO can apply for review of penalty order within **30 days** before the Commission.
6. **Disciplinary Recommendation:** Commission can recommend disciplinary action for willful non-compliance.

**Deterrent Effect (1 mark):**
The personal liability of PIO makes the Act a powerful tool because:
- Officers cannot hide behind departmental immunity.
- The financial penalty directly impacts the erring officer.
- Disciplinary action threat ensures institutional compliance.

**Jurisdictional Note:** No civil court has jurisdiction (Section 23); only writ jurisdiction under Articles 226/136 applies.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

### **A. KEY SECTIONS TO MEMORIZE**
- **Section 3** — Right to Information (citizens only).
- **Section 4** — Mandatory disclosure (17 categories).
- **Section 5** — Designation of PIO, APIO, Appellate Authority.
- **Section 6** — Application procedure + ₹10 fee.
- **Section 7** — 30 days, 48 hours life/liberty, 40 days third-party.
- **Section 8** — 10+ grounds of exemption.
- **Section 9** — Copyright infringement as ground for refusal.
- **Section 10** — Severability (partial disclosure).
- **Section 11** — Third-party information (notice mandatory).
- **Section 12/15** — Constitution of CIC/SIC.
- **Section 13/16** — Term = 5 years OR 65 years.
- **Section 18** — Powers of Commission.
- **Section 19** — Two-tier appeals.
- **Section 20** — Penalty = ₹250/day, max ₹25,000.
- **Section 22** — Override Official Secrets Act, 1923.
- **Section 23** — No civil court jurisdiction.
- **Section 24** — Exempted security/intelligence bodies.

### **B. KEY NUMBERS (MUST MEMORIZE)**
- Application Fee: **₹10**
- Penalty Rate: **₹250/day**
- Maximum Penalty: **₹25,000**
- Standard Response: **30 days**
- Life/Liberty: **48 hours**
- Third-Party Total: **40 days**
- First Appeal Period: **30 days**
- Second Appeal Period: **90 days**
- Term of CIC/SIC: **5 years or 65 years age**
- Information Commissioners per Commission: **10 maximum**
- CIC Salary Equivalent: **Chief Election Commissioner**
- Other IC Salary: **Secretary to Government**

### **C. KEY DEFINITIONS**
- Information (Section 2(f))
- Right to Information (Section 2(j))
- Public Authority (Section 2(h))
- Third Party (Section 2(n))
- PIO (Section 2(c))
- APIO (Section 5)

### **D. CONSTITUTIONAL LINKAGE**
- **Article 19(1)(a)** — Freedom of Speech & Expression includes Right to Know.
- **Article 226** — High Court Writ Jurisdiction.
- **Article 136** — Supreme Court Special Leave Petition.

### **E. IMPORTANT CASE LAWS**
- *Raj Narain v. Indira Gandhi (1975)* — Right to know is fundamental.
- *S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (1982)* — Right to information emphasized.
- *Indian Express Newspapers v. Union of India (1985)* — Free flow of information vital.

### **F. COMMON KTU EXAM QUESTIONS**
1. *"Explain the salient features of RTI Act 2005."* (10 marks / 14 marks)
2. *"Discuss Section 4 obligations."* (7 marks)
3. *"Explain penalty mechanism under Section 20."* (7 marks)
4. *"Compare RTI Act with Official Secrets Act."* (7 marks / 14 marks)
5. *"Discuss appeal mechanism under Section 19."* (7 marks)
6. *"Explain exemptions under Section 8."* (7 marks)
7. *"Discuss powers and functions of CIC/SIC."* (7 marks)
8. *"Explain the override effect of Section 22."* (3 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GOLDEN RULE FOR KTU ANSWERS:**
> Always begin with **Section reference + Brief Explanation + Example + Significance**. This 4-part structure fetches maximum marks and shows examiner your conceptual clarity.

topics.title: IT Act 2008 and its Amendments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IT Act 2008 and its Amendments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Information Technology Act, 2000** (as amended by the **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008**) is the primary cyber-law of India that provides the legal framework for **e-commerce, e-governance, data privacy, digital signatures, cybercrime, and cybersecurity**. It is modeled largely on the **UNCITRAL Model Law on Electronic Commerce (1996)** and was the second legislation in the world (after Singapore) to address e-commerce and digital transactions.

The **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008** is a comprehensive overhaul of the parent Act, which:
- Redefined several offenses (Sections 66A to 66F)
- Introduced **new cyber-crimes** like identity theft, cheating by personation, publishing obscene/sexually explicit material, and cyber-terrorism
- Strengthened the role of the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**
- Expanded the jurisdiction and powers of **Adjudicating Officers** and the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CyAT)**
- Made **intermediary liability** more stringent (Section 79)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students must be able to critically evaluate the **salient features of the IT Act 2008, the rationale behind the 2008 amendments, the digital signature & PKI framework, offences & penalties (Sections 43, 65, 66–66F, 67–67B, 69, 79, 80), and the structure of adjudication.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Traffic Police"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "Highway Code of the Internet":**
> Imagine the Internet as a vast **national highway** with billions of vehicles (data packets) zooming around. Before 2000, there were **no traffic rules** — anyone could cause a "digital accident" (a cybercrime) and walk away. The **IT Act, 2000** was the **first Highway Code** of this digital road — it set basic rules, gave vehicles a unique **license plate (Digital Signature Certificate)**, and appointed a **traffic police (Adjudicating Officer)**. The **2008 Amendment** was a **major upgrade** of this Highway Code — stricter speed limits (penalties), new types of vehicles recognized (e-records, e-governance), a **crime-investigation branch (Cyber-Crime Cells)**, and even an **anti-terror squad for the digital highway (Section 66F – Cyber Terrorism)**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Digital Signature** — A mathematical scheme for verifying the authenticity of digital messages/documents (equivalent to a hand-written signature under Section 3).
- **Asymmetric Crypto-system** — A pair of unique keys (Public Key + Private Key) used to encrypt/decrypt data.
- **Electronic Record** — Any data, record or data generated, image or sound stored, received or sent in an electronic form (Section 2(1)(t)).
- **Intermediary** — Any person who on behalf of another person receives, stores or transmits an electronic record (Section 2(1)(w)).
- **Computer** — Any electronic, magnetic, optical or other high-speed data processing device performing logical, arithmetic or memory functions (Section 2(1)(i)).
- **Cyber Café** — Any establishment from where access to the internet is offered for use to members of the public.
- **Secure System** — A system that has the prescribed safeguards for ensuring integrity, confidentiality and authenticity of electronic records (Section 2(1)(ze)).
- **PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)** — The end-to-end framework of hardware, software, policies and procedures used to issue, manage and revoke **Digital Signature Certificates (DSC)**.
- **Adjudicating Officer** — An officer appointed under Section 46 to adjudicate claims for compensation up to **₹25 crore** (raised from ₹5 crore in 2008).
- **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** — The apex authority under Section 17 who licenses and regulates Certifying Authorities.
- **Certifying Authority (CA)** — A person who has been granted a license to issue Digital Signature Certificates (Section 24).
- **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CyAT)** — A quasi-judicial body under Section 48 that hears appeals against orders of the Adjudicating Officer or CCA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants / Standards to Remember (for KTU 2024):**
> - Threshold for compensation claim: **Up to ₹25 crore** (post-2008 amendment).
> - **SHA-1 / SHA-2 hash algorithms** were the prescribed standards for digital signatures (now upgraded to SHA-256).
> - Penalties are typically **ad valorem** — up to **₹1 crore** or more for certain offenses (e.g., Section 44A — up to **₹10 lakh** for failure to furnish documents).
> - Cyber Appellate Tribunal was abolished in 2017 and its powers were transferred to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement & Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)** via the Finance Act 2017. However, the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal Act, 2000** was re-enacted by the **IT (Intermediary Guidelines & Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021** and the **Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Why Was the IT Act, 2000 Enacted? (The Genesis)

The IT Act 2000 was enacted in response to the **UNCITRAL Model Law** to:
1. **Legalize e-commerce** — give legal recognition to electronic records and digital signatures.
2. **Enable e-filing** — allow Government records to be maintained in electronic form.
3. **Prevent cybercrime** — penalize unauthorized access, damage to computer systems, and breach of confidentiality.
4. **Promote the IT industry** — make India a global hub for BPO and IT services.
5. **Facilitate the digital economy** — provide a uniform legal framework across India (replacing the Indian Penal Code, 1860 for digital acts).

### 2.2 Why the 2008 Amendment Was Needed

By 2006–2007, the IT Act was deemed inadequate because:
- It did not cover **new forms of cyber-crime** like phishing, identity theft, and obscenity.
- It lacked provisions for **cyber-terrorism**, which emerged as a serious threat (post-26/11 Mumbai attacks in 2008).
- It did not protect **sensitive personal data** explicitly.
- It was **technology-specific** (focused on digital signatures) instead of technology-neutral.
- The penalty cap (₹5 crore) and term of imprisonment were considered insufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-amendment → Post-amendment Mapping (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - Digital Signature → **Electronic Signature** (technology-neutral).
> - Section 43 (Damages) → **Section 43 expanded** with sub-sections 43A (Compensation for failure to protect data) and 43B (Punishment for offences in the name of a body corporate).
> - Section 65 (Tampering) → **Renumbered and strengthened**.
> - New **Section 66** (Computer-related offences) and **66A–66F** introduced.
> - **Section 67, 67A, 67B** introduced for obscenity.
> - **Section 69** strengthened with **interception, monitoring and decryption** powers.
> - **Section 79 (Intermediary liability)** — "safe harbour" introduced.

> The following is a **high-yield reference table**. Use the symbol `\vert` or `\mid` (not the bare pipe) wherever absolute value is needed.

| 65 | Tampering with computer source documents | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹2 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable | High |
| 66 | Computer-related offences (hacking with intent) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹5 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable | Very High |
| 66A | Sending offensive messages through communication service, etc. | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine | Cognizable, Bailable | **Struck down — Shreya Singhal v. UoI (2015)** |
| 66B | Dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource / communication | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both | Cognizable, Bailable | High |

| 67A | Publishing sexually explicit material | 1st: up to **5 years** + fine up to **₹10 lakh**; 2nd: up to **7 years** + fine up to **₹20 lakh** | Cognizable, Non-bailable | High |
