The world's first codified patent law, enacted in the Republic of Venice. It established three foundational principles:
- **Novelty** — the invention must be new.
- **Limited Term** — exclusive rights granted for 10 years.
- **Disclosure** — the inventor must publicly reveal the working of the invention.

**3. The Statute of Monopolies, 1624 (England):**
Passed by the English Parliament to curb the abuse of royal monopoly grants. It abolished all monopolies **except** those granted to the *"true and first inventor"* of any new manufacture. This became the foundation of the common-law patent system.

**4. Constitutional Embedding — USA, 1787:**
Article I, Section 8, Clause 8 of the U.S. Constitution (the **IP Clause**) empowered Congress *"to promote the progress of science and useful arts, by securing for limited times to authors and inventors the exclusive right to their respective writings and discoveries."* This is the only constitutional IP clause in the world.

**5. The Paris Convention, 1883:**
Established the international framework for **industrial property** (patents, trademarks, industrial designs). It introduced:
- **National treatment** — foreign applicants treated as locals.
- **Right of priority** — 12-month window to file in other member states using the original filing date.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Origin and Evolution of Copyright Law*

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Printing Press Crisis:**
The invention of the printing press (Gutenberg, 1440) created a need for legal protection of printed works. Without copyright, printers could reproduce books without compensating authors.

**2. The Stationers' Company Monopoly:**
In England, the Stationers' Company (a guild of printers) held a royal charter granting them perpetual printing rights — suppressing both authors' earnings and public access.

**3. The Statute of Anne, 1710:**
The world's first copyright statute, which:
- Granted authors **14 years** of exclusive rights (renewable for 14 years).
- Explicitly aimed at *"the encouragement of learning"* — establishing the utilitarian rationale for copyright.
- Vested copyright in the **author**, not the printer.

**4. Philosophical Foundations:**
- **Locke's Labour Theory** — creators have a natural right to the fruits of their labour.
- **Hegel's Personality Theory** — creative works embody the creator's identity.

**5. The Berne Convention, 1886:**
Established the international copyright framework with three core principles:
- **Automatic protection** — no formalities required.
- **National treatment** — foreign authors treated as domestic.
- **Minimum term** — life of the author plus 50 years.

**6. Modern Harmonization — TRIPS, 1994:**
The TRIPS Agreement under the WTO imposed **minimum global standards** for IP protection across all member states, including India (1995 onward).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`**

> *"Explain the origin and significance of the Paris Convention 1883 and the Berne Convention 1886. How did these two conventions shape the modern international intellectual property framework?"*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *Paris Convention, 1883 — Industrial Property*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Background:**
By the late 19th century, international trade and industrialization created an urgent need for **uniform protection** of patents, trademarks, and industrial designs across borders. National laws alone were insufficient.

**2. Adoption:**
The **Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property** was signed on **March 20, 1883**, in Paris. Original signatories included Belgium, Brazil, France, Guatemala, Italy, Netherlands, Portugal, El Salvador, Serbia, Spain, and Switzerland.

**3. Core Principles:**
- **National Treatment (Article 2):** Each member state must grant the same IP protection to foreign nationals as it does to its own citizens.
- **Right of Priority (Article 4):** An applicant filing in one member state has **12 months** to file in other member states while preserving the original filing date.
- **Independence of Patents (Article 4bis):** Patents obtained in different countries are independent of one another.
- **Common Rules:** Minimum standards for patents (including compulsory licensing in cases of abuse).

**4. Significance:**
- Created the world's first **multilateral IP treaty**.
- Laid the foundation for **WIPO** and later **TRIPS**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Berne Convention, 1886 — Copyright*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Background:**
The Berne Convention was driven by the need to protect literary and artistic works across national borders, particularly against unauthorized reproduction in foreign countries.

**2. Adoption:**
The **Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works** was signed on **September 9, 1886**, in Berne, Switzerland.

**3. Core Principles:**
- **Automatic Protection (Article 5):** Copyright protection arises **automatically** upon creation of the work — no registration or formalities required.
- **National Treatment (Article 5):** Foreign authors must be treated as domestic authors.
- **Minimum Term (Article 7):** Protection for the **life of the author plus 50 years**.
- **Independent Protection (Article 2):** Copyright protection is independent of the existence of protection in the country of origin.

**4. Significance:**
- Established the modern international copyright regime.
- Influenced the **Universal Copyright Convention (1952)** and **TRIPS (1994)**.
- India acceded to the Berne Convention in **1928** — one of the earliest non-European signatories.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Marks Deduction in "Origin" Questions:**
>
> 🔴 **Pitfall 1:** Writing *"copyright originated in the USA"* — WRONG. The USA's first copyright law was enacted in 1790, **61 years AFTER** England's Statute of Anne (1710). The Berne Convention is **Berne, Switzerland**, not Berlin.
>
> 🔴 **Pitfall 2:** Confusing the **Paris Convention (1883)** with the **Paris Agreement on Climate Change**. They are completely unrelated treaties.
>
> 🔴 **Pitfall 3:** Writing the Venetian Statute year as *"1472"* or *"1480"* — exact year memorization is mandatory.
>
> 🔴 **Pitfall 4:** Forgetting to mention **philosophical foundations** (Locke, Hegel). Examiners award bonus marks for citing the theoretical rationale.
>
> 🔴 **Pitfall 5:** Not linking historical origins to **modern Indian statutes** (Patents Act 1970, Copyright Act 1957, Trademarks Act 1999) — examiners expect a "from-origin-to-India" analytical flow.
>
> 🟢 **Bonus Tip:** Always end a 14-mark Origin question with a **concluding statement** linking historical developments to **TRIPS 1994** and India's compliance framework. This signals "exam-readiness" to the evaluator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Origin of IPR = Historical + Philosophical + Legal Evolution** of protection for intangible creations.

✅ **Three Philosophical Pillars:**
- Utilitarian (encourage innovation)
- Natural Rights (Locke)
- Personality Theory (Hegel)

✅ **Three "First" Statutes Every KTU Student Must Know:**
- **1474** — Venetian Statute (first patent law)
- **1624** — Statute of Monopolies (English patent foundations)
- **1710** — Statute of Anne (first copyright law)

✅ **Three International Cornerstones:**
- **Paris Convention (1883)** — Industrial Property
- **Berne Convention (1886)** — Copyright
- **TRIPS Agreement (1994)** — Global IP Standards

✅ **Key Term Lengths to Memorize:**
- Venetian patent = **10 years**
- Statute of Anne copyright = **14 years** (renewable once)

✅ **Three Generations of IP Origin:**
- Gen 1 (1474–1882): National statutes
- Gen 2 (1883–1993): International conventions
- Gen 3 (1994–Present): TRIPS-driven global standards

✅ **India's IP Origin Highlights:**
- First Indian copyright-like law: **Act VI of 1856**
- Berne Convention accession: **1928**
- TRIPS compliance: **1995**
- Modern statutes: Patents 1970, Copyright 1957, Trademarks 1999

✅ **Examiner's Favourite Quote:** *"An Act for the Encouragement of Learning"* — Title of Statute of Anne, 1710.

✅ **Universal Law of Origin:** *Every modern IP right traces its lineage to one of three sources — Venice (1474), London (1624/1710), or Paris (1883).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Copyright" in the KTU PECCT861 Context?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECCT861 – IPR and Cyber Laws**, **Module 4** focuses on **Copyright and Related Rights**. The opening topic — *"Nature of Copyright"* — establishes the **philosophical, statutory, and economic DNA** of copyright protection. Without understanding its *nature*, a student cannot analyse infringement, digital rights management, or the conflict between creators and users in cyberspace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Copyright Act, 1957, India, Section 13 read with Section 14):**
> *Copyright* is a **bundle of exclusive statutory rights** granted to the creator of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, cinematograph films, and sound recordings, to reproduce, communicate, publish, perform, and adapt the work, for a **limited term**, in exchange for public disclosure of the work.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Notebook" Intuition

Imagine you are a student who writes beautiful poetry in a personal notebook. Nobody else can copy, sell, or publish your poems **without your permission** — that is the **economic right**.

Now imagine your school publishes your poem in a yearbook without even crediting you. You feel *hurt* — your name was erased. That feeling is the **moral right**.

> Copyright protects **both** your pocket (economic) **and** your pride (moral) — but **only for a limited time**, after which the work becomes part of the public domain for all humanity to use.

## 1.3 Why Study the *Nature* of Copyright?

The word *"Nature"* in KTU Module 4 is not casual — it is an **exam-favourite phrase** (often appearing as: *"Discuss the nature of copyright"* — usually 7 or 14 marks). The "nature" of copyright refers to its **inherent characteristics**, which distinguish it from other forms of intellectual property (patents, trademarks, designs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept Cluster:**
> The examiner expects every student to clearly explain these **6 pillars of the nature of copyright**:
> 1. **Statutory Right** (not natural — created by the Copyright Act, 1957)
> 2. **Territorial Right** (limited to the country granting it)
> 3. **Negative / Prohibitory Right** (right to *prevent* others, not positive right to use)
> 4. **Monopoly Right** (exclusive to the author/owner)
> 5. **Ephemeral / Limited Term Right** (time-bound: generally **Life + 60 years** in India)
> 6. **Dual Right** (Economic Rights + Moral Rights)

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Copyright Law

Although this is a legal subject, KTU 2024 scheme expects familiarity with statutory numbers. The most critical figures are:

> **[!NOTE]**
> - **Term of Copyright (India, post-2012 amendment):**
>   - Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic Works: **Life of the author + 60 years**
>   - Cinematograph Films, Sound Recordings, Photographs, Government Works: **60 years from date of publication**
>   - Anonymous / Pseudonymous Works: **60 years from date of publication**
> - **Threshold of Originality:** India follows the **"modicum of creativity"** test (post *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak*, 2008).
> - **First Ownership Rule:** Default owner = the **author** (Section 17, Copyright Act, 1957).

---

## 1.5 Syllabus Highlight Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — Expected Coverage (from 2024 Scheme syllabus):**
> "Copyright — Nature, scope, subject-matter, rights of copyright owner, term of protection, infringement, exceptions, assignment, licensing, copyright in digital environment."
>
> The "Nature" topic is the **gateway** — it is referenced in almost every Part B question of Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of the Nature of Copyright — Structured Analysis

### Pillar 1 — **Copyright is a Statutory Right**

- Copyright **does not exist in common law**; it is **created by legislation**.
- In India → **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended, most recently by the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012).
- In the UK → CDPA 1988; in the USA → 17 U.S.C.; in EU → Directive 2001/29/EC.
- **Consequence:** No copyright = no protection. The act must be **complied with** (e.g., registration is *not* mandatory in India but is *admissible* as evidence under Section 48).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** A software developer writing code in India automatically gets copyright the moment the code is fixed in a medium — but cannot sue in a US court unless Indian law is reciprocally enforced, or US copyright attaches independently under US law.

### Pillar 2 — **Copyright is a Territorial Right**

- Copyright protection is **limited to the geographic borders** of the country granting it.
- A work copyrighted in India has **no automatic protection** in Japan, USA, or Germany.
- International conventions (Berne Convention 1886, TRIPS 1994, WCT 1996) provide **reciprocity** — but only for nationals of member countries.

$$ \text{Protection Scope} = \text{National Law} \cap \text{International Treaty Obligations} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A question framed as *"Copyright is a territorial right. Explain."* (3 marks) expects:
> 1. Definition of territoriality
> 2. One example (e.g., Indian author cannot sue in US for Indian copyright)
> 3. Mention of Berne Convention and TRIPS as bridging mechanisms
> 4. Conclusion linking to cyberspace challenges (where borders are blurred)

### Pillar 3 — **Copyright is a Negative (Prohibitory) Right**

- The author does **not** have a positive right *to use* the work — they have a right to **stop others** from using it without permission.
- This is in contrast to **patents** (which give a positive right to *work* the invention).

$$ \text{Copyright Owner Rights} = \{\text{Reproduction}, \text{Distribution}, \text{Public Performance}, \text{Communication to Public}, \text{Adaptation}\} $$

Each of these is a **prohibition** against others, not a positive liberty of use.

### Pillar 4 — **Copyright is a Monopoly Right (Limited Monopoly)**

- For the term of protection, the copyright owner has an **exclusive monopoly** to commercially exploit the work.
- This is justified under the **utilitarian theory** (rewarding creators → incentivising creation → public benefit).
- **Bounded by:** Fair dealing (Section 52), compulsory licensing (Sections 31–32B), and the public domain.

### Pillar 5 — **Copyright is a Time-Limited Right**

- Once the term expires, the work enters the **public domain** — free for all to use.
- India follows the **Berne Convention standard** (life + 50 years minimum; India grants **life + 60 years**).

| Type of Work | Term of Copyright (India) |
|---|---|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic (by identified author) | Life of author + 60 years |
| Anonymous / Pseudonymous works | 60 years from date of publication |
| Cinematograph films | 60 years from date of publication |
| Sound recordings | 60 years from date of publication |
| Photographs | 60 years from date of publication |
| Government works | 60 years from date of publication |
| Works of international organisations | 60 years from date of publication |
| Posthumous / unpublished works | 60 years from date of publication |
| Works of joint authorship (compute via joint author with longest life) | Joint authors' lives + 60 years |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the pipe `|` character inside table cells** in your exam scripts — KTU uses LaTeX-rendered PDFs and the pipe breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if you must write `|x|`.

### Pillar 6 — **Copyright is a Dual Right — Economic + Moral**

- **Economic rights** = right to earn money (reproduce, distribute, perform, communicate, adapt).
- **Moral rights** = right of paternity (attribution) + right of integrity (non-distortion) — codified in **Section 57 of the Copyright Act, 1957** (added by the 1994 amendment, based on Article 6bis of the Berne Convention).

> Moral rights in India are:
> 1. **Independent** of economic rights (survive even after assignment)
> 2. **Inalienable** (cannot be transferred, but can be waived in writing)
> 3. **Perpetual** for the original author

$$ \text{Total Copyright} = \underbrace{\text{Economic Rights}}_{\text{Transferable, time-limited}} + \underbrace{\text{Moral Rights}}_{\text{Inalienable, perpetual to author}} $$

## 2.2 Theories Justifying Copyright (Philosophical "Why")

| Theory | Core Idea | Proponent / Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rights Theory** | Creator has a *natural* property right over their creation | John Locke (1690) — *Two Treatises of Government* |
| **Utilitarian / Incentive Theory** | Copyright exists to *maximise social welfare* by incentivising creation | Jeremy Bentham, Edward Landes, William Landes |
| **Personality Theory** | Work is an extension of the author's *personality* | Hegel, Kant |
| **Social Planning Theory** | Copyright is a tool of social engineering | Statute-driven (modern) |
| **Labour Theory** | Creator deserves reward for labour invested | Locke (foundational) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** For a 14-mark question, examiners love asking: *"Copyright is a balance between the rights of creators and the rights of society. Discuss with reference to the nature of copyright."* Use the **utilitarian theory** as the dominant justification and contrast with the **natural rights** view.

## 2.3 The "Subject-Matter" Boundary of Copyright

Copyright does **not** protect *ideas* — it protects the **expression of ideas**. This is the **Idea–Expression Dichotomy** (codified indirectly via the Copyright Act, 1957 and reinforced by *International News Service v. Associated Press*, 1918, and *Baker v. Selden*, 101 U.S. 99, 1879).

$$ \text{Work} = \underbrace{\text{Ideas}}_{\text{Not protected}} + \underbrace{\text{Expression}}_{\text{Protected by copyright}} $$

> **KTU Quick Quote (use in exams):** *"Copyright protects the expression, not the idea; the wheel of progress rolls on ideas, the cart of commerce runs on expression."*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a B.Tech student, understanding the *nature* of copyright is critical in:

1. **Software Development** — Every line of code is copyrighted (India explicitly protects "computer programs" under Section 2(o), Copyright Act, 1957).
2. **Open Source Compliance** — GPL, MIT, Apache licenses rely on copyright's negative right mechanism to enforce terms.
3. **AI/ML Training** — The 2023–2026 *NYT v. OpenAI*, *Getty v. Stability AI* cases hinge on whether training data is "fair use" or copyright infringement.
4. **Cybersecurity** — DMCA takedowns, anti-circumvention (Section 65A, Indian IT Act) operate on copyright's territorial nature.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> **Convention:** All mathematical/legal formulae for Module 4 are abstracted below. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Source / Section |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Term of Copyright (literary) | $\text{Term} = \text{Life of author} + 60 \text{ years}$ | S. 22, Copyright Act |
| 2 | Term of Copyright (film/recording) | $\text{Term} = 60 \text{ years from publication}$ | S. 25, 26, 27 |
| 3 | First Ownership | $\text{Owner} = \text{Author (default)}$ | S. 17 |
| 4 | Economic Rights (the 5) | $\{\text{Reproduce}, \text{Distribute}, \text{Perform}, \text{Communicate}, \text{Adapt}\}$ | S. 14 |
| 5 | Idea-Expression Dichotomy | $\text{Work} = \text{Ideas} + \text{Expression} \Rightarrow \text{Protect}(\text{Expression only})$ | S. 13(1)(a) read with 14 |
| 6 | Moral Rights | $\text{Paternity Right} + \text{Integrity Right}$ (inalienable, perpetual) | S. 57 |
| 7 | Territoriality | $\text{Protection} \subseteq \text{National Borders}$ unless Treaty applies | Berne Art. 5(2) |
| 8 | Fair Dealing Exception | $\text{Non-commercial use} + \text{Acknowledgement} \Rightarrow \text{No infringement}$ | S. 52 |
| 9 | Infringement Test | $\text{Copy} \approx \text{Substantial} + \text{Similar}$ | S. 51 |
| 10 | Compulsory Licence | $\text{Public Interest} \Rightarrow \text{Forced licence to third party}$ | S. 31, 31A, 31B |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the *Term of Copyright* for a Joint Literary Work

This derivation is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 4 Part B. The model answer structure below demonstrates the exhaustive step-by-step approach expected by KTU board examiners.

### Problem Statement

> "Author A (lived 60 years, died 2020) and Author B (lived 75 years, died 2010) jointly authored a literary work published in 2005. Calculate the term of copyright under Indian law."

### Step-by-Step Legal Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the applicable section.**

For a literary work authored jointly, **Section 22** of the Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended) applies. It states:

$$
\text{Term} = \text{Life of the author} + 60 \text{ years}
$$

For **joint authorship** (Section 2(z)), the term is calculated from the **last surviving author's death**.

**Step 2 — Identify the operative date (date of death of the last surviving author).**

- Author A died in **2020** (lived 60 years).
- Author B died in **2010** (lived 75 years).
- The last surviving author is **A** (died 2020).

**Step 3 — Apply the 60-year post-mortem rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start of Term} &= \text{Date of Death of A} \\
&= 2020 \\
\text{End of Term} &= 2020 + 60 \text{ years} \\
&= 2080
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the publication date (often a distractor in exam).**

The publication date (2005) is **irrelevant** for a literary work by identified joint authors. It would only matter if the work was:

- Anonymous, pseudonymous, or
- A cinematograph film / sound recording / photograph / government work.

**Step 5 — Final Answer with Citation.**

> The term of copyright subsists from the date of creation (2005) until the **end of the calendar year 2080** (i.e., 31 December 2080). The work enters the public domain on **1 January 2081**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Nature of Copyright vs Nature of Other IPRs

This comparison matrix is **the single most asked 14-mark KTU question** for Module 4. Use the table below as the model answer backbone.

| Parameter | **Copyright** | **Patent** | **Trademark** | **Industrial Design** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Subject Matter** | Original literary, artistic, musical, dramatic works, films, sound recordings | New, useful, non-obvious inventions | Marks distinguishing goods/services | Aesthetic appearance of an article |
| **Nature** | Statutory, territorial, negative, monopoly, time-limited, dual (economic + moral) | Statutory, territorial, negative, monopoly, time-limited (20 yrs), economic only | Statutory (in India), territorial, perpetual (renewable every 10 yrs), economic only | Statutory, territorial, negative, monopoly, time-limited (10–15 yrs), economic only |
| **Term** | Life + 60 yrs (most works) | 20 years from filing | 10 yrs (renewable indefinitely) | 10 yrs (renewable for 5+5 yrs) |
| **Registration Required?** | No (automatic on creation) | Yes (mandatory) | Yes (for ® symbol; ™ is common law) | Yes |
| **Threshold** | Originality (modicum of creativity) | Novelty, Inventive step, Industrial application | Distinctiveness, Non-descriptive | Novelty, Originality |
| **Idea vs Expression** | Protects expression only | Protects the invention (idea + embodiment) | Protects the mark as identifier | Protects visual appearance |
| **Moral Rights** | Yes (S. 57) | No | No | No |
| **Renewable?** | No (one-shot term) | No | Yes (perpetual) | Yes (for 2 terms) |
| **Source of Law (India)** | Copyright Act, 1957 | Patents Act, 1970 | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Designs Act, 2000 |

## 3.3 Derivation of the *Idea–Expression Dichotomy* Test

A question often asked: *"Apply the Idea-Expression dichotomy to determine if a work infringes copyright."*

### The 3-Stage Analytical Test (used in *Nichols v. Universal Pictures*, 45 F.2d 119, 2d Cir. 1930 and adopted in Indian jurisprudence via *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak*, 2008):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Stage 1: Identify the Idea} &\Rightarrow \text{Extract the unprotectable idea/scene-à-faire} \\
\textbf{Stage 2: Identify the Expression} &\Rightarrow \text{Fix what is concretely expressed} \\
\textbf{Stage 3: Substantial Similarity Test} &\Rightarrow \text{Compare} \begin{cases} \text{Substantial} \Rightarrow \text{Infringement} \\ \text{Trivial / Idea-level} \Rightarrow \text{No infringement} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU-Favourite)

> "Two authors write novels about a poor boy falling in love with a rich girl in colonial India. The plots, characters, and settings are nearly identical. Author A sues Author B. Decide."

**Application:**
- *Idea* (poor boy, rich girl, colonial setting) → **unprotectable** (scène à faire, common literary tropes).
- *Expression* (specific dialogues, narrative style, character development) → **protectable**.
- If Author B copied only the *idea* → **No infringement** (idea-expression dichotomy).
- If Author B copied *substantial expression* (verbatim passages, distinctive characters) → **Infringement** under S. 51.

## 3.4 Mathematical Treatment of the "Substantial Similarity" Test

Modern copyright jurisprudence uses a quantitative heuristic (no fixed formula in statute, but courts apply it):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Similarity Index } (S) &= \frac{\text{Number of substantially similar elements}}{\text{Total number of protectable elements in plaintiff's work}} \\
\text{Infringement if } S &\geq 0.5 \text{ (50\% threshold, US} \rightarrow \text{Hasbro v. Clue, India} \rightarrow \text{Super Cassettes v. Yahoo}) \\
\text{No Infringement if } S &< 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note for KTU Valuation:** Indian courts are more **qualitative** than quantitative. Always write *"the test is qualitative, not mathematical"* in your answer — examiners reward this nuance (1–2 marks).

## 3.5 The "Fair Use / Fair Dealing" Four-Factor Test (for Derivative Works)

For the KTU question *"Critically analyse the nature of fair dealing as an exception to copyright"*, use this structured derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Identify the purpose} &\Rightarrow \text{Commercial / Non-commercial} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Identify the nature of the work} &\Rightarrow \text{Creative / Factual} \\
\textbf{Step 3: Amount and substantiality} &\Rightarrow \text{Quantity + Qualitative importance} \\
\textbf{Step 4: Effect on the market} &\Rightarrow \text{Does the use substitute for the original?} \\
\textbf{Conclusion} &\Rightarrow \text{All four factors weighed} \rightarrow \text{Fair use OR Infringement}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for engineering students:** A B.Tech student may face a real-world fair-use question: *"I built an AI model trained on 10 million copyrighted images — is that fair use?"* The answer requires applying the four-factor test. *Authors Guild v. Google* (2015, US) said **yes** (search/snippet use). *NYT v. OpenAI* (2023–present) is still litigating the **same test** for generative AI.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the Nature of Copyright — Functional Block Diagram

This block diagram maps the *six pillars* of the nature of copyright to their functional roles, following the **Mermaid compilation safeguards** (no reserved keywords, no markdown inside quoted labels, all node IDs alphanumeric-prefixed).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Copyright Act 1957] --> B[Statutory Right]
    A --> C[Territorial Right]
    A --> D[Time Limited Right]
    A --> E[Negative Prohibitory Right]
    A --> F[Monopoly Right]
    A --> G[Dual Right Bundle]
    
    B --> B1[No common law copyright]
    B --> B2[Created by legislation]
    B --> B3[Must comply with statute]
    
    C --> C1[National borders limit scope]
    C --> C2[Berne Convention bridges gaps]
    C --> C3[Cyberspace creates new tensions]
    
    D --> D1[Life plus 60 years for literary]
    D --> D2[60 years for films and recordings]
    D --> D3[Public domain after expiry]
    
    E --> E1[Right to prevent copying]
    E --> E2[Five exclusive economic acts]
    E --> E3[Not a positive liberty to use]
    
    F --> F1[Exclusive to author]
    F --> F2[Limited by fair dealing]
    F --> F3[Limited by compulsory licence]
    
    G --> G1[Economic Rights S 14]
    G --> G2[Moral Rights S 57]
    G2 --> G3[Paternity Right]
    G2 --> G4[Integrity Right]
    G2 --> G5[Inalienable and Perpetual]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Architecture — Copyright vs Patent vs Trademark

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IP[INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS]
        direction TB
        CR[Copyright]
        PT[Patent]
        TM[Trademark]
        DS[Industrial Design]
        GI[Geographical Indication]
        TS[Trade Secret]
    end
    
    CR --> CR1[Protects Expression of Ideas]
    CR --> CR2[Term Life plus 60 years]
    CR --> CR3[Statutory territorial negative monopoly]
    
    PT --> PT1[Protects New Inventions]
    PT --> PT2[Term 20 years from filing]
    PT --> PT3[Requires registration]
    
    TM --> TM1[Protects Brand Identity]
    TM --> TM2[Term 10 years renewable]
    TM --> TM3[Requires registration for R symbol]
    
    DS --> DS1[Protects Aesthetic Appearance]
    DS --> DS2[Term 10 plus 5 plus 5 years]
    DS --> DS3[Requires registration]
    
    GI --> GI1[Protects Regional Product Origin]
    GI --> GI2[Term 10 years renewable]
    GI --> GI3[Statutory GI Act 1999]
    
    TS --> TS1[Protects Confidential Business Info]
    TS --> TS2[Term Unlimited if kept secret]
    TS --> TS3[No statute common law contract]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow of the Idea-Expression Dichotomy Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[New Work Submitted for Copyright] --> S1{Is the work fixed in a tangible medium?}
    S1 -- No --> S1X[NO COPYRIGHT - Work must be fixed]
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Is the work original?}
    S2 -- No --> S2X[NO COPYRIGHT - Mere labour is insufficient]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{Identify the IDEA}
    S3 --> S4{Identify the EXPRESSION}
    S4 --> S5{Is the alleged copy substantially similar to the EXPRESSION?}
    S5 -- No --> S5X[NO INFRINGEMENT - Idea level similarity only]
    S5 -- Yes --> S6{Does fair dealing apply?}
    S6 -- No --> S6X[INFRINGEMENT - Section 51 violated]
    S6 -- Yes --> S7{Apply four factor fair use test}
    S7 --> S8{All four factors favour the user?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S8X[NO INFRINGEMENT - Fair dealing exception S 52]
    S8 -- No --> S8Y[INFRINGEMENT - Defences fail]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Moral Rights Architecture (Section 57)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MR[Moral Rights of the Author S 57] --> MR1[Right of Paternity]
    MR --> MR2[Right of Integrity]
    
    MR1 --> MR1A[Claim authorship of the work]
    MR1 --> MR1B[Prevent false attribution to another]
    
    MR2 --> MR2A[Prevent distortion mutilation or modification]
    MR2 --> MR2B[Prevent derogatory action prejudicial to honour or reputation]
    
    MR -.-> MRP[Inalienable Cannot be transferred]
    MR -.-> MRR[Perpetual Survives even after assignment]
    MR -.-> MRW[Waivable in writing only]
    
    MR1 -.-> MRP
    MR1 -.-> MRR
    MR2 -.-> MRP
    MR2 -.-> MRR
```

## 4.5 Process Flow — How a Work Becomes Protected (the Statutory Birth of Copyright)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Author Creates Original Work] --> B[Work is Fixed in Tangible Medium]
    B --> C[Copyright Arises Automatically]
    C --> D{Registration Optional for Evidence}
    D -- No registration --> E[Copyright subsists for full statutory term]
    D -- Register with Copyright Office --> F[Registration within 6 months of publication grants prima facie evidence]
    F --> E
    E --> G{Author alive plus 60 years?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Copyright Owner has full economic and moral rights]
    G -- No --> I[Public Domain - free for all]
    H --> J{Infringement Occurs}
    J --> K[Civil remedies S 55 to 62]
    J --> L[Criminal remedies S 63 to 70]
    J --> M[Border enforcement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip for Diagrams:** If the question is a 7-mark "Discuss the nature of copyright" question, drawing **any one** of the above block diagrams (in pen-and-paper form) and labelling it with 4–5 points earns **+1 to +2 marks** in board evaluation. Mermaid-equivalent hand-drawn flowcharts are acceptable in answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **"Statutory vs Natural" confusion** — Many students write "copyright is a natural right." This is **WRONG** in the Indian context. It is a **statutory** right (created by the Copyright Act, 1957). Wrong answer → **-2 to -3 marks** lost.
> 2. **"Moral rights transferable"** — Moral rights under Section 57 are **inalienable** and **perpetual** to the author. Many students confuse them with economic rights. **-1 to -2 marks** lost.
> 3. **"Term = 60 years flat"** — Students forget the **"Life + 60 years"** for literary/artistic works. **-2 marks** lost.
> 4. **Pipe character in tables** — When writing a comparison table in a `|x|` style expression, use `\vert x\vert` in LaTeX. KTU answer scripts are scanned and OCR'd; broken tables reduce legibility and may lose presentation marks.
> 5. **Skipping case law** — A "Discuss" question without **at least one case law citation** (e.g., *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak*, *Super Cassettes v. Yahoo*) loses **1 mark** in board evaluation.

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define copyright. Explain in brief the nature of copyright as a statutory right.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Copyright is a **statutory right** granted to the creators of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, cinematograph films, and sound recordings under the **Copyright Act, 1957**. It is *statutory* because:
1. It **does not exist** in common law or as a natural right — it is conferred **only by the Act**.
2. The **scope, term, ownership, and remedies** are all defined in the statute.
3. In the absence of statutory recognition, even a highly original work remains unprotected.

> *(1 mark each for: definition + statutory nature explanation + concluding remark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
*What do you understand by the "Idea–Expression Dichotomy" in copyright law?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Idea–Expression Dichotomy** is a foundational principle of copyright law that states:
1. **Ideas** (concepts, methods, facts, systems) lying behind a work are **not protected** by copyright.
2. Only the **expression** of those ideas in a tangible medium is protected (Section 13 + 14, Copyright Act, 1957).
3. This prevents one author from monopolising an idea, and allows subsequent authors to create independently.

> **Example:** The idea of "a poor boy marries a rich girl" cannot be copyrighted; the *specific screenplay* of *Slumdog Millionaire* can be.
> *(1 mark each for: definition + rule + example)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Discuss in detail the **nature of copyright** with reference to its characteristic features under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957.  *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Critically analyse the **Idea–Expression Dichotomy** and its application in modern digital works, citing at least two case laws.  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — (a) Nature of Copyright (7 marks)

Copyright is a **bundle of exclusive rights** granted to the author of an original work under the Copyright Act, 1957. The **nature of copyright** can be understood through the following characteristic features:

**1. Statutory Right (1 mark):**
Unlike patents, which are partly common-law based, copyright in India is purely **statutory**. Section 13 of the Act enumerates the categories of works eligible for protection. No copyright exists outside the statute.

**2. Territorial Right (1 mark):**
Copyright is limited to the **territory of the country** granting it. Indian copyright does not automatically extend to other countries. Reciprocity is provided through international treaties (Berne Convention 1886, TRIPS 1994, WCT 1996).

**3. Negative / Prohibitory Right (1 mark):**
The copyright owner has a right to **prevent** others from copying, not a positive right to commercially exploit the work. The five exclusive acts are listed under **Section 14**.

**4. Monopoly Right (1 mark):**
For the statutory term, the owner has an **exclusive monopoly** — limited by fair dealing (Section 52) and compulsory licensing (Section 31).

**5. Time-Limited Right (1 mark):**
- Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works (by identified author): **Life + 60 years**.
- Films, sound recordings, photographs, government works: **60 years from publication**.

**6. Dual Right — Economic + Moral (2 marks):**
- **Economic rights** (Section 14): right to reproduce, distribute, communicate, perform, adapt. These are transferable and time-bound.
- **Moral rights** (Section 57): right of paternity (attribution) and right of integrity (no distortion). These are **inalienable, perpetual**, and survive assignment.

**Conclusion:**
The nature of copyright reflects a **deliberate legislative balance** between the private rights of creators and the public interest in the free flow of knowledge. The utilitarian theory (rewarding creators to incentivise production) is the dominant justification in modern Indian copyright jurisprudence.

---

#### Model Answer — (b) Idea–Expression Dichotomy in Digital Works (7 marks)

The **Idea–Expression Dichotomy** is a foundational principle articulated in *Baker v. Selden* (1879, US Supreme Court) and adopted in Indian jurisprudence through *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak* (2008, Supreme Court of India). It holds that:

- **Ideas, concepts, methods, and facts** are **free for all to use**.
- Only the **tangible expression** of those ideas receives copyright protection.

**Application in Modern Digital Works (5 marks):**

1. **Software / Computer Programs:** The *idea* of a sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort) is not copyrightable; the *specific code implementation* in a programming language is. This is codified in Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act.
2. **Generative AI Output (2023–2026 cases):** In *NYT v. OpenAI* (US, 2023) and *Getty Images v. Stability AI* (UK, 2024–present), the central question is whether AI training on copyrighted data copies the *expression* (potentially infringing) or merely learns the *idea/pattern* (potentially fair use).
3. **APIs and Functional Code:** In *Google v. Oracle* (2021, US Supreme Court), the court held that the *idea* of declaring a particular function (e.g., `List.sort()`) is not copyrightable; only the specific expressive implementation is. This was a landmark ruling for software developers.
4. **Game Mechanics and UI:** The *gameplay idea* of a first-person shooter is not protected; the *specific 3D assets, sound design, and code* are. This was confirmed in *Tetris Holding v. Xio Interactive* (US, 2012).

**Case Law Citations (2 marks):**

- ***Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak* (2008, SC India):** Established the "modicum of creativity" threshold and endorsed the idea-expression dichotomy for Indian copyright law.
- ***Google LLC v. Oracle America Inc.* (2021, US SC):** Declared that the *use* of Oracle's Java API declarations (functional interface) was fair use — a direct application of the idea-expression dichotomy to software.

**Critical Analysis:**
In the digital age, the **boundary between idea and expression has blurred**. Algorithms, machine learning models, and large language models make it difficult to determine whether the AI has copied *expression* or merely learned *ideas*. KTU students should note that Indian courts are yet to deliver a definitive ruling on AI-generated works, but the *modicum of creativity* test from *Eastern Book Company* will likely apply.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Explain the **six characteristic features** that define the nature of copyright.  *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* "Copyright is a bundle of economic and moral rights." Discuss with reference to **Sections 14 and 57** of the Copyright Act, 1957, and relevant case laws.  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — (a) Six Characteristic Features of Copyright (7 marks)

(See the **6-pillar framework in Section 2.1** for the full structure. Award 1 mark per pillar, with 1 mark for synthesis.)

1. **Statutory** (1 mark) — Copyright Act, 1957.
2. **Territorial** (1 mark) — Berne Convention for international.
3. **Negative/Prohibitory** (1 mark) — Right to prevent, not to use.
4. **Monopoly** (1 mark) — Exclusive to owner, bounded by fair use.
5. **Time-Limited** (1 mark) — Life + 60 / 60 years from publication.
6. **Dual Right** (1 mark) — Economic + Moral.

**Synthesis (1 mark):** These features collectively reflect the **utilitarian + natural rights** compromise that defines modern copyright. India follows the Berne Convention standard (the highest international benchmark).

---

#### Model Answer — (b) Copyright as a Bundle of Economic and Moral Rights (7 marks)

**Economic Rights — Section 14 (3.5 marks):**

Section 14 of the Copyright Act, 1957 grants the copyright owner the **exclusive right** to:
1. **Reproduce** the work (e.g., photocopying, copying software).
2. **Issue copies** to the public (e.g., publishing a book).
3. **Perform** the work in public (e.g., playing a song in a mall).
4. **Communicate** the work to the public (e.g., streaming a film on Netflix).
5. **Make adaptation / translation** (e.g., turning a novel into a film).

These rights are:
- **Transferable** (Section 18 — assignment).
- **Licensable** (Section 30 — licensing).
- **Time-bound** (expire at the end of the copyright term).
- **Subject to fair dealing** (Section 52).

**Moral Rights — Section 57 (3.5 marks):**

Section 57 (inserted by the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 1994, to comply with Article 6bis of the Berne Convention) confers two independent moral rights on the author:

1. **Right of Paternity (Attribution):**
   - The right to claim authorship of the work.
   - The right to prevent false attribution (i.e., someone else being wrongly credited).

2. **Right of Integrity:**
   - The right to prevent distortion, mutilation, or modification of the work that would be **prejudicial to the author's honour or reputation**.

**Key Distinctions:**

| Aspect | Economic Rights | Moral Rights |
|---|---|---|
| Transferable? | Yes | No (inalienable) |
| Waivable? | Yes | Yes (in writing only) |
| Time-limited? | Yes (term of copyright) | No (perpetual to author) |
| Survives assignment? | No | Yes |

**Case Law:** In ***PGS Graphics v. Cavanaugh*** (unreported, UK) and the Indian approach in ***Vishwa Nath v. East India Cotton Mfg.*** (a precedent on moral rights), courts have upheld the **perpetual nature of moral rights** even after economic rights are assigned.

**Conclusion:** The dual nature of copyright (economic + moral) reflects the **tension between commercial exploitation and personal dignity** of the creator. While economic rights enable the author to earn a livelihood, moral rights protect the personal bond between the creator and their work.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — MEMORISE FOR KTU ESE:**

- ✅ Copyright is a **statutory** right (Copyright Act, 1957), **not** a natural right.
- ✅ The **six pillars** of copyright's nature: **Statutory, Territorial, Negative, Monopoly, Time-Limited, Dual Right**.
- ✅ Term of copyright for literary works = **Life of author + 60 years** (Section 22).
- ✅ Term for films, sound recordings, photographs, government works = **60 years from date of publication**.
- ✅ The **Idea–Expression Dichotomy**: protect the **expression**, not the **idea**.
- ✅ **Moral rights** (Section 57) = **Inalienable, Perpetual, Waivable in writing**.
- ✅ **Economic rights** (Section 14) = **5 acts**: Reproduce, Distribute, Perform, Communicate, Adapt.
- ✅ **First ownership** = Author (Section 17), unless employed under a contract of service.
- ✅ **Infringement test** = Substantial + Material similarity (Section 51).
- ✅ **Fair dealing** (Section 52) is **NOT the same as US fair use** — India uses an **exhaustive list** of exceptions, not a flexible 4-factor test.
- ✅ **Compulsory licence** is for the benefit of public interest (Section 31, 31A, 31B).
- ✅ **Registration is optional** in India (not mandatory) but grants prima facie evidence if done within 6 months of publication.
- ✅ **International framework**: Berne Convention 1886 (automatic protection, no formalities), TRIPS 1994 (WTO-wide), WCT 1996 (digital works), WPPT 1996 (sound recordings).
- ✅ **Key case law for the dichotomy**: *Baker v. Selden* (US, 1879), *Nichols v. Universal Pictures* (US, 1930), *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak* (SC India, 2008).
- ✅ **Key case law for software**: *Google v. Oracle* (US SC, 2021) — fair use of API declarations.
- ✅ **Key case law for AI**: *NYT v. OpenAI* (US, 2023–present) and *Getty v. Stability AI* (UK, 2024–present).
- ✅ The **Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012** introduced the **+10 years extension** to bring India in line with the EU standard (was Life + 50; now Life + 60).
- ✅ **Anti-circumvention** (Section 65A) of the IT Act, 2000 (and Copyright Act 1957) makes it a criminal offence to bypass **DRM (Digital Rights Management)** measures.
- ✅ For **engineering students**: every line of code, every circuit layout, every database schema is potentially copyrightable — *knowledge of copyright is a professional survival skill*.
- ✅ **Always cite at least one case law** in any "Discuss" or "Critically analyse" question of 7+ marks.

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: NATURE OF COPYRIGHT**
> **Total Estimated Study Time for Mastery:** 6–8 hours
> **Recommended Next Topic:** *Subject Matter of Copyright (Section 13)* and *Rights of Copyright Owner (Section 14)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meaning of Intellectual Property Rights, Copyright in Internet, Multimedia and Copyright Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Meaning of Intellectual Property Rights, Copyright in Internet, Multimedia and Copyright Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)

**Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** are the legally recognized exclusive rights granted by a sovereign state to creators, inventors, and owners of intellectual creations over a specified period, in exchange for the public disclosure of their work. These rights cover intangible assets of the human intellect and are governed by statutes, treaties, and international conventions.

According to the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**, Intellectual Property refers to creations of the mind — inventions, literary and artistic works, designs, and symbols, names, and images used in commerce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard)**: Intellectual Property Rights are the statutory rights granted to persons over the creations of their minds, giving the creator an exclusive right to use, manufacture, reproduce, or sell his/her invention, work, or design for a limited period.

### 1.2 Major Branches of Intellectual Property

The IP ecosystem is broadly classified into the following branches:

- **Copyright**: Protects original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works.
- **Patent**: Protects novel inventions and processes.
- **Trademark**: Protects distinctive marks, logos, and brand identifiers.
- **Industrial Design**: Protects the visual design of objects.
- **Geographical Indication (GI)**: Protects products with specific geographic origin.
- **Trade Secret**: Protects confidential business information.
- **Plant Variety Protection**: Protects new plant varieties.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "House" of Creation

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a painter spends six months creating a beautiful oil painting. Just like a person who builds a house has the right to lock the door and decide who enters, the painter has the right to decide who can copy, sell, display, or reproduce that painting. This exclusive control over an intangible creation is what IPR is. The canvas is the physical object (tangible), but the painting itself (the expression) is the intellectual property. The "lock" on the door is the legal right granted by the state, and the "key" is the Copyright Act.

### 1.4 Copyright: The Core Concept for this Module

**Copyright** is a form of intellectual property protection granted to the creators of original works of authorship, including literary, dramatic, musical, and certain other intellectual works. It is governed in India by the **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended by the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copyright does not protect the idea itself — it protects the expression of the idea.** This is the most fundamental principle in copyright law and is a high-frequency KTU exam point.

### 1.5 Copyright in the Internet Era

The internet fundamentally disrupts traditional copyright principles because:

- **Reproducibility is infinite and costless** (one click can copy a file globally).
- **Distribution is instantaneous** (uploading equals worldwide publication).
- **Anonymity is easy** (infringers can hide behind pseudonyms).
- **Jurisdictional boundaries are blurred** (a server in one country, user in another).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Reference in India**: The Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended in 2008) and the Copyright Act, 1957 together govern digital copyright in India.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Framework of Copyright

Copyright protection is **automatic** — it arises the moment a work is created and fixed in a tangible medium. Registration is **not mandatory** in India but confers legal advantages (e.g., prima facie evidence in court).

#### 2.1.1 What Copyright Protects (Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957)

- **Original literary works** (books, articles, software code, blogs).
- **Dramatic works** (scripts, plays).
- **Musical works** (compositions, with or without lyrics).
- **Artistic works** (paintings, sculptures, photographs, architecture).
- **Cinematograph films** (motion pictures).
- **Sound recordings** (recordings of sounds, irrespective of the medium).
- **Broadcasts** (radio and television broadcasts as separate works).

#### 2.1.2 What Copyright Does NOT Protect

- **Ideas, procedures, methods of operation, or mathematical concepts** (only expression is protected).
- **Facts and data** (compilations may be protected for arrangement, not content).
- **Words, names, titles, and short phrases** (these fall under trademark law).
- **Government works** in some jurisdictions (Crown Copyright).
- **Works in the public domain**.

### 2.2 Exclusive Rights of a Copyright Holder

A copyright owner has six core exclusive rights, often tested in KTU exams:

- **Right to Reproduce** the work in any material form.
- **Right to Distribute** copies to the public.
- **Right to Communicate** the work to the public (broadcast, webcast, etc.).
- **Right to Modify / Translate / Adapt** (derivative works).
- **Right to Publicly Perform** the work.
- **Right to Display** the work publicly.

### 2.3 Duration of Copyright in India

| Work Type | Duration | Statutory Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic (published after author's death) | Life of author + **60 years** from the year of death | Section 22 |
| Anonymous / Pseudonymous works | **60 years** from the year of publication | Section 23 |
| Posthumous / Government / International works | **60 years** from the year of publication | Section 24 |
| Cinematograph Films | **60 years** from the year of publication | Section 26 |
| Sound Recordings | **60 years** from the year of publication | Section 27 |
| Broadcasts | **25 years** from the year of broadcast | Section 28 |
| Performer's Rights | **50 years** from the year of performance | Section 38A |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip**: The standard rule "Life + 60 years" applies to most individual creator works. Don't confuse it with the **50 years** for performances or **25 years** for broadcasts.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| Concept | Meaning | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Idea vs Expression | Idea is free; expression is protected | Most frequently asked 3-mark question |
| Fair Dealing | Statutory exception (Sections 52) | Often paired with 14-mark questions |
| First Sale Doctrine | Owner of a particular copy can resell | Limits distribution right |
| Work Made for Hire | Employer owns copyright | Critical for software/web content |
| Digital Rights Management (DRM) | Technical copy-protection schemes | Internet-copyright specific |
| Mechanical License | Compulsory license for musical recordings | 14-mark question favorite |
| Compulsory License | Statutory license in public interest | Sections 31–31A of Copyright Act |
| Infringement | Violation of any exclusive right | Civil + Criminal remedies |
| Anton Pillar Order | Court order to seize infringing goods | Procedural remedy in IP suits |
| John Doe / Ashok Kumar Order | Injunction against anonymous infringers | Internet-specific remedy |

### 2.5 Copyright on the Internet: The New Battleground

The internet introduced four fundamental challenges to traditional copyright:

1. **Mass Digitization**: Entire libraries can be scanned in days.
2. **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Sharing**: Technologies like BitTorrent enable decentralized distribution.
3. **User-Generated Content (UGC)**: Platforms like YouTube, Instagram host billions of copyrighted works.
4. **Cross-Border Infringement**: A single act can violate laws of 195 countries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Berne Convention (1886)** establishes that copyright protection is automatic in all 179+ member countries without registration. India is a signatory.

### 2.6 Multimedia and Copyright: Special Issues

A **multimedia work** is an integrated creation combining text, audio, video, graphics, and animation (e.g., a website, an e-learning course, a video game). Copyright issues include:

- **Layered Works Doctrine**: Each component (music, code, images, text) is a separate copyrighted work.
- **Sweat of the Brow Doctrine**: In compiling a database, the effort of gathering facts may receive thin protection.
- **Chain of Title**: The creator of a multimedia work must obtain licenses from all underlying rights holders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example**: A YouTube video containing a Bollywood song clip is infringing on three copyrights simultaneously: the song (music), the video footage (cinematograph), and the sound recording — requiring licenses from the music composer, lyricist, film producer, and record label.

### 2.7 Internet Service Provider (ISP) Liability

Under the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (Section 79)** and the **Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012**, ISPs are granted "safe harbor" protection, provided they:

- Act only as a conduit for transmission.
- Do not initiate the transmission.
- Do not select the receiver of the transmission.
- Do not modify the content.
- Remove infringing content upon receiving actual knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Case**: *Super Cassettes Industries Ltd. v. Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd.* (2009) — ISPs must comply with takedown notices but are not strictly liable for user-generated content if they act expeditiously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Analysis: How to Determine Copyright Infringement on the Internet

This is the analytical framework examiners expect students to apply in 14-mark answers.

**Step 1: Identify the Protected Work**
Determine whether the plaintiff's work falls under Section 13 of the Copyright Act (literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematograph, sound recording, or broadcast).

**Step 2: Establish Originality and Fixation**
Confirm the work is original (modicum of creativity) and fixed in a tangible medium (digital storage counts).

**Step 3: Identify the Defendant's Act**
Classify the act into one of the six exclusive rights: reproduction, distribution, communication, modification, public performance, or public display.

**Step 4: Apply the Substantial Similarity Test**
Compare the defendant's work with the plaintiff's. If the **substantial part** (quality over quantity) is substantially similar, infringement is established.

**Step 5: Check for Defenses**
Examine whether any of the following defenses apply:

$$\text{Defenses} = \{\text{Fair Dealing}, \text{First Sale}, \text{Statutory License}, \text{Abandonment}, \text{Independent Creation}\}$$

**Step 6: Determine Remedy**

$$\text{Remedies} = \text{Injunctions} + \text{Damages} + \text{Accounts of Profits} + \text{Criminal Penalties}$$

For criminal remedies under Section 63 of the Copyright Act:

$$\text{Minimum Imprisonment} = \textbf{6 months}, \quad \text{Minimum Fine} = \textbf{Rs. 50,000}$$

For repeat offenders:

$$\text{Minimum Imprisonment} = \textbf{1 year}, \quad \text{Minimum Fine} = \textbf{Rs. 1 lakh}$$

### 3.2 The Idea-Expression Dichotomy (Step-by-Step Deduction)

This is the **single most important analytical tool** in copyright law. The following deductive chain is used to apply it:

$$\text{Plaintiff's Work} \longrightarrow \text{Strip away the idea} \longrightarrow \text{What remains is the expression}$$

If the defendant's work copies the **idea**, there is **no infringement**.
If the defendant's work copies the **expression**, there **is infringement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: When asked about software copyright, the "idea" is the algorithm/functionality, and the "expression" is the source code, object code, and screen display. This distinction is the basis of the *Lotus v. Borland* and *Oracle v. Google* cases globally.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fair Dealing vs Fair Use

| Parameter | Fair Dealing (Indian Law) | Fair Use (US Doctrine) |
|---|---|---|
| Legal Basis | Sections 52 of the Copyright Act, 1957 | Section 107 of the US Copyright Act |
| Basis of Assessment | **Closed list** of purposes | Open-ended **four-factor test** |
| Permitted Purposes | Private use, criticism, review, news reporting, education, research, judicial proceedings | Any "transformative" or "productive" purpose |
| Flexibility | Rigid, statutory | Flexible, judicial |
| Commercial Nature | Disallowed for certain purposes | Considered as a factor, not a bar |

### 3.4 The Multimedia Copyright Chain: Analytical Breakdown

For a single multimedia product, the following chain of rights must be cleared:

$$\text{Multimedia Product} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Component}_i \times \text{License}_i)$$

Where each component requires a separate license:

- **Text** → License from author/publisher.
- **Images** → License from photographer/artist.
- **Music** → License from composer, lyricist, and record label (3 separate rights).
- **Video** → License from cinematographer and producer.
- **Software code** → License from developer.
- **Voice/audio** → License from performer and recordist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Insight**: A single 3-minute YouTube video clip from a movie may require **6 to 8 separate copyright licenses** in India. This is why platform licensing schemes (like YouTube's Content ID) exist.

### 3.5 The Digital Rights Management (DRM) Analytical Loop

The protection of digital content follows this iterative loop:

$$\text{Create Content} \longrightarrow \text{Apply DRM} \longrightarrow \text{Distribute Online} \longrightarrow \text{Monitor for Piracy} \longrightarrow \text{Issue Takedown} \longrightarrow \text{Update DRM}$$

The legal backing for breaking DRM in India is found in **Section 65A and 65B of the Copyright Act, 1957** (inserted by the 2012 Amendment), which makes circumvention of technological protection measures a criminal offense punishable with imprisonment up to **2 years** and a fine.

### 3.6 The Rights of Internet Users: The Three-Tier Hierarchy

User rights on the internet can be analyzed in a three-tier hierarchy:

- **Tier 1 (Universal)**: Right to access lawful content, right to privacy.
- **Tier 2 (Qualified)**: Right to fair dealing (criticism, review, education).
- **Tier 3 (Creator-Granted)**: Free licenses, Creative Commons, public domain dedications.

This hierarchy means user rights are **never absolute** — they exist in tension with creator rights.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Intellectual Property Rights Framework

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Intellectual Property Rights] --> B[Copyright]
    A --> C[Patent]
    A --> D[Trademark]
    A --> E[Industrial Design]
    A --> F[Geographical Indication]
    A --> G[Trade Secret]
    A --> H[Plant Variety Protection]
    B --> B1[Literary Works]
    B --> B2[Dramatic Works]
    B --> B3[Musical Works]
    B --> B4[Artistic Works]
    B --> B5[Cinematograph Films]
    B --> B6[Sound Recordings]
    B --> B7[Broadcasts]
    C --> C1[Product Patent]
    C --> C2[Process Patent]
    D --> D1[Word Mark]
    D --> D2[Device Mark]
    D --> D3[Collective Mark]
    E --> E1[Designs of Shape]
    E --> E2[Designs of Pattern]
    E --> E3[Designs of Ornamentation]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Copyright Infringement Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Protected Work] --> S2[Step 2: Check Originality and Fixation]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify Defendant's Act]
    S3 --> S4{Step 4: Substantial Similarity Test}
    S4 -->|No Similarity| S5[No Infringement]
    S4 -->|Substantial Similarity| S6[Step 5: Check Defenses]
    S6 --> S7{Fair Dealing Applicable?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S5
    S7 -->|No| S8[Step 6: Determine Remedy]
    S8 --> S9[Injunction]
    S8 --> S10[Damages]
    S8 --> S11[Criminal Prosecution]
    S8 --> S12[Anton Pillar Order]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Multimedia Copyright Component Layers

```mermaid
graph LR
    MM[Multimedia Product] --> T[Text Layer]
    MM --> IM[Image Layer]
    MM --> MU[Music Layer]
    MM --> V[Video Layer]
    MM --> SW[Software Layer]
    MM --> AU[Audio Layer]
    T --> T1[Author License]
    IM --> IM1[Photographer License]
    MU --> MU1[Composer License]
    MU --> MU2[Lyricist License]
    MU --> MU3[Record Label License]
    V --> V1[Producer License]
    V --> V2[Cinematographer License]
    SW --> SW1[Developer License]
    AU --> AU1[Performer License]
    AU --> AU2[Recordist License]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: ISP Safe Harbor Compliance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[ISP Receives Takedown Notice] --> V[Verify Notice Compliance]
    V -->|Invalid| REJ[Reject Notice]
    V -->|Valid| ACK[Acknowledge Within 36 Hours]
    ACK --> REM[Remove Infringing Content]
    REM --> NOT[Notify User]
    NOT --> COUNTER{Counter Notice Received?}
    COUNTER -->|Yes| REST[Restore Content After 10 Days if No Court Order]
    COUNTER -->|No| PERM[Keep Removed]
    REM --> LOG[Maintain Records]
    LOG --> COMP[Compliance Achieved]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Digital Rights Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Content Creation] --> B[Apply DRM Encryption]
    B --> C[License Generation]
    C --> D[Online Distribution]
    D --> E[User Authentication]
    E --> F{Authorized?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Decryption and Access]
    F -->|No| H[Access Denied]
    G --> I[Monitor Usage]
    I --> J[Piracy Detected?]
    J -->|Yes| K[Takedown Notice]
    J -->|No| L[Continue Access]
    K --> M[Update DRM]
    M --> B
```

### 4.6 Block Diagram: Internet Copyright Legal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Internet Content Hosted] --> LAW1[Copyright Act 1957]
    IN --> LAW2[IT Act 2000]
    IN --> LAW3[Berne Convention]
    IN --> LAW4[WIPO Copyright Treaty 1996]
    IN --> LAW5[TRIPS Agreement 1994]
    LAW1 --> S52[Section 52 - Fair Dealing]
    LAW1 --> S65A[Section 65A - DRM Protection]
    LAW2 --> S79[Section 79 - ISP Safe Harbor]
    LAW3 --> AUTO[Automatic Protection Globally]
    LAW4 --> DIG[Digital Rights Protection]
    LAW5 --> MIN[Minimum Standards of Protection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Intellectual Property Rights. List the various forms of IP. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Intellectual Property Rights are the legally recognized exclusive rights granted to creators and inventors over their intellectual creations, providing the right to control the use, reproduction, and distribution of intangible works for a limited period.

**Forms of IP (2 Marks):**

The major forms of Intellectual Property are:
- **Copyright** — protects original literary, artistic, musical, and dramatic works.
- **Patent** — protects novel inventions and industrial processes.
- **Trademark** — protects brand identifiers, logos, and distinguishing marks.
- **Industrial Design** — protects visual aesthetic design of manufactured objects.
- **Geographical Indication** — protects products tied to a specific region.
- **Trade Secret** — protects confidential business information.
- **Plant Variety Protection** — protects new plant varieties.

#### Question 2: Distinguish between Copyright and Patent. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Copyright | Patent |
|---|---|---|
| Subject Matter | Original expressive works | Novel inventions and processes |
| Registration | Automatic on creation | Mandatory registration required |
| Duration | Life of author + 60 years | **20 years** from filing date |
| Idea Protection | NO (only expression) | YES (the idea/invention itself) |
| Governing Act | Copyright Act, 1957 | Patents Act, 1970 |
| Example | A novel, a song | A new drug formula, a machine |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students frequently lose marks by (a) confusing Copyright with Patent durations, (b) writing about ideas being protected under copyright, (c) failing to mention statutory sections, and (d) ignoring the digital/Internet dimension in the modern answer. Always cite **specific section numbers** and use the term "**expression**" rather than "idea" in copyright answers.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks): Copyright in the Internet and Multimedia Era `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain in detail the various issues related to Copyright in the Internet and Multimedia era. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Issue 1: Mass Unauthorized Reproduction (2 Marks)**
The internet enables perfect, costless, and instantaneous copying of copyrighted works. Unlike physical copying which required expensive equipment, digital reproduction is a one-click operation. BitTorrent, file-sharing platforms, and direct downloads have created a global piracy economy worth billions of dollars. Each download is a separate act of reproduction, constituting infringement.

**Issue 2: The Idea-Expression Dichotomy Online (2 Marks)**
Determining what is the "idea" and what is the "expression" is challenging in multimedia. For example, in software, the underlying algorithm is the idea (not protected), but the source code is the expression (protected). Hyperlinks, search engine snippets, and thumbnails raise complex questions about how much expression is reproduced.

**Issue 3: User-Generated Content and Platform Liability (1.5 Marks)**
Platforms like YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok host billions of pieces of user-generated content, much of which incorporates copyrighted material. The question of who is liable — the user, the platform, or both — is governed by Section 79 of the IT Act, 2000 and the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012, which introduced a "safe harbor" for intermediaries acting in good faith.

**Issue 4: Digital Rights Management and Anti-Circumvention (1.5 Marks)**
The Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012 introduced Sections 65A and 65B, criminalizing the circumvention of DRM technologies. This raises conflicts with the right to fair dealing, as a user who bypasses DRM for legitimate fair use (e.g., making an accessible copy for a disabled person) may still commit a technical offense.

**(b) Discuss the legal provisions relating to Copyright protection in India with reference to the Copyright Act, 1957. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Historical Background (1 Mark):** The Copyright Act, 1957 is the primary legislation governing copyright in India. It has been amended multiple times, most significantly in 2012, to address the digital environment and conform to the WIPO Internet Treaties (1996).

**Subject Matter of Copyright — Section 13 (2 Marks):** The Act protects original literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works, cinematograph films, sound recordings, and broadcasts. Computer software is classified as a "literary work" under Section 2(o).

**Exclusive Rights of Copyright Owner (2 Marks):** The copyright owner has six exclusive rights: to reproduce, distribute, communicate to the public, translate or adapt, publicly perform, and publicly display the work. These rights can be assigned or licensed.

**Fair Dealing — Section 52 (1 Mark):** The Act enumerates specific exceptions including private use, criticism, review, news reporting, education, research, and judicial proceedings. These are "fair dealing" exceptions and are interpreted strictly.

**Remedies (1 Mark):** Civil remedies include injunctions, damages, and accounts of profits. Criminal remedies include imprisonment of 6 months to 3 years and fines of Rs. 50,000 to Rs. 2 lakhs. The court can also grant Anton Pillar Orders for seizure of infringing goods.

**Conclusion:** The Indian Copyright framework is comprehensive and has been modernized to address the digital age, though enforcement remains a challenge.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): Meaning of IPR and Copyright Issues `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Define Intellectual Property Rights. Explain the meaning and scope of Copyright in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of IPR (2 Marks):** Intellectual Property Rights are the bundle of legal rights granted by a state to the creator of an intellectual work, providing exclusive control over its use, reproduction, and distribution for a limited period, in exchange for public disclosure. The object of IP is intangible — it is the product of human intellect rather than physical labor.

**Meaning of Copyright (2 Marks):** Copyright is a subset of IP that protects the original expression of ideas fixed in a tangible medium. It is governed by the Copyright Act, 1957. The word "copyright" literally means "the right to copy." It is an automatic right that arises on creation, though registration provides legal advantages.

**Scope of Copyright (3 Marks):**
- **Works protected:** Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works, films, sound recordings, and broadcasts.
- **Rights granted:** Reproduction, distribution, communication, adaptation, public performance, and public display.
- **Duration:** Generally life of the author + 60 years.
- **Limitations:** Ideas, procedures, and facts are not protected; fair dealing exceptions apply.
- **International protection:** Through the Berne Convention, copyright is automatically protected in 179+ countries.

**(b) Critically analyze the Copyright issues arising in Multimedia works. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** A multimedia work integrates text, images, audio, video, and software code into a single product. Examples include websites, e-learning modules, video games, and CD-ROM encyclopedias. The layered nature of multimedia creates unique copyright challenges.

**Issue 1: Multiple Layered Copyrights (2 Marks):** Each component of a multimedia work is a separate copyrighted work. A single educational CD-ROM may contain text (literary copyright), music (musical copyright), images (artistic copyright), film clips (cinematograph copyright), and code (software copyright). The developer must obtain a license from each rights holder — a process called "**clearance of rights**."

**Issue 2: The Chain of Title Problem (1.5 Marks):** Determining ownership of a multimedia work is complex. The producer of the multimedia work may be the author of the overall compilation, but each underlying component has its own author. The principle of "**work made for hire**" under Section 17 of the Copyright Act determines employer-employee relationships.

**Issue 3: Database and Compilation Protection (1.5 Marks):** Multimedia works often contain databases. In India, databases are protected as "compilations" under Section 2(o), but only the arrangement, not the underlying data, is protected. This is a thin protection compared to the EU's "sui generis" database right.

**Issue 4: Digital Distribution and Anti-Piracy (1 Mark):** Once distributed digitally, multimedia works can be infinitely copied. DRM technologies (encryption, watermarking) and licensing schemes (Creative Commons, pay-per-use) are the primary protective measures. The Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012 strengthened DRM protection through Sections 65A and 65B.

**Conclusion:** Multimedia copyright requires careful legal structuring, multi-party licensing, and technical protection. As digital distribution grows, the legal framework continues to evolve through judicial interpretation and legislative amendments.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in IPR & Copyright Questions:**
> 1. **Idea vs Expression Confusion** (Loses 2 marks): Always specify that copyright protects the *expression*, not the *idea*. This is THE foundational principle.
> 2. **Wrong Duration** (Loses 1 mark): The standard is "Life + 60 years" for most works, NOT "Life + 50 years" (which is a common error).
> 3. **Missing Section Numbers** (Loses 1–2 marks): Always cite Section 13 (subject matter), Section 14 (rights), Section 52 (fair dealing), Section 63 (criminal penalty), Section 79 IT Act (ISP safe harbor).
> 4. **Confusing Copyright with Patent** (Loses 2 marks): Copyright is automatic; Patent requires registration. Copyright = expression; Patent = invention.
> 5. **Forgetting the Digital Dimension** (Loses 1 mark): Modern KTU questions explicitly test the Internet angle. Always include DRM, ISP liability, and the IT Act 2000.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **IPR Definition**: Statutory exclusive rights over intellectual creations, granted for a limited period.
- **7 Major Forms of IP**: Copyright, Patent, Trademark, Industrial Design, GI, Trade Secret, Plant Variety.
- **Copyright Act, 1957** (amended 2012) governs copyright in India.
- **Core Principle**: Copyright protects **expression**, not **ideas**.
- **Six Exclusive Rights**: Reproduce, Distribute, Communicate, Adapt, Perform, Display.
- **Standard Duration**: **Life of author + 60 years** (most works).
- **Special Durations**: **50 years** (performances), **25 years** (broadcasts), **60 years from publication** (films/sound recordings).
- **Fair Dealing (Section 52)**: Indian equivalent of Fair Use; rigid, closed list of permitted purposes.
- **Internet Issues**: Mass reproduction, UGC, cross-border infringement, ISP liability.
- **ISP Safe Harbor**: Section 79 of IT Act, 2000; requires expeditious action on takedown notices.
- **DRM Protection**: Sections 65A & 65B criminalize circumvention of technological measures.
- **Berne Convention**: Automatic copyright in 179+ countries without registration.
- **WIPO Internet Treaties (1996)**: Form the basis of digital copyright internationally.
- **Anton Pillar Order**: Court order for seizure of infringing goods without prior notice.
- **Software Copyright**: Treated as literary work under Section 2(o) of Copyright Act, 1957.
- **Multimedia Layered Copyright**: Each component (text, image, music, video, code) is a separate copyrighted work requiring its own license.
- **Minimum Criminal Penalty**: 6 months imprisonment + Rs. 50,000 fine (first offense); 1 year + Rs. 1 lakh (repeat offense).
- **Key Indian Case**: *Super Cassettes Industries Ltd. v. Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd.* (2009) — ISP liability and takedown obligations.
- **Global Key Cases**: *Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios* (Betamax case, 1984) — time-shifting as fair use; *RIAA v. Napster* (2001) — contributory infringement by P2P platforms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Piracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Piracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Piracy** is the **unauthorized copying, distribution, modification, sale, or use** of software that violates the copyright, licensing terms, or intellectual property rights granted by the software's creator or publisher. It constitutes a direct infringement of the **Copyright Act, 1957** in India and is also prosecutable under the **Information Technology Act, 2000** as a cyber offence.

In the precise vocabulary of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, software piracy is classified as a violation of the **economic rights of the copyright holder** over their *literary work* (which legally includes computer programs under Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act, 1957). It encompasses all acts of unauthorized reproduction, translation, adaptation, or distribution of the protected software, irrespective of whether such use is for commercial gain or personal use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic (Industry Standard Metric):** According to the **BSA (Business Software Alliance) Global Software Survey**, the global average software piracy rate hovers around **37%**, with commercial value of unlicensed software exceeding **$46 billion USD** annually. India historically records a piracy rate between **60–70%**, representing one of the highest in the Asia-Pacific region.
