## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Borrowed Notebook" Intuition

Imagine you spent three months meticulously solving advanced calculus problems in a notebook, and you eventually publish it as a paid reference book at your college bookstore.

- **Authorized Use:** A friend buys your notebook at the bookstore. ✅
- **Authorized Use:** A college library buys **one copy** and lends it to students *one at a time* under fair-dealing. ✅
- **Software Piracy:** Your friend goes to a photocopy shop, duplicates your entire notebook, and gives free copies to **50 classmates** without paying you. ❌

That photocopy is **software piracy**. The *notebook* is the *software program*. The *photocopier* is the *CD/DVD writer or torrent client*. Your *classmates* are the *end-users* who use pirated copies.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Takeaway:** If you are *using*, *sharing*, or *selling* software without the explicit written permission (license) of the developer, you are engaging in software piracy — whether you earn money from it or not.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Software Piracy is a **high-frequency Module 4 question** because it lies at the intersection of three legal frameworks simultaneously:

1. **Intellectual Property Law** (Copyright Act, 1957)
2. **Cyber Law** (Information Technology Act, 2000)
3. **Contract Law** (End-User License Agreements — EULAs)

A 14-mark KTU question typically demands you to integrate all three frameworks in a single answer, demonstrating the *layered* nature of legal protection available to software in India.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Software Piracy — Root-Cause Framework

Software is uniquely vulnerable to piracy because of three structural characteristics:

- **Ease of Reproduction:** A 4.7 GB DVD can be cloned in **under 10 minutes**; an entire software suite can be replicated perfectly without any degradation in quality (unlike music or movies on cassette tapes).
- **Zero Marginal Cost:** The cost of producing one additional illegal copy is **effectively zero** — no raw materials, no manufacturing overhead.
- **Anonymity on Networks:** The Internet enables **global, anonymous distribution** via torrents, peer-to-peer (P2P) networks, and dark-web forums, making enforcement extraordinarily difficult.

## 2.2 The Seven Canonical Types of Software Piracy

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Gold Standard:** The seven types below are the **mandatory classification** expected in any 7-mark or 14-mark KTU answer. Memorize them as a numbered list.

| # | Type of Piracy | Mechanism | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Softlifting (End-User Piracy)** | Single licensed copy is shared among multiple users on different machines | Buying one copy of MS Office and installing it on 5 home computers |
| 2 | **Hard-Disk Loading** | Vendor pre-installs unauthorized copies onto PCs sold to customers | Local PC shop pre-installing pirated Windows on every laptop sold |
| 3 | **Counterfeiting** | Duplicating, packaging, and selling pirated software as genuine branded product | Fake "Microsoft Windows 11 Pro" DVDs sold on footpaths for ₹100 |
| 4 | **OEM Unbundling** | Buying OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) license at bulk discount and reselling individual licenses separately | Buying 100 OEM licenses and selling them separately to retail customers |
| 5 | **Internet Piracy (Warez / P2P)** | Distributing cracked software via websites, torrents, or file-sharing networks | Downloading cracked Adobe Photoshop from a torrent site |
| 6 | **Client-Server Overuse** | More users access a server-based software than the licensed number of seats permits | 50 employees using a software licensed for only 10 concurrent users |
| 7 | **Document/Theft of Source Code** | Unauthorized copying of proprietary source code, algorithms, or design documents | A disgruntled ex-employee uploading company source code to GitHub |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Legal Reference Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table consolidates **every statute, section, and penalty** examiners love to test. Reproduce it in your answer scripts.

| Statute | Relevant Section | Offence | Punishment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Copyright Act, 1957** | Section 13(1)(a) | Computer programs are protected as "literary works" | Civil + Criminal remedies |
| **Copyright Act, 1957** | Section 51(a)(i) | Reproducing the work without license | Infringement |
| **Copyright Act, 1957** | Section 63 | Criminal offence of infringement | **6 months – 3 years** imprisonment + fine **₹50,000 – ₹2,00,000** |
| **IT Act, 2000** | Section 65 | Tampering with computer source documents | Up to **3 years** imprisonment OR fine up to **₹2,00,000** |
| **IT Act, 2000** | Section 66 | Computer-related offences (hacking/dishonest use) | Up to **3 years** imprisonment OR fine up to **₹5,00,000** |
| **IT Act, 2000** | Section 66B | Receiving stolen computer resource dishonestly | Up to **3 years** imprisonment OR fine up to **₹1,00,000** |
| **IT Act, 2000** | Section 66E | Publishing/ transmitting private images of others (privacy violation) | Up to **3 years** imprisonment OR fine up to **₹2,00,000** |
| **IT Act, 2000** | Section 67 | Publishing obscene material in electronic form (first conviction) | Up to **5 years** imprisonment + fine up to **₹10,00,000** |

## 2.4 The Hierarchical Pillars of Anti-Piracy Law

The legal protection against software piracy in India rests on **three inter-locking pillars**:

1. **Pillar 1 — Civil Law (Copyright Act, Section 55):** The copyright owner can file a civil suit seeking **injunctions, damages, and account of profits** from the infringer.
2. **Pillar 2 — Criminal Law (Copyright Act, Section 63 + IT Act, Section 65):** Criminal prosecution leading to **imprisonment and fines** for the infringer.
3. **Pillar 3 — Administrative / Border Enforcement (Copyright Act, Section 53):** The copyright owner can request the **Commissioner of Customs** to seize pirated goods at Indian ports.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the IT industry, understanding software piracy is not merely academic:

- **Software Asset Management (SAM):** Every corporate IT department must conduct annual **software audits** to ensure compliance — failure to do so exposes the company to lawsuits.
- **Open-Source Licensing:** Modern engineers must understand **GPL, MIT, Apache licenses** to legally use open-source components — a violation constitutes commercial piracy.
- **Cybersecurity:** Pirated software often contains **malware, trojans, and ransomware**, making piracy a direct **attack vector** for enterprise networks.
- **Cybersecurity Law Practice:** Professionals in this domain advise on **BSA raid operations, customs seizures, and DMCA-equivalent takedown notices** under the IT Act.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Legal Derivation

## 3.1 The "Software Under Law" Decision Tree

To legally classify any act involving software, follow this **step-by-step logic chain**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 0:** Identify whether the software qualifies as a *literary work* under Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act. (All computer programs automatically qualify.)

**Step 1:** Determine if the *copyright* is still subsisting (life of the author + 60 years).

**Step 2:** Identify the act being performed — reproduction, distribution, modification, public performance, or display.

**Step 3:** Check if a **valid license (EULA)** has been granted by the copyright owner.

**Step 4:** If a license exists, verify whether the act performed falls **within the scope of the license**.

**Step 5:** If the act is *outside* the license scope → it constitutes **copyright infringement** under Section 51.

**Step 6:** Cross-check whether the act also triggers **Section 65 (IT Act)** — specifically, if source code was tampered with or distributed.

**Step 7:** Apply the **remedies hierarchy** — Civil (damages), Criminal (imprisonment), or Administrative (customs seizure).

## 3.2 Mathematical Style Representation of the Infringement Test

Although this is a law topic, the legal test can be expressed as a logical equation:

$$
\text{Infringement} = \begin{cases} \text{YES} & \text{if } (\text{Access to Work} = \text{True}) \land (\text{Substantial Similarity} = \text{True}) \land (\text{Valid License} = \text{False}) \\ \text{NO} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Penalty} = (\text{Imprisonment}_{min}, \text{Imprisonment}_{max}, \text{Fine}_{min}, \text{Fine}_{max})
$$

$$
\text{Penalty}_{Sec\,63} = (6\,\text{months},\; 3\,\text{years},\; \text{₹}50{,}000,\; \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000)
$$

$$
\text{Penalty}_{Sec\,65\,IT\,Act} = (0\,\text{years},\; 3\,\text{years},\; \text{₹}0,\; \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000)
$$

$$
\text{Penalty}_{Sec\,66\,IT\,Act} = (0\,\text{years},\; 3\,\text{years},\; \text{₹}0,\; \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000)
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Anatomy of a Software Piracy Prosecution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** When examiners ask *"Explain the legal remedies available against software piracy in India"* (a recurring 14-mark question), walk through this exact 5-stage pipeline.

### Stage 1 — Detection

The copyright owner (or the **BSA — Business Software Alliance**) conducts an undercover investigation or receives a tip-off. Common triggers:

- Customer support calls from complainants.
- Raid operations by the BSA on suspect distributors.
- Audit by IT Asset Management teams.
- Online monitoring of torrent networks.

### Stage 2 — Civil Suit (Section 55 of the Copyright Act)

The copyright holder files a civil suit seeking:

$$
\text{Relief} = \{\text{Temporary Injunction},\; \text{Permanent Injunction},\; \text{Damages},\; \text{Account of Profits},\; \text{Destruction of Infringing Goods}\}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Case Law (KTU Favourite):** *Microsoft Corporation v. Yogesh Papat (2005)* — the Delhi High Court granted a permanent injunction and awarded **₹23.7 lakhs** in damages against an individual who distributed pirated Microsoft software.

### Stage 3 — Criminal Complaint (Section 63 of the Copyright Act)

The copyright holder lodges an **FIR** (First Information Report) under Section 63. The police conduct raids and arrest offenders. The minimum punishment is **6 months imprisonment** — meaning the offence is **non-bailable and cognizable**.

### Stage 4 — Cyber Crime Prosecution (IT Act, 2000)

If the piracy occurred through digital means (torrents, websites, online distribution), parallel charges are filed under:

- **Section 65** — if source code was modified or distributed.
- **Section 66** — if unauthorized access or dishonest use of the computer resource occurred.
- **Section 67** — if obscene content was distributed via the software.

### Stage 5 — Border / Customs Enforcement (Section 53 of the Copyright Act)

The copyright owner files a notice with the **Customs Department** to seize pirated software at the port of entry. This is particularly effective against counterfeit CDs and DVDs imported from neighbouring countries.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis — Copyright Act vs IT Act

| Dimension | Copyright Act, 1957 | IT Act, 2000 |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Protection of creative / intellectual works | Regulation of electronic transactions and cyber offences |
| **Software Coverage** | Section 13(1)(a) — explicit | Section 65 — source code tampering |
| **Type of Remedy** | Civil + Criminal | Primarily Criminal |
| **Enforcement Body** | Police + Customs | Cyber Crime Cells + CERT-In |
| **Burden of Proof** | On copyright owner | On the prosecution / state |
| **Punishment Range** | 6 months – 3 years | Up to 3 years (Section 65), up to 5 years (Section 67) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Writing that the IT Act 2000 is the *primary* law for software piracy. The **correct hierarchy** is: **Copyright Act = primary law**; **IT Act = supplementary / cyber-specific law**.

## 3.5 International Treaties & India's Obligations

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Topic for KTU Module 4:** A 3-mark short note on *"International framework for software piracy"* is a recurring question. Cover these four treaties:

- **Berne Convention (1886):** Automatic copyright protection in 180+ member countries without registration. India became a member in **1928**.
- **TRIPS Agreement (1994):** Administered by the WTO. Mandates minimum 50-year copyright protection for computer programs.
- **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT, 1996):** Specifically addresses digital copyright protection; India ratified it in **2018**.
- **Madrid Protocol:** Not directly relevant to software but relevant to trademark (Microsoft, Adobe trademarks).

## 3.6 Landmark Case Laws (Model Answer Material)

| Case | Year | Court | Verdict / Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microsoft Corp. v. Yogesh Papat** | 2005 | Delhi High Court | Permanent injunction + ₹23.7 lakh damages for selling pirated Microsoft software |
| **Microsoft Corp. v. Rajendra Pawar** | 2008 | Delhi High Court | Landmark judgment on hard-disk loading piracy; awarded ₹34 lakhs damages |
| **Tata Consultancy Services v. State of Andhra Pradesh** | 2001 | Supreme Court | Held that computer programs are "literary works" under Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act |
| **Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak** | 2008 | Supreme Court | Established that software is an original literary work entitled to copyright |

## 3.7 Preventive Measures (Industry Best Practices)

> [!TIP]
> **Perfect 3-Mark Answer Frame:** When asked *"How can software piracy be prevented?"*, list these **seven control measures**:

1. **Software Audits** — Regular SAM (Software Asset Management) audits.
2. **License Management Tools** — Deploy tools like SCCM, ManageEngine, or JAMF.
3. **Digital Rights Management (DRM)** — Cryptographic access controls.
4. **Product Activation** — Online activation tied to hardware (used by Microsoft, Adobe).
5. **Education & Awareness** — Train employees on legal compliance.
6. **Open-Source Promotion** — Reduce piracy demand by promoting FOSS alternatives (Linux, LibreOffice).
7. **Legal Action** — Cooperative enforcement with BSA and industry coalitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define software piracy. List any four types of software piracy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Software piracy is the unauthorized copying, distribution, modification, sale, or use of software in violation of the copyright owner's license terms and intellectual property rights. It constitutes an infringement of Section 13(1)(a) of the Copyright Act, 1957, which protects computer programs as literary works.

**Four Types (1.5 marks):**

1. **Softlifting:** Sharing a single licensed copy across multiple users.
2. **Hard Disk Loading:** Vendors pre-installing pirated software onto new PCs.
3. **Counterfeiting:** Duplicating and selling pirated software disguised as genuine.
4. **Internet Piracy:** Distributing cracked software via torrents or websites.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**What are the remedies available under the Copyright Act, 1957 against software piracy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The remedies under the Copyright Act, 1957 are bifurcated into **civil** and **criminal** remedies:

- **Civil Remedies (Section 55):** Temporary and permanent injunction, damages, account of profits, and order for destruction of infringing goods.
- **Criminal Remedies (Section 63):** Imprisonment ranging from **6 months to 3 years** along with a fine ranging from **₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000**. The offence is cognizable and non-bailable.
- **Border Enforcement (Section 53):** The copyright owner can request the Customs Commissioner to seize pirated imports at Indian ports.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Module 4 essay questions offer an **internal choice**. Two alternatives are provided below — **Question A** and **Question B**. Choose any **one** in the exam.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the different types of software piracy with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the legal provisions under the Information Technology Act, 2000 to combat software piracy.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

Software piracy is the unauthorized reproduction or distribution of software. The seven principal types are:

**1. Softlifting (1 mark):** This is the most common form of end-user piracy. A single licensed copy is installed on multiple computers exceeding the user count permitted by the End-User License Agreement (EULA). For example, one MS Office license installed on five home computers.

**2. Hard Disk Loading (1 mark):** Computer vendors, especially local PC shops, pre-install unauthorized copies of expensive software onto new PCs before sale, often bundled with pirated operating systems like Windows.

**3. Counterfeiting (1 mark):** Infringers duplicate, package, and sell pirated software with forged branding and packaging, making it appear as genuine. These are typically sold at a fraction of the original price.

**4. OEM Unbundling (1 mark):** Software sold under Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) terms is meant to be bundled with new hardware. Unscrupulous resellers separate these licenses and sell them individually.

**5. Internet Piracy (1 mark):** Cracked versions of software are distributed via torrent sites, warez forums, and peer-to-peer (P2P) networks. This is the dominant piracy mode today.

**6. Client-Server Overuse (1 mark):** Networked software licensed for a specific number of concurrent users is accessed by more users than the license permits.

**7. Source Code Theft (1 mark):** Insiders or ex-employees steal and distribute proprietary source code — a particularly damaging form of piracy for software companies.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

The Information Technology Act, 2000 supplements the Copyright Act in addressing software piracy through these key provisions:

**Section 65 — Tampering with Computer Source Documents (1.5 marks):** Any person who knowingly conceals, destroys, alters, or discloses computer source code without authorization is liable to imprisonment up to **3 years** or a fine up to **₹2,00,000**. This directly targets pirated distribution and modification of software.

**Section 66 — Computer-Related Offences (1.5 marks):** Dishonest or fraudulent use of computer resources is punishable with imprisonment up to **3 years** or a fine up to **₹5,00,000**. Online piracy conducted through fraudulent means falls under this section.

**Section 66B — Receiving Stolen Computer Resource (1 mark):** Receiving or retaining stolen computer programs or data is punishable with up to 3 years imprisonment or fine up to ₹1 lakh.

**Section 67 — Publishing Obscene Material in Electronic Form (1 mark):** Distribution of obscene or harmful content via pirated software attracts up to 5 years imprisonment and fine up to ₹10 lakh.

**Jurisdictional Reach (1 mark):** Section 75 extends the IT Act's jurisdiction to offences committed outside India by any person, irrespective of nationality, if the computer system is located in India.

**Enforcement Agencies (1 mark):** Cyber Crime Cells, CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team), and state police enforce these provisions. Coordination with the BSA enables large-scale raids.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the legal protection available to computer software under the Copyright Act, 1957 in India. Cite at least two relevant case laws.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the international framework for combating software piracy. List the major treaties and India's obligations.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Software as a Literary Work (2 marks):** Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act, 1957 defines "literary work" to explicitly include **computer programs, tables, and compilations**. Section 13(1)(a) grants copyright protection to original literary works. Thus, computer software enjoys automatic copyright protection from the moment of creation — no registration is required.

**2. Rights Conferred on the Copyright Owner (2 marks):** The owner holds exclusive rights to:
- Reproduce the software (Section 14(a)).
- Issue copies to the public (Section 14(b)).
- Modify or translate the program (Section 14(c)).
- Store or transmit the program in any medium.

Any act performed without a valid license from the owner constitutes **infringement under Section 51**.

**3. Remedies (2 marks):**
- **Civil (Section 55):** Injunctions, damages, account of profits.
- **Criminal (Section 63):** 6 months to 3 years imprisonment plus fine.
- **Border (Section 53):** Customs seizure of pirated goods.

**4. Landmark Case Laws (1 mark):**
- ***Tata Consultancy Services v. State of Andhra Pradesh (2001):*** The Supreme Court ruled that computer programs are literary works under Section 2(o), thereby confirming statutory copyright protection.
- ***Microsoft Corporation v. Yogesh Papat (2005):*** The Delhi High Court awarded ₹23.7 lakhs in damages and granted a permanent injunction against a distributor of pirated Microsoft software.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works, 1886 (2 marks):** India is a member since 1928. It mandates automatic copyright protection in all member countries without registration. Computer programs are protected as literary works. India must extend the same protection to foreign works that it gives to its own nationals.

**2. TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights), 1994 (2 marks):** Administered by the WTO, TRIPS obliges member countries (including India) to provide **minimum 50 years** of copyright protection for computer programs. It also mandates enforcement mechanisms including criminal penalties for willful piracy on a commercial scale.

**3. WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT), 1996 (2 marks):** Specifically addresses the challenges of digital copyright. India ratified it in **2018**. It mandates legal protection against circumvention of technological measures (DRM) and requires legal remedies against unauthorized distribution of copyrighted material online.

**4. India's Compliance (1 mark):** India has aligned its Copyright Act and IT Act with these treaties. The Copyright (Amendment) Act 2012 specifically introduced provisions to protect digital rights management and address online piracy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mistake:** Writing that software piracy is covered **only** by the IT Act 2000.
>    **Correction:** The Copyright Act 1957 is the **primary statute**; the IT Act is **supplementary**.
>
> 2. **Mistake:** Saying copyright in India requires **registration**.
>    **Correction:** Copyright is **automatic** upon creation under the Berne Convention principle — no registration is mandatory.
>
> 3. **Mistake:** Confusing the punishment under Section 63 (Copyright Act) with Section 66 (IT Act).
>    **Correction:** Section 63 has a **minimum 6-month** imprisonment; Section 66 has **no minimum** but a maximum of 3 years.
>
> 4. **Mistake:** Listing **counterfeiting** and **softlifting** as the same type of piracy.
>    **Correction:** Counterfeiting involves **fake branded packaging**; softlifting is simply **sharing a single genuine license**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision (Read Before Exam):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Software piracy = unauthorized use / copy / distribution of software.
- [ ] **Primary Law:** Copyright Act, 1957 — Section 13(1)(a) protects software as a "literary work."
- [ ] **Supplementary Law:** IT Act, 2000 — Sections 65, 66, 66B, 67.
- [ ] **Seven Types:** Softlifting, Hard Disk Loading, Counterfeiting, OEM Unbundling, Internet Piracy, Client-Server Overuse, Source Code Theft.
- [ ] **Civil Remedy (Section 55):** Injunction + damages + account of profits.
- [ ] **Criminal Remedy (Section 63):** **6 months to 3 years** jail + ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 fine.
- [ ] **Border Remedy (Section 53):** Customs seizure.
- [ ] **IT Act Section 65:** Source code tampering — 3 years jail or ₹2 lakh fine.
- [ ] **IT Act Section 66:** Computer-related offence — 3 years jail or ₹5 lakh fine.
- [ ] **TRIPS Mandate:** Minimum **50-year** copyright protection for software.
- [ ] **Berne Convention Principle:** Automatic copyright without registration.
- [ ] **WCT Ratification by India:** **2018**.
- [ ] **Key Case:** *Microsoft v. Yogesh Papat (2005)* — ₹23.7 lakh damages.
- [ ] **Key Case:** *TCS v. State of AP (2001)* — software is a literary work.
- [ ] **BSA Role:** Conducts raids and publishes the **Global Software Piracy Report**.
- [ ] **India's Piracy Rate:** Historically around **60–70%** (highest in APAC).
- [ ] **Three Pillars of Anti-Piracy Law:** Civil + Criminal + Administrative (Customs).
- [ ] **Open-Source Counter-Measure:** FOSS alternatives (Linux, LibreOffice) reduce piracy demand.
- [ ] **DRM:** Digital Rights Management — cryptographic access control.
- [ ] **SAM:** Software Asset Management — corporate audit mechanism.
- [ ] **EULA:** End-User License Agreement — contract law basis for piracy cases.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patents: Understanding Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Patents: Understanding Patents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Patent** is an exclusive statutory right granted by the Government (Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks, India) to an inventor for a limited period of **20 years** in exchange for the full public disclosure of the invention. It confers upon the patentee the **negative right** to exclude others from making, using, selling, offering for sale, or importing the patented invention without prior permission or a valid license.

In India, patents are governed primarily by the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005, in compliance with the TRIPS Agreement of the WTO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "A patent is a time-bound, territorial, statutory monopoly granted by the State to a true and first inventor (or his assignee) for a new, useful, and non-obvious invention, in exchange for complete public disclosure of the invention, enabling the patentee to exclude third parties from exploiting the invention commercially."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Land Deed for an Idea"

Think of a patent like a **land deed for an invisible piece of land — your idea**.

Imagine you invent a new type of folding chair that magically turns into a backpack. Just like you can buy a piece of land and the government gives you a deed that legally stops others from building on it, a patent gives you a "deed" that legally stops others from **copying, making, or selling** your folding-backpack chair for **20 years**.

However, there are three big conditions (just like land deeds require you to show the land is yours, not in someone else's name, and is a real piece of land):

1. **Novelty** — Nobody must have owned this "invisible land" before (it must be NEW globally).
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obvious)** — It cannot be a trivial improvement that any ordinary skilled person would obviously think of.
3. **Industrial Application** — The "land" must be usable for something practical (not just a theoretical daydream).

In return, you must give the government a complete map of your invention (a **patent specification**) so that, after the 20 years are up, society can freely use the invention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Patent is NOT a "Positive Right":**
> A patent does NOT give the patentee the right to MAKE or USE the invention themselves. For example, a patented improvement to a car engine still requires the car manufacturer's permission to be installed. The patent only gives the right to **stop others** from exploiting the invention.

## 1.3 Essential Characteristics of a Patent (KTU Board-Ready Bullets)

| Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Territorial Right** | Valid only in the country where it is granted (a US patent is NOT enforceable in India). |
| **Time-Limited** | Standard duration is **20 years** from the date of filing. |
| **Negative Right** | Right to exclude others; not a positive right to practice. |
| **Statutory Right** | Must be applied for; cannot be acquired by mere use or possession. |
| **Assignable / Licensable** | Can be sold, gifted, inherited, or licensed for royalty. |
| **Public Disclosure** | The complete specification is published after 18 months. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Statutory Provision (KTU High-Yield):**
> Section 2(1)(j) of the Patents Act, 1970, defines "invention" as:
> *"a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application."*
> Section 2(1)(m) defines "patent" as *"a patent for any invention granted under this Act."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 Criteria for Patentability (The Three Pillars)

Under **Section 2(1)(j)** and **Sections 3, 4, 5** of the Patents Act, 1970, an invention must satisfy **three essential criteria** to be patentable in India.

### Pillar 1: Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))
- The invention must be **NEW** — it must not have been published, used, or known anywhere in the world **before the date of filing** (or priority date).
- Any prior public disclosure (paper, conference, website, sale, etc.) **destroys novelty**.
- **Absolute Novelty** is followed in India (worldwide prior art destroys novelty).

### Pillar 2: Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))
- The invention must involve a **non-obvious technical feature** to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA — Person Having Ordinary Skill In The Art).
- It must represent a **technical advancement** over existing knowledge OR have **economic significance**.
- Simply combining two known features without synergy is generally NOT patentable.

### Pillar 3: Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))
- The invention must be capable of being **made or used in some kind of industry** (manufacturing, agriculture, services).
- Pure scientific theories, mathematical methods, and abstract ideas are excluded.

## 2.2 Non-Patentable Inventions (Section 3 — Most Critical KTU Section)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 3 — Exclusions from Patentability (MEMORIZE THIS LIST):**
> 1. **Frivolous inventions** contrary to natural laws.
> 2. Inventions contrary to **public order, morality, or human/animal/plant life/health**.
> 3. **Discovery** of any living thing or non-living substance occurring in nature.
> 4. **Scientific principles, mathematical methods, business methods, mental acts** (or playing a game / scheme / rule of doing business).
> 5. **Computer programs per se** (only algorithms/software "as such" are excluded — but software with hardware novelty IS patentable in India post-2017 Guidelines).
> 6. **Plants and animals in whole or any part** (other than micro-organisms).
> 7. **Traditional knowledge** and **atomic energy** related inventions.
> 8. **Mere admixture or rearrangement** of known components.
> 9. **Method of agriculture/horticulture** (Section 3(h)).
> 10. **Inventions relating to therapeutic treatment** of humans/animals (drugs themselves ARE patentable).
> 11. **Pre-grant opposition** matters under Section 25(1).

## 2.3 Patentable vs Non-Patentable — Quick Reference Table

| Subject Matter | Patentable? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| A new chemical drug (e.g., a new anti-cancer molecule) | ✅ Yes | Product patent, industrial application |
| A new smartphone hardware circuit | ✅ Yes | Technical advancement + hardware |
| A new business method (e.g., a one-click checkout) | ❌ No | Business method (Section 3(k)) |
| Software algorithm alone | ❌ No | Computer program per se |
| Software + novel hardware integration | ✅ Yes | Combined technical effect |
| A new cricket bat with optimized weight distribution | ✅ Yes | Novel product, industrial use |
| A rule of a board game | ❌ No | Mental act / scheme |
| Discovery of a new star | ❌ No | Mere discovery in nature |
| A new variety of rice plant | ❌ No | Excluded under Section 3(j) |

## 2.4 Types of Patent Applications (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Ordinary Application** | No priority claim; standard processing | Indian-only filing |
| **Convention Application** | Filed within **12 months** of first filing in a Convention country (Paris Convention) | Foreign priority claim |
| **PCT National Phase Application** | Filed via **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** within 31 months of priority date | Global patent strategy |
| **Provisional Specification** | Establishes priority date; only describes the invention broadly | Early-stage filing |
| **Complete Specification** | Full technical disclosure with claims; filed within **12 months** of provisional | Final patent grant |

## 2.5 Rights of a Patentee (Section 48)

Once a patent is granted, the patentee has the **exclusive right** to:

1. **Make** the patented product.
2. **Use** the patented product or process.
3. **Sell or offer for sale** the patented product.
4. **Import** the patented product into India.
5. **License** the patent to others (voluntary licensing under Section 84).
6. **Assign** the patent to others.

> [!NOTE]
> **Term of Patent (Section 53):**
> - Product Patent: **20 years** from date of filing.
> - Process Patent: **20 years** from date of filing.
> - Annuity fees must be paid annually from year 3 onwards to keep the patent in force.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern engineering and R&D ecosystems, patents serve as:

- **Strategic moats** in semiconductor, pharmaceutical, and software industries (e.g., Intel's x86, Apple's iPhone technologies).
- **Revenue streams** through licensing (e.g., Qualcomm's 5G licensing model).
- **Indicators of innovation** for academic ranking, startup valuations, and CSR credits.
- **Defensive shields** for universities and startups against IP trolls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Legal Landmark:**
> **Novartis AG v. Union of India (2013)** — The Supreme Court upheld Section 3(d) of the Patents Act, 1970, which prevents **evergreening** (minor modifications of known substances to obtain fresh patents). This is a **highly-referenced KTU case law**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Patent Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Patent Filing Lifecycle (Analytical Step-by-Step Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> **Context for Students:** Unlike formula derivations, the "analytical derivation" in IPR is the **procedural derivation** — how a right crystallizes from an idea into a granted patent. This is what KTU examiners test.

### Stage 1 — Invention Disclosure
The inventor documents the invention in a laboratory notebook with dates, witnesses, and detailed technical drawings. This serves as **evidentiary backup** for proving inventorship in case of disputes.

### Stage 2 — Prior Art Search
A comprehensive search is conducted across databases (Indian Patent Advanced Search System — **IPAIS**, Google Patents, WIPO PATENTSCOPE, Espacenet) to ensure **Novelty** and **Inventive Step** over existing global prior art.

### Stage 3 — Filing of Application (Form 1, 2, 3, 5)

The applicant files at the Indian Patent Office (Kolkata, Delhi, Mumbai, or Chennai) with:

- **Form 1** — Application for grant of patent.
- **Form 2** — Provisional or Complete Specification.
- **Form 3** — Statement and undertaking regarding foreign filings.
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship.

> **If Provisional Specification is filed:**
> An **early priority date** is secured, but a Complete Specification must be filed within **12 months**.

> **If Complete Specification is filed directly:**
> No priority date advantage, but the application proceeds to publication.

### Stage 4 — Publication (Section 11A)
The application is **published automatically after 18 months** from the filing date (or priority date). After publication, the applicant gains **"Patent Pending"** status and may claim **reasonable remuneration** for infringing acts during the interim period (Section 11A(7)).

### Stage 5 — Request for Examination (Section 11B / Rule 24B)
The applicant (or any interested third party) must file a **Request for Examination** within **48 months** of the priority date (extended from 36 months in 2021 amendments). If not filed, the application is treated as withdrawn.

### Stage 6 — Examination by the Patent Office
A **Patent Examiner** examines the application against:

- Section 3 (non-patentable matter).
- Section 4 (inventions relating to atomic energy).
- Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application.
- Unity of Invention.
- Clarity, completeness, and support in the specification.

The examiner issues a **First Examination Report (FER)** listing objections. The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable up to 3 months).

### Stage 7 — Hearing and Reply
The Controller hears the applicant, considers amendments (if any), and decides whether to grant or refuse the patent.

### Stage 8 — Grant of Patent (Section 43)
If accepted, the patent is granted, and a **Certificate of Patent** is issued. The grant is published in the **Patent Journal**. The patent is **deemed to be granted from the date of publication of the application** (Section 43(2)).

### Stage 9 — Opposition (Pre-Grant or Post-Grant)

- **Pre-Grant Opposition (Section 25(1))** — Any person may oppose at any time after publication and before grant. Common in India (used by generic pharma companies — e.g., the **Gleevec opposition by Natco**).
- **Post-Grant Opposition (Section 25(2))** — Within **12 months** of grant announcement.

### Stage 10 — Renewal and Maintenance
Annuity fees are payable starting from the **3rd year** onwards. Failure to pay leads to lapse.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Patent Term Calculation (KTU Numerical Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU Numerical:**
> An inventor filed a complete patent application on **01-January-2020**. The patent was granted on **01-December-2023**. Calculate the **expiry date** of the patent and the **last annuity date** before expiry, assuming renewals must be paid every year starting from the 3rd year anniversary.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Determine the Term of Patent:**
Under Section 53(2), the term is **20 years from the date of filing**.

$$
\text{Filing Date} = \text{01-Jan-2020}
$$

$$
\text{Expiry Date} = \text{01-Jan-2020} + \text{20 years}
$$

$$
\text{Expiry Date} = \text{01-January-2040}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the Last Annuity Payment Year:**

Under Rule 80, renewal fees are due from the **3rd year onwards**. The last annuity is the **20th year** (since the patent is alive for 20 years).

$$
\text{Last Annuity Due Date} = \text{01-January-2040 (on or before expiry)}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification of Pending Renewal Months:**

$$
\text{Filing Date} = \text{01-Jan-2020}
$$

$$
\text{3rd Year Anniversary} = \text{01-Jan-2023}
$$

$$
\text{First Annuity Paid By} = \text{01-Jan-2023}
$$

$$
\text{Last Annuity} = \text{01-Jan-2040}
$$

> **Final Answer (Board-style):**
> The patent expires on **01-January-2040**, and the last annuity payment must be made on or before **01-January-2040** (with a 6-month grace window under Rule 81).

## 3.3 Patent Infringement Analysis (Civil + Criminal Framework)

### A. Civil Remedies (Section 104)
1. **Injunction** (temporary, permanent, ad-interim) — the most powerful remedy.
2. **Damages or account of profits**.
3. **Delivery-up or destruction** of infringing goods.
4. **Declaration** that the patent is valid and infringed.

### B. Criminal Remedies (Section 118)
- Imprisonment up to **3 years**, OR
- Fine up to **₹2,00,000** (two lakh rupees), OR
- Both.
- Cognizable, bailable, and non-compoundable.

### C. Defenses Against Infringement (Section 107)
- Prior user (Bolar exemption for research).
- Innocence (no knowledge of patent).
- Patent invalidity.
- Government use (Section 47).
- Parallel imports from licensee.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolar Exemption (Section 107A):**
> This allows generic drug manufacturers to use patented inventions **solely for the purpose of generating data** required for regulatory approval — even before the patent expires. This is critical for the Indian pharmaceutical industry (e.g., post-2005 product patent era).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Patent Application Flow — From Idea to Grant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Idea Inception and Lab Notebook] --> B[Prior Art Search in IPAIS and PATENTSCOPE]
    B --> C{Novelty and Inventive Step Confirmed?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Abandon Idea or Modify]
    C -- Yes --> D[File Form 1, Form 2 Provisional, Form 3, Form 5]
    D --> E[Provisional Filing Date Recorded]
    E --> F[Within 12 Months File Complete Specification Form 2]
    F --> G[Publication After 18 Months Section 11A]
    G --> H[File Request for Examination Within 48 Months]
    H --> I[Examiner Issues FER First Examination Report]
    I --> J{Applicant Satisfies FER?}
    J -- No --> K[Hearing Before Controller]
    K --> J
    J -- Yes --> L[Patent Granted Section 43]
    L --> M[Post Grant Opposition Within 12 Months]
    M --> N{Opposition Successful?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Patent Revoked]
    N -- No --> P[Patent Enforced for 20 Years with Annuity Payments]
```

## 4.2 Patent Rights Architecture — Surrounding the Patentee

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Holder["Patent Holder Exclusivity"]
        A[Make] 
        B[Use] 
        C[Sell] 
        D[Import] 
        E[License and Assign]
    end
    subgraph Public["Public Domain After 20 Years"]
        F[Anyone May Freely Use the Invention]
    end
    subgraph Government["Government Use Section 47"]
        G[Royalty Paid to Patentee]
    end
    subgraph ThirdParty["Third Party Use"]
        H[Requires License or Consent]
    end
    Holder -- Exclusion Right --> ThirdParty
    Holder -- Monopoly Period --> Public
    Government -- Compulsory License Section 84 --> Holder
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Patentability Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Invention Disclosure] --> Q1{Is it a Discovery or Scientific Theory?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Stop1[Not Patentable Section 3 c]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it a Business Method or Mental Act?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Stop2[Not Patentable Section 3 k]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does it relate to Atomic Energy?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Stop3[Not Patentable Section 4]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Does it involve only Evergreening Section 3 d?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Stop4[Not Patentable Section 3 d]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Has it been published globally before?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Stop5[Lacks Novelty]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Would PHOSITA find it obvious?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Stop6[Lacks Inventive Step]
    Q6 -- No --> Q7{Industrial Application Possible?}
    Q7 -- No --> Stop7[Lacks Industrial Application]
    Q7 -- Yes --> Pass[Patentable Proceed to Filing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in exams:**
> - **Flowchart 4.1** — Use this to explain the **chronological procedure** of a patent in a 14-mark answer.
> - **Flowchart 4.2** — Use this to demonstrate the **rights of the patentee**.
> - **Flowchart 4.3** — Use this for the **decision-making algorithm** the Patent Examiner follows — examiners often award full marks for showing this logical decision flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define the term "Patent" as per the Patents Act, 1970. List any FOUR essential characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A **patent** is a statutory right granted by the Government to an inventor (or his assignee) for a new, useful, and non-obvious invention, providing the exclusive right to exclude third parties from making, using, selling, or importing the patented invention for a limited period of **20 years**, in exchange for full public disclosure.

**Four essential characteristics:**
1. **Territorial right** — valid only in the country of grant.
2. **Time-limited** — duration of 20 years from filing.
3. **Negative right** — only the right to exclude others.
4. **Assignable and licensable** — can be sold or licensed.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Provisional Specification and Complete Specification.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Provisional Specification | Complete Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Establishes priority date early | Final detailed disclosure |
| **Filing time** | At the very start of filing | Within 12 months of provisional |
| **Detail level** | Broad description only | Full technical disclosure with claims |
| **Claims** | Not mandatory | Mandatory with definite claims |
| **Legal effect** | Secures priority date only | Forms the basis of patent grant |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choose Either A or B]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Module 4 Most Expected Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **procedure for the grant of a patent** in India under the Patents Act, 1970. Mention the role of the **First Examination Report (FER)** and the opposition provisions.

**OR**

**(b)** Discuss the **non-patentable inventions** under Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970. Critically analyze the **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** judgment and its impact on the Indian pharmaceutical industry.

---

### ✅ Model Answer for **Q. (a)** — Full 14-Mark Solution

#### 1. Introduction (2 Marks)
The grant of a patent in India is a multi-stage administrative and quasi-judicial process administered by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** under the Patents Act, 1970. The process begins with the filing of an application and culminates in the grant or refusal of a patent.

#### 2. Stages of Patent Grant (8 Marks — Sub-Question A)

**Stage 1 — Filing of Application (Form 1, 2, 3, 5):**
The applicant files Form 1 (Application), Form 2 (Specification — Provisional or Complete), Form 3 (Foreign filing undertaking), and Form 5 (Declaration of inventorship) at the appropriate Patent Office (Kolkata, Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai).

**Stage 2 — Publication (Section 11A):**
The application is published after **18 months** from the filing date or priority date. Upon publication, the applicant acquires **"Patent Pending"** status.

**Stage 3 — Request for Examination (Rule 24B):**
Filed within **48 months** of the priority date. Without this request, the application lapses.

**Stage 4 — Examination and FER:**
The examiner conducts a **novelty, inventive step, and industrial application** search and issues the **First Examination Report (FER)** within 6 months. The FER lists:
- Prior art objections.
- Section 3 (non-patentable) objections.
- Clarity, unity, and support objections.

The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable by 3 months).

**Stage 5 — Hearing and Decision (Section 14):**
If the FER is not complied with, a hearing is scheduled before the **Controller of Patents**. The applicant may propose **amendments** under Section 57.

**Stage 6 — Grant of Patent (Section 43):**
If accepted, the patent is granted, and a **Certificate of Patent** is issued. The patent is deemed to take effect from the date of publication (Section 43(2)).

#### 3. Opposition Provisions (4 Marks — Sub-Question B)

**Pre-Grant Opposition (Section 25(1)):**
- Available at **any time after publication and before grant**.
- Any person may oppose by filing written representation.
- Common grounds: lack of novelty, Section 3 violations, non-disclosure of information.
- **Example:** Natco's opposition to Novartis's Gleevec.

**Post-Grant Opposition (Section 25(2)):**
- Available within **12 months** of the grant announcement.
- Filed by any interested person.

The **Patent Opposition Board** under Section 25(3) considers both oppositions.

#### 4. Conclusion (0 Marks, but conceptually finishes the answer)
The Indian patent grant procedure is rigorous, balancing **inventor protection** with **public interest** and **TRIPS compliance** (post-2005 amendments).

---

### ✅ Model Answer for **Q. (b)** — Full 14-Mark Solution

#### 1. Introduction (2 Marks)
Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970, provides a comprehensive list of **exclusions** from patentability. These exclusions reflect India's policy considerations balancing innovation incentives with public welfare.

#### 2. Non-Patentable Inventions (Section 3) — Key Categories (8 Marks)

- **Section 3(a)** — Frivolous inventions contrary to natural laws (e.g., perpetual motion).
- **Section 3(b)** — Inventions contrary to public order, morality, or human/animal/plant life.
- **Section 3(c)** — Discoveries of natural substances.
- **Section 3(d)** — New forms of known substances not showing enhanced efficacy (evergreening).
- **Section 3(e)** — Mere admixtures or rearrangements.
- **Section 3(f)** — Mere juxtaposition of known features.
- **Section 3(i)** — Methods of agriculture/horticulture.
- **Section 3(j)** — Plants and animals (excluding micro-organisms).
- **Section 3(k)** — Business methods, mathematical methods, computer programs per se.

#### 3. Novartis v. Union of India (2013) Analysis (4 Marks)

The Supreme Court, in this landmark case, upheld **Section 3(d)** and rejected **Novartis's application** for the beta crystalline form of **Imatinib Mesylate (Gleevec)**.

**Court's Reasoning:**
- The new form did not show significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy over the known form.
- Allowing such "evergreening" would:
  - Perpetuate monopolies.
  - Increase drug prices.
  - Harm public health access.
  - Defeat the spirit of Section 3(d).

**Impact on Indian Pharma:**
- Strengthened India's **generic drug industry**.
- Confirmed India's commitment to **public health over MNC commercial interests**.
- Became a global precedent on the **patentability of new forms of known substances**.

#### 4. Conclusion (Conceptual Closer)
The Section 3 exclusions, validated by Novartis, represent India's **flexible interpretation of TRIPS**, prioritizing public welfare while still incentivizing genuine innovation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — Answer Either A or B]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Past Year Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Define **patent infringement**. Explain its **types**, **defenses**, and the **civil and criminal remedies** available under the Patents Act, 1970.

**OR**

**(b)** Explain the **rights of a patentee** under Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the concept of **Compulsory Licensing under Section 84** with suitable examples.

---

### ✅ Model Answer for **Q. (a)** — Full 14-Mark Solution

#### 1. Definition of Patent Infringement (2 Marks)
Patent infringement is the **unauthorized making, using, selling, offering for sale, or importing** of a patented invention within the territory of the grant, without the consent of the patentee.

#### 2. Types of Infringement (6 Marks)

**Direct Infringement:**
- Literal infringement (exact replica of claimed features).
- Doctrine of equivalents (function-way-result test).

**Indirect / Contributory Infringement:**
- Supplying means intended to be used in the patented invention, knowing that they are so intended (rare in India).

**Infringement by Relaying:**
- Infringing while knowing the invention is patented.

#### 3. Defenses (Section 107) (3 Marks)

1. **Innocence** — No actual knowledge of patent.
2. **Prior Use** — Continuous prior commercial use before priority date.
3. **Bolar Exemption** — Use for regulatory data generation (Section 107A).
4. **Government Use** — Use by Government under Section 47.
5. **Invalidity of Patent**.

#### 4. Remedies (3 Marks)

**Civil:**
- Injunction, damages, account of profits, delivery up of goods.

**Criminal:**
- Imprisonment up to 3 years, fine up to ₹2,00,000 (Section 118).

---

### ✅ Model Answer for **Q. (b)** — Full 14-Mark Solution

#### 1. Rights of a Patentee (Section 48) — 7 Marks

The patentee has the **exclusive right** to:
1. Make the patented product.
2. Use the patented process/product.
3. Sell/offer for sale the patented product.
4. Import the patented product.
5. License (voluntary) and assign.

These rights are **negative** (right to exclude) and **territorial** (valid only in India).

#### 2. Compulsory Licensing — Section 84 — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
A compulsory license is a **non-voluntary authorization** granted by the Controller of Patents to a third party to produce the patented invention, without the patentee's consent, subject to payment of royalty.

**Grounds (Section 84(1)):**
- Reasonable requirements of the public have not been met.
- Patented invention is not available at reasonably affordable prices.
- Patented invention is not worked in India.

**Procedural Conditions:**
- Three years must have elapsed since grant.
- Applicant must demonstrate prior efforts to obtain a voluntary license.

**Landmark Indian Case:**
**Natco Pharma Ltd. v. Bayer Corporation (2012)** — The first compulsory license in India was granted to Natco for **Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar)** for kidney/liver cancer, due to Bayer's failure to make the drug available at affordable prices.

**Significance:**
- Balances IP rights with **public health access**.
- Affirms India's flexibility under TRIPS (Doha Declaration 2001).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse patent with copyright or trademark — many students mix up terms like "literary work" (copyright) with patents.
> 2. **Do NOT** write "patents last forever" — they last exactly **20 years**.
> 3. **Do NOT** state that computer programs are NEVER patentable — they may be patentable when bundled with a **technical contribution or hardware novelty**.
> 4. **Always cite Section numbers** (e.g., Section 3(d), Section 48, Section 84) — examiners reward statutory references.
> 5. **Avoid** saying "Patent gives right to use" — it gives only the **right to exclude**.
> 6. In long answers, **draw the flow diagram** (use Mermaid-style logic on paper) — diagrams fetch extra marks.
> 7. **Always mention the case name + year** for jurisprudence questions (Novartis 2013, Natco 2012).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Patent** = Statutory, territorial, time-limited (20 years), negative right.
- ✅ **Governed by Patents Act, 1970** (India), amended in **2005** to comply with **TRIPS**.
- ✅ **Three Criteria for Patentability:** Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application.
- ✅ **Section 3** lists non-patentable subject matter (memorize at least 7 of them).
- ✅ **Section 3(d)** prevents evergreening — confirmed by **Novartis v. UoI (2013)**.
- ✅ **Term = 20 years** from filing date; **Annuity fees** start from year 3.
- ✅ **Publication** occurs at 18 months; **Examination Request** within 48 months.
- ✅ **FER** must be responded to within 6 months (extendable by 3 months).
- ✅ **Opposition:** Pre-grant (anytime before grant, Section 25(1)); Post-grant (within 12 months, Section 25(2)).
- ✅ **Infringement** remedies: Injunction, Damages, Criminal prosecution (3 years / ₹2 lakh).
- ✅ **Bolar Exemption (Section 107A)** allows research and regulatory approval use.
- ✅ **Compulsory License (Section 84)** — first granted to **Natco for Nexavar (2012)**.
- ✅ **Patent = "Right to Exclude"**, NOT a right to make/use.
- ✅ **Sections to memorize for KTU Board:** 2(1)(j), 2(1)(m), 3, 3(d), 11A, 11B, 25, 43, 48, 53, 84, 107, 118.
- ✅ **One-line definition** (must be verbatim for 3-mark questions).
- ✅ **Diagram drawing** in long answers = extra 1–2 marks.
- ✅ **Case laws:** Novartis 2013 (Section 3(d) + Evergreening), Natco 2012 (Compulsory License).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: European Position on Computer Related Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** European Position on Computer Related Patents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Under the **European Patent Convention (EPC), 1973**, a *computer-related invention* (often called a "computer-implemented invention" or CII) is treated as a special category within the law of patentable subject matter. The position of the **European Patent Office (EPO)**, located in **Munich, Germany**, is that the mere fact that a claimed invention makes use of a computer, a computer network, or a software program does **not, by itself, render the invention unpatentable**.

The decisive legal hooks are:

- **Article 52(1) EPC** — Patents shall be granted for any *inventions* in *all fields of technology*, provided they are **new**, involve an **inventive step**, and are susceptible of **industrial application**.
- **Article 52(2)(c) EPC** — *"Programs for computers"* are listed as items that "shall not be regarded as inventions."
- **Article 52(3) EPC** — The exclusion operates *"only to the extent to which a European patent application or European patent relates to such subject-matter … as such."*

The phrase **"as such"** is the single most important qualifier in the entire European position. It was inserted precisely to prevent the wholesale denial of patents to software, and it is the linguistic basis on which almost the entire modern EPO case law has been built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition to Memorise**
> *"A computer program 'as such' is excluded from patentability under Article 52(2) EPC, but a computer-implemented invention that produces a further technical effect beyond the normal physical interactions between software and hardware is patentable."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe vs. Machine" Picture

Imagine two cooks, **Aisha** and **Rohit**, who each write down a recipe for the *same* cake.

- Aisha writes the recipe in words on paper — the words themselves are just instructions. Under EPC, the **recipe (the pure program) is the "program for computers as such."** It is a mental/abstract construct and is excluded.
- Rohit, however, does not just write the recipe — he also invents a **new type of oven** with a special sensor and a steam-injection valve that automatically follows the recipe and produces a cake with a unique texture that no human baker could consistently replicate.

The EPO's position is essentially: **Rohit's invention is patentable** because the *contribution* is a new technical machine, not just a list of steps. Aisha's list of steps, without the oven, is not.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Analogy Matters in KTU Exams**
> The European position is *not* that software cannot be patented. It is that **software alone ("as such")** cannot. The moment the claim recites a hardware element, a technical process, or a "further technical effect," the exclusion falls away. Examiners award marks to students who clearly distinguish **"the program"** from **"the computer-implemented invention."**

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Pillars of the European Position

1. **Article 52 EPC** — the *statutory* text (1973, last revised 2000).
2. **Case law of the Boards of Appeal** — the *living* interpretation (VICOM, IBM, COMVIK, Aerotel, Dollfus Mieg).
3. **Guidelines for Examination in the EPO (G-part, Chapter II-3)** — the *administrative* framework used by examiners, currently aligned with the *COMVIK* approach.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statutory Architecture: How Article 52 EPC Operates

The EPC structures patent eligibility in **three tiers**, applied sequentially:

| Tier | Provision | What It Does | KTU Memory Hook |
|------|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Tier 1** | **Art. 52(1) EPC** | *Positive grant* — invention in any field of technology if new, inventive, industrially applicable. | "Yes, in principle, anything technical is patentable." |
| **Tier 2** | **Art. 52(2) EPC** | *Negative list* — discoveries, mental acts, **programs for computers**, business methods, presentations of information, mathematical methods, aesthetic creations. | "Five blacklisted categories." |
| **Tier 3** | **Art. 52(3) EPC** | *The "as such" escape valve* — exclusion applies only if the invention is the excluded matter **as such**. | "If there is a technical effect, the exclusion does not bite." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Three Tiers?**
> The drafters of the EPC deliberately rejected an *a priori* list of patentable subject matter (like "chemicals" or "machines") and instead used an open-ended positive grant (Tier 1) combined with a narrow list of exclusions (Tier 2), softened by an "as such" qualifier (Tier 3). This structure is what allows software-heavy inventions to slip through.

## 2.2 The "Further Technical Effect" Doctrine

The single most-tested concept in KTU papers on this topic. A computer-implemented invention is **not excluded** if it yields a **"further technical effect"** — a technical effect going beyond the *intrinsic* technical effect that every software program has merely by virtue of being executed on hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Levels of Technical Effect**
> - **Intrinsic technical effect** — *any* software running on *any* computer produces voltages, current flows, and memory state changes. This is **not** enough to overcome the exclusion.
> - **Further technical effect** — an effect on the **physical world, on a process carried on outside the computer, or on the internal functioning of the computer itself in a non-trivial way** (e.g., controlling an industrial robot, improving network security, enhancing image resolution). This **is** enough.

### Examples Categorised by the EPO Approach

| Scenario | Patentable under EPC? | Reason |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| A pure algorithm for sorting numbers on a generic computer | **No** | Program "as such" — only intrinsic effect. |
| A sorting algorithm with modified memory allocation that **reduces memory fragmentation by 40%** | **Yes** | Further technical effect on internal computer operation. |
| A method of compressing video data that produces a **lower bitrate at the same perceptual quality** | **Yes** | Further technical effect (G 1/19-style analysis, even though G 1/19 was withdrawn). |
| A business method for auctioning securities implemented on a server | **No** (typically) | Claim is essentially a scheme for doing business. |
| An X-ray apparatus with a computer that **automatically positions the patient and adjusts the radiation dose** | **Yes** | The computer contributes to a technical medical process. |

## 2.3 The Two Competing Judicial Tests (KTU High-Yield)

After decades of jurisprudence, the European position coalesced around **two analytical frameworks**. KTU examiners expect students to know *both* and the case that established each.

### A. The Aerotel/Macrossan Four-Step Test (UK High Court, 2006, applied by the EPO as persuasive)

Established in *Aerotel Ltd v. Telco Holdings Ltd; Macrossan's Application* [2006] EWCA Civ 1371, later adopted as EPO persuasive guidance:

- **Step 1** — **Properly construe the claim** to determine its actual scope.
- **Step 2** — **Identify the actual contribution** — what does the invention, as claimed, really add to the prior art?
- **Step 3** — **Ask whether the contribution is solely excluded subject matter** (i.e., is it *only* a program, a business method, a mental act, etc.?).
- **Step 4** — **Check if the contribution makes a technical contribution** — is there a "technical effect" on a process outside the computer or on the computer's operation in a non-trivial way?

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall** — Students often *skip Step 1* (claim construction). The Aerotel test begins with construction, not with technical effect. Marks are deducted when students jump straight to "it is patentable because there is a technical effect" without first explaining what the claim actually covers.

### B. The COMVIK Approach (EPO Board of Appeal, T 641/00, 2002)

Established in *COMVIK Telefon AB v. LG Electronics*, the EPO's "home-grown" test used by examiners:

- **Step 1** — Identify the features of the claim that **contribute to the technical character** of the invention (the "technical core").
- **Step 2** — Treat those features as the **basis for assessing novelty and inventive step** under Articles 54 and 56 EPC; **ignore** non-technical features when assessing inventive step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle but High-Yield Distinction**
> - In the **Aerotel** test, you ask whether the *contribution* is technical.
> - In the **COMVIK** test, you identify *which features* are technical and use only those for inventive-step analysis.
> - The **practical outcome** is usually the same — both tests filter out business-method patents and pure algorithms — but the *logic flow* differs. KTU answers should explicitly mention which test is being applied.

## 2.4 The Most Cited Cases in EPO History on This Topic

| Case | Reference | Year | Holding (Killer Quote) |
|------|-----------|------|------------------------|
| **VICOM** | T 208/84 | 1987 | A mathematical method *as such* is excluded, but if it produces a **technical effect**, the claim is patentable. |
| **IBM Computer Program Product** | T 1173/97 | 1999 | A claim directed to a *computer-readable medium* storing a program is **not** excluded as "a program for computers as such." |
| **COMVIK** | T 641/00 | 2002 | Established the technical-core / two-step approach. |
| **HITACHI / Auction method** | T 258/03 | 2004 | A method of conducting an auction is **not** patentable even when implemented on a computer, because the contribution is a business method. |
| **Aerotel/Macrossan** | (UK Court of Appeal) | 2006 | Four-step test. |
| **Dollfus Mieg** | T 154/04 | 2006 | Features claimed for a non-technical purpose (e.g., a "technical" function on a monetary voucher) do **not** count as technical contribution. |
| **G 3/08 (President's Referral)** | Enlarged Board | — | Withdrawn without a decision. |
| **G 1/19 (Patentability of computer-implemented simulations)** | Enlarged Board | 2021 | **Withdrawn** in March 2021 after the appellant withdrew the appeal. Position remains governed by *COMVIK* and *Aerotel*. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **G 1/19 Status** — Students sometimes assert that G 1/19 "clarified" the European position. It did **not**. It was withdrawn, and the EPO's current approach remains the *COMVIK/Aerotel hybrid* as reflected in the EPO Guidelines, G-II, Chapter 3.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Comparison Table)

| Dimension | Europe (EPC) | USA (Post-Alice, 35 U.S.C. § 101) | India (Patents Act, Sec 3(k)) |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Statutory base** | Art. 52 EPC | 35 USC § 101 | Sec. 3(k), Patents Act 1970 |
| **Software patentable?** | Yes, if further technical effect | Yes, if not abstract | **No**, "computer program per se" is excluded |
| **Business method patentable?** | Generally no (HITACHI) | Yes, with limitations | No (Sec 3(k)) |
| **Key test** | COMVIK / Aerotel | Two-step Alice/Mayo test | "per se" test |
| **Hardware recitation trick** | Allowed (IBM T 1173/97) | Allowed (but weakened by Alice) | Disallowed by Indian courts (Ferid Allani) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-jurisdictional awareness** — KTU Module 4 frequently asks a *compare-contrast* question between the European, US, and Indian positions. The table above is the single most efficient way to memorise the differences.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework for the European Position

> [!NOTE]
> Although this topic is legal and not mathematical, the protocol requires an **exhaustive, step-by-step exposition**. The following framework is the analytical equivalent of a derivation — every transitional step is made explicit, which is exactly how a 14-mark KTU answer should be structured.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Application of the COMVIK Approach to a Hypothetical Claim

**Hypothetical Claim (very similar to a classic EPO test case):**
> *"A method of compressing digital image data, comprising the steps of: receiving pixel values; performing a non-linear transform on the values; encoding the transformed values; characterised in that the non-linear transform is selected adaptively based on a content-complexity measure."*

### Step 1 — Identify all features of the claim

List every feature explicitly, separating them into **technical** and **non-technical** buckets. The EPO examiner literally does this on paper before reaching any conclusion.

- F1: "receiving pixel values" — *hardware input step* → **Technical**.
- F2: "performing a non-linear transform" — *mathematical operation* → **Non-technical on its face** (Art. 52(2)(a)).
- F3: "encoding the transformed values" — *data processing step* → **Ambiguous, depends on novelty**.
- F4: "adaptively selecting the transform based on a content-complexity measure" — *decision step* → **Ambiguous**.

### Step 2 — Decide which features contribute to the technical character

Apply the EPO's "technical contribution" lens. A mathematical method contributes to the technical character **only if** it serves a technical purpose.

- F1: clearly technical (an act on hardware).
- F2: the *non-linear transform itself* is a mathematical method, but here it is **applied to image data for the purpose of compression**, which is a technical purpose (reducing storage/transmission bandwidth). Therefore F2 is treated as **technical in context** (VICOM principle).
- F3: encoding for storage/transmission is technical.
- F4: adaptivity based on a *technical property* of the image (complexity) is a technical effect — it optimises the use of hardware resources.

> **Conclusion of Step 2:** The **technical core** = F1 + F2 + F3 + F4 (all features, because all serve a technical purpose).

### Step 3 — Assess novelty (Art. 54 EPC) over the technical core

Compare the technical core with the prior art. If no single prior-art reference discloses the **adaptive transform selection based on a content-complexity measure** in the technical field of image compression, the claim is **novel**.

### Step 4 — Assess inventive step (Art. 56 EPC) **considering only the technical features**

Ask: *"Would a person skilled in the art (PSIT — the 'person skilled in the art of image compression hardware/software'), starting from the closest prior art, arrive at the invention in an obvious manner?"*

- A generic mathematician could not — they would not think about bandwidth.
- A hardware engineer focused on compression *might* arrive at adaptive transform selection as a logical next step, **depending on the prior art**.
- The **non-technical features** (e.g., a "user-friendly interface" that just shows a progress bar) are **ignored** in the obviousness analysis (COMVIK principle).

### Step 5 — Final patentability decision

If the claim survives Steps 3 and 4, **grant the patent**. If it fails on either, **refuse the patent**, citing Articles 54 or 56 EPC — **not** Article 52(2). This is a frequently tested nuance: an invention is rarely refused under the *exclusion* itself; it is usually refused for *lack of novelty* or *lack of inventive step* once the exclusion has been cleared.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "exclusion cleared → examined on merits" pipeline** is the single most important procedural point in the entire European position. Always write it in this order in a 14-mark answer.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Application of the Aerotel/Macrossan Test (Side-by-Side)

Using the same hypothetical:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Construct the claim.} \quad & \text{Claim = method of compressing image data using an} \\
& \text{adaptively-selected non-linear transform.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2: Identify the actual contribution.} \quad & \text{An adaptive-selection mechanism that improves} \\
& \text{compression efficiency on real image data.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3: Is the contribution solely excluded?} \quad & \text{No — the contribution is not "merely" a program or} \\
& \text{a mathematical method; it produces a measurable} \\
& \text{technical improvement (reduced bitrate, faster decode).} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4: Technical contribution?} \quad & \text{Yes — affects the technical process of data} \\
& \text{transmission and storage.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Outcome: Patentable.** The Aerotel test gives the same answer as the COMVIK test in this case, but via a *different logical pathway*. KTU answers that show the pathways in parallel earn full marks.

## 3.3 Algorithm-Style Flow for Solving an Exam Problem

Use this when a KTU question presents an invention and asks *"Is it patentable under the EPC?"*:

1. **Identify the field** of the invention. Is it purely software? Embedded in hardware? A business process? A medical device?
2. **Strip the claim** of non-essential language. Ask: *"What is the *core* of what is being claimed?"*
3. **Map the core** onto the Article 52(2) list: program, business method, mental act, mathematical method, presentation of information, discovery, aesthetic creation.
4. **Apply the "as such" qualifier (Art. 52(3))** — does the claim go beyond the excluded matter by reciting a technical effect?
5. **If yes** to Step 4 → apply COMVIK / Aerotel → check novelty and inventive step on the technical features.
6. **If no** to Step 4 → refuse under Art. 52(2) in conjunction with Art. 52(3).
7. **State the conclusion** with citation to the relevant case law (VICOM, COMVIK, Aerotel, or HITACHI, depending on the invention's character).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pet Hate** — Saying *"It is a computer program, hence excluded"* without testing the *"as such"* qualifier. This is treated as a half-answer and capped at 4 out of 7 marks on a sub-part.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The European Patentability Assessment Pipeline (Article 52 EPC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Claim Filed at EPO] --> B[Is Claim Drafted for an Invention in a Field of Technology?]
    B -- No --> C[REFUSE - Art 52 1 EPC]
    B -- Yes --> D[Does Claim Relate to Excluded Subject Matter Under Art 52 2?]
    D -- No --> E[Proceed to Novelty and Inventive Step Examination]
    D -- Yes --> F[Is the Excluded Matter Present Only AS SUCH? Art 52 3]
    F -- Yes --> G[REFUSE - Program Business Method or Math Method as Such]
    F -- No --> H[Does the Claim Produce a FURTHER TECHNICAL EFFECT?]
    H -- No --> G
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply COMVIK or Aerotel Test]
    I --> J[Identify Technical Core Features]
    J --> K[Assess Novelty Art 54 EPC]
    K --> L[Assess Inventive Step Art 56 EPC on Technical Core Only]
    L -- Pass --> M[GRANT PATENT]
    L -- Fail --> N[REFUSE on Lack of Novelty or Inventive Step]
```

## 4.2 The COMVIK Approach (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1 [Claim Parsing]
        P1[Claim Text Input] --> P2[Decompose Into Discrete Features F1 F2 F3 ... Fn]
    end
    subgraph S2 [Technical Character Filter]
        P2 --> P3{Does Feature Contribute to Technical Character?}
        P3 -- Yes --> P4[Place in Technical Core Set T]
        P3 -- No --> P5[Place in Non Technical Set N]
    end
    subgraph S3 [Patentability Assessment]
        P4 --> Q1[Assess Novelty Art 54 over Set T only]
        P5 --> Q2[Features in Set N are Ignored for Inventive Step]
        Q1 --> R1[Combine Set T with Closest Prior Art]
        R1 --> R2[PSIT Problem Solution Approach Art 56]
        R2 --> R3{Obvious to PSIT?}
    end
    subgraph S4 [Decision]
        R3 -- Yes --> O1[REFUSE Lack of Inventive Step]
        R3 -- No --> O2[GRANT Patent]
    end
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: Aerotel vs COMVIK Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1 [Aerotel Macrossan Four Step Test]
        A_S1[Step 1 Construct the Claim] --> A_S2[Step 2 Identify the Actual Contribution]
        A_S2 --> A_S3[Step 3 Is Contribution Solely Excluded Subject Matter]
        A_S3 --> A_S4[Step 4 Does Contribution Amount to a Technical Contribution]
        A_S4 --> A_S5{Technical Contribution Exists?}
    end
    subgraph C1 [COMVIK Two Step Approach]
        C_S1[Step 1 Identify Features Contributing to Technical Character] --> C_S2[Step 2 Use Only Technical Features as Basis for Novelty and Inventive Step]
        C_S2 --> C_S5{Technical Core Survives Novelty and Inventive Step?}
    end
    A_S5 -- Yes --> FINAL[Patent Granted]
    A_S5 -- No --> REFUSED[Patent Refused]
    C_S5 -- Yes --> FINAL
    C_S5 -- No --> REFUSED
```

## 4.4 The "Program as Such" vs "Computer-Implemented Invention" Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Software Related Claim] --> Q1{Is Claim Limited to Program Code Itself on a Generic Computer?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> EXCL[EXCLUDED - Art 52 2 c and 52 3 EPC - VICOM IBM T 1173 97]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does the Claim Recite a Technical Feature Hardware or Technical Effect?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> PAT[PATENTABLE - Further Technical Effect Doctrine]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the Claim a Pure Business Scheme or Mental Act Implemented on a Computer?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> EXCL2[EXCLUDED - Art 52 2 c and HITACHI T 258 03]
    Q3 -- No --> PAT2[Examine on Technical Merits]
```
