# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> *"What is meant by the phrase 'programs for computers as such' under the European Patent Convention? Why is this phrase significant?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The phrase appears in **Article 52(2)(c) EPC** and excludes *computer programs* from the list of patentable inventions. The qualifier **"as such"**, added by **Article 52(3) EPC**, limits the exclusion to *pure* programs — those that produce no further technical effect beyond the normal execution of software on hardware. The significance is that a *computer-implemented invention* (a program tied to a technical process, hardware contribution, or technical effect) is **not** excluded and may be patentable. This wording allows the EPO to grant patents on software-heavy inventions while still honouring the EPC's policy of denying monopolies on abstract ideas, and was confirmed in cases such as *VICOM (T 208/84)*, *IBM (T 1173/97)* and *COMVIK (T 641/00)*.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for the statutory citation; 1 mark for explaining "as such"; 1 mark for the case-law illustration.

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
> *"Briefly explain the COMVIK approach used by the European Patent Office to assess computer-related inventions."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **COMVIK approach**, established by the EPO Board of Appeal in **Case T 641/00 (COMVIK Telefon AB v. LG Electronics, 2002)**, is a two-step test for assessing the patentability of computer-implemented inventions:

1. **Identify the technical features** of the claim — those features that contribute to the technical character of the invention. Non-technical features (e.g., purely commercial, administrative, or mental acts) are separated out.
2. **Assess novelty (Art. 54 EPC) and inventive step (Art. 56 EPC)** based *only* on the technical features; the non-technical features are disregarded for inventive-step analysis.

The approach avoids refusing claims under the Article 52(2) exclusion and instead channels computer-implemented inventions into the normal substantive examination pipeline. It is currently reflected in the **EPO Guidelines for Examination, G-II, Chapter 3**.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for case citation; 1 mark for the two steps; 1 mark for the practical effect.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model Question]**
> *"Discuss the European position on the patentability of computer-related inventions under the European Patent Convention (EPC). In your answer, explain the role of Article 52 EPC, the 'as such' qualifier, and the COMVIK and Aerotel/Macrossan tests. Cite at least four relevant judicial decisions."*

#### (a) Article 52 EPC and the Statutory Framework (7 marks)

The European position on computer-related patents is governed primarily by **Article 52 EPC** of the European Patent Convention, 1973. The provision is structured in three layers:

- **Article 52(1) EPC** grants patents for "any inventions, in all fields of technology" subject to the conditions of novelty, inventive step, and industrial applicability. This is an **open-ended, technology-neutral** grant clause.
- **Article 52(2) EPC** provides a list of items that "shall not be regarded as inventions." This list includes: (a) discoveries, scientific theories, and mathematical methods; (b) aesthetic creations; (c) schemes, rules, and methods for performing mental acts, playing games, **or doing business, and programs for computers**; and (d) presentations of information.
- **Article 52(3) EPC** states that the exclusion "shall exclude patentability … only to the extent to which a European patent application or European patent relates to such subject-matter or activities *as such*."

The crucial phrase is **"as such"**. It was inserted to ensure that the exclusion does not apply to inventions that *involve* excluded matter but *also* produce a technical effect or contribute to a technical process. A pure algorithm running on a generic computer is excluded; an algorithm that controls an industrial process, improves the technical operation of the computer, or produces a technical effect on a physical system is not.

The **statutory framework alone** is insufficient, because Article 52(2)(c) uses the general term "programs for computers" without defining what falls outside it. Hence the EPO's Boards of Appeal have developed a rich body of case law to interpret the "as such" qualifier. The most important early decision is **VICOM (T 208/84, 1987)**, in which the Board held that a method of digitally processing images was patentable because the mathematical operations produced a technical effect on a physical entity (the image data). A second foundational case is **IBM's Computer Program Product (T 1173/97, 1999)**, in which the Board allowed a claim directed to a *computer-readable medium storing a program*, holding that such a claim was not to a program "as such." Together, VICOM and IBM established that hardware recitation and technical effect are the gateways through which software claims enter the patent system.

> **7-mark mark-split:** 2 marks for the three layers of Art. 52; 2 marks for the "as such" analysis; 2 marks for the VICOM/IBM discussion; 1 mark for concluding with the policy outcome.

#### (b) The COMVIK and Aerotel/Macrossan Tests (7 marks)

Two analytical frameworks dominate the European position. The first, developed internally by the EPO Boards of Appeal, is the **COMVIK approach** (Case T 641/00, 2002). It proceeds in two steps:

1. **Identify the technical core** — the features of the claim that contribute to the technical character of the invention.
2. **Assess novelty (Art. 54) and inventive step (Art. 56) using only the technical core** — non-technical features are *ignored* in the obviousness analysis. The approach is reflected in the EPO Guidelines, G-II, Chapter 3.

The second framework, the **Aerotel/Macrossan four-step test**, was articulated by the UK Court of Appeal in 2006 (Aerotel Ltd v. Telco Holdings Ltd; Macrossan's Application [2006] EWCA Civ 1371). It has been treated as persuasive authority at the EPO and in the UK. The four steps are:

1. **Properly construe the claim.**
2. **Identify the actual contribution** over the prior art.
3. **Ask whether the contribution is solely excluded subject matter.**
4. **Check if the contribution makes a technical contribution.**

The **practical outcomes** of the two tests are usually identical, but the *pathways* differ. COMVIK focuses on features and ignores non-technical features at the inventive-step stage; Aerotel focuses on the contribution as a whole. The HITACHI / auction-method case (T 258/03, 2004) shows the limits of both tests: an auction method implemented on a computer is still a business method "as such" and is refused, because the contribution is a scheme for doing business, not a technical process. The **Dollfus Mieg** case (T 154/04, 2006) further held that features claimed for a non-technical purpose (such as "a monetary voucher that can be read by a computer") do not count as a technical contribution.

A case that is often *misrepresented* in textbooks is the **President's Referral G 1/19 (Patentability of computer-implemented simulations)**, which was *withdrawn* in 2021 after the appellant withdrew the appeal. The Enlarged Board of Appeal therefore did **not** clarify the European position, and the COMVIK/Aerotel hybrid remains the operative law. KTU students should *not* assert that G 1/19 settled the position.

In summary, the European position is best described as a **functional, effect-based approach**: software *as such* is excluded, but a computer-implemented invention that produces a *further technical effect* (beyond the intrinsic effects of running any program) is patentable, and the substantive examination proceeds normally under the novelty and inventive-step provisions of the EPC.

> **7-mark mark-split:** 2 marks for COMVIK; 2 marks for Aerotel; 1 mark for HITACHI / Dollfus Mieg; 1 mark for the G 1/19 clarification; 1 mark for the synthesis.

### Question B (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted Model Question]**
> *"Critically analyse the European Patent Office's approach to computer-related patents. Compare it briefly with the Indian position under Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970. In your answer, also discuss the implications of the IBM Computer Program Product case and the HITACHI auction-method case."*

#### (a) Critical Analysis of the EPO Approach (7 marks)

The EPO's approach to computer-related inventions is grounded in **Article 52 EPC** and refined by decades of Board of Appeal decisions. Its **strengths** are:

- **Technology neutrality** — Art. 52(1) EPC is open-ended and does not lock the system into a pre-internet view of patentable subject matter.
- **Effect-based reasoning** — the "further technical effect" doctrine allows software to be patented when it produces a real-world technical improvement, such as improved compression, faster networks, or better medical imaging.
- **Predictability through case law** — VICOM, IBM, COMVIK, Aerotel, HITACHI, and Dollfus Mieg form a relatively coherent body of jurisprudence that examiners can apply.

Its **weaknesses** are:

- **Subjective line-drawing** — determining whether a software claim produces a "further technical effect" involves value judgments. Critics argue that the EPO has, in practice, granted broad software patents contrary to the original EPC drafters' intent.
- **Withdrawn referrals** — G 3/08 and G 1/19 were both withdrawn, leaving the position dependent on Board of Appeal case law rather than a binding Enlarged Board ruling.
- **Business-method resistance** — the HITACHI decision (T 258/03) strictly excludes business methods, but the line between a business method and a technical method is increasingly blurred in modern fintech and AI inventions.

The **IBM Computer Program Product** case (T 1173/97, 1999) is a milestone: it allowed a claim drafted to a *computer-readable medium storing data instructions*. The decision is sometimes criticised because the "computer-readable medium" is essentially a *legal-form* drafting trick — the underlying invention is the software itself. However, the EPO's logic was that the claim is not to the program *as such* but to a physical carrier. The decision opened the door to software patents in Europe and remains controversial.

> **7-mark mark-split:** 2 marks for strengths; 2 marks for weaknesses; 2 marks for IBM analysis; 1 mark for concluding observation.

#### (b) Comparison with India and the HITACHI Case (7 marks)

India's position is governed by **Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970**, which excludes *"a computer program per se"* from patentability. The Indian Patents Office and the courts have interpreted this strictly: a claim that recites a computer program as the *substance* of the invention is refused. However, a claim that ties the program to a *novel hardware architecture* or a *technical effect on a physical process* may be allowed. The Delhi High Court's decision in *Ferid Allani v. Union of India* (2012) suggested that hardware-tied software may be patentable, but the **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** and subsequent decisions have generally taken a restrictive line.

| Aspect | Europe (EPC, Art. 52) | India (Sec. 3(k)) |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Software per se | Excluded but bypassable via "further technical effect" | Excluded by statute — *per se* language |
| Hardware-tied software | Patentable (IBM T 1173/97) | Possibly patentable (Ferid Allani) |
| Business method | Excluded (HITACHI) | Excluded |
| AI / ML inventions | Patentable if technical contribution | Highly restricted |

The **HITACHI auction-method case** (T 258/03, 2004) is the most cited EPO decision on business methods. The Board refused a patent on a method of conducting an auction implemented on a computer, holding that the contribution was a *scheme for doing business* within the meaning of Article 52(2)(c) EPC. The decision reinforces that the EPC excludes business methods even when they are technically implemented. The Indian position (Sec. 3(k) read with Sec. 3(m)) is structurally similar: both jurisdictions refuse pure business methods, although India's *per se* language is broader and less forgiving than Europe's *"as such"* qualifier. The *practical implication* is that European applicants have a wider drafting toolkit (e.g., reciting a computer-readable medium, claiming a server, or describing a technical effect) than Indian applicants, who must often rely on hardware claims or fall back on copyright and trade-secret protection.

> **7-mark mark-split:** 2 marks for Indian position; 2 marks for the comparative table; 2 marks for HITACHI analysis; 1 mark for the practical implication.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the "as such" qualifier.** Always quote Art. 52(3) EPC and explain its effect. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Treating "programs for computers" as absolutely excluded.** This is wrong; the European position allows software patents in a wide range of cases.
> 3. **Citing G 1/19 as a settled authority.** It was withdrawn. Examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 4. **Confusing COMVIK and Aerotel.** Both are correct, but they are *different* tests. Examiners expect both to be named separately.
> 5. **Ignoring the comparative dimension.** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the ability to compare positions across jurisdictions (Europe / USA / India). A Part B answer that does not compare is capped around 10 of 14 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Statutory basis:** Article 52 EPC — specifically **52(1) (grant), 52(2) (exclusion list), 52(3) ("as such" qualifier)**. Always cite all three.
- **"As such" qualifier** is the cornerstone of the European position; without it, the EPC would deny patents to most software-related inventions.
- **"Further technical effect"** doctrine: a software-related invention is patentable when it produces an effect beyond the intrinsic technical effect of running any program on any computer.
- **COMVIK (T 641/00, 2002)** — two-step EPO approach: identify technical core → assess novelty/inventive step on technical core only.
- **Aerotel/Macrossan (UK, 2006)** — four-step test: construct claim → identify contribution → is contribution solely excluded? → is there a technical contribution?
- **VICOM (T 208/84, 1987)** — mathematical method that produces a technical effect is patentable.
- **IBM Computer Program Product (T 1173/97, 1999)** — computer-readable medium claim is not a program "as such."
- **HITACHI (T 258/03, 2004)** — auction method on a computer remains a business method "as such" and is excluded.
- **Dollfus Mieg (T 154/04, 2006)** — features claimed for a non-technical purpose do not count as technical contribution.
- **G 1/19 (2019/2021)** — *withdrawn*; do not cite as binding.
- **EPO Guidelines, G-II, Chapter 3** — current administrative framework; uses the COMVIK approach.
- **Comparative quick-recall:** Europe = effect-based, India = "per se" exclusion, USA = Alice/Mayo two-step test.
- **Drafting tip for applicants:** always include a *technical feature* (hardware, technical effect, or technical process) in the claim to escape the Art. 52(2) exclusion.
- **Examination pipeline:** exclusion cleared → novelty (Art. 54) → inventive step (Art. 56) → grant. Most refusals happen at the inventive-step stage, not at the exclusion stage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legal Position of U.S. on Computer Related Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Legal Position of U.S. on Computer Related Patents # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Computer-Related Invention (CRI)** or **Computer-Implemented Invention (CII)** in U.S. patent law refers to any invention that claims the use of a computer, computer network, or other programmable apparatus, where one or more features of the claimed invention are realized wholly or partially by means of a computer program. Under U.S. law, such inventions are examined for patent eligibility primarily under **35 U.S.C. § 101**, which defines the four statutory categories of patentable subject matter: **process, machine, manufacture, and composition of matter**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT861 – Module 4):** > "Legal Position of U.S. on Computer Related Patents" requires students to understand the statutory basis (35 U.S.C. § 101), the landmark Supreme Court precedents (Diehr, Benson, Flook, Bilski, Mayo, Alice), and the operative two-step framework (Alice/Mayo test) used by the USPTO and courts to determine patent eligibility of software and business-method inventions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patent Gate" Imagine a four-door gate guarding a theme park. To enter, an invention must pass through **all four doors** of U.S. patent law: | Door | Statute | Requirement | |---|---|---| | Door 1 | **35 U.S.C. § 101** | Is it a *process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter*? | | Door 2 | **35 U.S.C. § 102** | Is it *novel* (no prior art)? | | Door 3 | **35 U.S.C. § 103** | Is it *non-obvious* to a person having ordinary skill in the art (PHOSITA)? | | Door 4 | **35 U.S.C. § 112** | Is the specification *written, enabled, and definite*? | **Computer-related patents** frequently get stuck at **Door 1 (§ 101)** because software, algorithms, and business methods are often viewed as "abstract ideas" — the courtroom's way of saying *"this looks like mental gymnastics, not a physical invention."* The U.S. legal position is essentially a set of rules determining *when* a computer-implemented idea is allowed past this first door. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** In the U.S., you *can* patent software — but **not** the software in the abstract. You must show that the computer is doing something **concrete, technical, and transformative**, not merely performing arithmetic or running a business method in a generic computer. ## 1.3 Scope of the Topic The U.S. legal position governs: - **Software patents** (algorithms, code-implemented methods) - **Business method patents** (e.g., Amazon's "1-Click" checkout) - **Hardware-software hybrid patents** (e.g., a medical device controlled by software) - **Internet/Networking patents** (routing, encryption, UI innovations) The position has evolved dramatically since **1972**, reflecting a tension between protecting innovation and preventing monopolization of *mental steps*. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Statutory Foundation — 35 U.S.C. § 101 The U.S. Patent Act, codified under **Title 35 of the United States Code**, Section 101, states: > *"Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title."* This is the **"Patent-Eligible Subject Matter"** provision. Crucially, the U.S. Supreme Court has long recognized **three judicial exceptions** — categories of things that, even if novel and non-obvious, are *not* patentable: 1. **Laws of nature** 2. **Natural phenomena** 3. **Abstract ideas** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Judicial Exceptions to § 101:** These three exceptions are *not* written in the statute — they were carved out by judges interpreting § 101. They exist to prevent the patent system from tying up the basic tools of scientific and technological innovation. ## 2.2 The Alice/Mayo Two-Step Test (Operative Framework) Since **Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank (2014)**, the controlling U.S. framework for analyzing computer-related patent eligibility is the **two-step test** derived from *Mayo Collaborative Services v. Prometheus Laboratories (2012)*. ### **Step 1 (Alice Step 1 / Mayo Step 1): Is the claim "directed to" a patent-ineligible concept?** The examiner (or court) asks: Does the claim "recite" or "involve" a law of nature, natural phenomenon, or abstract idea? > For software patents, this is the **"abstract idea"** prong. The USPTO has identified three sub-categories of abstract ideas: > - **Mathematical concepts** (algorithms, formulas, equations) > - **Mental processes** (concepts performable in the human mind or with a pen and paper) > - **Certain methods of organizing human activity** (fundamental economic practices, commercial interactions, managing personal behavior) If **NO** → the claim is patent-eligible (assuming it meets §§ 102, 103, 112). If **YES** → proceed to Step 2. ### **Step 2 (Alice Step 2 / Mayo Step 2): Is there an "inventive concept" that transforms the claim into a patent-eligible application?** The examiner asks: Does the claim contain *"additional elements"* — features beyond the abstract idea — that amount to *"significantly more"* than the judicial exception itself? > "Significantly more" requires an **inventive concept**, not merely: > - *"Apply it on a computer"* (insufficient) > - *"Apply it on the Internet"* (insufficient) > - *"Well-understood, routine, conventional activities"* previously known to the industry (insufficient) > - **Mere field-of-use labeling** (e.g., "in the field of e-commerce") (insufficient) **Sufficient** "significantly more" examples: a specific hardware improvement, a non-conventional arrangement of components, a transformation of an article into a different state, or a non-routine technical solution to a technological problem. ## 2.3 The USPTO 2019 Revised Patent Subject Matter Eligibility Guidance (PEG) The USPTO's **2019 Revised PEG** (84 Fed. Reg. 50, January 7, 2019) operationalizes the Alice/Mayo test into a more examiner-friendly flow: | Step | Question | Outcome | |---|---|---| | **Step 1** | Does the claim recite a statutory category (process, machine, manufacture, composition)? | If NO → reject under § 101. If YES → proceed. | | **Step 2A, Prong One** | Does the claim recite a judicial exception (law of nature, natural phenomenon, abstract idea)? | If NO → eligible. If YES → proceed. | | **Step 2A, Prong Two** | Is the judicial exception integrated into a "practical application"? | If YES → eligible (no need for Step 2B). If NO → proceed. | | **Step 2B** | Does the claim add an "inventive concept" (significantly more)? | If YES → eligible. If NO → reject. | > [!NOTE] > **The "Practical Application" Shortcut:** Prong Two of Step 2A is crucial. If a software claim recites a judicial exception **but** applies it in a manner that imposes meaningful limits on the exception's use (e.g., a specific technical improvement to the functioning of the computer itself or a particular machine), the claim is eligible **without** needing to reach Step 2B. This was a major clarification in the 2019 PEG. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — The Six Landmark Cases | Case (Year) | Holding | Significance for Computer Patents | |---|---|---| | **Gottschalk v. Benson** (1972) | A method of converting BCD numerals to binary is **not patentable** because it would "wholly pre-empt" the mathematical formula. | Established that **pure algorithms are unpatentable**. First major "software is not patentable" signal. | | **Parker v. Flook** (1978) | A method of updating alarm limits in catalytic conversion is unpatentable; post-solution activity cannot rescue an abstract idea. | Reaffirmed that **adding routine steps to an abstract idea is not enough**. | | **Diamond v. Diehr** (1981) | A rubber-curing process using a computer to implement the Arrhenius equation **IS patentable**. | Established that **software used as part of a physical process** is patent-eligible. The "as a whole" rule. | | **Bilski v. Kappos** (2010) | The "machine-or-transformation" test is **not the exclusive** test for patent eligibility of processes. | Killed the Federal Circuit's rigid test; opened the door for business-method patents. | | **Mayo v. Prometheus** (2012) | A method of calibrating drug dosage based on metabolite levels is **not patentable** (law of nature). | Codified the **two-step framework** later adopted in Alice. | | **Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank** (2014) | Computer-implemented method for mitigating settlement risk in financial transactions is **not patentable** (abstract idea). | Applied the Mayo framework to **software and business methods**; tightened § 101 scrutiny dramatically. | | **Enfish v. Microsoft** (2016) | A **self-referential database** is patent-eligible because it improves computer functionality itself. | First post-Alice CAFC decision holding software patent-eligible on Step 1 — emphasizing **technical improvement to the computer**. | | **DDR Holdings v. Hotels.com** (2014) | Internet-centric system that creates a hybrid website is **eligible** — the claimed solution is "necessarily rooted in computer technology." | A narrow post-Alice lifeline: software that solves an **Internet-specific problem** can be eligible. | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Software Industry:** Companies like Microsoft, Google, Apple, and Amazon spend billions annually on software R&D. The § 101 framework determines whether their innovations can be protected. - **AI/ML Patents:** The USPTO and courts are currently applying Alice/Mayo to **AI inventions** — generic applications of neural networks to "analyze data" are typically rejected; specific technical improvements to model architecture or training efficiency may survive. - **Fintech & Cryptography:** Business-method patents (e.g., blockchain consensus mechanisms) face the **highest rejection rates** under Step 2A, Prong One. - **Medical Devices:** Hybrid hardware-software devices (e.g., MRI machines, pacemakers) are generally **eligible** because the software controls a tangible device (Diehr line of cases). # 3. Step-by-Step Application & Analytical Matrix Because this is a legal topic, "derivations" are replaced by **step-by-step legal reasoning chains** and **case-by-case analytical matrices** — the same methodology students must demonstrate in KTU ESE answers. ## 3.1 Applying the Alice/Mayo Test — A Generalized Reasoning Chain **Scenario:** A startup files a U.S. patent application claiming *"A computer-implemented method for ranking search results based on user click-through rates, comprising: receiving a query; ranking results using a click-rate algorithm; and displaying them in a browser."* ### **Step 1 of Alice — Is the claim "directed to" a judicial exception?** We evaluate the claim's "focus" — what is it fundamentally about? - The claim is about **ranking information based on user behavior** → this is a **method of organizing human activity** (specifically, a fundamental economic practice of advertising and information retrieval). - Alternatively, it could be characterized as a **mathematical concept** (an algorithm for ranking). - **Conclusion:** The claim is "directed to" an abstract idea. **Proceed to Step 2.** ### **Step 2A, Prong One (USPTO 2019 PEG) — Recites a judicial exception?** - **YES** — the ranking algorithm is a mathematical concept, and the practice of ranking search results is an organizing human activity concept. ### **Step 2A, Prong Two (USPTO 2019 PEG) — Integrated into a practical application?** - The claim does **not** recite a specific technical improvement to the computer's functionality. - It does **not** transform an article. - It merely recites *"apply the algorithm on a generic computer and display the results."* - **Conclusion:** The judicial exception is **not** integrated into a practical application. **Proceed to Step 2B.** ### **Step 2B — Inventive concept (significantly more)?** - The additional elements (generic computer, generic browser, generic display) are all **well-understood, routine, and conventional** activities previously known in the art. - No specific hardware improvement, no non-conventional arrangement, no technical innovation beyond the abstract idea itself. - **Conclusion:** **No inventive concept** → claim is **NOT patent-eligible** under § 101. **Outcome:** The application would be rejected on § 101 grounds. This mirrors the USPTO's frequent rejection of generic "do it on a computer" claims. ## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — How U.S. Courts Decide The following matrix summarizes how courts have ruled across six representative fact patterns: | Fact Pattern | Directed To (§ 101 Step 1)? | Inventive Concept (Step 2)? | Final Ruling | Leading Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | Algorithm for converting numeral systems (no specific application) | YES — abstract idea (math) | NO — no additional meaningful limits | **Ineligible** | *Benson* (1972) | | Computer-implemented rubber-curing using Arrhenius equation | NO — physical transformation of an article | (Not reached) | **Eligible** | *Diehr* (1981) | | Alarm-limit update in catalytic converter (post-solution activity) | YES — abstract idea (math) | NO — routine post-solution steps | **Ineligible** | *Flook* (1978) | | Hedging in commodities markets via computer | YES — abstract idea (commercial) | NO — generic computer implementation | **Ineligible** | *Alice Corp.* (2014) | | Self-referential database table that improves search speed | NO — improvement to computer functionality | (Not reached) | **Eligible** | *Enfish v. Microsoft* (2016) | | Hybrid web page preventing "look-and-feel" violations | YES — arguably abstract, but rooted in Internet technology | YES — solves Internet-centric problem | **Eligible** | *DDR Holdings* (2014) | | Blockchain consensus protocol for trust verification | YES — abstract idea (organizing activity) | NO (typically) — generic computer | **Likely Ineligible** | USPTO post-*Alice* trend | | ML model with novel architecture reducing GPU memory by 40% | NO — improvement to computer functionality | (Not reached) | **Likely Eligible** | USPTO 2024 AI/SI guidance direction | ## 3.3 The Diehr "As a Whole" Doctrine — Detailed Walkthrough In *Diamond v. Diehr*, the Supreme Court articulated a critical rule that still governs: > *"A claim drawn to subject matter otherwise statutory does not become non-statutory simply because it uses a mathematical formula, computer program, or digital computer."* — *Diehr*, 450 U.S. 175, 187 (1981). **The reasoning chain:** 1. The applicants claimed a complete process for curing rubber — not just a formula. 2. They used the **Arrhenius equation** (a well-known mathematical formula) to calculate cure time. 3. The computer's role was to **automate temperature measurement and feedback control**. 4. The process physically transformed raw rubber into cured rubber — an industrial process tied to a machine. **Conclusion:** The Court held that, **as a whole**, the claim was a patent-eligible industrial process. The presence of a mathematical formula did not, by itself, make the claim abstract. This is the foundational principle that allows software to be patented when it is **operationally tied to a tangible, physical process or technological improvement**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Tip — Diehr "As a Whole":** When answering KTU questions on this topic, always cite *Diehr* when discussing software that controls a physical transformation. Examiners reward the "as a whole" reasoning rather than a one-line conclusion. ## 3.4 Alice Step 2 — The "Significantly More" Doctrine In *Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank*, 573 U.S. 208 (2014), the Supreme Court held that a computer-implemented method for mitigating settlement risk in financial transactions was **not patent-eligible**. The reasoning chain: 1. **Step 1 finding:** The claims were "directed to" the abstract idea of *"intermediated settlement"* — a fundamental economic practice. 2. **Step 2 finding:** The additional elements (a "data processing system," a "communications controller") were **generic computer components performing generic computer functions**. 3. **Key principle:** *"Wholly generic computer implementation is not generally the sort of 'additional element' that supplies an 'inventive concept.'"* — *Alice*, 573 U.S. at 222. **The 2014 Alice decision created a "Section 101 crisis"** — the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office's rejection rate under § 101 spiked, and the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit issued numerous decisions narrowing software patent eligibility. The situation has gradually stabilized post-2019 due to the USPTO's Revised PEG. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison — Pre-Alice vs. Post-Alice Landscape | Dimension | Pre-*Alice* (Before 2014) | Post-*Alice* (2014 onwards) | Post-2019 PEG (Current) | |---|---|---|---| | Software patentability | Broadly allowed | Drastically narrowed | Somewhat stabilized | | Business method patents | Mostly allowed (after *Bilski*) | Subject to Alice/Mayo | Subject to Alice/Mayo, but PEG clarifies path | | Most common rejection ground | § 102 (novelty) / § 103 (obviousness) | § 101 (subject matter) | § 101 (subject matter) | | Examiner framework | Flexible | Strict two-step | Three-prong PEG with "practical application" | | Burden of proof | Applicant's burden (typical) | High burden on applicant | High burden, but clearer roadmap | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Alice/Mayo Test Decision Flow (USPTO PEG-Aligned) This Mermaid block maps the **complete decision logic** that a U.S. patent examiner (or court) follows when analyzing a computer-related invention under 35 U.S.C. § 101. It corresponds directly to the 2019 Revised Patent Subject Matter Eligibility Guidance. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Claim Filed for Computer-Related Invention"]:::start B{"STEP 1:
Recites a statutory category?
(process / machine /
manufacture / composition)"}:::gate C["REJECT under Section 101
(No statutory category)"]:::reject D{"STEP 2A, Prong One:
Recites a judicial exception?
(law of nature / natural
phenomenon / abstract idea)"}:::gate E["ELIGIBLE under Section 101
(No judicial exception)"]:::eligible F{"STEP 2A, Prong Two:
Integrated into a
practical application?"}:::gate G{"STEP 2B:
Adds an inventive concept?
(significantly more than
the abstract idea)"}:::gate H["ELIGIBLE under Section 101
(Practical application found)"]:::eligible I["ELIGIBLE under Section 101
(Inventive concept found)"]:::eligible J["REJECT under Section 101
(No inventive concept)"]:::reject A --> B B -- NO --> C B -- YES --> D D -- NO --> E D -- YES --> F F -- YES --> H F -- NO --> G G -- YES --> I G -- NO --> J classDef start fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 classDef gate fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100 classDef eligible fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 classDef reject fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of the U.S. Computer-Patent Legal Framework The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **four-tier statutory structure** of U.S. patent law, mapping each statute to its functional role in evaluating a computer-related invention. The dashed line highlights that **§ 101 is the gatekeeper** most relevant to software eligibility disputes. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph US["U.S. PATENT LAW FRAMEWORK - Title 35 USC"] direction TB S101["Section 101
SUBJECT MATTER ELIGIBILITY
Statutory Categories +
Judicial Exceptions"]:::gate S102["Section 102
NOVELTY
No anticipation by prior art"]:::req S103["Section 103
NON-OBVIOUSNESS
Inventive step over PHOSITA"]:::req S112["Section 112
WRITTEN DESCRIPTION
Enablement, Definiteness,
Best Mode"]:::req end IN["Computer-Related
Invention Claim"]:::input OUT["Issued U.S. Patent"]:::output IN --> S101 S101 -- "Eligible Subject Matter" --> S102 S102 -- "Novel" --> S103 S103 -- "Non-Obvious" --> S112 S112 -- "Sufficient Disclosure" --> OUT S101 -. "Most common rejection
ground for software" .-> REJ["Section 101 REJECTION"]:::reject classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 classDef gate fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#BF360C classDef req fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C classDef reject fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C ``` ## 4.3 The Three Judicial Exceptions — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A["JUDICIAL EXCEPTIONS TO 35 USC 101"]:::title B["Laws of Nature
(E = mc squared, gravity)"]:::law C["Natural Phenomena
(naturally occurring DNA,
natural minerals)"]:::nature D["Abstract Ideas"]:::abstract D1["Mathematical Concepts
(algorithms, formulas)"]:::sub D2["Mental Processes
(concepts performable in
the human mind)"]:::sub D3["Certain Methods of
Organizing Human Activity
(business methods,
commercial interactions)"]:::sub A --> B A --> C A --> D D --> D1 D --> D2 D --> D3 classDef title fill:#263238,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef law fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B classDef nature fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 classDef abstract fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C classDef sub fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#F57F17 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern — Part A:** Two-mark conceptual questions that test definitional clarity. Answers should be **concise (3-4 lines)**, precise, and reference the relevant statute or case. --- ### **Q1. What are the four statutory categories of patentable subject matter under 35 U.S.C. § 101?** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Under **35 U.S.C. § 101**, the four statutory categories of patentable subject matter are: 1. **Process** (a method or series of steps for performing a function) 2. **Machine** (a concrete device or apparatus consisting of parts) 3. **Manufacture** (an article produced from raw or prepared materials) 4. **Composition of Matter** (a combination of two or more substances) Any invention, including a computer-related invention, must fall within one of these four categories to be patent-eligible. In addition, the U.S. Supreme Court has recognized three **judicial exceptions** — laws of nature, natural phenomena, and abstract ideas — which are not patentable even if they fall within a statutory category. --- ### **Q2. State the holding in *Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank International* (2014).** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Question]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** In *Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank International*, **573 U.S. 208 (2014)**, the U.S. Supreme Court held that claims directed to a computer-implemented method for mitigating settlement risk in financial transactions are **not patent-eligible** under 35 U.S.C. § 101. The Court applied the **two-step framework** from *Mayo v. Prometheus* (2012): - **Step 1:** The claims were "directed to" the abstract idea of intermediated settlement — a fundamental economic practice. - **Step 2:** The additional elements (generic computer, data processing system, communications controller) constituted **"wholly generic computer implementation"** and did not supply an "inventive concept" sufficient to transform the abstract idea into patent-eligible subject matter. The decision significantly tightened U.S. patent eligibility for software and business-method inventions. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern — Part B:** A single 14-mark question typically divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with **internal choice** between two completely independent alternatives. The model answers below follow the KTU valuation key expectation of (i) statutory citation, (ii) leading case citation, (iii) reasoned application, and (iv) conclusion. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample, High-Yield Module 4 Question]` **(a)** Explain the **statutory framework** for computer-related patent eligibility in the United States under 35 U.S.C. § 101. Discuss the **three judicial exceptions** recognized by the U.S. Supreme Court. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Alice/Mayo two-step test** and apply it to determine the patent eligibility of the following claim: > *"A computer-implemented method for ranking search results on a website, comprising: (a) receiving a user query; (b) calculating a relevance score for each result using a mathematical algorithm; (c) sorting the results based on the score; and (d) displaying them on a generic web browser."* **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer — Part A(a) [7 marks]** The U.S. patent system is governed by **Title 35 of the United States Code**. The threshold inquiry for any patent is **Section 101 — Patent-Eligible Subject Matter**, which states: > *"Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor..."* These four categories — **process, machine, manufacture, and composition of matter** — are the only statutory avenues for patent protection in the United States. **Computer-related inventions** must be drafted to fall within at least one of them. A method claim reciting software steps typically qualifies as a "process"; a system claim recited in hardware/structural terms typically qualifies as a "machine." However, the U.S. Supreme Court has long recognized **three judicial exceptions** to § 101 that are derived from judicial interpretation rather than the statutory text: 1. **Laws of Nature** — fundamental principles of nature such as gravity (E = mc², Boyle's Law, etc.). Patent protection on laws of nature would preempt their use by the entire public. 2. **Natural Phenomena** — naturally occurring substances, products, or processes, such as a naturally occurring DNA sequence (*Diamond v. Chakrabarty, 447 U.S. 303 (1980)*). 3. **Abstract Ideas** — including: - **Mathematical concepts** (algorithms, formulas) — *Gottschalk v. Benson, 409 U.S. 63 (1972)*. - **Mental processes** (concepts performable in the human mind) — *Synopsys, Inc. v. Mentor Graphics Corp.* - **Certain methods of organizing human activity** (fundamental economic or commercial practices) — *Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank (2014)*. The Supreme Court has justified these exceptions as necessary to prevent the patent system from monopolizing the basic tools of scientific and technological progress — tools that must remain freely available for future innovation. In the context of computer-related inventions, the **abstract-idea exception** is the most frequently invoked ground for rejecting software and business-method claims. *(Answer completion: 7 marks — 2 marks for statutory framework, 3 marks for the three exceptions with case citations, 2 marks for the rationale of judicial exceptions.)* --- #### **Model Answer — Part A(b) [7 marks]** We apply the **two-step Alice/Mayo test**, as operationalized in the USPTO 2019 Revised Patent Subject Matter Eligibility Guidance (PEG). **Step 1 (Alice Step 1 / PEG Step 2A Prong One): Is the claim "directed to" a patent-ineligible concept?** - The claim describes a method of **ranking search results based on a relevance score**, computed via a mathematical algorithm. - This is **"directed to"** an abstract idea because: - Ranking based on relevance is a **mathematical concept** (PEG-recognized sub-category of abstract ideas). - Alternatively, it can be viewed as a **method of organizing human activity** (information retrieval and presentation in e-commerce). - Following *Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank* (2014) and *Electric Power Group v. Alstom* (CAFC 2016), collecting information, analyzing it, and displaying results is a **conventional information-processing task** long recognized as abstract. - **Conclusion of Step 1:** The claim IS directed to a judicial exception. **Proceed to Step 2.** **Step 2A, Prong Two (PEG): Is the judicial exception integrated into a "practical application"?** - The claim does **not** recite any specific improvement to the **functioning of the computer itself** (cf. *Enfish v. Microsoft*, 2016, where a self-referential database improved search speed). - The claim does **not** transform an article into a different state. - The "invention" is performed on a **generic web browser** and a **generic computer** — the only technical elements mentioned. - **Conclusion of Prong Two:** The judicial exception is **NOT** integrated into a practical application. **Proceed to Step 2B.** **Step 2B (Alice Step 2): Is there an "inventive concept" — "significantly more"?** - The additional elements consist of: - A **generic computer** performing data processing. - A **generic web browser** for display. - **Routine steps** (receive, calculate, sort, display). - Under *Alice*, *"wholly generic computer implementation is not generally the sort of 'additional element' that supplies an 'inventive concept.'"* — 573 U.S. at 222. - There is **no specific hardware improvement**, no novel technical arrangement, and no solution to a technological problem rooted in computer science. - **Conclusion of Step 2B:** No inventive concept. **Final Ruling:** The claim is **NOT patent-eligible** under 35 U.S.C. § 101. It would be rejected by the USPTO on subject-matter-eligibility grounds. To be salvaged, the claim would need to be amended to recite, e.g., a specific algorithmic improvement to search speed, a particular hardware acceleration, or a concrete technical solution to a networking problem. *(Answer completion: 7 marks — 2 marks for Step 1, 2 marks for Step 2A Prong Two, 2 marks for Step 2B, 1 mark for the final ruling with a remedial suggestion.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Sample, Alternative 14-Mark Question]` **(a)** Critically examine the **landmark U.S. Supreme Court decisions** that shaped the legal position on computer-related patents. Discuss at least **four** major cases, with their facts, holdings, and contribution to the evolution of U.S. law. **(7 marks)** **(b)** How does the USPTO's **2019 Revised Patent Subject Matter Eligibility Guidance (PEG)** streamline the application of the Alice/Mayo test? Explain its **three-prong analytical structure** with a hypothetical illustration. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer — Part B(a) [7 marks]** The U.S. legal position on computer-related patents has been shaped by a series of Supreme Court decisions over five decades. The four most influential cases are: **1. *Gottschalk v. Benson*, 409 U.S. 63 (1972)** - **Facts:** The applicants claimed a method for converting binary-coded decimal (BCD) numerals to pure binary form, implemented on a general-purpose digital computer. - **Holding:** The claim was **not patentable** under § 101. The mathematical algorithm for number conversion was an "abstract idea," and allowing a patent would "wholly pre-empt" the formula's use across all fields. - **Contribution:** Established that **pure algorithms, even if novel, are not patentable**. This was the first major signal that software-in-the-abstract could not be monopolized. **2. *Parker v. Flook*, 437 U.S. 584 (1978)** - **Facts:** The applicant claimed a method for updating alarm limits in a catalytic conversion process, using a mathematical formula. - **Holding:** **Not patentable.** The presence of a mathematical formula did not, by itself, render the claim abstract; however, post-solution activity (the steps after the algorithm) that is "insignificant" or "conventional" cannot transform an abstract idea into a patent-eligible application. - **Contribution:** Reinforced the principle that **routine, conventional post-solution steps** cannot rescue an otherwise abstract claim. **3. *Diamond v. Diehr*, 450 U.S. 175 (1981)** - **Facts:** The applicants claimed a process for curing synthetic rubber that used the Arrhenius equation (a well-known mathematical formula) to calculate cure time, with a computer controlling the press. - **Holding:** **Patentable.** The Court held that *"a claim drawn to subject matter otherwise statutory does not become non-statutory simply because it uses a mathematical formula, computer program, or digital computer."* The claim, **considered as a whole**, was a patent-eligible industrial process for transforming raw rubber into cured rubber. - **Contribution:** Established the **"as a whole" doctrine** — that the focus must be on the claim as an integrated whole, not on individual elements. This is the foundational case allowing software patents when the software is operationally tied to a tangible process. **4. *Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank International*, 573 U.S. 208 (2014)** - **Facts:** Alice Corp. held a patent on a computer-implemented method for mitigating settlement risk in financial transactions using a third-party intermediary. - **Holding:** **Not patentable.** The claims were directed to the abstract idea of intermediated settlement, and the additional generic computer components did not supply an "inventive concept." - **Contribution:** Applied the Mayo two-step framework to software/business-method patents, **dramatically tightening § 101 scrutiny**. The decision created the modern framework still in use today. **Synthesis:** The arc from *Benson* (1972) → *Diehr* (1981) → *Alice* (2014) reflects a long-running judicial balancing act: protecting genuine software innovation while preventing monopolization of mental steps. The current position, post-*Alice* and clarified by the 2019 PEG, favors narrower, technically-grounded claims. *(Answer completion: 7 marks — 1.5 marks per case for facts/holding/contribution, 1 mark for synthesis.)* --- #### **Model Answer — Part B(b) [7 marks]** The USPTO's **2019 Revised Patent Subject Matter Eligibility Guidance** (84 Fed. Reg. 50, Jan. 7, 2019) was issued to address examiner and applicant confusion following *Alice*. It structures the Alice/Mayo test into a **three-prong analytical framework** plus a preliminary step. **The Three-Prong Structure:** **Preliminary Step (Step 1 of the PEG):** Does the claim recite a **statutory category** (process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter)? If no, reject immediately. If yes, proceed to Step 2A. **Step 2A, Prong One:** Does the claim **recite** a judicial exception (law of nature, natural phenomenon, abstract idea)? If no, the claim is eligible. If yes, proceed to Prong Two. **Step 2A, Prong Two:** Is the judicial exception **integrated into a "practical application"**? If yes, the claim is eligible (no need to reach Step 2B). This prong looks for: - A specific improvement to the functioning of a computer or technology (e.g., *Enfish*). - A particular machine or manufacture (e.g., *Diehr*'s rubber press). - A transformation of an article. - Other meaningful limits on the use of the judicial exception. If no, proceed to Step 2B. **Step 2B:** Does the claim add an **"inventive concept"** — additional elements that amount to "significantly more" than the judicial exception? If yes, eligible. If no, reject. **Why the PEG is a "streamline" over pure Alice/Mayo analysis:** 1. **Practical Application Shortcut:** The PEG's Prong Two provides a **new eligibility pathway** that was not explicit in *Alice*. Even if a claim is "directed to" a judicial exception, it can still be eligible if the exception is integrated into a practical application — without needing to satisfy the more demanding "inventive concept" requirement of Step 2B. 2. **Clearer Sub-Categories:** The PEG catalogues three sub-categories of abstract ideas (mathematical concepts, mental processes, organizing human activity) and provides examples, reducing examiner inconsistency. 3. **Examiner Workflow:** The flowchart-style approach gives examiners a decision tree, reducing ad hoc judgments. **Hypothetical Illustration:** > Claim: *"A self-referential database table stored in computer memory, wherein each row contains column metadata enabling faster query processing compared to conventional databases."* - **PEG Step 1:** Recites a "machine" (computer memory) — statutory category. Proceed. - **PEG Step 2A, Prong One:** The claim arguably recites an abstract idea (data storage/retrieval). Proceed to Prong Two. - **PEG Step 2A, Prong Two:** The self-referential table **improves the functioning of the computer itself** (faster query processing) — this is a **practical application** under *Enfish v. Microsoft* (2016). **Eligible.** No need to reach Step 2B. **Conclusion:** The 2019 PEG has not changed the law, but it has substantially **clarified the analytical process**, providing applicants and examiners with a structured roadmap to determine patent eligibility of computer-related inventions. *(Answer completion: 7 marks — 3 marks for the three-prong structure with explanations, 2 marks for the rationale of streamlining, 2 marks for the hypothetical illustration.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > - **Mere case-name dropping without reasoning:** Examiners deduct up to 40% marks if you only write *"In Alice Corp. the Supreme Court held software is not patentable"* without applying the two-step test. Always **apply** the test to a fact pattern. > - **Confusing § 101 with § 103:** Students often discuss "novelty" and "inventive step" (§ 102/§ 103) when the question is about § 101 **subject-matter eligibility**. Keep them separate. > - **Forgetting the statutory citation:** Always cite **35 U.S.C. § 101** in answers — this is the primary statute for this topic. > - **Ignoring the 2019 PEG:** The PEG is the **operative framework** for examiners. Citing only *Alice*/*Mayo* without mentioning the PEG results in incomplete answers. > - **In Part B answers, students forget to "conclude":** Always end with a clear **eligible / ineligible** ruling plus a brief **remedial suggestion** if ineligible. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Examinations — Module 4: Legal Position of U.S. on Computer Related Patents** ### **Statutory Foundation** - ☑ **35 U.S.C. § 101** is the operative statute. The four statutory categories are **process, machine, manufacture, composition of matter**. - ☑ Three **judicial exceptions**: laws of nature, natural phenomena, abstract ideas. - ☑ Computer-related inventions are **patentable in principle** but face heavy scrutiny under § 101. ### **Landmark Cases (Must-Know)** - ☑ ***Benson* (1972):** Pure algorithms are unpatentable. - ☑ ***Flook* (1978):** Routine post-solution activity cannot rescue an abstract idea. - ☑ ***Diehr* (1981):** Software used in a physical industrial process is patent-eligible — the **"as a whole" doctrine**. - ☑ ***Bilski* (2010):** Machine-or-transformation test is **not exclusive**; business methods remain potentially patentable. - ☑ ***Mayo* (2012):** Codified the two-step framework for natural laws. - ☑ ***Alice Corp.* (2014):** Applied Mayo to software/business methods; generic computer implementation is **not "significantly more."** ### **The Two-Step Alice/Mayo Test (Operative)** - ☑ **Step 1:** Is the claim "directed to" a judicial exception (typically an abstract idea)? - ☑ **Step 2:** Is there an "inventive concept" / "significantly more"? ### **The USPTO 2019 Revised PEG (Three Prongs)** - ☑ **Step 1:** Statutory category? - ☑ **Step 2A, Prong One:** Recites a judicial exception? - ☑ **Step 2A, Prong Two:** Integrated into a **practical application**? (If yes → eligible; shortcut) - ☑ **Step 2B:** Inventive concept / significantly more? ### **Key Sub-Categories of "Abstract Ideas"** - ☑ Mathematical concepts (algorithms, formulas) - ☑ Mental processes (human-mind-performable) - ☑ Certain methods of organizing human activity (business methods, commercial practices) ### **Practical Indicators of Patent-Eligible Software** - ☑ Improves the functioning of the computer itself (*Enfish*). - ☑ Solves an Internet-centric technical problem (*DDR Holdings*). - ☑ Tied to a physical transformation (*Diehr*). - ☑ Novel technical improvement to a specific hardware component. - ☑ Recites a specific, non-conventional arrangement of computing elements. ### **Practical Indicators of Patent-Ineligible Software** - ☑ "Apply algorithm X on a generic computer." - ☑ Business method disembodied from technical innovation (*Alice*). - ☑ "Step that could be performed in the human mind." - ☑ Routine data collection, analysis, and display (*Electric Power Group*). ### **Mnemonic for Quick Recall — "BFD-MBA"** - ☑ **B**enson — algorithms - ☑ **F**look — routine steps - ☑ **D**iehr — physical process (the "as a whole" doctrine) - ☑ **M**ayo — two-step framework created - ☑ **B**ilski — business methods potentially patentable - ☑ **A**lice — generic computer = no inventive concept > **Final Note for KTU Students:** When answering any question on this topic in the ESE, structure your answer in four layers: **(i)** Statutory citation (35 U.S.C. § 101), **(ii)** Leading case citation (Benson / Diehr / Alice), **(iii)** Application of the Alice/Mayo test (or PEG) to the facts, **(iv)** Clear conclusion. This structure aligns with KTU's outcome-based evaluation and maximizes marks.

topics.title: Indian Position on Computer Related Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT861 - IPR AND CYBER LAWS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Indian Position on Computer Related Patents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟦 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Computer Related Invention (CRI)** in the Indian patent context refers to any invention that claims or incorporates at least one novel feature realized through the use of a computer, computer network, or other programmable apparatus, where the invention may or may not include a computer program element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Computer-related inventions" are inventions involving the use of computers, computer networks, or other programmable apparatus, and include the following — business methods, computer programs per se, and algorithms."*

In India, the patentability of such inventions is primarily governed by **Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2002 via the Patent (Amendment) Ordinance, 2004, and Patents Rules, 2006).

## 1.2 The Statutory Exclusion — Section 3(k)

Section 3(k) of the Indian Patents Act explicitly states:

> *"A mathematical or business method or a computer program or algorithms per se or a computer program per se are not inventions and shall not be considered as capable of registration as an invention."*

This is a **subject-matter exclusion**, NOT a novelty or inventive step rejection.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe vs. The Dish"

Think of a computer program as a **recipe book**:
- A **recipe** itself (the list of ingredients and steps) cannot be patented — it is information.
- However, a **new chemical compound** created using a novel process described in the recipe **CAN** be patented — because the *output* is a new, tangible, technical contribution.
- Similarly, **a computer program "per se"** (just lines of code in isolation) is **not patentable** in India.
- But if that program produces a **new technical effect, hardware interaction, or measurable improvement in a technical system**, it *may* be patentable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Per Se Doctrine:** The Latin phrase *per se* means "by itself" or "in isolation." Section 3(k) excludes computer programs *as they exist in isolation*, but does NOT exclude them when integrated into a technical process that produces a novel technical outcome.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning in Indian Patent Law |
|---|---|
| **Per Se** | "By itself" — a thing considered in isolation, stripped of its technical application context |
| **Technical Contribution** | A feature that solves a technical problem using technical means |
| **Business Method** | A method of conducting commercial activity (e.g., auction, insurance underwriting) |
| **Algorithm** | A finite, defined sequence of computational steps producing a result |
| **Examiner** | A patent officer empowered to test patentability under the Act |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟦 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statutory Framework — Pyramidal Hierarchy

India's position on computer-related patents rests on a four-tier legal pyramid:

**Tier 1 — Domestic Statute:**
- **The Patents Act, 1970** (as amended): The mother legislation.
- **Section 3(k)**: The explicit non-patentability clause for CRIs.

**Tier 2 — Administrative Guidelines:**
- **CRI Guidelines, 2016**: First comprehensive manual for examiners.
- **Revised CRI Guidelines, 2021 (Office Memorandum dated 19.09.2021)**: Refined framework post-*Ferid Allani* judgment.

**Tier 3 — Judicial Precedents (Binding):**
- *Yahoo! Inc. v. Controller of Patents* (Delhi HC, 2014) — "technical contribution" test originated.
- *Ferid Allani v. Union of India* (Delhi HC, 2015) — Clarified that Section 3(k) is to be read as a whole, not piece-meal.
- *Microsoft Technology Licensing LLC v. Controller General of Patents* (Delhi HC, 2016) — Allowed patent for a computer-readable medium.
- *M/S Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson v. Intex Technologies* — Reinforced novelty assessment.
- *Novozymes v. Assistant Controller of Patents* (2015) — Drawn to the broader interpretation of "invention."

**Tier 4 — International Obligations:**
- **TRIPS Agreement, Article 27**: Requires patents to be available for inventions in *all fields of technology*, subject to limited exclusions.
- **DOHA Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health (2001)**: Recognizes sovereign right to define patentability criteria.

## 2.2 The Four-Part Patentability Test (The "Technical Contribution" Test)

A CRI is patentable in India if, and only if, it satisfies **all four limbs** of the judicial test laid down in *Yahoo v. CGPDTM* and reinforced in *Ferid Allani*:

1. **Novelty:** The invention must not fall under prior art (Section 2(1)(j)).
2. **Inventive Step:** A person skilled in the art (PSITA) must not have arrived at it as an obvious next step (Section 2(1)(ja)).
3. **Industrial Application:** It must be capable of industrial application (Section 2(1)(ac)).
4. **Technical Contribution:** It must produce a *technical effect* or *technical advancement* — solving a technical problem by technical means.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU High-Yield Mnemonic: "N-I-I-T"**
> **N**ovelty → **I**nventive Step → **I**ndustrial Application → **T**echnical Contribution.
> All four must be answered "YES" for a CRI to be granted. A single "NO" = rejection.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield "Formula" Sheet (Legal Tests as Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> In legal/management subjects, "formulas" are replaced by **tests, criteria, and binary decision rules** which the examiner expects students to reproduce verbatim.

| **Test / Rule** | **Stated As** | **Origin** | **Applicability** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical Contribution Test | $\text{Patentable} \iff \text{Novel} \land \text{Inventive} \land \text{Industrial} \land \text{TechnicalEffect}$ | Yahoo v. CGPDTM (2014) | All CRI applications post-2014 |
| Hardware Test | $\text{Patentable} \iff \text{NewHardware} \lor \text{TechnicalImprovement} \lor \text{NewMethodOfManufacture}$ | Ferid Allani (2015) | Read down of Sec 3(k) |
| Fanuc Corp Test | $\text{Not Per Se} \iff \text{PSITA would expect} \lor \text{anticipate} \lor \text{deduce the effect}$ | Microsoft v. CGPDTM (2016) | Computer-implemented inventions |
| Section 3(k) Exclusion | $\text{Excluded} \iff \text{MathMethod} \lor \text{BusinessMethod} \lor \text{ProgramPerSe} \lor \text{AlgorithmPerSe}$ | Patents Act 1970 | Hard statutory bar |
| TRIPS Article 27 Compliance | $\text{Patents Available} \iff \forall \text{FieldOfTechnology (with limited exclusions)}$ | WTO TRIPS | Macro policy check |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Safeguard:** The phrase "technical contribution" was **not** in the original Patents Act 1970. It is a **judicial interpolation** introduced by the Delhi High Court. Examiners expect students to attribute it correctly to case law, NOT to the statute.

## 2.4 What is NOT Patentable vs. What IS Patentable

| **EXCLUDED (Section 3(k))** | **POTENTIALLY PATENTABLE (post-screening)** |
|---|---|
| A computer program code listing | A program stored on a hardware medium producing technical effect |
| Pure mathematical formula $f(x) = x^2$ | Cryptographic algorithm implemented in secure hardware |
| Abstract algorithm for sorting data | Compression algorithm that improves storage efficiency measurably |
| Business method of online auction (abstract) | A method of conducting an online auction with novel cryptographic verification |
| Mental method of mental calculation | A machine-readable medium carrying instructions for industrial process control |
| Software per se on a generic computer | Software coupled to specific hardware producing novel output |

## 2.5 The 2021 Revised CRI Guidelines — Key Shifts

The **Office Memorandum dated 19.09.2021** (issued by the Office of the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks) clarified the following for examiners:

- Computer programs **per se** remain excluded.
- But computer programs are **not excluded** in **every form** — only when claimed as such.
- If the claim is for a **system** (hardware + software architecture) or a **process** producing a technical effect, the application is **to be examined on merits**.
- Examiners were directed to use the **"novel hardware"** and **"technical advancement"** tests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2021 guidelines mark a **pragmatic shift** from the rigid 2016 position. The current Indian stance can be summarized as: *"Program per se is out. Program embedded in a technical system is potentially in."*

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

For KTU students entering software/IT industries, this topic governs:
- **Software patent filing strategy** for startups (huge in Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune).
- **Freedom-to-operate (FTO) analysis** for tech products.
- **IP valuation** of software-heavy products.
- **Cross-border licensing** between US (where software is patentable) and India (where it's restricted).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟦 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Patentability Decision Pipeline

A KTU examiner expects students to write the **step-by-step screening algorithm** that a patent examiner follows upon receiving a CRI application. Here is the exhaustive, step-by-step walkthrough.

**Step 0 — Application Received:**
An applicant files Form 1 + Form 2 (provisional or complete specification) with the Indian Patent Office.

**Step 1 — First-Level Filter (Section 3(k) Bar):**

The examiner asks:

$$
\text{Is the claim directed to} \begin{cases} \text{a mathematical method per se?} \\ \text{a business method per se?} \\ \text{a computer program per se?} \\ \text{an algorithm per se?} \end{cases}
$$

If the answer is **YES to all**, the examiner issues a **First Examination Report (FER)** under Section 3(k) alone and the application proceeds to hearing.

**Step 2 — Second-Level Filter (Construction of Claims):**

Following the *Ferid Allani* doctrine, the examiner must read the claim **as a whole**:

$$
\text{Claim Construction Rule} = \text{Read claims as a whole} + \text{Combine elements} \neq \text{isolate "computer program" tag}
$$

That is, the examiner must NOT look at the computer program tag in isolation; they must see if the program, when read with its hardware, produces a technical effect.

**Step 3 — Technical Effect Assessment:**

$$
\text{Technical Effect} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 4 (Section 2 tests)} \\ \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Reject under Sec 3(k)} \end{cases}
$$

A *technical effect* includes:
- A change in the operation of a hardware device.
- A measurable improvement in speed, accuracy, memory, energy.
- A new method of manufacture or processing.

**Step 4 — Novelty (Section 2(1)(j)) Check:**

$$
\text{Novelty} \iff \text{No single prior art reference anticipates every element of the claim}
$$

The examiner searches databases: Indian, USPTO, EPO, JPO, WIPO. If anticipating, rejection.

**Step 5 — Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja)) Check:**

$$
\text{InventiveStep} \iff \text{PSITA}_{\text{domain}} \text{would not have arrived at the invention obviosuly}
$$

A *Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA)* is a hypothetical competent practitioner in the relevant field, NOT a layperson and NOT a genius.

**Step 6 — Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac)) Check:**

$$
\text{IndustrialApp} \iff \text{Invention has a definite, useful, tangible result in any industry}
$$

A software that does not produce an industrial-utility result is rejected.

**Step 7 — Final Order:**

$$
\text{Outcome} = \begin{cases} \text{Grant (if all 4 filters pass)} \\ \text{Reject (if any one fails)} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Case Law Walkthrough — Yahoo v. Controller (2014)

This is the **single most cited case** for this topic in KTU papers.

**Facts of the Case:**
- Yahoo! Inc. filed a patent application for a system/method of generating a customized user profile based on clickstream data, with an interactive dashboard.
- The Controller of Patents rejected the application under Section 3(k), holding it to be a "computer program per se."

**Issue:**
- Whether the application falls under Section 3(k)?
- Whether the claims, read as a whole, produce a technical contribution?

**Arguments Advanced:**

For Yahoo:
- The invention is not just software — it provides a *technically better* database query mechanism, faster response time, and a real-time interactive dashboard on a server-client system.
- The integration of clickstream tracking, profile generation, and live dashboard updates is a *technical system*, not mere data processing.
- A blanket reading of Section 3(k) would violate TRIPS Article 27 which mandates patents in *all* fields of technology.

For the Controller:
- The claims merely describe a method of presenting information on a screen — this is a *mental/business method*.
- The "technical effect" is just better screen display — not a hardware-level technical contribution.

**Holding (Justice Pratibha M. Singh):**
- The court held that Section 3(k) must be read as a whole.
- The words "per se" indicate that only those mathematical methods, business methods, computer programs, or algorithms that are claimed **in isolation** are excluded.
- If the claim is for a system producing a **technical effect** or **technical advancement**, the same is **not excluded** by Section 3(k).
- The case was remanded to the Controller for reconsideration.

**Key Principle Established:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Technical Contribution Test} = \text{Examine claims as a whole} + \text{Identify hardware/technical integration}}
$$

## 3.3 Case Law Walkthrough — Microsoft Technology Licensing LLC (2016)

**Facts:**
- Microsoft filed a patent for a "computer-readable medium" containing instructions to enforce software license compliance.

**Holding:**
- The court held that a claim for a *computer-readable medium* is not the same as a claim for a *computer program per se*.
- A tangible medium (CD, USB, hard disk) carrying the program is a **physical article** and is patent-eligible subject matter.
- The court also accepted the **Fanuc Test**:

$$
\text{Fanuc Test} \iff \text{PSITA} \underset{\text{would expect / anticipate / deduce}}{\text{would not}} \text{the technical effect produced}
$$

## 3.4 Case Law Walkthrough — Ferid Allani v. UoI (2015)

**Facts:**
- Ferid Allani's patent for a method of transmitting data securely over a network was rejected.
- The matter reached the Delhi High Court.

**Holding (Justice Manmohan Singh):**
- The word "per se" must be interpreted strictly.
- Section 3(k) is a *whitelist of exclusions*, not a blacklist.
- Computer programs that are part of a hardware system producing a new technical contribution **ARE patentable**.
- The court prescribed the **Hardware Test**:

$$
\text{Hardware Test} = \begin{cases} \text{New hardware produced?} \\ \text{Improvement in known hardware?} \\ \text{New method of manufacture using a computer?} \end{cases}
$$

If any of the three yields YES, the invention is not excluded.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Final Synthesis Table

| **Claim Type** | **Section 3(k) Bar?** | **Patentable?** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure algorithm for sorting numbers | YES (alg per se) | ❌ NO | No hardware, no technical effect |
| Program on a USB drive | NO (tangible medium) | ✅ Potentially YES | Fanuc/Microsoft ratio |
| E-commerce auction method (abstract) | YES (business per se) | ❌ NO | Pure business logic |
| Auction system with encrypted server architecture | NO (technical integration) | ✅ YES | Ferid Allani / Hardware Test |
| Software on generic computer | YES (PSITA obvious) | ❌ NO | Microsoft (Fanuc) test fails |
| Software on specific hardware (e.g., DSP, FPGA) producing new output | NO | ✅ YES | Hardware test satisfied |
| Mathematical formula $y = \sin(x)$ | YES (math per se) | ❌ NO | Pure mental/math method |
| Mathematical formula implemented in radar to reduce noise | NO (application) | ✅ YES | Technical effect established |
| AI/ML model weights (per se) | YES (alg per se) | ❌ NO | Patent office 2021 stand |
| AI/ML system integrated in autonomous vehicle (collision avoidance) | NO | ✅ Potentially YES | Technical effect on hardware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The "Potentially" qualifier means the application must STILL pass novelty, inventive step, and industrial application. The technical contribution test only gets you *past Section 3(k)*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟦 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — CRI Patentability Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CRI Application Received at IPO"] --> B{"Is claim a math or business method or program or algorithm per se?"}
    B -- "YES (all four)" --> C["Issue FER under Section 3k"]
    B -- "NO or Mixed" --> D{"Read claims as a whole (Ferid Allani rule)"}
    D --> E{"Does the claim produce a technical effect on hardware?"}
    E -- "NO" --> F["Reject under Section 3k"]
    E -- "YES" --> G["Pass Section 3k filter, proceed to merits"]
    G --> H{"Novel under Section 2(1)j?"}
    H -- "NO" --> I["Reject for lack of novelty"]
    H -- "YES" --> J{"Inventive step under Section 2(1)ja?"}
    J -- "NO" --> K["Reject for obviousness to PSITA"]
    J -- "YES" --> L{"Industrial application under Section 2(1)ac?"}
    L -- "NO" --> M["Reject for lack of utility"]
    L -- "YES" --> N["Grant Patent"]
    C --> O["Hearing for Applicant"]
    O --> P{"Applicant Amends Claims?"}
    P -- "YES" --> D
    P -- "NO" --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Four-Tier Legal Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tier1["Tier 1: Domestic Statute"]
        A1["Patents Act 1970"]
        A2["Section 3k Exclusion"]
    end
    subgraph Tier2["Tier 2: Administrative Guidelines"]
        B1["CRI Guidelines 2016"]
        B2["Revised CRI Guidelines 2021"]
    end
    subgraph Tier3["Tier 3: Judicial Precedents"]
        C1["Yahoo v CGPDTM 2014"]
        C2["Ferid Allani v UoI 2015"]
        C3["Microsoft v CGPDTM 2016"]
    end
    subgraph Tier4["Tier 4: International Obligation"]
        D1["TRIPS Article 27"]
        D2["Doha Declaration 2001"]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    A2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    A1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    B2 --> C2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Venn Diagram — The Patentable CRI Universe

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Section 3k Exclusion Zone"]
        A1["Math Per Se"]
        A2["Business Per Se"]
        A3["Algorithm Per Se"]
        A4["Program Per Se"]
    end
    subgraph B["Technical Contribution Zone"]
        B1["Hardware Integration"]
        B2["Technical Effect"]
        B3["New Method of Manufacture"]
    end
    subgraph C["Overlapping Patentable Zone"]

    end
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2

    A -. "NOT here" .-> C
    B -. "Must be in both" .-> C
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant APP as Applicant
    participant IPO as Indian Patent Office

    APP->>IPO: File Form 1 and Form 2 Specification
    IPO->>EXM: Forward Application
    EXM->>EXM: Step 1 Section 3k Filter

    EXM->>EXM: Step 3 Technical Effect Test
    EXM->>EXM: Step 4 Novelty Search
    EXM->>EXM: Step 5 Inventive Step Test
    EXM->>EXM: Step 6 Industrial Application
    alt All Tests Pass
        EXM->>CTL: Recommend Grant

    else Any Test Fails

    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟦 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**"What do you understand by the term 'computer program per se' as used in Section 3(k) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970? Why is it excluded from patentability?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 80 words):**

The phrase **"computer program per se"** appears in **Section 3(k)** of the Patents Act, 1970. The Latin term *per se* means "in itself" or "in isolation." Section 3(k) excludes computer programs **considered in isolation**, i.e., as a mere set of instructions on a computer-readable medium without any novel hardware or technical effect. The exclusion is based on the policy stance that abstract software, mathematical formulas, and pure algorithms do not qualify as a *technical invention* in the Indian framework, even though they may be novel and non-obvious. However, when integrated with hardware to produce a technical contribution, they are NOT excluded (per *Yahoo v. CGPDTM*).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
**"State the 'Technical Contribution' test laid down by the Delhi High Court for patentability of computer-related inventions in India."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 70 words):**

The **Technical Contribution Test** was propounded by the Delhi High Court in *Yahoo! Inc. v. Controller of Patents* (2014) and reinforced in *Ferid Allani v. Union of India* (2015). The test states that a computer-related invention (CRI) is patentable if its **claims, read as a whole**, produce a **technical effect** or **technical advancement** in a technical field. The test requires examining the integration of the program with hardware to determine whether it solves a technical problem using technical means, and is not merely a software-in-isolation.

---

## 📝 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** A 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must attempt **ONE of two alternatives** (OR option). Both alternatives below are independent.

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** "Discuss in detail the statutory position of computer-related inventions under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Critically analyse Section 3(k) with reference to relevant case law." (7 marks)
